topics.title: Planning an Investigation Programme for Geotechnical Investigation – Necessity, Scope and Objectives, Planning of a Sub-surface Exploration Program, Factors to be Considered, Reconnaissance, Preliminary and Detailed Investigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planning an Investigation Programme for Geotechnical Investigation – Necessity, Scope and Objectives, Planning of a Sub-surface Exploration Program, Factors to be Considered, Reconnaissance, Preliminary and Detailed Investigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Geotechnical Investigation (Subsurface Exploration)** is a systematic, phased, and engineered program of site characterization activities — comprising desk study, reconnaissance, preliminary exploration, and detailed investigation — undertaken to determine the subsurface stratigraphy, groundwater regime, and engineering properties of soil and rock masses at a proposed civil engineering project site, so as to enable safe, economical, and serviceable foundation and earthwork design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A geotechnical investigation is *not* a single act of drilling; it is a **planned decision-making workflow** that progressively narrows geological uncertainty from regional scale → site scale → foundation-element scale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Diagnosis" Model

Think of a geotechnical investigation exactly like a **doctor diagnosing a patient before surgery**:

| Medical Workflow | Geotechnical Equivalent |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| Patient's medical history & symptoms | **Reconnaissance** (existing maps, walkover, prior reports) |
| First physical check-up & vitals | **Preliminary Investigation** (few boreholes, broad stratigraphy) |
| Blood tests, MRI, CT scan | **Detailed Investigation** (closely spaced boreholes, SPT, CPT, lab tests) |
| Surgeon's operation plan | **Geotechnical Design Report & Foundation Recommendation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Lock:** Just as a surgeon will not operate without an MRI, a geotechnical engineer **must not** design foundations without a phased, well-planned investigation. Skipping reconnaissance is like operating blind.

## 1.3 Necessity — Why is Geotechnical Investigation Mandatory?

The necessity arises from the **inherent heterogeneity and anisotropy** of natural soil/rock deposits and the catastrophic consequences of design based on assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Reasons for Investigation (must-know for KTU):**
> 1. **Safety** — Prevention of bearing capacity failure, excessive settlement, slope failure, liquefaction.
> 2. **Economy** — Avoidance of *over*-conservative (wasteful) and *under*-conservative (unsafe) design.
> 3. **Compliance** — Statutory requirement under **IS 1892 (Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation)** and **IS 2131** (for SPT).
> 4. **Selection of Foundation Type** — Shallow vs. deep foundation decision.
> 5. **Construction Planning** — Dewatering, excavation support, ground improvement needs.
> 6. **Environmental & Legal** — Contamination assessment, right-of-way, neighbor protection.

## 1.4 Scope of Geotechnical Investigation

The scope defines the **breadth** of the study and typically includes:

- Determination of soil/rock profile (stratigraphy) with depth.
- Evaluation of **in-situ** properties (SPT-N, CPT, permeability).
- Determination of **groundwater table** position and its fluctuation.
- Recovery of disturbed and **undisturbed samples** for laboratory testing.
- Identification of problematic soils (expansive, collapsible, organic, liquefiable).
- Assessment of chemical properties (pH, sulfates, chlorides) for durability.
- Geotechnical parameters for slope stability, earth pressure, and bearing capacity.

## 1.5 Objectives of a Well-Planned Investigation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** Examiners love the phrase *"The objective is to obtain the required subsurface information at the **least cost** consistent with **adequate accuracy** and **safety**."*

The primary objectives are:

1. To establish the **vertical and lateral extent** of each soil/rock stratum.
2. To determine the **engineering properties** (strength, compressibility, permeability) of each stratum.
3. To locate the **groundwater table** and assess seepage conditions.
4. To identify any **geological hazards** (faults, cavities, soft pockets, artesian conditions).
5. To recommend the **type, depth, and bearing capacity** of the most suitable foundation.
6. To provide data for **earthwork design** (cuts, fills, embankments, retaining structures).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Planning of a Sub-Surface Exploration Program — The Master Framework

A KTU-grade exploration program is built on **four pillars**: Purpose, Phase, Plan, and Prudence.

### Pillar 1: Define the **Purpose**
- Type of project (building, bridge, dam, tunnel, highway, offshore).
- Type of loading (static, dynamic, seismic).
- Type of foundation envisaged (shallow, deep, mat, pile).

### Pillar 2: Define the **Phase**
The program is universally divided into **three sequential phases** (this is the most frequently asked KTU structure):

**Phase A — Reconnaissance**
**Phase B — Preliminary Investigation**
**Phase C — Detailed Investigation**

### Pillar 3: Define the **Plan**
- Number of boreholes.
- Depth of each borehole.
- Spacing of boreholes.
- Type of sampling and in-situ tests.
- Location of groundwater observation wells.

### Pillar 4: Apply **Prudence**
- Stop work if anomalies are detected (e.g., refusal, sudden soft pocket, gas).
- Modify plan adaptively — never follow a rigid plan into a geological surprise.

## 2.2 Factors to be Considered in Planning

These factors **directly control** the spacing, depth, and number of boreholes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10 Governing Factors (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Type and size of the project** (a 50-storey tower needs deeper, denser exploration than a single-storey house).
> 2. **Anticipated type of foundation** (shallow → shallow boreholes; pile → deeper boreholes).
> 3. **Geological complexity of the site** (homogeneous alluvium vs. complex weathered rock).
> 4. **Spacing of footings / loaded areas** (closer footings need closer boreholes).
> 5. **Magnitude and distribution of structural loads** (uniform vs. concentrated).
> 6. **Accessibility of the site** for drilling rigs.
> 7. **Availability of existing geotechnical data** (previous reports, geological maps).
> 8. **Local building codes and statutory requirements** (IS 1892, IS 2131, IS 2720).
> 9. **Budget and time constraints** (always balanced against required accuracy).
> 10. **Environmental and groundwater considerations** (water table depth, contamination risk).

## 2.3 Spacing of Boreholes (IS 1892 & ASCE Guidelines)

The following table is a **must-memorize KTU asset**:

| Type of Project | Spacing of Boreholes |
|-----------------|----------------------|
| Multi-storey buildings, water tanks | **15 m to 30 m** |
| Industrial & residential layouts | **30 m to 60 m** |
| Highways & runways (linear) | **250 m to 500 m** along centreline |
| Dams & large reservoirs | **30 m to 100 m** (with closer spacing at critical sections) |
| Bridges (per pier/abutment) | **1 to 2 boreholes per abutment, 1 to 2 per pier** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If the structure is of a regular plan, place boreholes near the **corners and centre**. If irregular, place at the **footing locations**.

## 2.4 Depth of Exploration (The Critical Calculation)

The required depth of exploration is governed by two criteria:
- **Stress Influence Zone** (where induced stress is significant).
- **Weak Stratum** detection (must penetrate any soft/compressible layer).

### KTU Formula Sheet (with units)

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| Minimum depth below footing (cohesive soil) | $D_{min} = 1.5 \cdot B$ to $2.0 \cdot B$ | m | $B$ = footing width |
| Minimum depth below footing (cohesionless soil) | $D_{min} = 3.0 \cdot B$ to $4.0 \cdot B$ | m | Loose sand requires more |
| Depth for mat foundation | $D_{min} = 1.5 \cdot B_{mat}$ | m | $B_{mat}$ = mat width |
| Depth for pile foundation | $D_{min} = (D_f + 5) \text{ m or 10}\cdot B_{pile}$, whichever is greater | m | $D_f$ = pile depth |
| Depth for high-rise (>15 storey) | $D_{min} = (H/3) \text{ to } (H/2)$ | m | $H$ = building height |
| Stress influence depth (general) | $\sigma_z = 0.1 \cdot q$ contour depth | m | 10% of applied stress rule |
| SPT N-value zone of influence | $D = (N/15) \cdot B$ (empirical) | m | Not in IS, useful for design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Breaking Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute values and conditions are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to keep tables intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in practice:**
> - In the **Kerala context** (KTU-specific relevance), lateritic and alluvial soils show extreme variability — investigation prevents the **catastrophic settlement failures** seen in Kochi and Calicut.
> - For **high-rise buildings in Trivandrum and Kochi** (e.g., Marine Drive, Technopark), the $(H/3)$ to $(H/2)$ rule governs because pile foundations extend into weathered rock.
> - For **NH-66 highway widening projects**, the 250 m spacing along the centreline balances cost with adequate stratigraphic mapping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the "Depth = 2B to 3B" Rule for Shallow Foundations

This is the most-asked derivation in KTU for this module. It comes from **Boussinesq's elastic stress distribution** theory.

### Step 1: Stress at Depth due to a Strip Footing

For a flexible strip footing of width $B$ carrying a uniform pressure $q$, the vertical stress at depth $z$ on the centreline (from Boussinesq) is:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{q}{\pi} \left[ 2 \alpha + \sin(2\alpha) \right]
$$

where $2\alpha$ is the angle subtended at the point by the loaded width.

### Step 2: Express in terms of $z/B$

Substituting the geometry, $\tan(\alpha) = B / (2z)$, the equation becomes:

$$
\sigma_z = \frac{q}{\pi} \left[ 2 \alpha + \sin(2\alpha) \right], \quad \text{where } \alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{B}{2z}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Compute the 10% Influence Depth

We need to find $z$ at which $\sigma_z / q = 0.1$ (the conventional 10% influence zone). Solving iteratively:

$$
\frac{\sigma_z}{q} = 0.1 \implies \frac{1}{\pi} \left[ 2 \alpha + \sin(2\alpha) \right] = 0.1
$$

This gives $2\alpha \approx 33.6°$, hence:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{B}{2z} = \tan(16.8°) \approx 0.302
$$

Therefore:

$$
z = \frac{B}{2 \times 0.302} \approx 1.66 \cdot B
$$

### Step 4: Apply Factor of Safety on Depth

For design safety and to capture a weak stratum if present:

$$
D_{min} \approx 1.5 \cdot B \quad \text{(lower bound, cohesive soil)}
$$

$$
D_{min} \approx 2.0 \cdot B \quad \text{(recommended, mixed soil)}
$$

$$
D_{min} \approx 3.0 \cdot B \text{ to } 4.0 \cdot B \quad \text{(cohesionless/loose sand)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cohesionless needs more depth:** Loose sand has low stiffness, so the stress bulb spreads wider and deeper. The 10% stress contour reaches a greater depth, demanding deeper exploration to capture all settlement-contributing layers.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reconnaissance vs. Preliminary vs. Detailed

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparative matrix is the single most important table for KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the contrast.**

| Parameter | Reconnaissance | Preliminary Investigation | Detailed Investigation |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Stage** | Pre-feasibility | Feasibility / tender design | Final design / construction |
| **Objective** | Identify geological framework | Broad stratigraphy & major anomalies | Detailed properties for each stratum |
| **Methods** | Desk study, aerial photos, geological maps, site walkover, talking to locals | Few widely spaced boreholes, trial pits, geophysical surveys | Closely spaced boreholes, SPT, CPT, undisturbed sampling, lab testing |
| **Borehole Spacing** | None drilled | 30 m to 100 m | 10 m to 30 m |
| **Borehole Depth** | Not applicable | $1.5B$ to $2.0B$ | $2.0B$ to $3.0B$ or to rock |
| **Sampling** | None | Disturbed | Disturbed + Undisturbed |
| **Tests** | Visual classification | Index tests (sieve, Atterberg) | Full lab (triaxial, consolidation, permeability) + in-situ (SPT, CPT, VST) |
| **Cost** | Very low (≈ 1-2% of total) | Moderate (≈ 10-15%) | High (≈ 80-85%) |
| **Output** | Geological feasibility report | Preliminary borehole log & soil profile | Final geotechnical design report with foundation recommendation |
| **Decision Made** | Site selection | Foundation type (shallow vs deep) | Exact depth, dimensions, bearing capacity |

## 3.3 Decision Logic — When to Move from Preliminary to Detailed

The transition is triggered by answering these four questions:

1. Has the **stratigraphy** been established broadly? If yes → proceed.
2. Have any **major anomalies** (sinkholes, soft pockets, organic layers) been identified? Document and add boreholes.
3. Is the **groundwater table** at a known, stable position? Install piezometer if not.
4. Are the **loads and foundation type** finalized? If yes, detailed investigation can be precisely targeted.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Calculate minimum depth of exploration for a 3 m × 3 m square footing in loose sand carrying a column load of 1500 kN.**

**Step 1:** Compute the contact pressure.

$$
q = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1500}{3 \times 3} = 166.67 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the soil condition. **Loose sand** → cohesionless.

**Step 3:** Apply the rule for cohesionless soil:

$$
D_{min} = 3.0 \cdot B = 3.0 \times 3.0 = 9.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the upper bound rule for loose sand:

$$
D_{min} = 4.0 \cdot B = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5:** Select the recommended design depth (3B for normal loose sand, 4B for very loose):

$$
\boxed{D_{min} = 9.0 \text{ m (recommended)}, \text{ extend to } 12.0 \text{ m if } N < 10}
$$

**Step 6:** Cross-check with stress influence (Boussinesq 10% rule): for square footing, $z \approx 1.5B$ to $2.0B$ for the 10% stress zone, but to capture **all settlement-contributing layers** in loose sand, the **3B rule is more conservative and is preferred**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Boreholes must be drilled to a minimum **9 m** below ground level (or 9 + footing depth from GL), and one boring per footing cluster with at least **3 SPTs per metre** in the founding zone.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Phased Investigation Workflow (Master Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Conception] --> B[Phase 1: Reconnaissance]
    B --> B1[Desk Study: Geological Maps]
    B --> B2[Site Walkover & Visual Inspection]
    B --> B3[Review of Past Reports & Local Knowledge]
    B1 --> C{Is Site Feasible?}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -- No --> C1[Select Alternate Site or Reject]
    C -- Yes --> D[Phase 2: Preliminary Investigation]
    D --> D1[Wide Spacing Boreholes 30-100 m]
    D --> D2[Trial Pits and Geophysical Surveys]
    D --> D3[Index Tests: Sieve, Atterberg]
    D1 --> E{Foundation Type Decided?}
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E -- No --> E1[Add Boreholes, Re-evaluate]
    E -- Yes --> F[Phase 3: Detailed Investigation]
    F --> F1[Closely Spaced Boreholes 10-30 m]
    F --> F2[SPT, CPT, VST In-Situ Tests]
    F --> F3[Undisturbed Sampling for Lab Tests]
    F1 --> G[Geotechnical Design Report]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Foundation Type, Depth, Capacity]
    G --> G2[Earthwork & Slope Design Parameters]
    G --> G3[Construction Recommendations]
```

## 4.2 Borehole Spacing Decision Tree (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Project Type Identified] --> S2{Structure Type?}
    S2 -- Multi-storey Building --> S3[Spacing: 15-30 m]
    S2 -- Industrial Layout --> S4[Spacing: 30-60 m]
    S2 -- Highway or Runway --> S5[Spacing: 250-500 m along CL]
    S2 -- Dam or Reservoir --> S6[Spacing: 30-100 m, critical at abutments]
    S2 -- Bridge --> S7[1-2 BHs per pier and abutment]
    S3 --> D1{Soil Condition Known?}
    S4 --> D1
    S5 --> D1
    S6 --> D1
    S7 --> D1
    D1 -- Yes, Homogeneous --> D2[Adopt Lower Bound Spacing]
    D1 -- No, Heterogeneous --> D3[Adopt Upper Bound Spacing + Add Probes]
    D2 --> D4[Final Spacing Selected]
    D3 --> D4
```

## 4.3 Depth of Exploration Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Footing Width B and Soil Type] --> P2{Soil Category?}
    P2 -- Cohesive Clay --> P3[Apply D = 1.5B to 2.0B]
    P2 -- Cohesionless Sand --> P4[Apply D = 3.0B to 4.0B]
    P2 -- Mixed / Unknown --> P5[Apply D = 2.0B and Add One SPT Every 1.5 m]
    P3 --> P6{Is Soft Stratum Below?}
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Extend Depth Through Entire Soft Stratum + 3 m into Stiff Layer]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Bore to Recommended Depth, Terminate in Competent Stratum]
    P7 --> P9[Final Depth D]
    P8 --> P9
```

## 4.4 The Three-Phase Comparative Block (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[PHASE 1: RECONNAISSANCE]
        R1[Inputs: Maps, Photos, History] --> R2[Process: Visual + Desk]
        R2 --> R3[Output: Feasibility Verdict]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[PHASE 2: PRELIMINARY]
        PR1[Inputs: Few Boring Locations] --> PR2[Process: Boring, Sampling, Index Tests]
        PR2 --> PR3[Output: Broad Profile, Foundation Type]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[PHASE 3: DETAILED]
        DT1[Inputs: Closely Spaced Borings] --> DT2[Process: SPT, CPT, UDS, Lab Tests]
        DT2 --> DT3[Output: Final Design Parameters]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
    PHASE3 --> FINAL[Geotechnical Design Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Define the term 'Geotechnical Investigation'. State its necessity in any major civil engineering project."**

**Model Answer:**
Geotechnical investigation is the systematic engineering study of subsurface soil and rock conditions at a project site to determine their physical, mechanical, and hydraulic properties for safe and economical foundation design.

**Necessity:**
- To ensure **structural safety** against bearing capacity failure, settlement, and slope instability.
- To enable **economic design** — avoiding over- or under-conservative foundations.
- To satisfy **statutory codes** (IS 1892, IS 2131, IS 2720).
- To aid in **selection of foundation type** (shallow vs. deep).
- To identify **problematic soils** (expansive, organic, liquefiable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students who write only 2 points lose 1 mark. Always write 4–5 crisp points for a 3-mark question.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Differentiate between reconnaissance and preliminary investigation."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Reconnaissance | Preliminary Investigation |
|---------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Objective | Site feasibility | Broad stratigraphy and foundation type |
| Methods | Desk study, walkover, maps | Few widely spaced boreholes, trial pits |
| Sampling | None | Disturbed samples only |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate |
| Output | Feasibility report | Preliminary profile and recommendation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **necessity, scope, and objectives** of a geotechnical investigation program. State the **IS codes** that govern the planning of such a program in India.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A proposed building has a **raft foundation of 20 m × 20 m** resting on **medium stiff clay**. The contact pressure is estimated at **120 kN/m²**. Determine the **minimum depth of exploration** using the **Boussinesq 10% stress influence criterion** and recommend the number of boreholes.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Necessity** (2 marks):
- Subsurface heterogeneity makes design assumptions unsafe.
- Prevents catastrophic failures (bearing, settlement, slope, liquefaction).
- Mandated by IS 1892 for any major civil engineering project.
- Foundation economy depends on accurate soil parameters.

**Scope** (2 marks):
- Establishing soil/rock stratigraphy laterally and vertically.
- Determining in-situ and laboratory engineering properties.
- Locating groundwater table and its fluctuation.
- Identifying problematic soils and contamination.
- Providing parameters for foundation, slope, and earthwork design.

**Objectives** (2 marks):
- Obtain required subsurface information at the **least cost consistent with accuracy and safety**.
- Provide data for selection of safe and economical foundation.
- Detect subsurface anomalies before construction.

**IS Codes** (1 mark):
- **IS 1892** – Subsurface investigation for foundations.
- **IS 2131** – Standard Penetration Test.
- **IS 2720** – Laboratory testing of soils (multiple parts).
- **IS 4968** – Dynamic cone penetration test.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Raft size: $B = 20$ m (square)
- Soil: Medium stiff clay
- Contact pressure: $q = 120$ kN/m²

**Step 1:** Apply Boussinesq 10% stress influence rule for a **square footing**:

For a square footing of width $B$, the depth at which the vertical stress equals 10% of the applied pressure is approximately:

$$
z_{0.1q} \approx 1.5 \cdot B = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cohesive soil rule:

$$
D_{min} = 1.5 \cdot B = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the upper bound (to capture weak stratum):

$$
D_{min} = 2.0 \cdot B = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Adopt the more conservative (and recommended) value:

$$
\boxed{D_{min} = 40 \text{ m below ground level}}
$$

**Step 5:** Number of boreholes (for a raft, IS 1892 recommends 4–6 boreholes for a large raft):

- **Recommended: 5 boreholes** (4 at corners + 1 at centre), each drilled to **40 m** or **5 m into the bearing stratum**, whichever is greater.
- SPT every **1.5 m** or at every change of stratum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Do NOT** use the strip-footing formula for a square raft. The Boussinesq influence depth differs.
> - **Do NOT** forget to add 1 mark for stating the number and spacing of boreholes in the conclusion.
> - **Do NOT** skip the units (write `m`, not just numbers).
> - **Do NOT** ignore the "penetrate 3–5 m into the bearing stratum" clause — this is a favourite examiner trap.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **factors to be considered in planning a sub-surface exploration program**. State how the spacing of boreholes varies with the **type of structure**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **bridge project** has 4 piers and 2 abutments in **alluvial soil overlying soft clay**. As a geotechnical engineer, outline a **preliminary investigation plan** specifying borehole locations, spacing, depth, and tests to be conducted.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Ten Governing Factors** (5 marks — state any 7 to 8 with one-line explanation each):
1. Type and size of project
2. Anticipated type of foundation
3. Geological complexity
4. Spacing of footings / loaded areas
5. Magnitude and distribution of loads
6. Site accessibility
7. Availability of existing data
8. Local codes (IS 1892)
9. Budget and time
10. Groundwater and environmental factors

**Spacing Table** (2 marks):

| Structure | Spacing |
|-----------|---------|
| Multi-storey building | 15–30 m |
| Industrial layout | 30–60 m |
| Highway | 250–500 m |
| Bridge | 1–2 BHs per pier/abutment |
| Dam | 30–100 m |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Preliminary Investigation Plan for Bridge:**

**Borehole Locations** (2 marks):
- 1 borehole at **each abutment** → 2 boreholes.
- 1 borehole at **each pier** → 4 boreholes.
- **Total = 6 boreholes** plus 1 or 2 **control boreholes** in the flood plain.
- Additional boreholes if stratigraphy shows sudden changes.

**Spacing** (1 mark):
- Boreholes along bridge axis: **30 m to 50 m**.
- Additional offset boreholes 5–10 m from the centreline to check lateral variation in riverbed.

**Depth** (2 marks):
- All boreholes to penetrate the **entire soft clay layer** plus **5 m into the stiff stratum below** or **3 times the pile diameter**, whichever is greater.
- Typical preliminary depth: **30 m to 40 m** in alluvial-over-clay conditions.

**Tests** (2 marks):
- **SPT every 1.5 m** and at every stratum change.
- **Undisturbed sampling** in clay layers (Shelby tube or piston sampler).
- **Vane Shear Test (VST)** in soft clay.
- **Pumping test or falling head test** for permeability.
- **Index tests**: Atterberg limits, grain size, water content, specific gravity.
- **Chemical tests**: pH, sulfates, chlorides (for pile durability).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes for Bridge Questions:**
> - Forgetting **scour depth consideration** — boreholes must extend below anticipated scour level.
> - Not specifying **disturbed vs. undisturbed sampling** distinction.
> - Missing **chemical testing** which is mandatory for marine/coastal bridges (Kerala context).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 times before the exam):**

- [ ] Geotechnical investigation = **phased, planned, decision-driven** process.
- [ ] **Three phases**: Reconnaissance → Preliminary → Detailed.
- [ ] Reconnaissance uses **no boring**; it relies on **desk study and walkover**.
- [ ] Preliminary uses **few widely spaced boreholes** (30–100 m) to decide **foundation type**.
- [ ] Detailed uses **closely spaced boreholes** (10–30 m) to provide **final design parameters**.
- [ ] **Spacing for buildings** = 15–30 m; **spacing for highways** = 250–500 m.
- [ ] **Depth for cohesive soil** = $1.5B$ to $2.0B$.
- [ ] **Depth for cohesionless soil** = $3.0B$ to $4.0B$.
- [ ] **Depth for raft** = $1.5 \cdot B_{mat}$.
- [ ] **Depth for high-rise** = $(H/3)$ to $(H/2)$.
- [ ] **Depth for piles** = pile tip + 5 m or 10 × pile diameter.
- [ ] **Cost split** ≈ 1–2% Recon, 10–15% Preliminary, 80–85% Detailed.
- [ ] **IS 1892** is the master code for sub-surface investigation.
- [ ] **IS 2131** governs the **Standard Penetration Test**.
- [ ] **IS 2720** (multiple parts) governs **laboratory testing of soils**.
- [ ] The 10% stress influence rule is derived from **Boussinesq's theory**.
- [ ] Boreholes must **penetrate the entire soft stratum** + 3–5 m into the stiff layer.
- [ ] In **Kerala lateritic/alluvial soils**, always add **chemical testing** (pH, sulfates).
- [ ] Bridge investigations require boreholes at **each pier and abutment**, plus scour depth consideration.
- [ ] The objective of investigation: **least cost, adequate accuracy, full safety**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024):** "If you remember the three phases, the spacing table, the depth rules, and Boussinesq — you will score full marks on this topic."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I.S. Guidelines for Deciding the Number, Size, Spacing and Depth of Boreholes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** I.S. Guidelines for Deciding the Number, Size, Spacing and Depth of Boreholes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Subsoil exploration** is the systematic process of determining the nature, composition, and engineering properties of the soil and rock strata underlying a proposed construction site. The planning of a **borehole programme** — deciding *how many* boreholes to drill, *how large* they should be, *how far apart* they should be placed, and *how deep* they should extend — is governed by Indian Standard codes, primarily **IS 1892:1979** (Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations), with cross-references to **IS 1080:1985**, **IS 2131:1981**, and **IS 8009 (Part 1):1976**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key IS Codes for Borehole Planning**
> - **IS 1892:1979** — Subsurface investigation for foundations (primary)
> - **IS 1080:1985** — Design and construction of simple spread foundations
> - **IS 8009 (Part 1):1976** — Calculation of settlements (for depth determination)
> - **IS 2131:1981** — Standard Penetration Test
> - **IS 1498:1970** — Classification of soils for general engineering purposes

### Definition of Each Parameter

- **Number of Boreholes (N):** The minimum count of exploratory vertical drill holes required to define the stratigraphic profile of a site with adequate reliability.
- **Size of Boreholes (D):** The diameter of the borehole, generally ranging from **100 mm to 300 mm**, dictated by the sampling and testing requirements.
- **Spacing of Boreholes (S):** The horizontal distance between adjacent boreholes, determined by the uniformity of the deposit and the type/scale of structure.
- **Depth of Boreholes (H):** The vertical penetration below ground level, typically extended up to a zone where the induced stress becomes negligible (≤ **10%** of the applied net pressure for cohesionless soils and ≤ **5%** for cohesive soils).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are a **doctor diagnosing a patient**. Before prescribing treatment, you would:
1. **Take multiple blood samples (Number)** — one sample is never enough; you need several to confirm a pattern.
2. **Use a needle of a specific gauge (Size)** — too thin, you cannot extract enough; too thick, you hurt the patient unnecessarily.
3. **Spread sampling points across the body (Spacing)** — to capture variation in different organs.
4. **Probe deep enough to reach the organ of concern (Depth)** — superficial samples miss the actual disease.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geotechnical Parallel:** A geotechnical engineer is essentially a "doctor for the earth." A borehole programme is the **diagnostic protocol**, and IS 1892 acts as the **clinical guideline** to ensure the subsurface is characterized reliably before a "prescription" (the foundation design) is written.

The earth is **anisotropic and heterogeneous** — meaning soil properties vary spatially in unpredictable ways. Hence, a one-size-fits-all approach fails, and IS guidelines provide a **rational, experience-based framework** to balance **reliability** with **cost-efficiency**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Philosophy of IS 1892

The IS guidelines are based on three core engineering principles:

1. **Pressure Bulb Concept (Boussinesq, 1885):** Stresses induced by a foundation dissipate with depth and lateral spread. The borehole must penetrate the entire **zone of significant stress influence**.
2. **Heterogeneity of Natural Deposits:** Alluvial, residual, and marine soils have variable stratification, requiring dense sampling in critical zones.
3. **Consequence of Failure:** Critical structures (dams, nuclear plants, high-rises) demand higher redundancy in subsurface information than ordinary residential buildings.

## 2.2 Number of Boreholes — IS 1892 Recommendations

The number is governed by the **footprint area** of the proposed structure and the **uniformity of the deposit**.

| Structure Type | Number of Boreholes (as per IS 1892) |
|---|---|
| Small residential building (up to 200 m²) | Minimum **1**, but **3** recommended at corners and centre |
| Multi-storey building (up to 1000 m²) | **1 per 200–300 m²** of built-up area |
| Large commercial/industrial (> 1000 m²) | **1 per 100–250 m²** of built-up area |
| Bridges | Minimum **1 borehole per pier and per abutment** |
| Earth dams | Boreholes along the **dam axis, upstream toe, and downstream toe** at regular intervals |
| Roads / Highways | **1 per 500 m** (preliminary); **1 per 100–150 m** (detailed) |
| Railways | **1 per 250–500 m** along alignment |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Examinations:** For ordinary buildings on uniform soil, assume **1 borehole per 200 m²**. For bridges, always **1 borehole per pier/abutment**.

## 2.3 Size of Boreholes — Diametric Recommendations

The diameter is chosen based on the **type of sample** required and the **ground conditions**.

| Soil/Rock Type | Recommended Borehole Diameter | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Soft clays, loose sands | **100 mm** | Standard penetration sampling only |
| Medium clays, medium dense sands | **150 mm** | Allows undisturbed sampling |
| Stiff clays, dense gravelly soils | **200 mm** | For undisturbed sample extraction |
| Soft/weathered rock | **150–200 mm** | For core drilling |
| Hard rock | **NX (75 mm) core / 150 mm borehole** | Double-tube core barrel for recovery |
| Large foundation investigations | Up to **300 mm** | For in-situ vane shear or plate load test access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum practical diameter = 100 mm.** Below this, sampling tubes cannot be lowered. For pile boreholes, diameter increases to **300–600 mm** depending on pile size.

## 2.4 Spacing of Boreholes — Stratification-Based Logic

The spacing depends on the **degree of heterogeneity** of the deposit and the **stage of investigation**.

| Stage of Investigation | Spacing (Linear Projects) | Spacing (Buildings) |
|---|---|---|
| **Preliminary / Reconnaissance** | **500 m to 1000 m** | **50–100 m** grid |
| **Detailed** | **100 m to 200 m** | **20–40 m** grid |
| **Supplementary** (critical zones) | **30 m to 50 m** | **10–15 m** grid |

For **uniform deposits** (homogeneous alluvium), spacing can be relaxed; for **variable deposits** (fill, marine clays with sand lenses), spacing must be reduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Code Quote (IS 1892):** *"The spacing of boreholes should be such that the variations in the thickness and properties of the strata are detected with sufficient accuracy to permit safe and economic design."*

## 2.5 Depth of Boreholes — The Critical Engineering Decision

Depth is the **most critical** parameter because an inadequate depth can lead to undetected weak compressible layers below the foundation level, causing catastrophic **differential settlements**.

### 2.5.1 Empirical Rules from IS 1892

| Foundation Type | Minimum Borehole Depth Below GL |
|---|---|
| Isolated footing (B = width) | **1.5 B to 2 B** |
| Strip footing (B = width) | **3 B to 4 B** |
| Raft / Mat foundation | **1.5 B to 2 B** (minimum **6 m**) |
| Pile foundation (friction pile) | **Up to pile tip + 5 m** |
| Pile foundation (end-bearing) | **Up to hard rock + 1.5 D_pile** |
| Earth dams | **0.5 to 1.0 times the dam height** or to impervious stratum |
| Bridge piers (open foundation) | **2 B to 3 B** |

### 2.5.2 Analytical Approach: Pressure Influence Zone

The depth should extend to the level where the **net stress increase (Δσ)** due to the foundation load becomes negligible compared to the **effective overburden pressure (σ′₀)**.

For **cohesionless soils:** Stop when $\Delta\sigma \leq 0.10\,q_{net}$

For **cohesive soils:** Stop when $\Delta\sigma \leq 0.05\,q_{net}$

where $q_{net}$ is the net contact pressure.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta\sigma_z = \dfrac{q \cdot B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)}$ | $q$ = net pressure, $B,L$ = footing dimensions, $z$ = depth | kPa, m | **2:1 method** for stress at depth |
| 2 | $\Delta\sigma_z = q \cdot I_\sigma$ | $I_\sigma$ = Boussinesq influence factor | dimensionless | **Boussinesq's method** |
| 3 | $I_\sigma = \dfrac{3}{2\pi} \left[\dfrac{1}{1+(r/z)^2}\right]^{5/2}$ | $r$ = radial distance, $z$ = depth | – | Influence factor under circular load |
| 4 | $H_{BH} = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 2\,B$ (isolated) | $B$ = footing width | m | **IS 1892** for spread footings |
| 5 | $H_{BH} = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 3\,B$ (raft) | $B$ = raft width | m | **IS 1892** for mat foundations |
| 6 | $H_{BH} = L_p + 5 \text{ to } 10\,$m (friction pile) | $L_p$ = pile length | m | Pile tip + socketing zone |
| 7 | $H_{BH} = L_p + 1.5\,D_p$ (end-bearing pile) | $D_p$ = pile diameter | m | Pile tip into rock |
| 8 | $N_{BH} = \dfrac{A_{built-up}}{A_{per\,BH}}$ | $A$ = area in m² | m² | Number of boreholes for buildings |
| 9 | $N_{BH} = N_{piers} + N_{abutments}$ | – | – | Bridges (≥ 1 BH per support) |
| 10 | $\Delta\sigma_z / q = 0.10$ (cohesionless) | – | – | Termination criterion for sandy soils |
| 11 | $\Delta\sigma_z / q = 0.05$ (cohesive) | – | – | Termination criterion for clayey soils |

> [!TIP]
> **Absolute Value Notation:** In equations, use $\vert x \vert$ as `$\vert x \vert$` (NOT `|x|`) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Rise Construction (e.g., Kochi Metro pillars):** Proper borehole depth prevents missing the deeply weathered rock profile, avoiding pile tip settlement.
- **Bridge Piers across Backwaters:** Adequate borehole depth identifies marine clay pockets, preventing **lateral squeeze** failures.
- **Idukki Dam-type Projects:** Borehole spacing along the dam axis detects concealed **seepage channels** in karstic limestone.
- **Smart City Pavements:** Borings detect expansive black cotton soils, dictating whether **CBR stabilization** or full depth replacement is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stress at Depth — The 2:1 Method

The 2:1 method is the **simplest analytical tool** used by IS 1892 and KTU examiners to determine the borehole depth termination criterion.

### Step 1: Starting Assumption

Assume the load from the foundation spreads out in a **pyramidal frustum** with side slopes of **1 vertical : 2 horizontal**. At depth $z$ below the foundation base, the area over which the load is distributed increases.

$$A_z = (B + z)(L + z)$$

where $B$ and $L$ are the foundation width and length, respectively, and $z$ is the depth below the footing base.

### Step 2: Apply Equilibrium of Vertical Loads

By equilibrium, the total load at the surface (foundation level) equals the total load at depth $z$:

$$P_{total} = q \cdot B \cdot L = \Delta\sigma_z \cdot A_z = \Delta\sigma_z \cdot (B+z)(L+z)$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Stress Increment

Rearranging for $\Delta\sigma_z$:

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)}$$

This is the **2:1 stress distribution equation**.

### Step 4: For a Square Footing (B = L)

Substituting $L = B$:

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B^2}{(B+z)^2}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Termination Criterion

For **cohesionless soils** (per IS 1892 and IS 8009):

$$\frac{\Delta\sigma_z}{q} = 0.10$$

Substitute into the equation:

$$\frac{B^2}{(B+z)^2} = 0.10$$

Take the reciprocal:

$$(B+z)^2 = 10 B^2$$

$$B + z = \sqrt{10} \cdot B$$

$$z = (\sqrt{10} - 1) B = (3.162 - 1) B = 2.162\,B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result for Square Footing on Sand:** Borehole depth below footing level $\approx \mathbf{2.16\,B}$ ≈ 2B (which matches IS 1892 empirical rule of **1.5B to 2B** conservatively).

### Step 6: Verification for a Strip Footing (L → ∞)

For a strip footing, the load spreads in only one direction:

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B}{B + z}$$

For $\Delta\sigma_z / q = 0.10$:

$$\frac{B}{B+z} = 0.10 \implies B + z = 10 B \implies z = 9 B$$

But IS 1892 recommends **3B to 4B** for strip footings, which is conservative because it accounts for the **critical zone of compressibility**, not just stress decay.

### Step 3.2: Detailed Worked Example

**Problem:** A square footing of size **3 m × 3 m** is proposed with a net contact pressure of **150 kPa**. The soil is medium sand. Determine the required borehole depth below the footing base using the 2:1 method and IS 1892 criteria.

**Given:**
- $B = L = 3$ m
- $q_{net} = 150$ kPa
- Soil type: medium sand (cohesionless)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the stress equation:**

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B^2}{(B+z)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply termination criterion (10% rule for sand):**

$$\frac{\Delta\sigma_z}{q} = 0.10$$

$$\frac{B^2}{(B+z)^2} = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically:**

$$(B+z)^2 = \frac{B^2}{0.10} = 10 B^2$$

$$B + z = \sqrt{10} \cdot B = 3.162 B$$

$$z = 2.162 \cdot B = 2.162 \times 3 = 6.486 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply IS 1892 empirical rule (1.5B to 2B):**

$$z_{min} = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ m}$$
$$z_{max} = 2 \times 3 = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Final Design Depth:**

The **2:1 method gives 6.486 m**, while IS 1892 recommends **6.0 m** as a conservative upper bound. Therefore, the **borehole must be drilled to at least 6.5 m below the proposed footing base**, plus a 2 m allowance for seating the casing.

$$\boxed{H_{BH} \text{ (below GL)} = z + D_f + 2 = 6.5 + 1.5 + 2 = 10.0 \text{ m}}$$

where $D_f = 1.5$ m is the assumed depth of foundation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Borehole Depth by Soil Type

| Soil Type | Termination Criterion | Practical z/B (Square) | Practical z/B (Strip) | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dense sand / gravel | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.10$ | 2.0 B | 9 B | Low compressibility |
| Medium sand | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.10$ | 2.2 B | 9 B | Moderate compressibility |
| Loose sand | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.10$ (use Boussinesq) | 2.5 B | 10 B | High settlement risk |
| Stiff clay (OCR > 2) | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.05$ | 3.0 B | 19 B | Low consolidation |
| Soft clay (OCR ≈ 1) | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.05$ | 4.0 B | 19 B | High consolidation settlement |
| Peat / Organic | $\Delta\sigma_z / q \leq 0.02$ | Full compressible layer | Full compressible layer | Extreme settlement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Borehole Planning Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the complete decision logic an engineer follows while finalizing a borehole programme as per IS 1892.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Identify Structure Type]
    B --> B1[Building]
    B --> B2[Bridge]
    B --> B3[Dam or Embankment]
    B --> B4[Linear Project Road or Rail]

    B1 --> C1{Footprint Area}
    C1 -->|less than 200 sqm| D1[1 to 3 boreholes]
    C1 -->|200 to 1000 sqm| D2[1 per 200 to 300 sqm]
    C1 -->|greater than 1000 sqm| D3[1 per 100 to 250 sqm]

    B2 --> C2[Number of Piers and Abutments]
    C2 --> D4[1 borehole per support minimum 3 total]

    B3 --> C3[Dam Axis and Toe Lines]
    C3 --> D5[Grid spacing 30 to 50 m along dam axis]

    B4 --> C4{Investigation Stage}
    C4 -->|Preliminary| D6[Spacing 500 to 1000 m]
    C4 -->|Detailed| D7[Spacing 100 to 200 m]

    D1 --> E[Determine Soil Type and Foundation Type]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E

    E --> E1{Foundation Type}
    E1 -->|Isolated Footing| F1[Depth equals 1.5B to 2B]
    E1 -->|Strip Footing| F2[Depth equals 3B to 4B]
    E1 -->|Raft Foundation| F3[Depth equals 1.5B to 2B min 6m]
    E1 -->|Pile Foundation| F4[Depth equals pile tip plus 5m]

    F1 --> G[Apply Stress Termination Criterion]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G --> G1{Soil Type}
    G1 -->|Cohesionless| H1[Stop when delta sigma is 10 percent of q]
    G1 -->|Cohesive| H2[Stop when delta sigma is 5 percent of q]
    G1 -->|Organic| H3[Penetrate full compressible layer]

    H1 --> I[Borehole Diameter Selection]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> I1{Soil Condition}
    I1 -->|Soft clay or loose sand| J1[100 mm diameter]
    I1 -->|Medium soil| J2[150 mm diameter]
    I1 -->|Stiff or dense soil| J3[200 mm diameter]
    I1 -->|Rock| J4[NX core 150 mm hole]

    J1 --> K[Final Borehole Programme Output]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Borehole Stratigraphy Log (Typical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SURFACE["SURFACE LAYER"]
        S1[Topsoil 0 to 0.5 m]
    end

    subgraph LAYER1["LAYER 1: STIFF CLAY"]
        L1A[0.5 to 3.0 m Brown stiff clay N equals 12]
    end

    subgraph LAYER2["LAYER 2: MEDIUM SAND"]
        L2A[3.0 to 8.0 m Grey medium sand N equals 18 SPT]
    end

    subgraph LAYER3["LAYER 3: SOFT CLAY"]
        L3A[8.0 to 14.0 m Soft grey clay N equals 4 SPT Water Table at 2 m]
    end

    subgraph LAYER4["LAYER 4: DENSE SAND"]
        L4A[14.0 to 20.0 m Dense sand N equals 35 SPT End of Borehole]
    end

    SURFACE --> LAYER1
    LAYER1 --> LAYER2
    LAYER2 --> LAYER3
    LAYER3 --> LAYER4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Pressure Bulb Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FOOT["FOOTING BASE"]
        FB[Footing B by L at Depth Df]
    end

    subgraph INFL["INFLUENCE ZONE"]
        P1[Zone 1: 0 to 0.5B High Stress delta sigma greater than 50 percent of q]
        P2[Zone 2: 0.5B to 1B Moderate Stress delta sigma 20 to 50 percent of q]
        P3[Zone 3: 1B to 2B Low Stress delta sigma 10 to 20 percent of q]
        P4[Zone 4: beyond 2B Negligible Stress delta sigma less than 10 percent of q]
    end

    FOOT --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    P4 --> TERMINATE[Borehole Termination Level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The borehole **must** penetrate through Zones 1, 2, and 3 because these are the zones where structural loads induce meaningful stress changes. Terminating in Zone 4 is acceptable for shallow foundations on homogeneous soil.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the IS code provisions for deciding the spacing of boreholes for a building of 600 m² built-up area on alluvial soil.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As per **IS 1892:1979**, for a building of 600 m² built-up area on alluvial soil, the borehole spacing recommendations are:

1. For **detailed investigation**: boreholes should be spaced at **20 m to 40 m** in a grid pattern.
2. **Number of boreholes required**: 600 / 250 ≈ **3 to 5 boreholes** (using 1 borehole per 100–250 m²).
3. One borehole each should be located at the **corners and at the centre** of the building footprint to capture stratigraphic variability in the alluvial deposit.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** Code reference (1) + spacing values (1) + number and placement (1).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the termination criterion for borehole depth in (a) cohesionless soils and (b) cohesive soils, as per IS 1892.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The borehole is terminated at the depth where the **net stress increase** ($\Delta\sigma_z$) due to the foundation load becomes **insignificant** compared to the contact pressure.

| Soil Type | Termination Criterion |
|---|---|
| Cohesionless (sand/gravel) | $\Delta\sigma_z \leq 0.10\,q_{net}$ (**10% rule**) |
| Cohesive (clay) | $\Delta\sigma_z \leq 0.05\,q_{net}$ (**5% rule**) |

This ensures that the compressible layer below the termination depth contributes **negligible settlement** to the total.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Indian Standard guidelines (IS 1892)** for deciding the **number, size, and spacing** of boreholes for different types of civil engineering structures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A proposed **multi-storey building** with a **raft foundation** of size **30 m × 20 m** is to be constructed on **soft clay** deposit. The **net contact pressure** is **80 kPa**. Determine the **required borehole depth** below ground level using the **2:1 stress distribution method** and IS 1892 criteria. Assume **depth of foundation $D_f$ = 2 m**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | Guideline (IS 1892) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Number** | 1 per 200–300 m² (residential); 1 per 100–250 m² (commercial); 1 per pier/abutment (bridges); grid along dam axis | Determines redundancy of subsurface data |
| **Size** | 100 mm (soft soil); 150 mm (medium); 200 mm (stiff); 150 mm with NX core (rock) | Dictated by sampling requirement |
| **Spacing** | 500–1000 m (preliminary); 100–200 m (detailed); 30–50 m (critical) for linear projects; 20–40 m grid for buildings | Based on heterogeneity and project stage |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $B = 30$ m, $L = 20$ m, $q_{net} = 80$ kPa, Soft clay, $D_f = 2$ m.

**Step 1 — Stress equation (2:1 method):**

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply 5% rule for soft clay (cohesive):**

$$\frac{\Delta\sigma_z}{q} = 0.05$$

$$\frac{B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)} = 0.05$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{30 \times 20}{(30+z)(20+z)} = 0.05$$

$$(30+z)(20+z) = \frac{600}{0.05} = 12000$$

**Step 4 — Expand and solve the quadratic:**

$$600 + 30z + 20z + z^2 = 12000$$

$$z^2 + 50z + 600 = 12000$$

$$z^2 + 50z - 11400 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$z = \frac{-50 + \sqrt{2500 + 45600}}{2} = \frac{-50 + \sqrt{48100}}{2}$$

$$z = \frac{-50 + 219.32}{2} = \frac{169.32}{2} = 84.66 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check with IS 1892 empirical rule (1.5B to 2B for raft):**

$$z_{IS} = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ m to } 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Final design depth (governing value):**

The **2:1 method gives 84.66 m**, but for raft foundations in soft clay, the **influence zone is much deeper**. Hence, the borehole must extend to **at least 85 m below the footing base** to capture the full consolidation zone.

$$H_{BH} \text{ (below GL)} = 84.66 + D_f + 2 = 84.66 + 2 + 2 = \boxed{88.66 \text{ m} \approx 89 \text{ m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **factors** that govern the **depth of boreholes** for various foundation types with reference to **IS 1892**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **bridge pier** of size **8 m × 6 m** carries a **net load of 12,000 kN**. The subsoil consists of **dense sand** overlying rock at **20 m depth**. Determine the **borehole depth** as per IS 1892 and verify it using the **2:1 method**. Take unit weight of soil $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Factors governing borehole depth:**

1. **Type and size of foundation** — Depth = 1.5B to 4B depending on footing type.
2. **Pressure influence zone** — Depth where $\Delta\sigma_z$ becomes 5–10% of $q_{net}$.
3. **Compressible layer thickness** — For clays, must penetrate full compressible stratum.
4. **Groundwater table** — Borehole must extend below WT to assess sub-soil conditions.
5. **Scour depth (bridges in rivers)** — Borehole deeper than scour zone.
6. **Pile tip requirements** — For end-bearing piles, borehole goes 1.5D_p into rock.
7. **Presence of cavities or weak zones** — For karstic limestone, deeper exploration is essential.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $B = 8$ m, $L = 6$ m, $P_{net} = 12{,}000$ kN, Rock at 20 m, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³.

**Step 1 — Calculate net contact pressure:**

$$q_{net} = \frac{P_{net}}{B \cdot L} = \frac{12000}{8 \times 6} = \frac{12000}{48} = 250 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — IS 1892 empirical rule (isolated footing, dense sand):**

$$z = 2B = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m below footing base}$$

**Step 3 — 2:1 method verification (10% rule for dense sand):**

$$\frac{B \cdot L}{(B+z)(L+z)} = 0.10$$

$$\frac{48}{(8+z)(6+z)} = 0.10$$

$$(8+z)(6+z) = 480$$

**Step 4 — Expand the quadratic:**

$$48 + 8z + 6z + z^2 = 480$$

$$z^2 + 14z - 432 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic:**

$$z = \frac{-14 + \sqrt{196 + 1728}}{2} = \frac{-14 + \sqrt{1924}}{2} = \frac{-14 + 43.86}{2} = 14.93 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with rock level:**

Since the rock is at **20 m below GL** and assuming $D_f = 2$ m, rock is at **18 m below footing base**. The borehole **must extend up to rock** for a bridge pier.

**Step 7 — Final borehole depth:**

$$H_{BH} = \max(14.93, 18) = 18 \text{ m below footing base}$$

$$H_{BH} \text{ (below GL)} = 18 + D_f + 2 = 18 + 2 + 2 = \boxed{22 \text{ m below ground level}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing B with B²:** Many students write $B^2$ for a rectangular footing. Always use $B \cdot L$.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2 m casing allowance:** The borehole must extend 2 m beyond the termination depth to seat the casing — losing this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong termination criterion:** Sand = 10%, Clay = 5% — swapping these will give a depth that is **too shallow or too deep** by 30–40%.
> 4. **Not checking against rock level:** For piles or bridge piers, the rock overrides the stress criterion. The borehole must always extend **to the rock**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Quick-Fire Facts
- **Primary IS Code:** IS 1892:1979
- **Sand termination:** 10% rule ($\Delta\sigma_z \leq 0.10\,q$)
- **Clay termination:** 5% rule ($\Delta\sigma_z \leq 0.05\,q$)
- **Isolated footing depth:** 1.5B to 2B
- **Strip footing depth:** 3B to 4B
- **Raft foundation depth:** 1.5B to 2B (min 6 m)
- **Friction pile depth:** $L_p + 5$ to $10$ m
- **End-bearing pile depth:** $L_p + 1.5 D_p$ into rock
- **Bridge borehole:** Minimum 1 per pier + 1 per abutment
- **Standard borehole diameter:** 100 mm (soft), 150 mm (medium), 200 mm (stiff/rock)
- **Building borehole density:** 1 per 100–250 m² (commercial), 1 per 200–300 m² (residential)
- **Preliminary spacing (linear):** 500–1000 m
- **Detailed spacing (linear):** 100–200 m
- **Critical zone spacing:** 30–50 m
- **2:1 method formula:** $\Delta\sigma_z = \dfrac{qBL}{(B+z)(L+z)}$
- **Boussinesq method formula:** $\Delta\sigma_z = q \cdot I_\sigma$
- **Empirical formula for square footing on sand:** $z \approx 2.16 B$
- **Empirical formula for square footing on clay:** $z \approx 3.16 B$

### Common Student Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Using $B^2$ instead of $B \cdot L$ in the 2:1 formula for rectangular footings.
- ❌ Confusing the 10% and 5% rules between sand and clay.
- ❌ Neglecting the 2 m casing allowance below the termination depth.
- ❌ Forgetting to extend boreholes **to rock** for piles and bridge piers.
- ❌ Misinterpreting "depth" — IS 1892 specifies depth **below the foundation level**, not below GL. Add $D_f$ for total depth.

### Mnemonics for Quick Recall
- **"SCAN 10-5":** **S**and = 10%, **C**lay = 5%, **A**lways **N**egligible.
- **"I-S-R-P":** **I**solated (1.5B–2B), **S**trip (3B–4B), **R**aft (1.5B–2B), **P**ile (tip + 5m).
- **"D-S-M-R":** Borehole **D**iameter — **S**oft=100mm, **M**edium=150mm, **R**ock=150mm with NX core.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploration Techniques – Methods of Exploration: Open Pits, Trenches, Shafts, Boreholes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Exploration Techniques – Methods of Exploration: Open Pits, Trenches, Shafts, Boreholes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Site Exploration (Subsurface Investigation):** The systematic process of determining the surface and subsurface conditions of a proposed construction site, including the stratigraphy, groundwater table, and engineering properties of the soil and rock masses, through direct physical access methods.

**Methods of Direct Exploration** are categorized based on the **depth of access** and the **size of the opening** created in the ground. The four principal methods are:

| S.No. | Method | Typical Depth Range | Opening Type |
|-------|--------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Open Pits | **0 to 3 m** (shallow) | Large rectangular pit |
| 2 | Trenches | **0 to 6 m** (shallow-medium) | Long narrow excavation |
| 3 | Shafts | **3 m to 30+ m** (deep) | Vertical passage (person-entry) |
| 4 | Boreholes | **Up to 200+ m** (very deep) | Small diameter (150–300 mm) circular hole |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Doctor's Examination of the Earth":**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. They don't just look at the skin — they may use a stethoscope (a *borehole*, narrow and deep), perform a small incision to peek inside (*open pit/trench*, shallow and wide), or in extreme cases, perform a full exploratory surgery (*shaft*, deep and person-accessible). The Earth is the "patient," and the geotechnical engineer is the doctor. The method chosen depends on **how deep** the "ailment" (weak layer, water table, cavity) is suspected to be.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of soil (γ):** 16–22 kN/m³ (typical range)
- **Angle of repose (φ):** 30°–40° for granular soils
- **Standard borehole diameter:** **150 mm (NX size)** to **300 mm (HX size)**
- **Minimum shaft diameter for personnel entry:** **1.0 m to 1.2 m**
- **Maximum economical depth of borehole:** ~ **100 m** (rotary drilling)

## 1.4 Selection Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The choice of exploration method is governed by:
> 1. **Depth of investigation required** (function of structure type and loading).
> 2. **Type of soil/rock** anticipated.
> 3. **Groundwater conditions.**
> 4. **Purpose of investigation** (foundation design, slope stability, dam, tunnel, etc.).
> 5. **Economy and accessibility** of equipment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open Pits

**Operational Logic:**
- A rectangular excavation is made manually or by machine (JCB/backhoe) to expose shallow soil strata.
- Depth typically limited to **1.5 m to 3.0 m** due to safety and stability concerns.
- Used to inspect the **actual visual condition** of subsoil — color, texture, layering, moisture.

**Step-by-Step Decision Logic:**
1. Mark the pit location based on preliminary site plan.
2. Excavate vertically until either (a) hard stratum is reached, or (b) pit walls begin to collapse.
3. Log the soil profile on all four walls.
4. Take disturbed/undisturbed samples from each stratum.
5. Backfill upon completion.

**Advantages:**
- Direct visual inspection.
- Low cost for shallow depths.
- Easy sample collection.

**Limitations:**
- Limited depth (stability issues).
- Caving in loose sands below water table.
- Cannot be used in urban areas (space constraint).

## 2.2 Trenches

**Operational Logic:**
- Long, narrow excavations (length >> width), used when the **lateral variation** of strata is the primary concern (e.g., along a dam axis or pipeline alignment).
- Depth range: **3 m to 6 m**.
- Side slopes are battered (1:1 or flatter) to prevent collapse.

**Why Trenches?**
- For linear structures (highways, canals, pipelines, dams), trenches provide a **continuous profile** along the alignment, unlike isolated pits.
- A pit gives a "point" profile; a trench gives a "line" profile.

## 2.3 Shafts

**Operational Logic:**
- A vertical (sometimes inclined) passage of **1.0 m to 3.0 m diameter**, large enough for **personnel entry and descent using ladders or hoists**.
- Used when:
  - Depth > 6 m (where pits/trenches become uneconomical/unsafe).
  - Direct in-situ observation of deep strata is essential.
  - Underground space mapping is required (tunnels, caverns, mine shafts).

**Engineering Details:**
- Lined with timber, steel ribs, or concrete rings to prevent collapse.
- Equipped with **ventilation, lighting, dewatering pumps, and safety cages** (for depths > 6 m).
- Bored or sunk using the "percussion" or "Chicago" method.

## 2.4 Boreholes

**Operational Logic:**
- A **cylindrical hole of small diameter (150–300 mm)** drilled by mechanical rigs.
- Most common method of modern subsurface exploration.
- Can be drilled to **hundreds of meters** using rotary or percussion rigs.
- Samples are retrieved using **SPT, split-spoon, Shelby tubes, or core barrels**.

**Drilling Methods (Brief Recall):**
- **Auger Boring** (shallow, dry soils, depth < 10 m).
- **Rotary Drilling** (deep, rocky strata, uses drilling mud).
- **Percussion Drilling** (hard rock, cable-tool method).
- **Wash Boring** (soft to medium soils below water table).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for Module 1 Exploration Methods. These appear frequently in KTU board examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Depth of exploration for **buildings/isolated footings** | $D = 1.5\,B$ to $2.0\,B$ | m | $B$ = footing width |
| 2 | Depth of exploration for **heavy structures/dams** | $D = 1.5$ to $2 \times$ width, or to bedrock | m | Always extend to competent stratum |
| 3 | Depth of exploration for **roads/airfields** | $D = 1.5$ to $2.0$ m below subgrade | m | Subgrade = formation level |
| 4 | Spacing of boreholes (light buildings) | $S = 20$ to $30$ m | m | Grid pattern |
| 5 | Spacing of boreholes (heavy structures) | $S = 10$ to $15$ m | m | Closer spacing |
| 6 | Spacing for **dams/barrages** | $S = 30$ to $100$ m along axis | m | Cross-section profile |
| 7 | Minimum shaft diameter (person entry) | $d_{min} = 1.0$ to $1.2$ m | m | Safety regulation |
| 8 | Stability of vertical cut (granular soil) | $H_{c} = \dfrac{4c}{\gamma}\,\tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | m | $c$ = cohesion, $\gamma$ = unit weight, $\phi$ = friction angle |
| 9 | Side slope of pit/trench (cohesive soil) | $1V : 1H$ (approx.) | — | Stable up to 3–4 m |
| 10 | Cost factor | Open pit < Trench < Shaft < Borehole | — | Depth-wise ordering |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Open Pits:** Pre-construction inspection for small residential buildings, road widening projects.
- **Trenches:** Pipeline alignment, canal excavation, fault-tracing in dam abutments.
- **Shafts:** Bridge pier foundations, underground metro stations, exploratory mining.
- **Boreholes:** Universal method for high-rise buildings, offshore platforms, nuclear power plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Depth of Exploration for Different Structures

### Step 1 — Establish the Governing Stress Influence Zone

For a footing of width $B$ transmitting a pressure $q$ to the ground, the **vertical stress influence** extends approximately to a depth of $1.5B$ to $2.0B$ below the foundation level. This is derived from Boussinesq's elastic stress distribution:

$$\sigma_z = \dfrac{3q}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{z^3}{R^5}$$

where $R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2}$ is the radial distance from the point load.

### Step 2 — Apply the "Significant Stress" Criterion (2:1 Method)

The depth of exploration must reach a level where the **stress increase** from the structure is approximately **10% of the applied pressure** (Terzaghi's rule). For a strip footing of width $B$:

$$\sigma_z = \dfrac{qB}{B+z}$$

Setting $\sigma_z = 0.10\,q$ and solving:

$$0.10\,q = \dfrac{qB}{B+z}$$

$$B + z = \dfrac{B}{0.10} = 10B$$

$$z = 9B \quad \text{(excessive)}$$

### Step 3 — Practical KTU-Adopted Criterion

For engineering practice, KTU textbooks adopt the empirical rule:

$$\boxed{D = 1.5\,B \text{ to } 2.0\,B}$$

where:
- $D$ = depth of exploration
- $B$ = width of foundation / dimension of loaded area

### Step 4 — Convert to Numerical Examples

**Example 1:** Isolated square footing, $B = 2.0$ m

$$D = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(lower bound)}$$

$$D = 2.0 \times 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(upper bound)}$$

Hence, open pits (depth ≤ 3 m) **may suffice** for shallow footings.

**Example 2:** Multi-storey building raft, $B = 20$ m

$$D = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ m} \quad \text{(lower bound)}$$

$$D = 2.0 \times 20 = 40 \text{ m} \quad \text{(upper bound)}$$

Boreholes are mandatory; shafts may be used for confirmation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Spacing Between Boreholes (for Heavy Structures)

### Step 1 — Recognize Subsurface Variability

Soil strata vary laterally. For a "homogeneous" assumption, the spacing $S$ should not exceed a value that **misses significant anomalies** (e.g., soft pockets, cavities).

### Step 2 — Apply IS 1892 / KTU Recommended Spacing

For a 5-storey to 10-storey building (heavy load):

$$S_{min} = 10 \text{ m}, \qquad S_{max} = 15 \text{ m}$$

For a low-rise (< 3 storeys):

$$S_{min} = 20 \text{ m}, \qquad S_{max} = 30 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Step-by-Step Justification

A spacing closer than $S_{min}$ wastes resources; a spacing wider than $S_{max}$ risks missing critical weak zones, leading to differential settlement failures.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Exploration Methods

| Parameter | Open Pit | Trench | Shaft | Borehole |
|-----------|----------|--------|-------|----------|
| **Depth Range** | 0–3 m | 0–6 m | 3–30+ m | 0–200+ m |
| **Opening Size** | Large rectangular | Long narrow | 1.0–3.0 m dia. | 150–300 mm dia. |
| **Person Entry** | Yes (safe) | Yes (cautious) | Yes (with safety gear) | **No** |
| **Visual Inspection** | **Full 4 walls** | **Full longitudinal section** | Full shaft wall | **None** (indirect via samples) |
| **Equipment** | Manual / JCB | Manual / excavator | Rig + lining | Drilling rig + casing |
| **Cost (per m depth)** | Low | Low–Medium | High | Medium–High |
| **Speed** | Fast | Fast | Slow | Medium |
| **Sample Type** | Disturbed + Block | Disturbed + Block | Disturbed + Block | Disturbed + Undisturbed + Core |
| **Best Suited For** | Shallow foundations, soil profile logging | Linear projects, lateral variation | Bridge piers, deep soil/rock inspection | Universal — buildings, dams, offshore |
| **Major Limitation** | Caving below WT | Surface disruption | Ventilation, safety | No visual access |
| **Stability of Opening** | Self-stable in stiff clay | Self-stable in stiff clay | Needs lining | Cased with steel pipes |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Method Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 12-storey building with a raft foundation of dimensions 30 m × 25 m is to be constructed on alluvial soil. Select the most appropriate method of exploration and justify the depth.

### Step 1 — Determine Depth of Exploration

$$B = \text{effective width} = \max(30, 25) = 30 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 1.5 \times B = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 2.0 \times B = 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}$$

$$\therefore D \in [45, 60] \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Each Method

- **Open Pit:** Depth ≤ 3 m — **Not feasible** (far too shallow).
- **Trench:** Depth ≤ 6 m — **Not feasible**.
- **Shaft:** Depth ≤ 30 m — **Not feasible** (insufficient depth; safety costs high).
- **Borehole:** Depth 0–200+ m — **Suitable** ✓

### Step 3 — Conclusion

**Boreholes** are the appropriate choice. Spacing = 10–15 m (heavy structure). Drilling method = **rotary** with bentonite mud (alluvial soil below water table). Samples collected at every 1.5 m interval and at every stratum change via SPT.

$$\boxed{\text{Method: Borehole; Depth: 45–60 m; Spacing: 10–15 m}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Exploration Methods by Depth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Site Exploration Required"] --> B{"Depth of Investigation?"}
    B -->|0 to 3 m| C["Open Pit"]
    B -->|0 to 6 m| D["Trench"]
    B -->|3 m to 30 m| E["Shaft"]
    B -->|0 m to 200 m plus| F["Borehole"]
    C --> C1["Manual or JCB excavation"]
    C --> C2["Visual inspection of 4 walls"]
    D --> D1["Linear excavation along alignment"]
    D --> D2["Continuous profile logging"]
    E --> E1["Vertical passage with lining"]
    E --> E2["Personnel entry with safety cage"]
    F --> F1["Drilled by rig auger or rotary"]
    F --> F2["Sample retrieval SPT core"]
    C1 --> G["Disturbed and block samples"]
    C2 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["Soil Profile and Engineering Properties"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Borehole Investigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Site Reconnaissance"] --> B["Marking of Borehole Locations"]
    B --> C["Rig Setup and Mobilization"]
    C --> D["Drilling Operation"]
    D --> E{"Soil Type?"}
    E -->|Soft Soil| F["SPT at 1.5 m interval"]
    E -->|Stiff Clay| G["Shelby Tube Sampling"]
    E -->|Rock| H["Core Drilling with Diamond Bit"]
    F --> I["Soil Profile Log"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Laboratory Testing"]
    J --> K["Foundation Design Parameters"]
    K --> L["Geotechnical Report"]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Pit vs. Trench vs. Shaft vs. Borehole (Plan View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["Open Pit - Plan View"]
        A1["Rectangular pit 1.5 m x 1.5 m"]
        A2["Depth up to 3 m"]
    end
    subgraph P2["Trench - Plan View"]
        B1["Long narrow cut Length 10 m plus"]
        B2["Width 0.6 to 1.0 m"]
    end
    subgraph P3["Shaft - Plan View"]
        C1["Circular or square"]
        C2["Diameter 1.0 to 3.0 m"]
    end
    subgraph P4["Borehole - Plan View"]
        D1["Circular hole"]
        D2["Diameter 150 to 300 mm"]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Sketch of Exploration Methods (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["GROUND SURFACE"] --> A2["Open Pit - Depth 0 to 3 m"]
    A2 --> A3["Trench - Depth 0 to 6 m"]
    A3 --> A4["Shaft - Depth 3 to 30 m with lining"]
    A4 --> A5["Borehole - Depth 0 to 200 m plus with casing"]
    B1["SOIL STRATA"] --> B2["Top soil and weathered zone"]
    B2 --> B3["Soft to medium clay or sand"]
    B3 --> B4["Stiff clay or dense sand"]
    B4 --> B5["Weathered rock"]
    B5 --> B6["Bedrock"]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Exploration Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start"] --> Q1{"Type of Structure?"}
    Q1 -->|Low rise Building| Q2{"Depth Required?"}
    Q1 -->|High rise Building| Q3["Borehole"]
    Q1 -->|Dam or Barrage| Q4["Trench plus Borehole along axis"]
    Q1 -->|Bridge Pier| Q5["Shaft or Borehole"]
    Q1 -->|Pipeline or Canal| Q6["Trench"]
    Q2 -->|Less than 3 m| Q7["Open Pit"]
    Q2 -->|3 to 6 m| Q8["Trench"]
    Q2 -->|More than 6 m| Q3
    Q3 --> END["END - Borehole Investigation"]
    Q4 --> END
    Q5 --> END
    Q6 --> END
    Q7 --> END
    Q8 --> END
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between an open pit and a trench as methods of subsurface exploration. State the typical depth range for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open Pit | Trench |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| Shape | Roughly square/rectangular, compact | Long and narrow (length >> width) |
| Typical Depth | 0–3 m | 0–6 m |
| Purpose | Point profile at a specific location | Continuous profile along a linear alignment (highway, dam axis, pipeline) |
| Cost | Low | Slightly higher (more excavation volume) |
| Best For | Foundations of small buildings | Tracing lateral variation of strata |

**Depths:** Open pit = **0–3 m**; Trench = **0–6 m**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a shaft in geotechnical exploration? When is it preferred over a borehole?**

**Model Answer:**

A **shaft** is a vertical (or inclined) passage of diameter **1.0 m to 3.0 m** that allows **personnel entry** for direct visual inspection of deep subsoil/rock conditions. It is typically lined with timber, steel ribs, or concrete rings.

**Shafts are preferred over boreholes when:**
1. The depth of investigation is **moderate (3 m to 30 m)** and direct visual access is essential.
2. The project requires **in-situ testing of large volumes** of soil/rock (plate load test in shaft).
3. **Underground excavation** mapping is needed (tunnels, caverns, foundation pits for bridge piers).
4. Borehole sampling is **inconclusive** and physical confirmation is required.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget to state the depth range** explicitly — losing 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing **shaft and borehole** (shaft = person-entry, borehole = no entry) — a recurring error.
> 3. Failing to **justify method selection with depth and structure type** — must always show calculation $D = 1.5B$ to $2.0B$.
> 4. Skipping **advantages and limitations** in comparative questions — each carries ~2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified for 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various methods of exploration** used in geotechnical engineering. Compare open pits, trenches, shafts, and boreholes in terms of depth, opening size, sample type, and suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A proposed 4-storey residential building has isolated column footings each of size **2.5 m × 2.5 m**. Determine the **required depth and spacing of boreholes**. Discuss the method of drilling suitable for a sandy soil with groundwater table at **2.0 m** below ground level. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The four principal methods of exploration are:

1. **Open Pits** — Rectangular excavations of depth **0 to 3 m**; allow direct visual inspection of soil profile; used for shallow foundations; cannot extend below the water table due to caving.

2. **Trenches** — Long, narrow excavations (depth 0–6 m) along linear alignments; provide a **continuous profile**; ideal for dams, highways, and pipelines.

3. **Shafts** — Vertical passages of **1.0–3.0 m diameter** allowing personnel entry; depth ranges from **3 m to 30+ m**; used when direct deep inspection is necessary; must be supported by timber/concrete/steel lining.

4. **Boreholes** — Small diameter (150–300 mm) drilled holes using auger, rotary, or percussion methods; the **most universal method**; can reach 100+ m; samples obtained via SPT, Shelby tubes, or rock cores.

**Comparison Table** (as derived in Section 3.3 above).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Depth of exploration:**

$$B = 2.5 \text{ m (footing width)}$$

$$D = 1.5 \times B = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 2.0 \times B = 2.0 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 3.75 \text{ m to } 5.0 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — Spacing of boreholes:**

For a 4-storey building (low–medium rise):

$$S = 20 \text{ m to } 30 \text{ m}$$

(Boreholes placed on a grid pattern with a minimum of **3 boreholes** at the three corners of the site).

**Step 3 — Drilling method:**

Since the soil is **sandy** and the **groundwater table is at 2.0 m depth** (i.e., borehole will penetrate below WT):

- **Auger boring is unsuitable** (cannot hold open below WT).
- **Rotary drilling with bentonite mud** is the recommended method — the mud stabilizes the borehole walls and prevents caving of the sand.

$$\boxed{\text{Depth: 5.0 m; Spacing: 20–30 m; Method: Rotary with mud}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **method of sinking a shaft** for deep exploration. List the **safety precautions** to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a proposed **highway embankment** of height **8 m** to be constructed on a soft clay deposit, determine the **depth of exploration and type of method** to be adopted. The embankment base width is **30 m**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Shaft Sinking — Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Marking and Setup:** The shaft location is marked, and a temporary platform with a **hoist/winch and safety cage** is erected.
2. **Excavation:** Excavation begins from the surface. A circular shaft of **1.0–1.5 m diameter** is preferred (square shafts 1.2 m × 1.2 m also common).
3. **Lining Installation:** As excavation proceeds, **timber/steel/concrete rings** are installed to support the walls. In loose soils, **close timbering** (Poling boards and struts) is used.
4. **Verticality Check:** A plumb bob or theodolite is used to ensure verticality at every 1.5 m depth.
5. **Dewatering:** If water is encountered, **pumps or sump wells** are used.
6. **Ventilation and Lighting:** For depths > 6 m, **blower fans** and **electric lighting (flameproof)** are mandatory.
7. **Logging and Sampling:** At each stratum change, samples are collected; geological features (joints, fissures, water seepage) are recorded.
8. **Backfilling:** On completion, the shaft is backfilled in layers and compacted.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Use of **safety helmets, harnesses, and steel-caged hoists**.
- Continuous **ventilation** (avoidance of gas accumulation).
- **Two-way communication** between top and bottom.
- First-aid and **standby rescue team** at surface.
- Strict **no-smoking** policy (in gassy strata).
- Daily **inspection of timber/steel lining** for distress.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Determine depth of exploration:**

For an embankment of height $H = 8$ m and base width $B = 30$ m:

$$D = 1.5 \times B = 1.5 \times 30 = 45 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 2.0 \times B = 2.0 \times 30 = 60 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 45 \text{ to } 60 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 2 — Note:** For embankments on **soft clay**, the depth must also be sufficient to identify the **thickness of the compressible layer**, which governs **consolidation settlement**. IS 1892 recommends extending the borehole at least to a depth where the **stress increase is 10% of the embankment pressure**, or to a hard stratum, whichever is shallower.

**Step 3 — Method Selection:**

- Open Pit: ✗ (depth too low)
- Trench: ✗ (depth too low; also linear method not ideal here)
- Shaft: ✓ but **expensive** for 45+ m depth
- **Borehole: ✓ — Recommended** (rotary drilling with mud)

**Step 4 — Spacing:**

For a linear embankment, boreholes are placed along the **centerline** at:

$$S = 30 \text{ m to } 100 \text{ m (for embankments/dams)}$$

with additional boreholes at **critical sections** (tallest portion, weakest subsoil).

$$\boxed{\text{Method: Borehole; Depth: 45–60 m; Spacing: 30–100 m along centerline}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Open Pit** = shallowest (0–3 m), square/rectangular, visual inspection of 4 walls, manual/JCB.
- ☐ **Trench** = long and narrow, depth 0–6 m, used for **linear projects** (highways, dams, pipelines).
- ☐ **Shaft** = 1.0–3.0 m diameter, person-entry, depth 3–30+ m, requires **lining + ventilation + safety cage**.
- ☐ **Borehole** = 150–300 mm diameter, **no person entry**, depth up to 200+ m, uses SPT/Core sampling.
