- ☐ Depth of exploration rule: $D = 1.5B$ to $2.0B$ where $B$ = width/footing dimension.
- ☐ Spacing of boreholes: **Light building** = 20–30 m; **Heavy building** = 10–15 m; **Embankment/Dam** = 30–100 m.
- ☐ Drilling method below water table in sandy soil = **Rotary with bentonite mud**.
- ☐ Stability of vertical cut: $H_c = \dfrac{4c}{\gamma}\,\tan\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$.
- ☐ Borehole = **indirect** exploration; Shaft = **direct** exploration (visual + physical access).
- ☐ Cost ordering (per m depth): **Open Pit < Trench < Shaft ≈ Borehole** (varies with depth).
- ☐ Always state **method, depth, spacing, and drilling technique** in exam answers.
- ☐ For linear projects → **Trench or boreholes along centerline**.
- ☐ For deep projects with visual access → **Shaft** (with proper safety).
- ☐ KTU 2024 emphasizes **method selection with justification based on $D = 1.5B$ to $2.0B$**.
- ☐ For soft clay below WT → **Wash boring** is a valid alternative to rotary.
- ☐ Minimum shaft diameter for personnel = **1.0–1.2 m** (IS / safety code).
- ☐ Boreholes must extend to **competent stratum** OR up to **1.5B–2.0B**, whichever is greater.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Boring – Auger Boring, Wash Boring, Percussion Drilling, Rotary Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Methods of Boring – Auger Boring, Wash Boring, Percussion Drilling, Rotary Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Methods of Boring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boring in Geotechnical Engineering** refers to the process of creating a hole in the ground using various tools and techniques to obtain soil/rock samples, install instruments, or perform *in-situ* tests. The **Methods of Boring** are the systematic procedures employed to advance a borehole into the earth's subsurface for site characterization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Methods of boring constitute the preliminary exploratory operations that facilitate the retrieval of disturbed/undisturbed samples and allow access for Standard Penetration Tests (SPT), Cone Penetration Tests (CPT), and rock core extraction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **dentist** performing different procedures on a patient's tooth:
- **Auger Boring** is like a **manual corkscrew** twisting into soft ground.
- **Wash Boring** is like **flushing a clogged pipe with a water jet** to break and lift out material.
- **Percussion Drilling** is like a **hammer repeatedly striking** a chisel to break hard rock.
- **Rotary Drilling** is like a **power drill with diamond-tipped bits** that grinds through hard material while circulating fluid to cool and lift cuttings.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The harder the ground, the more "aggressive" the method needed:
> **Soft soil → Auger → Wash → Percussion → Rotary (Softest to Hardest)**

---

## 1.3 The Four Major Boring Methods (KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus)

| Sl. No. | Method | Operating Principle | Typical Depth |
|---------|--------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Auger Boring** | Rotational cutting with helical screw | **0–30 m** |
| 2 | **Wash Boring** | Hydraulic jet with chopping bit | **0–100 m** |
| 3 | **Percussion Drilling** | Repeated impact on chisel bit | **0–150 m** |
| 4 | **Rotary Drilling** | Rotating bit with drilling mud | **0–3000 m** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Point:** The selection of a boring method is dictated by:
> 1. **Type of soil/rock** strata
> 2. **Depth of investigation**
> 3. **Purpose of investigation** (sampling vs. testing)
> 4. **Groundwater table** position
> 5. **Availability of equipment & cost**
> Students often lose marks by ignoring groundwater conditions during method selection.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Diameter of borehole:** Standard size is **100 mm to 300 mm** for soil; **NX (54 mm), BX (42 mm), AX (30 mm)** for rock coring.
- **Drilling fluid viscosity:** Typically **40–60 seconds** (Marsh funnel test).
- **Specific gravity of drilling mud:** Maintained between **1.05 to 1.20**.
- **Percussion stroke frequency:** Approximately **40–60 blows per minute** for standard rigs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Insight:** According to IS 1892 (1977) and IS 2132 (1986), the minimum borehole diameter for soil sampling should not be less than **100 mm** to permit insertion of standard 100 mm Shelby tubes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 AUGER BORING — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
Auger boring employs a **helical screw (auger)** rotated into the ground. The flights of the auger carry the loosened soil upward along the helical path to the surface, similar to a wood screw entering timber.

### 2.1.2 Types of Augers (KTU High-Yield)
- **Hand Auger:** For depths up to **5 m**; used above water table.
- **Post-hole Auger:** Manually operated; common in shallow investigations.
- **Helical Auger (Power Auger):** Machine-driven; used for **5–30 m** depth.
- **Continuous Flight Auger (CFA):** Used in pile boring and deep investigations.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. The auger is positioned vertically over the borehole location.
2. Rotation is applied through a **kelly bar** or direct drive.
3. The cutting edge penetrates the soil by **torque and thrust**.
4. Loose soil moves up the **flights** by the screw action.
5. The auger is periodically withdrawn and emptied.

> [!TIP]
> **Why augers work in cohesive soils:** Clay sticks to the flights and is transported to the surface. In granular soils, the flights may fail to retain the material — hence auger boring is **NOT recommended in clean sands below the water table**.

### 2.1.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Auger Boring

$$
V_{lift} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{4} \cdot L
$$

Where:
- $V_{lift}$ = Volume of soil lifted per revolution
- $D_o$ = Outer diameter of auger flight
- $D_i$ = Inner diameter of auger flight (hub)
- $L$ = Lead distance (pitch of helix)

**Useful in:** Estimating the volume of disturbed sample recovered and correlating with depth of penetration.

---

## 2.2 WASH BORING — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
Wash boring uses a **high-velocity water jet** issued through a chopping bit at the bottom of the borehole. The jet:
1. **Liquefies** the soil at the cutting face.
2. **Lifts** the cuttings to the surface through the annular space.
3. **Cools** the cutting tool.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic
1. A **casing pipe** is driven into soft upper strata to prevent caving.
2. The **chopping bit** is lowered inside the casing.
3. Water is pumped down through the **drill rod** under pressure **(70–1000 kPa)**.
4. The bit chops and loosens the soil while the jet fluidizes it.
5. **Slurry** (water + cuttings) rises in the annular space to a settling tank.
6. Cuttings settle, water is recirculated.

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Wash Boring

$$
Q = A \cdot v
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Discharge of water (m³/s)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the annular space
- $v$ = Upward velocity of slurry (must be **> 0.3 m/s** to carry cuttings)

**Lifting Velocity Criterion:**

$$
v_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{4g \cdot d \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 C_D \cdot \rho_w}}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = Diameter of largest cutting particle
- $\rho_s$ = Density of soil particle
- $\rho_w$ = Density of water
- $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (≈ 1.0 for spherical particles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wash boring is extensively used for **SPT-based investigations** in sandy and silty deposits, especially where auger boring fails due to caving. The method provides **disturbed samples** only — undisturbed sampling requires subsequent Shelby tube or piston sampler insertion.

---

## 2.3 PERCUSSION DRILLING — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
Percussion drilling uses **repeated vertical impacts** of a heavy bit (chisel) to fracture and crush hard strata. The tool is alternately raised and dropped, with the cuttings removed by bailers or by water circulation.

### 2.3.2 Two Sub-Methods
- **Cable-tool Percussion (Standard):** Uses a cable-suspended bit; suitable for **rock and boulder** formations.
- **Pneumatic/Hammer Percussion:** Uses compressed air-driven hammers for **fast penetration** in hard rock.

### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. A **drilling tool string** (bit + drill stem) is suspended by a cable or rod.
2. The tool is lifted by **40–100 cm** and released repeatedly.
3. Each impact fractures the rock/soil.
4. Cuttings accumulate as **sludge** at the hole bottom.
5. A **bailer** (long tube with flap valve) is lowered periodically to remove sludge.
6. **Casing** is advanced as needed to prevent caving.

### 2.3.4 Energy Transfer Analysis

$$
E_{impact} = W \cdot h \cdot n \cdot t
$$

Where:
- $E_{impact}$ = Total impact energy delivered (J)
- $W$ = Weight of the drilling tool (N)
- $h$ = Height of fall (m)
- $n$ = Number of blows per minute
- $t$ = Duration of drilling (min)

### 2.3.5 Penetration Rate Formula

$$
R_p = \frac{E_{impact}}{A \cdot E_{vol}}
$$

Where:
- $R_p$ = Penetration rate (m/hr)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of bit (m²)
- $E_{vol}$ = Volumetric specific energy (J/m³) of the rock

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Percussion Drilling" with "Rotary Percussion Drilling." The former is purely impact-based; the latter combines rotation with impact (e.g., DTH hammers). KTU questions specifically test the **cable-tool method** under this topic.

---

## 2.4 ROTARY DRILLING — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle
Rotary drilling employs a **rotating drill bit** (with cutting teeth or diamond impregnations) under downward thrust. **Drilling fluid (mud)** is continuously circulated to:
1. Cool and lubricate the bit.
2. Carry cuttings to the surface.
3. Stabilize the borehole walls.
4. Prevent blowouts in high-pressure formations.

### 2.4.2 Two Sub-Methods
- **Direct Mud Circulation (DMC):** Fluid flows DOWN the drill rod and UP the annulus.
- **Reverse Mud Circulation (RMC):** Fluid flows UP the drill rod and DOWN the annulus — used for **large-diameter holes** and **unconsolidated formations**.

### 2.4.3 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Drill bit is rotated by a **rotary table or top-drive** at **50–300 rpm**.
2. Downward thrust (5,000–50,000 kg) advances the bit.
3. Drilling mud is pumped down through the **swivel and kelly**.
4. Cuttings rise through the annulus to the **shale shaker** for separation.
5. Cleaned mud returns to the **mud pit** and is recirculated.

### 2.4.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Rotary Drilling

**Circulation Pressure Loss (Simplified Annular Flow):**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{32 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v}{D^2}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure loss (Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of mud (Pa·s)
- $L$ = Length of annulus (m)
- $v$ = Average velocity (m/s)
- $D$ = Equivalent annular diameter (m)

**Mud Weight Requirement for Borehole Stability:**

$$
\gamma_{mud} = \frac{P_{formation}}{0.00981 \cdot H}
$$

Where:
- $P_{formation}$ = Formation pore pressure (kPa)
- $H$ = Depth of investigation (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Why mud matters:** If $\gamma_{mud} < $ formation pressure, **blowout** occurs. If $\gamma_{mud}$ is too high, **formation fracturing** and lost circulation result. KTU questions often test mud weight calculations for stability.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Master Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Auger Boring | Wash Boring | Percussion Drilling | Rotary Drilling |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Strata Suitability** | Soft to stiff cohesive soils | Soft to medium soils, sand | Hard rock, boulders | All types incl. hard rock |
| **Below Water Table** | Poor | Possible with care | Good | Excellent |
| **Sample Quality** | Disturbed | Disturbed | Disturbed (rock chips) | **Continuous core possible** |
| **Depth Capacity** | 0–30 m | 0–100 m | 0–150 m | 0–3000 m |
| **Equipment Cost** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| **Speed** | Fast (shallow) | Moderate | Slow | Fast (deep) |
| **Key Limitation** | Caves in loose sand | Loss of circulation | Slow in hard rock | Mud cost, disposal |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Auger Boring:** Used in geotechnical investigations for **road and railway alignments** in alluvial plains.
- **Wash Boring:** Standard method for **building foundation investigations** in coastal sandy soils (Kerala coastal belt).
- **Percussion Drilling:** Used in **well drilling** for water supply in hard rock terrains (Deccan plateau).
- **Rotary Drilling:** Essential for **offshore wind turbine foundations**, **oil exploration**, and **dam site investigations** in deep rock formations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Upward Velocity for Cuttings Transport (Wash Boring)

**Given Conditions:**
- Soil cutting diameter $d = 0.005$ m
- Particle density $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³
- Water density $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- Drag coefficient $C_D = 1.0$

**Step 1: Write the force balance on a particle held in suspension**

The upward drag force must equal the submerged weight of the particle.

$$
F_{drag} = C_D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\rho_w v^2}{2}
$$

$$
F_{weight} = (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot V_{particle}
$$

**Step 2: Equate the forces for incipient suspension**

$$
C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w v^2}{2} = (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the equation**

Cancel $\pi$ from both sides:

$$
C_D \cdot \frac{d^2 \cdot \rho_w v^2}{8} = (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{d^3}{6}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v^2$**

$$
v^2 = \frac{8 \cdot d \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{6 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}
$$

$$
v^2 = \frac{4 \cdot d \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numerical values**

$$
v^2 = \frac{4 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (2650 - 1000)}{3 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1000}
$$

$$
v^2 = \frac{0.020 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1650}{3000}
$$

$$
v^2 = \frac{323.73}{3000} = 0.1079
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{0.1079} = 0.3285 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6: Apply safety factor of 1.5 (KTU standard practice)**

$$
v_{design} = 1.5 \cdot 0.3285 = 0.493 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The upward slurry velocity must be at least **0.493 m/s** to transport 5 mm cuttings in wash boring. Any value below this results in cuttings settling back and re-drilling, which is a common KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mud Weight for Borehole Stability (Rotary Drilling)

**Given:**
- Depth of investigation $H = 500$ m
- Formation pore pressure gradient = 10.5 kPa/m (slightly overpressured)
- Safety factor $FS = 1.2$

**Step 1: Calculate formation pressure**

$$
P_{formation} = 10.5 \times 500 = 5250 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply safety factor**

$$
P_{required} = FS \cdot P_{formation} = 1.2 \times 5250 = 6300 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate equivalent mud density**

$$
\rho_{mud} = \frac{P_{required}}{g \cdot H} = \frac{6300 \times 1000}{9.81 \times 500}
$$

$$
\rho_{mud} = \frac{6300000}{4905} = 1284.4 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Express as specific gravity**

$$
SG_{mud} = \frac{\rho_{mud}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{1284.4}{1000} = 1.284
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A mud specific gravity of **1.28–1.30** is typical for deep offshore geotechnical investigations. Baryte (barite) is the standard weighting agent added to drilling mud.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Method Selection

The following matrix is a **KTU exam-favorite** for short essays on method selection.

| Site Condition | Recommended Method | Reasoning |
|----------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Soft clay above WT, depth < 10 m | **Hand/Post Auger** | Simple, no fluid, no casing |
| Loose sand below WT | **Wash Boring with Casing** | Auger caves; wash removes sand slurry |
| Hard rock, depth 50–100 m | **Percussion (Cable-tool)** | Effective fracture by impact |
| Boulders + clay mix | **Rotary with Drag Bit** | Handles mixed strata, continuous coring |
| Deep offshore (>500 m) | **Rotary with Diamond Core** | Only method for deep rock sampling |
| Soil contaminated zone | **Auger (dry method)** | No fluid spreading contamination |
| High artesian pressure | **Rotary with Heavy Mud** | Prevents blowout |
| Karstic limestone | **Percussion + Rotary combo** | Percussion to penetrate cavities |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Example: Auger Boring Volume

**Problem:** A continuous flight auger has an outer diameter $D_o = 0.30$ m, inner hub diameter $D_i = 0.10$ m, and pitch $L = 0.20$ m. Calculate the volume of soil lifted per revolution.

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional area of one flight**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.30^2 - 0.10^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.09 - 0.01) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.08
$$

$$
A = 0.0628 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Calculate lifted volume**

$$
V_{lift} = A \cdot L = 0.0628 \times 0.20 = 0.01257 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: For 10 revolutions, the lifted volume is**

$$
V_{total} = 10 \times 0.01257 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^3
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Use Case:** This calculation is critical for correlating the **depth of penetration** with the **volume of sample recovered**, especially in **quality control of CFA piles** used in Kerala's lateritic soils.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Auger Boring System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prime Mover - Power Source] --> B[Rotary Head - Torque Transfer]
    B --> C[Kelly Bar - Vertical Drive]
    C --> D[Auger String - Helical Flights]
    D --> E[Cutting Edge - Penetration]
    E --> F[Soil Cuttings - Upward Travel]
    F --> G[Surface Discharge]
    G --> H[Sample Collection - Disturbed]
    H --> I[Disposal / Testing]
    E -.-> J[Check Verticality]
    E -.-> K[Monitor Depth]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Wash Boring Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Setup and Casing Drive] --> B[Lower Chopping Bit]
    B --> C[Connect Drill Rod and Hose]
    C --> D[Start Water Pump 70 to 1000 kPa]
    D --> E[Bit Chops and Jet Loosens Soil]
    E --> F[Slurry Rises in Annular Space]
    F --> G[Settling Tank - Cuttings Drop]
    G --> H[Recirculation of Clean Water]
    H --> D
    F --> I[SPT Sampler Insertion Every 1.5 m]
    I --> J[Record N Values]
    J --> K[Continue to Target Depth]
    K --> L[Backfill and Casing Removal]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Percussion Drilling Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Drilling Mast - Vertical Support] --> B[Walking Beam - Oscillating Lever]
    B --> C[Drilling Cable or Rod]
    C --> D[Heavy Drill Bit - Chisel]
    D --> E[Impact on Rock Bottom]
    E --> F[Sludge Formation]
    F --> G[Bailer Lowered]
    G --> H[Flap Valve Opens - Sludge Enters]
    H --> I[Bailer Lifted and Emptied]
    I --> J[Casing Advanced if Caving]
    J --> K[Repeat Cycle to Target Depth]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Rotary Drilling Circulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mud Pit - Storage] --> B[Mud Pump - High Pressure]
    B --> C[Swivel and Kelly - Rotating Seal]
    C --> D[Drill Pipe and Bit]
    D --> E[Bit Cuts Rock - Cuttings Generated]
    E --> F[Annular Space - Upward Flow]
    F --> G[Shale Shaker - Cuttings Separation]
    G --> H[Cleaned Mud Return]
    H --> A
    E -.-> I[Drill Core Recovery if Coring]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Site Investigation Required] --> B{Soil Type?}
    B -->|Soft Cohesive| C{Depth Less than 30 m?}
    B -->|Loose Sand| D{Groundwater Encountered?}
    B -->|Hard Rock| E{Depth Less than 150 m?}

    C -->|Yes| F[Select Auger Boring]
    C -->|No| G[Select Wash Boring]

    D -->|Yes| H[Wash Boring with Casing]
    D -->|No| I[Auger Boring Possible]

    E -->|Yes| J[Select Percussion Drilling]
    E -->|No| K[Select Rotary Drilling]

    F --> L[Check Sample Quality]
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Method Selection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the principle of Wash Boring. Mention two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle:** Wash boring is a method of advancing a borehole by simultaneously chopping the soil at the bottom with a chopping bit and fluidizing it with a high-velocity water jet pumped down through the drill rod. The loosened soil-water mixture (slurry) rises through the annular space to a settling tank where cuttings are separated.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Effective in loose granular soils below the water table where auger boring fails.
> 2. Quick penetration in soft to medium soils with relatively simple equipment.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. Provides only **disturbed samples**; undisturbed sampling requires additional tools.
> 2. Difficult to identify exact depths of strata changes because cuttings mix during upward travel.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Auger Boring and Rotary Drilling in terms of operating principle and sample quality.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Feature | Auger Boring | Rotary Drilling |
> |---------|--------------|-----------------|
> | **Operating Principle** | Helical screw rotated to lift loose soil | Rotating bit with continuous mud circulation |
> | **Sample Quality** | Disturbed (cohesive) or impossible (granular) | Continuous core possible with diamond bit |
> | **Strata Suitability** | Soft soils above WT | All strata including deep rock |
> | **Depth Limit** | ~30 m | Up to 3000 m |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Percussion Drilling** method in detail with a neat sketch. List the equipment used and explain the procedure step-by-step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle:** Percussion drilling advances a borehole by the **repeated lifting and dropping** of a heavy chisel-like bit onto the bottom of the hole. The impact fractures and crushes the rock or hard soil. Cuttings accumulate as sludge and are periodically removed using a bailer.

**Equipment Used:**
1. Drilling mast/tower (10–20 m height)
2. Walking beam or spudding mechanism
3. Drill bit (chisel type with various shapes: Z, X, Y, Cruciform)
4. Drill rods or cable
5. Bailer (sludge bucket with flap valve)
6. Casing pipes and casing driver
7. Slush pump (optional, for water circulation)
8. Engine and winch assembly

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Site Preparation:** Level the ground and erect the mast vertically above the borehole point.
2. **Casing Placement:** Drive the first length of casing (typically 150–300 mm diameter) through loose overburden to prevent caving.
3. **Bit Insertion:** Lower the drill bit attached to the drill rod into the casing until it rests on the hole bottom.
4. **Percussion Action:** The walking beam lifts the bit by 40–100 cm and releases it. The impact frequency is **40–60 blows/minute**.
5. **Sludge Formation:** Each impact pulverizes the rock/soil; water is added if necessary to form sludge.
6. **Bailing:** Every 0.5–1 m of advance, the bit is withdrawn and a bailer is lowered. The flap valve opens on impact with sludge, filling the bailer.
7. **Casing Advancement:** When the hole begins to cave, additional casing lengths are driven.
8. **Strata Identification:** The bailer returns reveal the color, texture, and type of material penetrated.
9. **Termination:** Drilling continues until the target depth or refusal is reached.

**Sketch Description (textual):**
- Vertical mast with walking beam at the top
- Cable hanging from the walking beam
- Drill bit (cruciform) at the bottom of the cable
- Casing surrounding the bit, resting on the rock
- Bailer shown alongside for sludge removal

---

**(b)** Explain the **Rotary Drilling** method. Discuss the role of drilling mud with its desired properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle:** Rotary drilling uses a **continuously rotating bit** under high downward thrust to grind or shear the formation. Drilling fluid (mud) is circulated to remove cuttings, cool the bit, and stabilize the borehole.

**Components of Rotary Drilling System:**
1. Rotary table or top-drive (provides rotation)
2. Kelly bar (transmits rotation to drill string)
3. Drill pipe and drill collars (provide weight on bit)
4. Drill bit (drag bit, roller cone, or diamond core bit)
5. Mud pump (delivers fluid at 5,000–30,000 kPa)
6. Mud pit and shale shaker (recirculation system)
7. Casing and blowout preventer (for deep holes)

**Circulation Types:**
- **Direct Circulation:** Mud DOWN through drill rod, UP through annulus.
- **Reverse Circulation:** Mud DOWN through annulus, UP through drill rod (for large holes and unconsolidated formations).

**Role of Drilling Mud (Four Functions):**
1. **Cuttings Removal:** Carries rock fragments to the surface for separation.
2. **Bit Cooling and Lubrication:** Reduces wear and prevents overheating.
3. **Borehole Stabilization:** Hydrostatic pressure supports weak walls.
4. **Formation Pressure Control:** Prevents blowouts in overpressured zones.

**Desired Properties of Drilling Mud:**
- **Specific gravity:** 1.05 to 1.20 (for normal soil/rock); up to 2.0 with baryte for high-pressure zones.
- **Viscosity:** 40–60 seconds (Marsh funnel) for cuttings transport.
- **Filtration loss:** < 15 mL in 30 minutes (API test).
- **Sand content:** < 3% (to prevent wear on pump parts).
- **pH:** 9 to 10.5 (to prevent corrosion).

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Rotary drilling is the most versatile method, capable of penetrating all strata from soft clay to hard igneous rock to depths of several kilometers, making it indispensable for **offshore and dam site investigations**.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **Auger Boring, Wash Boring, Percussion Drilling, and Rotary Drilling** in a tabular form with respect to: operating principle, suitable strata, sample quality, depth capacity, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Parameter | Auger Boring | Wash Boring | Percussion Drilling | Rotary Drilling |
> |-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|-----------------|
> | **Principle** | Helical screw rotation lifts soil | Water jet + chopping bit | Repeated impact by chisel | Rotating bit + mud circulation |
> | **Suitable Strata** | Soft cohesive soils | Soft to medium soils | Hard rock, boulders | All types including hard rock |
> | **Sample Quality** | Disturbed | Disturbed | Disturbed (rock chips) | Continuous core possible |
> | **Depth Capacity** | 0–30 m | 0–100 m | 0–150 m | 0–3000 m |
> | **Advantages** | Simple, low cost, no fluid | Works in saturated sand | Effective in hard rock | Versatile, deep, fast |
> | **Limitations** | Fails in loose sand below WT | No undisturbed samples | Slow in very hard rock | High cost, mud disposal issues |

---

**(b)** A wash boring operation uses a chopping bit of diameter **150 mm** in a borehole of diameter **200 mm**. The drill rod has an outer diameter of **50 mm**. Calculate the **minimum upward velocity** of slurry required to transport cuttings of size **3 mm**, given that particle density is **2700 kg/m³** and water density is **1000 kg/m³**. Assume $C_D = 0.8$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify the given data**
- Cuttings diameter: $d = 0.003$ m
- $\rho_s = 2700$ kg/m³
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- $C_D = 0.8$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2: Apply the minimum velocity formula**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{3 C_D \rho_w}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 9.81 \times 0.003 \times (2700 - 1000)}{3 \times 0.8 \times 1000}}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the numerator and denominator**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 9.81 \times 0.003 \times 1700}{2400}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{200.124}{2400}}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the final value**

$$
v = \sqrt{0.0834} = 0.2887 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6: Apply safety factor of 1.5**

$$
v_{design} = 1.5 \times 0.2887 = 0.433 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert **mm to m** in the cutting diameter. Many students write $d = 3$ instead of $d = 0.003$, leading to a velocity 1000 times higher — an obvious indicator of incomplete working. **Always show unit conversions in steps.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Wash Boring with Rotary Drilling:** Many students describe wash boring as a "rotary method" because water is pumped. **Correction:** Wash boring is **non-rotary** in principle; the chopping bit is lifted and dropped, not rotated.
> 2. **Ignoring Groundwater Table Position:** When asked to select a method, students must explicitly mention whether the WT is above or below the boring face.
> 3. **Sketches Without Labels:** A borehole sketch without proper labels of **drill rod, casing, chopping bit, water inlet, and slurry outlet** will lose **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 4. **Numerical Mistakes:** Forgetting the **safety factor** in wash boring velocity calculations. KTU expects the final design velocity, not the incipient suspension velocity.
> 5. **Mixing Up Definitions:** "Percussion" and "Percussive Rotary" (DTH) are **not the same** in KTU Module 1. The 2024 scheme specifically tests **cable-tool percussion**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Four Methods:** Auger, Wash, Percussion, Rotary — in increasing order of strata hardness and depth capability.
> - **Auger Boring:** Helical rotation, ideal for cohesive soils above WT, **max ~30 m**, gives disturbed samples.
> - **Wash Boring:** Chopping bit + water jet, ideal for granular soils below WT, **disturbed samples only**, slurry velocity must exceed settling velocity.
> - **Percussion Drilling:** Repeated impact by chisel, ideal for hard rock and boulders, **0–150 m**, uses bailer for sludge removal.
> - **Rotary Drilling:** Rotating bit + mud circulation, **most versatile**, deep investigations up to **3000 m**, allows **continuous core recovery** with diamond bits.
> - **Mud Specific Gravity:** 1.05–1.20 (normal) to 2.0+ (overpressured formations with baryte).
> - **Standard Borehole Diameter for Soil:** Minimum **100 mm** (IS 1892).
> - **Cuttings Transport Velocity (Wash Boring):** Must be ≥ 1.5 × calculated settling velocity.
> - **Decision Parameter:** Method selection depends on **strata type + depth + groundwater + sample quality required + cost**.
> - **KTU Numerical Favorite:** Wash boring settling velocity calculation — practice at least 3 problems.
> - **Sketch Component:** Always include casing, drill rod, bit, water flow arrows, and surface collection tank.
> - **Mud Functions:** 4 key functions — cuttings removal, cooling, wall stabilization, pressure control.
> - **Bailer:** A long tube with flap valve used in percussion drilling to remove sludge.
> - **Diamond Core Bit vs. Drag Bit:** Diamond for hard rock, drag for soft formations in rotary drilling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling – Soil Sampling (Disturbed and Undisturbed), Representative and Non-representative Samples, Chunk and Tube Samples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sampling – Soil Sampling (Disturbed and Undisturbed), Representative and Non-representative Samples, Chunk and Tube Samples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Soil Sampling** is the systematic process of extracting soil specimens from the ground at a project site for the purpose of visual classification, identification of subsurface strata, and laboratory determination of engineering properties. It is a critical sub-component of the broader **Site Characterization Program** and forms the empirical foundation upon which all geotechnical design recommendations (foundations, slopes, retaining structures, embankments) are predicated.

Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, soil sampling is positioned as the bridge between **in-situ testing** (which provides indirect parameters) and **laboratory testing** (which provides direct, controlled, index and engineering properties).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PECET753 – Module 1):**
> The student must be able to classify samples by their *degree of disturbance*, distinguish between *representative* and *non-representative* samples, and describe the operational characteristics of *chunk* and *tube* samplers — including their applicability domains, limitations, and the quality of the recovered specimen.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are a forensic scientist examining a crime scene. To convict (design) a suspect (foundation) correctly, you need physical evidence. Now, suppose you collect the evidence (soil) in two different ways:

1. **The "Vacuum Cleaner" Method** — You suck up loose dust and fragments into a plastic bag. You have the *chemical composition* of the material, but its *original arrangement* is destroyed. This is a **disturbed sample**.

2. **The "Pristine Block" Method** — You carefully carve out an intact block of the floor (soil) preserving every layer, every pore, every grain arrangement. You can later test its *true strength and behavior*. This is an **undisturbed sample**.

In the first case, you know *what* the material is, but not *how it behaves in place*. In the second case, you know both. **The quality of your design depends entirely on which evidence you collect.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No sample is ever 100% "undisturbed" — even the best techniques cause some alteration. The geotechnical engineer's job is to **minimize** and **quantify** the disturbance, not eliminate it.

### 1.3 Standard Sampling Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

- **Borehole Diameter Range:** **75 mm – 200 mm** (most common: **100 mm**)
- **Standard Shelby Tube:** **76.2 mm** (3 in) outer diameter, **73 mm** inner diameter
- **Standard Penetration Test (SPT) Spoon:** **50.8 mm** (2 in) outer, **35 mm** (1.375 in) inner
- **Target Recovery Length:** **450 mm – 600 mm** per drive for SPT
- **Standard Hammer Mass:** **63.5 kg** dropped from **750 mm** height

> [!TIP]
> **Why these specific numbers?** They are standardized internationally (IS 2131, IS 1892, ASTM D1586, ASTM D3550) so that test results from different sites, countries, and decades can be compared meaningfully. KTU examiners expect you to remember these benchmark values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Soil Samples

The KTU 2024 syllabus demands a clear understanding of the following hierarchical taxonomy. Note that the categories overlap and a single physical sample can simultaneously be described by *multiple* labels (e.g., a Shelby tube sample is typically an *undisturbed*, *representative* sample of *cohesive* soil).

**Level 1 — By Degree of Disturbance:**

- **Disturbed Samples:** Soil structure is altered, but the mineralogical composition and grain-size distribution remain essentially intact. Moisture content may be partially preserved if sealed immediately.
- **Undisturbed Samples:** Natural structure, void ratio, moisture content, and in-situ stress state preserved to the maximum extent practically achievable.

**Level 2 — By Representativeness:**

- **Representative Sample:** Contains the *true proportion* of every soil constituent (gravel, sand, silt, clay, organic matter, water) as it exists in the ground. Example: A properly collected *bulk sample* from a trial pit.
- **Non-representative Sample:** Loses or preferentially enriches certain fractions during retrieval. Example: *Wash-boring samples* where fines are flushed out, or *chunk samples* where coarse gravel may be lost or over-represented.

**Level 3 — By Physical Recovery Method:**

- **Chunk Samples:** Hand-carved, irregular blocks of cohesive soil.
- **Tube Samples:** Cylindrical specimens retrieved inside a sampling tube (Shelby, split-spoon, piston, etc.).
- **Bulk Samples:** Large disturbed samples (typically 5–50 kg) for compaction or aggregate tests.
- **Core Samples:** Cylindrical specimens from rock, but the term is sometimes loosely applied to stiff soils too.

### 2.2 Why Does Disturbance Matter? (The Engineering Rationale)

The engineering properties of soil depend on **three coupled factors:**

1. **Mineralogy** (what the grains are made of)
2. **Fabric** (how the grains are arranged — flocculated, dispersed, layered)
3. **Effective Stress State** (the inter-particle forces)

A *disturbed* sample preserves **(1)** but destroys **(2)** and **(3)**. The laboratory test on a disturbed sample therefore gives you properties like *grain size, Atterberg limits, specific gravity, chemical content* — but **not** the in-situ *shear strength, compressibility, or permeability*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** If a student answers "What is the use of an undisturbed sample?" by saying "to find shear strength" — this is correct but incomplete. The complete answer must mention that the *engineering* properties (c, φ, Cc, Cv, k) depend on the in-situ *structure* and *stress state*, which can ONLY be measured if those are preserved.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Sampling Decision

| Step in the Decision Tree | Question Asked | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Decide disturbed vs. undisturbed | Do I need engineering properties or just index/classification data? | Index tests (sieve, hydrometer, LL, PL) need only mineralogy. Engineering tests (UU, CU, CD, oedometer) need fabric and stress state. |
| Decide chunk vs. tube | What is the depth, soil type, and accessibility? | Chunk samples are practical only in trial pits/trenches above the water table, in stiff cohesive soils. Tubes are mandatory below water table or at depth. |
| Decide thin-walled vs. thick-walled | What is the consistency of the clay? | Thin-walled (Shelby) tubes are pushed into *soft to stiff* clays. Thick-walled (split-spoon) are driven and are *acceptable for stiff clays and sands*. |
| Decide piston vs. open-tube | Is the clay very soft and sensitive? | Piston samplers hold the sample in place by suction, preventing loss in *very soft, sensitive* marine or alluvial clays. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for the Markdown Table Below:** The absolute-value bars `|...|` have been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` to comply with the markdown rendering safeguard. This is essential to prevent the KTU PDF compiler from breaking the table.

| # | Parameter Name | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Definitions | Typical / Standard Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Area Ratio** (Ar) | $A_r \;=\; \dfrac{D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}}{D_i^{2}} \times 100\,\%$ | $D_o$ = outer diameter of cutting edge; $D_i$ = inner diameter of cutting edge | $\leq 10\%$ for *good* samplers; $\leq 25\%$ acceptable | Quantifies the *annular overcut* that displaces soil. Lower Ar = less disturbance. |
| 2 | **Inside Clearance Ratio** (Ci) | $C_i \;=\; \dfrac{D_s - D_e}{D_e} \times 100\,\%$ | $D_s$ = inner diameter of sample tube; $D_e$ = diameter of cutting edge (tip) | $0.5\% - 3\%$ optimum | Allows elastic swelling of sample to enter tube without excessive wall friction. |
| 3 | **Outside Clearance Ratio** (Co) | $C_o \;=\; \dfrac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100\,\%$ | $D_w$ = outer diameter of sampler head/wall; $D_o$ = outer diameter of cutting edge | $0\% - 2\%$ typical | Reduces wall friction on outside of sampler. |
| 4 | **Recovery Ratio** (Lr) | $L_r \;=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{recovered}}}{L_{\text{penetration}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $L_{\text{recovered}}$ = actual length of sample in tube; $L_{\text{penetration}}$ = driven/pushed depth | $\sim 100\%$ ideal; $< 80\%$ indicates *poor* recovery | Diagnoses sample quality and sampler performance. |
| 5 | **Sample Quality Index** (SQD) | $\text{SQD} = f\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta e}{e_0}\right)$ | $\Delta e = e_0 - e_{\text{Lab}}$; $e_0$ = in-situ void ratio (from oedometer at $\sigma'_0$); $e_{\text{Lab}}$ = lab void ratio on sample reconsolidated to $\sigma'_0$ | SQD A (very good): $\Delta e/e_0 \le 0.04$; B: $0.04 - 0.07$; C: $0.07 - 0.14$; D: $> 0.14$ | Lunne & Andersen's classification of *undisturbed* clay sample quality. |
| 6 | **Blow Count** (N) | $N = \sum_{N_2 + N_3}$ | $N_2, N_3$ = blows for 2nd and 3rd 150 mm penetrations of SPT | Empirical (no fixed value) | Foundation design in sands (correlated with $\phi$, $D_r$, $E_s$). |
| 7 | **Standard Sampler Drive Force** | $F_{\text{drive}} = W \cdot h \cdot n_{\text{eff}}$ | $W = 63.5\,\text{kg}$; $h = 0.75\,\text{m}$; $n_{\text{eff}} \approx 0.60$ | $F \approx 286\,\text{J}$ per blow (theoretical) | Energy delivered per SPT hammer drop (theoretical free-fall). |

### 2.5 The Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic abstractions. They are used **every day** by:

- **Geotechnical consultants** deciding whether a Shelby tube sample or a piston sample is needed for a high-rise foundation in Kochi's marine clays.
- **Highway engineers** computing SPT N-values to design flexible pavements on Kuttanad's alluvial deposits.
- **Offshore engineers** using *area ratio* and *clearance ratio* to design deep-water samplers for the KG Basin or Mumbai High.
- **Dam engineers** validating whether *chunk samples* from a trial pit truly represent the core material of a proposed earth dam.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the Master Equation:** $\text{Design Reliability} \propto \text{Sample Quality}$. The formulas in the table above are the *engineering language* in which sample quality is quantified.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Area Ratio of a Soil Sampler (Theoretical Justification)

The **Area Ratio (Ar)** is the single most important parameter in sampler design. It quantifies *how much soil is displaced and remolded* during sampling relative to the soil that actually enters the tube. The derivation proceeds from first principles.

**Starting Concept:** When a sampler is pushed or driven into the ground, the soil immediately *ahead* of the cutting tip is compressed and forced *outward* (laterally) into the surrounding soil mass. Simultaneously, soil *enters* the tube through the tip. The area over which this disturbance occurs is the **annular area** between the outer and inner diameters of the cutting edge.

**Step 1 — Define the geometric areas involved.**

Let:
- $D_o$ = outer diameter of the cutting edge (m)
- $D_i$ = inner diameter of the cutting edge (m)

The **outer cross-sectional area** of the cutting tip:
$$
A_o \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_o^{2}
$$

The **inner cross-sectional area** of the cutting tip (the area through which sample enters):
$$
A_i \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_i^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the annular (displaced) area.**

The annular area represents the volume of soil that is *sheared and remolded* per unit penetration:
$$
A_{\text{annular}} \;=\; A_o - A_i \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the sample-entry area.**

This is the area that the *recovered* sample actually occupies inside the tube:
$$
A_{\text{sample}} \;=\; A_i \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\cdot D_i^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the dimensionless Area Ratio.**

The Area Ratio is defined as the *ratio* of the disturbed (annular) area to the recovered sample area, expressed as a percentage:
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{A_{\text{annular}}}{A_{\text{sample}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

Substitute the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}\right)}{\frac{\pi}{4}\cdot D_i^{2}} \times 100\,\%
$$

The constant $\frac{\pi}{4}$ cancels from numerator and denominator:
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}}{D_i^{2}} \times 100\,\%
$$

This is the **final form** of the Area Ratio. For *high-quality* sampling, $A_r \le 10\%$. For *acceptable* sampling, $A_r \le 25\%$. Above $25\%$, the sample is *severely disturbed* and only suitable for index testing.

**Step 6 — Sanity check with the standard Shelby tube.**

Standard Shelby tube dimensions: $D_o = 76.2\,\text{mm}$, $D_i = 73.0\,\text{mm}$.
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{(76.2)^{2} - (73.0)^{2}}{(73.0)^{2}} \times 100\,\%
$$
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{5806.44 - 5329.00}{5329.00} \times 100\,\%
$$
$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{477.44}{5329.00} \times 100\,\% \;\approx\; 8.96\,\%
$$

**Result: $A_r \approx 9\%$, which is in the *high-quality* range — consistent with the Shelby tube's reputation as a *thin-walled* sampler.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sample Quality Designation (SQD) After Lunne & Andersen

The Lunne-Anderson criterion is the modern KTU-recommended standard for classifying *undisturbed* clay samples.

**Step 1 — Establish the reference in-situ void ratio ($e_0$).**

The in-situ void ratio is *not* the void ratio of the *recovered* sample (which has already been disturbed). It is determined by taking a sub-sample of the recovered soil and running an **oedometer test**, reconsolidating it stepwise back to the estimated *in-situ effective vertical stress* $\sigma'_{v0}$. The void ratio at the end of this reconsolidation step is the *corrected* in-situ void ratio:
$$
e_0 \;=\; f\!\left(\sigma'_{v0}\right) \quad \text{(from reconsolidation branch of } e \text{ vs. } \log \sigma' \text{ curve)}
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the laboratory initial void ratio ($e_{\text{Lab}}$).**

Immediately upon extrusion from the sampler (or after trimming the test specimen), measure the *actual* initial void ratio of the sample as prepared in the laboratory:
$$
e_{\text{Lab}} \;=\; \frac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_{\text{bulk}}} - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the change in void ratio.**

$$
\Delta e \;=\; e_0 - e_{\text{Lab}}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to a dimensionless ratio.**

$$
\frac{\Delta e}{e_0} \;=\; \frac{e_0 - e_{\text{Lab}}}{e_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Lunne-Anderson classification table.**

| SQD Class | $\Delta e \,/\, e_0$ Range (for OCR = 1 – 2) | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Very Good to Excellent** | $\le 0.04$ | Suitable for advanced tests: $C_v$, $C_c$, $c'/{\phi}'$, stress-path tests |
| **B — Good** | $0.04 - 0.07$ | Suitable for routine $C_c$, $c_u$ (UU/CU), $k$ |
| **C — Fair** | $0.07 - 0.14$ | Only *index* properties and *qualitative* strength reliable |
| **D — Poor / Disturbed** | $> 0.14$ | Treat as *remolded*; do not use for engineering design |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Disturbed vs. Undisturbed Samples (Master Comparison)

| Property / Criterion | **Disturbed Sample** | **Undisturbed Sample** |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure (Fabric)** | Destroyed or significantly altered | Preserved as close to in-situ as practical |
| **Void Ratio (e)** | Not representative of in-situ | Representative (if sample is high quality) |
| **Moisture Content (w)** | Approximately preserved if sealed; but volume-mass relation is lost | Preserved along with volume-mass relation |
| **In-Situ Stress State** | Completely lost | Partially preserved; full restoration requires lab reconsolidation |
| **Suitable Tests** | Grain size, Atterberg limits, specific gravity, compaction, chemical/mineralogical | All of disturbed tests + UU, CU, CD triaxial, consolidation, permeability |
| **Typical Samplers** | Auger cuttings, bag samples, wash samples, bulk samples | Shelby tube, piston sampler, block sample, Laval sampler, Sherbrooke sampler |
| **Cost & Effort** | Low | High (special equipment, slow procedure, careful handling) |
| **Depth of Recovery** | Any depth | Limited by sampler type (up to ~70 m with piston in soft clay) |
| **KTU Board Weight** | High (definitions, applications) | High (theoretical questions + numerical Ar problems) |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Representative vs. Non-representative Samples

| Property / Criterion | **Representative Sample** | **Non-representative Sample** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | All soil constituents present in *true proportion* | One or more constituents *under-represented* or *over-represented* |
| **Fines Content** | Preserved accurately | Often *lost* during sampling (wash boring) or *enriched* (decantation) |
| **Coarse Fraction** | Preserved accurately | May be *excluded* by sampler aperture (small-diameter tube) |
| **Grain-Size Distribution** | True curve obtainable | Skewed — too many fines or too many coarse particles |
| **Typical Examples** | Properly taken *bulk* sample from trial pit; large-diameter piston sample in soft clay | Wash-boring cuttings, SPT spoon in gravelly sand, chunk sample that lost gravel chunks |
| **Acceptable Use** | All classification AND engineering tests (if also undisturbed) | Only *qualitative* identification; never for design |
| **KTU Pitfall** | Students confuse "representative" with "undisturbed" — they are *orthogonal* concepts | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Orthogonality:** "Disturbed/Undisturbed" and "Representative/Non-representative" are **two independent axes**. A sample can be:
> - Disturbed + Representative → Bulk bag sample from trial pit
> - Disturbed + Non-representative → Wash-boring cuttings
> - Undisturbed + Representative → Ideal Shelby tube sample in clay
> - Undisturbed + Non-representative → Thin piston sample in gravelly clay (loses gravel)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Chunk Sample vs. Tube Sample

| Criterion | **Chunk Sample** | **Tube Sample** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Hand-carved, irregular block of cohesive soil | Cylindrical soil column inside a rigid tube (Shelby, split-spoon, piston) |
| **Sampling Depth** | Shallow (limited to trial pit / trench depth, usually $\le 3 - 5\,\text{m}$) | Any depth reachable by borehole ($5 - 70+\,\text{m}$) |
| **Soil Type Suitability** | Stiff to hard, *fissured* clays; clay shales | Soft to stiff clays (Shelby); very soft sensitive clays (piston); all soils (split-spoon) |
| **Disturbance Level** | Low if carefully carved; higher if soil is desiccated during handling | Low for Shelby/piston; higher for split-spoon (driven) |
| **Sample Size** | Typically $200 - 400\,\text{mm}$ cubes; can be larger | Fixed by tube length (usually $450 - 600\,\text{mm}$) and diameter ($35 - 100\,\text{mm}$) |
| **Sealing & Transport** | Wrapped in cling film, then aluminum foil, then wax-sealed, then packed in a rigid box with cushioning | Tube ends sealed with wax or plastic caps; tube itself is the container |
| **Orientation** | Can be *oriented* (mark top/bottom) | Usually vertical (or inclined, as drilled) |
| **Cost** | Moderate (requires pit excavation) | High (requires rig, drillers, sampler) |
| **KTU Board Weight** | Moderate (descriptive question) | High (numerical on Ar, descriptive on methods) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Master Taxonomy of Soil Samples (Mermaid Compliant)

The following block diagram provides a functional, top-down view of how all sample types inter-relate. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; no reserved keywords are used as node names; no markdown or HTML tags appear inside labels.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[SOIL SAMPLES] --> nodeB[By Degree of Disturbance]
    nodeA --> nodeC[By Representativeness]
    nodeA --> nodeD[By Physical Recovery Method]

    nodeB --> nodeE[Disturbed Samples]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Undisturbed Samples]

    nodeC --> nodeG[Representative Samples]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Non Representative Samples]

    nodeD --> nodeI[Chunk Samples]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Tube Samples]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Bulk Samples]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Wash Boring Cuttings]

    nodeE --> nodeM[Auger Cuttings]
    nodeE --> nodeN[Bag Samples]
    nodeE --> nodeO[Split Spoon SPT]

    nodeF --> nodeP[Shelby Thin Wall Tube]
    nodeF --> nodeQ[Piston Sampler]
    nodeF --> nodeR[Block Sample]
    nodeF --> nodeS[Laval Sampler]

    nodeJ --> nodeP
    nodeJ --> nodeQ
    nodeJ --> nodeO
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Operational Workflow of a Tube Sampling Operation

The flowchart below traces the complete sampling procedure, from borehole preparation through laboratory delivery. This is the operational chain examiners expect students to be able to draw and label.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Borehole Advanced to Sampling Depth] --> stepB[Clean Borehole Bottom]
    stepB --> stepC[Check Groundwater Level]
    stepC --> stepD{Select Sampler Type}
    stepD -->|Soft to Stiff Clay| stepE[Attach Shelby Thin Wall Tube]
    stepD -->|Very Soft Sensitive Clay| stepF[Attach Stationary Piston Sampler]
    stepD -->|Sand or Stiff Clay| stepG[Attach Split Spoon Sampler]
    stepE --> stepH[Lower Sampler on Drill Rods]
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Push or Drive into Soil]
    stepI --> stepJ[Record Drive Length and Resistance]
    stepJ --> stepK[Withdraw Sampler Slowly]
    stepK --> stepL[Measure Recovery Length]
    stepL --> stepM[Seal Tube Ends with Wax and Caps]
    stepM --> stepN[Label with Project ID, Depth, Orientation]
    stepN --> stepO[Transport Vertically to Laboratory]
    stepO --> stepP[Lab Storage in Humid Room]
    stepP --> stepQ[End: Sample Ready for Testing]
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Sources and Pathways of Sample Disturbance

This diagram isolates the *causal chain* of disturbance, mapping each source to its physical mechanism and observable consequence. Useful for KTU questions that ask "Discuss the causes of sample disturbance."

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Sources of Disturbance] --> srcB[Mechanical Disturbance]
    srcA --> srcC[Stress Relief]
    srcA --> srcD[Moisture Change]
    srcA --> srcE[Chemical Change]
    srcA --> srcF[Strata Mixing]

    srcB --> effB1[Excessive Area Ratio]
    srcB --> effB2[Driving Shock and Vibration]
    srcB --> effB3[Cutting Edge Dull or Damaged]

    srcC --> effC1[Removal of Overburden Load]
    srcC --> effC2[Negative Pore Pressure on Extraction]
    srcC --> effC3[Effective Stress Reduction]

    srcD --> effD1[Evaporation During Handling]
    srcD --> effD2[Freezing in Transit]
    srcD --> effD3[Water Gain from Rain or Condensation]

    srcE --> effE1[Oxidation of Sulphides]
    srcE --> effE2[Leaching of Salts]

    srcF --> effF1[Sloughing from Borehole Walls]
    srcF --> effF2[Heaving at Borehole Base]

    effB1 --> out[Loss of In-Situ Fabric and Stress State]
    effB2 --> out
    effB3 --> out
    effC1 --> out
    effC2 --> out
    effC3 --> out
    effD1 --> out
    effD2 --> out
    effD3 --> out
    effE1 --> out
    effE2 --> out
    effF1 --> out
    effF2 --> out
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Block-Level Architecture of a Shelby Thin-Walled Tube Sampler

A schematic of the principal physical components of a standard Shelby tube assembly, drawn in Mermaid block form for clarity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    compA[Top Cap and Vent Valve] --> compB[Head Section with Check Valve]
    compB --> compC[Thin Walled Steel Tube]
    compC --> compD[Sharp Beveled Cutting Edge]
    compD --> compE[Soil Sample Inside Tube]
    compE --> compF[Bottom Retainer if Required]
    compF --> compG[Drill Rod Connection at Top]

    compC -.-> annA[Wall Thickness typically 1.2 to 1.6 mm]
    compD -.-> annB[Inner Edge Sharp Outer Edge Slightly Recessed]
    compA -.-> annC[Allows Air and Water Escape During Push]
    compE -.-> annD[Undisturbed Specimen for UU CU and Consolidation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The thin wall (typically 1.2–1.6 mm thick) and the *slightly recessed* outer cutting edge are the two design innovations that minimize disturbance. Compare this to the *split-spoon* (thick-walled, driven) which uses a heavier, hardened cutting shoe and is therefore significantly more disruptive.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between **disturbed** and **undisturbed** soil samples. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer:**

- A *disturbed sample* is one in which the natural soil structure, void ratio, and in-situ stress state have been significantly altered during the sampling process, although the mineralogical composition and grain-size distribution remain essentially intact. Examples: (i) auger cuttings from a rotary auger, and (ii) wash-boring samples retrieved through the drilling fluid return.

- An *undisturbed sample* is one in which the natural soil structure, void ratio, moisture content, and (to the maximum practical extent) the in-situ effective stress state are preserved. The mineralogy is obviously also preserved. Examples: (i) a Shelby thin-walled tube sample from a soft clay stratum, and (ii) a hand-carved chunk (block) sample from the wall of a trial pit in stiff clay.

> A *tabular form* earns full marks more reliably than a paragraph form. Always state what is preserved, what is lost, and give a labeled example for each category.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What is the **Area Ratio** of a soil sampler? Why is a low Area Ratio desirable?

**Model Answer:**

The Area Ratio (Ar) of a soil sampler is defined as the ratio of the *annular cross-sectional area* (between the outer and inner diameters of the cutting tip) to the *inner cross-sectional area* (the area through which the sample enters the tube), expressed as a percentage:

$$
A_r \;=\; \frac{D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}}{D_i^{2}} \times 100\,\%
$$

where $D_o$ and $D_i$ are the outer and inner diameters of the cutting edge, respectively.

A **low Area Ratio is desirable** because the annular area represents the *volume of soil that is sheared and remolded* as the sampler advances. A low Ar means *less soil is disturbed* per unit penetration, and the sample entering the tube is therefore a *truer* representation of the in-situ soil. The conventional KTU standard is $A_r \le 10\%$ for *high-quality* sampling and $A_r \le 25\%$ for *acceptable* sampling.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A, 14 Marks]**

#### Question A

**(a)** Classify the different **types of soil samples** based on (i) degree of disturbance and (ii) representativeness. Give suitable examples for each class. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **various causes of sample disturbance** in geotechnical investigation and the **measures** adopted to minimize them. **[7 Marks]**

##### Model Solution to (a)

Soil samples are classified on two orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — Degree of Disturbance:**

- *Disturbed samples*: Natural structure, void ratio, and stress state altered; mineralogy preserved. Used for index/classification tests. Examples: auger cuttings, bag samples, wash-boring returns, SPT split-spoon (for sand at least).

- *Undisturbed samples*: Natural structure, void ratio, moisture content, and stress state preserved as much as practically possible. Used for engineering property tests (UU, CU, CD, oedometer, permeability). Examples: Shelby tube, piston, block/chunk, Laval, Sherbrooke.

**Axis 2 — Representativeness:**

- *Representative samples*: All soil constituents (gravel, sand, silt, clay, organic) present in *true in-situ proportions*. Examples: a properly collected bulk sample from a trial pit, a large-diameter piston sample.

- *Non-representative samples*: One or more constituents preferentially lost or enriched. Examples: wash-boring cuttings (fines lost), thin piston sample in gravelly soil (gravel lost), SPT spoon in sand (fines lost).

**Cross-combination (orthogonal concept):**

| | Representative | Non-representative |
|---|---|---|
| **Disturbed** | Bulk bag from trial pit | Wash-boring cuttings |
| **Undisturbed** | Shelby tube in soft clay | Thin piston in gravelly clay |

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Causes of disturbance** and corresponding **minimization measures**:

| Cause | Mechanism | Minimization Measure |
|---|---|---|
| (1) Excessive Area Ratio | Large annulus remolds soil | Use thin-walled (Shelby) sampler with Ar $\le 10\%$ |
| (2) Dull or damaged cutting edge | Soil torn rather than cut | Replace cutting edge after every 5–10 samples |
| (3) Driving shock (for thick-walled samplers) | Vibration remolds clay | Use *static push* (hydraulic ram) instead of hammer drive |
| (4) Stress relief after extraction | Negative pore pressure, swelling | Test sample as soon as possible; reconsolidate in lab |
| (5) Moisture loss/gain | Evaporation or wetting alters $w$ | Seal with wax, store in humid room, transport quickly |
| (6) Soil adhesion to tube wall | Friction causes compression | Apply inside clearance ($C_i = 0.5\% - 3\%$), use lubricant |
| (7) Chemical change | Oxidation of sulphides, leaching | Test mineralogy quickly; for sensitive soils, use inert wax |
| (8) Strata mixing | Sloughing of borehole walls | Use drilling mud; clean borehole before sampling |
| (9) Improper handling | Vibration, tilting, jolting in transit | Pack in rigid box with foam; transport vertically |
| (10) Improper storage | Freezing, drying in lab | Store in temperature- and humidity-controlled room |

#### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Describe the **Shelby thin-walled tube sampling** method in detail, with a neat sketch and a discussion of its **limitations**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **chunk sampling** method. Discuss its **applicability, advantages, and limitations** compared to tube sampling. **[7 Marks]**

##### Model Solution to (a) — Shelby Tube Sampling

**Equipment:** A seamless steel tube, approximately 600 mm long, with an outer diameter of 76.2 mm, an inner diameter of 73.0 mm (giving a wall thickness of about 1.6 mm), and a *sharp beveled cutting edge* at the bottom. The top has a head that connects to the drill rod and a small vent valve.

**Area Ratio of standard Shelby tube:**
$$
A_r = \frac{(76.2)^2 - (73.0)^2}{(73.0)^2} \times 100\% \approx 9\%
$$

This is in the *high-quality* range.

**Procedure:**

1. Advance the borehole to the desired sampling depth using a clean-out auger or rotary bit.
2. Clean the bottom of the borehole thoroughly to remove loose cuttings.
3. Attach the Shelby tube to the drill rod and lower it to the bottom of the hole.
4. Push the tube *statically* (without hammer blows) into the soil using a hydraulic ram at a steady rate (typically 25 mm/s). A continuous, smooth push is critical to avoid disturbance.
5. Stop pushing when the tube has penetrated its full length (or when refusal is reached).
6. Rotate the drill rod 1½ to 2 turns to *shear off* the sample at the bottom.
7. Withdraw the sampler slowly and carefully to avoid suction disturbance.
8. Measure the *recovery length*. Seal both ends with wax, label, and transport to the lab.

**Limitations of Shelby Tube Sampling:**

1. **Unsuitable for very soft, sensitive clays** — the sample may be lost as the tube is withdrawn (suction effect).
2. **Unsuitable for sands and gravels** — these soils cannot be retained inside a thin-walled tube.
3. **Limited to depths accessible by the drilling rig** (typically < 50 m without specialized piston equipment).
4. **Wall friction** can compress the sample if the inside clearance is too low; *excessive* clearance can cause sample disturbance.
5. **Sample length is limited** to the tube length (typically 450–600 mm per push).
6. **Each push destroys the sample location** — cannot resample the same horizon easily.

##### Model Solution to (b) — Chunk Sampling

**Definition:** A chunk (or block) sample is a hand-carved, irregular block of cohesive soil, typically $200 \times 200 \times 200\,\text{mm}$ in dimension, retrieved from the *face, base, or side wall* of a trial pit, trench, or large-diameter shaft.

**Procedure:**

1. Excavate a trial pit (or expose a stratum in a trench) to the required depth using a backhoe or hand tools.
2. Trim a *pilaster* of soil slightly larger than the desired sample size, leaving it standing proud of the pit face by about 50–75 mm.
3. Carefully cut around the pilaster with a knife, trowel, or wire saw, isolating it from the parent soil.
4. Undercut the base with a flat blade and lift the chunk out gently.
5. Immediately wrap the chunk in multiple layers: cling film (to retain moisture), then aluminum foil, then a coating of molten microcrystalline wax (to seal against air and moisture loss).
6. Mark the **top and orientation** of the chunk clearly.
7. Pack the chunk in a rigid wooden or thermocol box with cushioning material (sawdust, foam) to prevent vibration damage.
8. Transport carefully, vertically, to the laboratory.

**Applicability:**

- **Stiff to hard, fissured clays** (e.g., lateritic clays of central Kerala, black cotton soils).
- **Clay shales and weathered rocks** where the *fissure pattern* and *in-situ fabric* are of engineering interest.
- **Shallow depths only** — typically $\le 5\,\text{m}$, the practical limit of trial pit excavation.
- **Above the water table** — otherwise the pit floods and the sample disintegrates.

**Advantages over tube sampling:**

| Feature | Chunk | Tube |
|---|---|---|
| Larger sample size | ✓ (200–400 mm cubes) | ✗ (capped by tube length) |
| Orientation marking | ✓ Easy | ✗ Difficult (usually vertical) |
| Visual identification of fissures, fabric | ✓ Excellent | ✗ Limited |
| Cost at shallow depth | Lower (no rig) | Higher (rig mobilization) |
| Penetration of hard crust | ✗ Difficult | ✓ Drill can penetrate |

**Limitations of chunk sampling:**

1. **Depth-limited** — only feasible above the water table and at shallow depth.
2. **Disturbance during carving** — the outer 5–10 mm of the chunk is *always* remolded and must be trimmed off in the lab.
3. **Time-consuming and labor-intensive** — not economical for large projects.
4. **Not suitable for soft, sensitive, or saturated clays** — they will slump or lose shape.
5. **Sample size variation** — quality depends heavily on the operator's skill.
6. **Cannot be obtained below the water table** without expensive dewatering.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic — A Checklist for the KTU 2024 Examiner's Pen**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Disturbed/Undisturbed" with "Representative/Non-representative":** These are *two independent classifications*, not synonyms. A sample can be disturbed yet representative (e.g., bulk bag from a trial pit). A student who conflates them loses 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Area Ratio calculation:** Writing $A_r = 0.09$ instead of $A_r = 9\%$. The numerical value without the percentage sign costs 0.5 mark.
>
> 3. **Writing "Area Ratio" instead of the full symbol $A_r$ and equation:** Examiners expect the *formula* with all variables defined.
>
> 4. **Listing only "stress relief" as a cause of disturbance:** There are at least *six* major causes (mechanical, stress relief, moisture, chemical, strata mixing, handling). A student who lists two loses marks.
>
> 5. **Saying chunk samples are "undisturbed":** They are *less* disturbed than auger cuttings, but the carving process *does* cause some disturbance at the surfaces. A more accurate answer is "*less disturbed* than tube samples in stiff fissured clay" with a brief justification.
>
> 6. **Skipping the orientation issue:** Chunk samples can be *oriented*; tube samples usually cannot. This is a favourite 1-mark differentiator.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the SQD (Sample Quality Designation) classification:** A 7-mark question that omits Lunne & Andersen loses up to 2 marks.
>
> 8. **Not mentioning the 63.5 kg / 750 mm SPT standard:** This is a direct KTU 2024 syllabus hit. Forgetting it is a 0.5–1 mark penalty.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Block — Print This Page Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Disturbed sample** = structure altered, mineralogy preserved. Used for *index* tests.
- [ ] **Undisturbed sample** = structure, void ratio, moisture, stress state preserved. Used for *engineering* tests.
- [ ] **Representative sample** = all soil constituents in *true proportion*.
- [ ] **Non-representative sample** = some constituents *lost or enriched* (e.g., fines in wash boring).
- [ ] The two classifications are **orthogonal** (independent axes) — not the same thing.
- [ ] **Area Ratio** $A_r = (D_o^2 - D_i^2)/D_i^2 \times 100\%$. Good sampler: $A_r \le 10\%$.
- [ ] **Inside Clearance** $C_i$ ideal: **0.5% – 3%**. Outside Clearance $C_o$ ideal: **0% – 2%**.
- [ ] **Recovery Ratio** $L_r = L_{\text{recovered}}/L_{\text{penetration}} \times 100\%$. Target $\approx 100\%$.
- [ ] Standard **Shelby tube**: 76.2 mm OD, 73.0 mm ID, $A_r \approx 9\%$, 600 mm long.
- [ ] **SPT** uses a **63.5 kg** hammer dropped **750 mm**, through a **50.8 mm OD** split spoon.
- [ ] **Chunk sample**: hand-carved, shallow, stiff clay, oriented, sealed in wax, transported in rigid box.
- [ ] **Tube sample**: cylindrical, deep, any consistency, sealed at both ends with wax caps.
- [ ] **Six causes of disturbance**: mechanical, stress relief, moisture, chemical, strata mixing, handling.
- [ ] **Lunne-Anderson SQD**: A (excellent, $\Delta e/e_0 \le 0.04$) → B (good, 0.04–0.07) → C (fair, 0.07–0.14) → D (poor, > 0.14).
- [ ] **Piston sampler** is preferred over Shelby for *very soft, sensitive* marine clays.
- [ ] Always *push statically* — never hammer — when sampling clays for undisturbed specimens.
- [ ] Always *seal immediately* with wax and store in a *humid room* — moisture change is the silent killer of sample quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sounding Methods – Standard Penetration Test: Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sounding Methods – Standard Penetration Test: Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** is a widely used, in-situ dynamic penetration test performed in a borehole to determine the geotechnical engineering properties of soil. It is conducted as per **IS 2131:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002)** and **IS 6926:1973** in India, and **ASTM D1586** internationally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Definition (KTU Syllabus):** The SPT is an in-situ dynamic penetration test wherein a standard split-spoon sampler is driven into the soil at the bottom of a borehole by means of a **63.5 kg (140 lb) hammer** falling freely from a height of **750 mm (30 inches)**. The number of blows required to drive the sampler through **three successive 150 mm penetrations** is recorded, and the **SPT 'N' value** is defined as the sum of the blows for the **last two 150 mm penetrations** (i.e., the final 300 mm of penetration).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to push a thin hollow tube into a packed mound of sand using a rubber mallet. 

- If the sand is loose, the tube slides in easily with a few taps.
- If the sand is dense, you need many forceful taps.
- If the sand contains gravel, the tube may refuse to penetrate at all.

**SPT works on the exact same principle** — instead of your hand, a standardized 63.5 kg hammer does the "tapping," and instead of a tube, a standardized split-spoon sampler does the pushing. The number of hammer blows required is a direct measure of the soil's **in-situ density and shear strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Standard"?** The test is "standard" because the dimensions of the sampler, weight of the hammer, drop height, and penetration intervals are all rigidly fixed by international codes. Without these standards, results from one site would be incompatible with another.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Hammer mass | **63.5 kg** (140 lb) |
| Drop height | **750 mm** (30 in) |
| Sampler outer diameter | **51 mm** (2 in) |
| Sampler inner diameter | **35 mm** (1.375 in) |
| Penetration intervals | **3 × 150 mm** (total 450 mm) |
| Recorded N-value interval | **Last 300 mm** |
| Drill rod type | **AW or NW flush-coupled steel** |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** SPT is a **blow count-based empirical test**, NOT a direct measurement of strength. Correlations are empirical, so its reliability depends heavily on **standardization of the procedure**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The SPT can be broken into **four distinct operational stages**:

### Stage 1: Borehole Preparation
- A borehole is advanced to the desired test depth using rotary or auger drilling.
- The hole must be **clean of cuttings** and the bottom should be relatively undisturbed.
- Casing or drilling mud may be used to maintain hole stability in cohesionless soils below the water table.

### Stage 2: Sampler Insertion
- The **split-spoon sampler** is attached to the bottom of the **drill rod string**.
- The entire assembly is lowered to the bottom of the borehole.
- The drill rod must be vertical, and the sampler must rest on the undisturbed soil at the borehole base.

### Stage 3: Hammer Driving (The Core Test)
- A 63.5 kg hammer is repeatedly lifted and dropped from 750 mm using either:
  - **Donut hammer** (older, less efficient)
  - **Safety hammer** (with internal guide)
  - **Automatic (trip) hammer** (most modern and efficient — energy ratio 60–100%)
- The blows are counted for three 150 mm intervals.
- The blows for the **first 150 mm** (seating drive) are noted but **discarded**.
- The blows for the **second and third 150 mm** are summed → this is the **N-value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The N-value is the SUM of blows for the LAST 300 mm of penetration.** This is a frequent trap in exams — students often write N as the total of all 450 mm. This is incorrect.

### Stage 4: Sampler Retrieval & Logging
- The sampler is withdrawn.
- The **soil sample (disturbed)** inside is carefully preserved in airtight containers for visual classification, moisture content, and laboratory index tests.
- The penetration resistance, sample description, and depth are recorded on the borehole log.

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Affects N |
|---|---|---|
| Seating drive (150 mm) | Accommodates disturbed/remolded zone at borehole base | Discarded to avoid underestimation |
| 300 mm drive | Reflects true in-situ soil resistance | Direct input for N |
| Clean borehole bottom | Prevents fall-in of cuttings giving false high N | Increases accuracy |
| Sampler condition (sharp cutting edge) | Worn shoes reduce penetration resistance | Affects blow count consistency |
| Hammer type | Energy delivered varies (45–100% of theoretical) | Critical for N corrections |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are critical for KTU University Exam questions. Memorize the corrected N-value formulas.**

### Basic SPT N-Value Equations

$$
N_{measured} = N_2 + N_3
$$

where $N_2$ = blows for 2nd 150 mm, $N_3$ = blows for 3rd 150 mm.

### Corrections Applied to SPT N-Value (IS 2131 / Bowles Recommendations)

| Correction | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Overburden Pressure Correction** | $N_c = N \cdot C_N$ where $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)$ (Peck) | Normalizes N to a reference effective stress of 100 kPa (≈ 2000 psf) |
| **Dilatancy Correction (Sands > GW table)** | $N' = 15 + 0.5(N - 15)$ for $N > 15$ in fine sand below water table | Compensates for pore-pressure buildup that artificially increases N |
| **Water Table / Silt Correction** | $N' = 15 + 0.5(N - 15)$ (for silty/clayey fine sand) | Adjusts for apparent over-resistance |
| **Hammer Energy Correction** | $N_{60} = N \cdot \frac{E_h}{60}$ | Normalizes to standard 60% energy ratio |
| **Borehole Diameter Correction** | Apply factor: 1.00 (65–115 mm), 1.05 (150 mm), 1.15 (200 mm) | Adjusts for variable hole diameter |

### Empirical Correlations from N-Value

| Parameter | Correlation (Cohesionless Soils) |
|---|---|
| Relative Density $D_r$ | $D_r(\%) = \sqrt{\frac{N_{corr}}{0.6 \cdot \sigma'_{vo}}}$ (Skempton) |
| Friction Angle $\phi$ | $\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 N_c - 0.00054 (N_c)^2$ (Peck) or $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{N}{12\sigma'_{vo} + 25}\right)$ (Kulhawy) |
| SPT Modulus $E_s$ | $E_s = 0.5$ to $1.0 \cdot N$ (MPa) — approximate |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design:** SPT N-values are directly used in **Meyerhof and Hansen bearing capacity equations** for shallow foundations in sand.
- **Liquefaction Assessment:** Corrected SPT blow counts are the **primary input** for simplified liquefaction analysis (Seed-Idriss method).
- **Pile Design:** N-values provide estimates of pile end-bearing and shaft friction in cohesionless soils.
- **Soil Profiling:** Combined with sampling, SPT builds a continuous stratigraphic log for the entire borehole depth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed SPT Field Procedure — Step-by-Step

Below is the **complete chronological procedure** as specified by **IS 2131:1981**.

### Step 1: Site Preparation & Rig Setup
Position the rotary drilling rig (or tripod and casing pipe setup) over the test location. Ensure the rig is level and the mast is vertical (plumb check). Mark the test depth intervals on the drill rod string for accurate recording.

### Step 2: Borehole Advancement
Advance the borehole to the first test depth using a suitable method:
- **In stiff cohesive soils:** Use augers or rotary drilling with bentonite slurry.
- **In granular soils below water table:** Use casing with rotary wash boring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Step:** The bottom of the borehole must be **clean of loose cuttings** before lowering the sampler. Cuttings may fall in and falsely increase the N-value.

### Step 3: Assembling the SPT Sampler
1. Disassemble the split-spoon sampler (two halves).
2. Insert the **brass liner** (if used) inside the lower half.
3. Place the **ball check valve** at the top coupling.
4. Clamp the two halves together with the **drive shoe** (cutting edge) at the bottom.
5. Attach the sampler to the **drill rod string** (typically AW or NW rods).

### Step 4: Lowering the Sampler
Lower the assembled sampler to the bottom of the borehole using the drill rod string. Rest it gently on the undisturbed soil at the borehole base. **Do not drop it** — this would disturb the test zone.
