### Step 5: Attaching the Hammer System
Attach the hammer (63.5 kg) to the **drive weight assembly**. The hammer is repeatedly lifted to 750 mm and released freely. Mark the 750 mm height clearly on the guide rod.

### Step 6: Driving the Sampler
Drive the sampler with the hammer and count the blows:

$$
N_1 = \text{blows for 1st } 150 \text{ mm (seating drive)}
$$

$$
N_2 = \text{blows for 2nd } 150 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
N_3 = \text{blows for 3rd } 150 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refusal Criteria:** Driving is stopped if **50 blows** are required for any 150 mm penetration, or if **100 blows** are required for the full 300 mm test length, or if **10 successive blows produce no advance**. The penetration achieved must be recorded.

### Step 7: Record the N-Value
Compute the SPT N-value:

$$
N = N_2 + N_3
$$

The first 150 mm blow count $N_1$ is **discarded** as it represents seating into disturbed soil.

### Step 8: Retrieve the Sample
Withdraw the sampler to the surface. Open the split-spoon halves, carefully transfer the **disturbed soil sample** to an airtight glass jar, label it with depth, project, and borehole number.

### Step 9: Continue to Next Depth
Advance the borehole by approximately **0.75 m to 1.5 m**, and repeat Steps 3–8 for the next test depth. **Test intervals are typically 1.5 m to 3.0 m** depending on project requirements.

### Step 10: Final Recording
Document all data in the borehole log: hammer type, hammer efficiency, rod length, casing depth, water table depth, sample description, and observed N-values.

---

## 3.2 Derivations of Key Corrections

### Derivation 1: Overburden Pressure Correction (Peck, Hanson, Thornburn, 1974)

The corrected SPT N-value for a reference effective overburden pressure of 1 ton/ft² (≈ 100 kPa) is:

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N_{measured} \cdot C_N
$$

where the correction factor $C_N$ is given by:

$$
C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The measured N-value depends on the existing effective overburden pressure $\sigma'_{vo}$ at the test depth.
2. $\sigma'_{vo}$ is calculated as:

$$
\sigma'_{vo} = \sigma'_{v0,\text{above WT}} + \sigma'_{v0,\text{below WT}}
$$

$$
\sigma'_{vo} = \left(\sum \gamma_i \cdot \Delta z_i\right)_{\text{above WT}} + \left(\sum \gamma'_i \cdot \Delta z_i\right)_{\text{below WT}}
$$

where $\gamma'$ is the buoyant (submerged) unit weight.

3. A reference effective stress of 2000 psf (≈ 100 kPa) is adopted.
4. $C_N \leq 2.0$ is the standard upper limit to avoid overcorrection in very loose soils.

### Derivation 2: Dilatancy Correction (Terzaghi & Peck, 1948)

For **fine or silty sands below the water table**, SPT N-values may be artificially high because pore water cannot drain quickly during the rapid dynamic driving. Apply:

$$
(N_1)_{60,corr} = 15 + 0.5 \cdot (N_{60} - 15) \quad \text{for } N_{60} > 15
$$

**Explanation:**
- When $N > 15$, the soil is dilative (dense), and excess pore pressure under undrained impact falsely raises the count.
- The correction reduces N by 50% of the excess above 15, capping the correction implicitly.

### Derivation 3: SPT to Relative Density (Skempton, 1986)

For clean sand:

$$
\frac{N_{60}}{D_r^2} = a + b \cdot \sigma'_{vo}
$$

Solving for $D_r$:

$$
D_r^2 = \frac{N_{60}}{a + b \cdot \sigma'_{vo}}
$$

$$
D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{60}}{a + b \cdot \sigma'_{vo}}}
$$

For normally consolidated clean sand ($a \approx 0$, $b \approx 0.6$ at $\sigma'$ in kPa):

$$
D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_{60}}{0.6 \cdot \sigma'_{vo}}} \quad (\text{in decimal, multiply by 100 for \%})
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Typical N-Value Interpretation

| N-Value (Blows/300mm) | Consistency / Density (Sand) | Consistency (Clay) | Approx. $\phi$ (°) | SPT $q_c$ correlation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 4 | Very loose | Very soft | < 28 | $q_c \approx 4 N$ |
| 4 – 10 | Loose | Soft | 28 – 30 | $q_c \approx 4 N$ |
| 10 – 30 | Medium dense | Medium stiff | 30 – 36 | $q_c \approx 4 N$ |
| 30 – 50 | Dense | Stiff | 36 – 41 | $q_c \approx 4 N$ |
| > 50 | Very dense | Very stiff | > 41 | $q_c \approx 4 N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The correlation $q_c \approx 4N$ (where $q_c$ is in kg/cm²) is a widely used approximate link between SPT N and CPT cone resistance (Meigh, 1987).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SPT Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions between all physical components** of the SPT system, the energy flow, and the data acquisition path.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Drilling Rig Mast and Setup] --> B[AW or NW Drill Rod String]
    B --> C[Split Spoon Sampler Assembly]
    C --> C1[Drive Shoe Cutting Edge]
    C --> C2[Brass Liner with Soil Sample]
    C --> C3[Ball Check Valve Air Escape]
    C --> D[Drive Weight Assembly]
    D --> D1[63.5 kg Hammer]
    D --> D2[750 mm Drop Guide]
    D --> D3[Anvil and Striking Head]
    A --> E[Energy Source]
    E --> E1[Manual Pulley or Auto Trip]
    D --> F[Energy Transfer to Sampler]
    F --> G[Penetration into Soil]
    G --> G1[First 150 mm Seating Drive N1]
    G --> G2[Second 150 mm N2]
    G --> G3[Third 150 mm N3]
    G2 --> H[Record Blow Count]
    G3 --> H[Record Blow Count]
    H --> I[Compute N equals N2 plus N3]
    I --> J[Apply Corrections CN Dilatancy Energy]
    J --> K[Final Corrected N60 Value]
    C2 --> L[Sample Recovery to Surface]
    L --> M[Soil Classification and Lab Tests]
    K --> N[Engineering Design Parameters]
    M --> N
    N --> N1[Bearing Capacity]
    N --> N2[Liquefaction Assessment]
    N --> N3[Pile Load Capacity]
    N --> N4[Soil Stratigraphy Log]
```

## 4.2 SPT Procedure Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Position Rig] --> S2[Advance Borehole to Test Depth]
    S2 --> S3[Clean Borehole Bottom]
    S3 --> S4[Assemble Split Spoon Sampler]
    S4 --> S5[Lower Sampler on Drill Rods]
    S5 --> S6[Rest Sampler on Soil Surface]
    S6 --> S7{Seating Drive 150 mm}
    S7 -->|Record N1 Discarded| S8[Drive 2nd 150 mm]
    S8 --> S9[Drive 3rd 150 mm]
    S9 --> S10{Refusal Criteria Met?}
    S10 -->|No| S11[Compute N equals N2 plus N3]
    S10 -->|Yes 50 blows per 150 mm| S12[Record Refusal and Penetration]
    S12 --> S11
    S11 --> S13[Withdraw Sampler to Surface]
    S13 --> S14[Preserve Disturbed Sample]
    S14 --> S15{More Test Depths Required?}
    S15 -->|Yes| S2
    S15 -->|No| S16[End: Compile Borehole Log]
```

## 4.3 SPT Sampler Cross-Section — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Top Coupling Threaded to Drill Rod] --> VALVE[Ball Check Valve for Air Release]
    VALVE --> SPOON[Split Spoon Barrel Two Halves]
    SPOON --> LINER[Brass Liner Optional Inside]
    SPOON --> SHOE[Drive Shoe with Cutting Edge]
    SHOE --> SOIL[Penetrating Soil Formation]
    TOP --> STRESS[Vertical Driving Force from Hammer]
    STRESS --> SOIL
    SOIL --> RESPONSE[Soil Resistance Reaction]
    RESPONSE --> STRESS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.1:** The diagram traces the **physical energy path** (hammer → rods → sampler → soil) and the **data path** (blow counts → N-value → corrections → design). The ball check valve at the top of the sampler allows air and water to escape during driving, preventing suction resistance that would artificially inflate the blow count.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value. What is the significance of discarding the first 150 mm penetration?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The SPT N-value is defined as the **sum of the number of blows required to drive the standard split-spoon sampler through the second and third 150 mm penetrations** (i.e., the last 300 mm of a 450 mm total drive) using a 63.5 kg hammer falling from 750 mm.
>
> **Significance of discarding the first 150 mm (1 mark):**
> - The first 150 mm is the **seating drive** that pushes the sampler through the **disturbed, remolded zone** at the bottom of the borehole caused by drilling.
> - This zone is not representative of in-situ soil properties.
> - Discarding it ensures N reflects the **undisturbed soil response**, improving the empirical correlation with density and strength.

---

### Question 2: List any three corrections applied to the SPT N-value and state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Overburden Pressure Correction ($C_N$):** Normalizes the measured N to a reference effective overburden stress of 100 kPa, since the same soil at different depths gives different N-values due to confinement.

2. **Dilatancy Correction:** Applied to saturated fine sands and silts where positive pore pressure buildup during rapid driving artificially increases N; the formula $N' = 15 + 0.5(N - 15)$ corrects for this.

3. **Hammer Energy Correction ($N_{60}$):** Different hammer types deliver different energy ratios (45–100% of theoretical). $N_{60} = N \cdot (E_h/60)$ normalizes to the standard 60% energy ratio for cross-comparison of results.

*(1 mark each: 0.5 mark for stating the correction, 0.5 mark for its purpose.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Standard SPT (14 Marks)

> **Q. A. (a)** Describe in detail the **procedure of conducting a Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** in the field, with a neat sketch of the equipment used. List the **IS code specifications** for the test. (7 marks)

> **Q. A. (b)** A standard penetration test was conducted at a depth of 6.0 m below ground level in a cohesionless soil deposit. The observed N-value was **24 blows**. The soil has a unit weight of **18 kN/m³** above the water table (which is at 2.0 m depth) and a saturated unit weight of **20 kN/m³** below. Compute the **corrected N-value** using (i) overburden correction (Peck's formula) and (ii) dilatancy correction. Use $C_N$ upper limit of 2.0. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A. (a): SPT Procedure & IS Codes (7 marks)

**Equipment sketch description (2 marks):**

The SPT setup consists of:
- A **drilling rig** (rotary or tripod) with vertical mast
- **AW/NW drill rods** (rigid, flush-coupled)
- **Split-spoon sampler** (51 mm OD, 35 mm ID, 600 mm long, with cutting shoe and brass liner)
- **Drive-weight assembly** (63.5 kg hammer, anvil, guide rod)
- **Trip mechanism or auto hammer** for consistent drop

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. Advance the borehole to the test depth using rotary or auger drilling.
2. Clean the borehole bottom; lower the assembled split-spoon sampler to the bottom.
3. Rest the sampler gently on undisturbed soil.
4. Drive the sampler with a 63.5 kg hammer falling freely from 750 mm.
5. Record blows for three 150 mm intervals: $N_1$ (seating, discarded), $N_2$, $N_3$.
6. The SPT N-value = $N_2 + N_3$.
7. Withdraw the sampler; preserve the soil sample in airtight containers.
8. Note hammer type, rod length, casing depth, and water table for corrections.

**IS Code Specifications (1 mark):**

- **IS 2131:1981** — Method of standard penetration test for soils
- **IS 6926:1973** — Specifications for split-spoon samplers
- **IS 1498:1970** — Classification and identification of soils (for sample description)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. A. (b): Corrected N-Value Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Depth of test = 6.0 m
- Observed N = 24
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ above water table
- $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ below water table
- Water table depth = 2.0 m

**Step 1: Compute effective overburden pressure at 6.0 m (2 marks)**

The total depth below WT = $6.0 - 2.0 = 4.0$ m
The depth above WT = 2.0 m

$$
\sigma_{vo} = 18 \times 2.0 + 20 \times 4.0 = 36 + 80 = 116 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
u = \gamma_w \times 4.0 = 9.81 \times 4.0 = 39.24 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\sigma'_{vo} = 116 - 39.24 = 76.76 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Peck's Overburden Correction $C_N$ (2 marks)**

$$
C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_{vo}}\right)
$$

**Important:** $\sigma'_{vo}$ in Peck's formula is in psf. Convert: $76.76 \text{ kPa} \times 20.885 = 1603 \text{ psf}$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{1603}\right) = 0.77 \times \log_{10}(1.2477)
$$

$$
C_N = 0.77 \times 0.0960 = 0.074
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:** Many students apply Peck's formula directly in kPa. This is INCORRECT. The constant 2000 is in psf. The correct procedure is to convert $\sigma'_{vo}$ to psf, OR use the **metric equivalent formula** $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(100/\sigma'_{vo} \text{ in kPa})$, OR use **Liao and Whitman's formula** $C_N = \sqrt{100/\sigma'_{vo}}$ (in kPa). Choose one approach and be consistent.

**Using Liao-Whitman (commonly accepted alternative):**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{100}{76.76}} = \sqrt{1.3028} = 1.141
$$

Since $C_N < 2.0$, it is acceptable.

**Step 3: Corrected N-value (1 mark)**

$$
N_c = N \times C_N = 24 \times 1.141 = 27.4
$$

**Step 4: Apply Dilatancy Correction (2 marks)**

For $N > 15$ in fine sand below water table:

$$
N' = 15 + 0.5 \times (N_c - 15) = 15 + 0.5 \times (27.4 - 15)
$$

$$
N' = 15 + 0.5 \times 12.4 = 15 + 6.2 = 21.2
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
N'_{corrected} \approx 21 \text{ blows/300 mm}
$$

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q. B. (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **components and functioning of the SPT apparatus**. What are the various **types of hammers** used in SPT, and how does the hammer type affect the N-value? (7 marks)

> **Q. B. (b)** During an SPT in loose to medium sand below the water table, the following results were obtained at four successive test depths: N = 8, 11, 14, 19. The water table is at the ground surface. The soil has a saturated unit weight of 19 kN/m³. The effective overburden pressures at the test depths are: 38, 76, 114, 152 kPa. Apply the **overburden correction** using Liao-Whitman's method, and tabulate the **corrected N-values** with corresponding relative density. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B. (a): SPT Apparatus & Hammer Types (7 marks)

**Apparatus components (3 marks):**

The SPT apparatus consists of the following parts:
1. **Drill rods:** AW or NW flush-coupled steel rods that connect the surface hammer to the subsurface sampler.
2. **Split-spoon sampler:** A 600 mm long, 51 mm OD, 35 mm ID steel tube split longitudinally into two halves. A **drive shoe** (cutting edge) is fitted at the bottom, and a **coupling** at the top connects to the rods.
3. **Drive weight assembly:** Includes the hammer, anvil, and guide mechanism.
4. **Hammer:** 63.5 kg, lifted to 750 mm and dropped freely.
5. **Trip mechanism / rope and pulley system:** For lifting and releasing the hammer.

**Hammer types and energy ratio (4 marks):**

| Hammer Type | Energy Ratio (%) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Donut Hammer** (rope-pulley) | 45 – 60 | Older system; friction losses reduce delivered energy |
| **Safety Hammer** (internal guide) | 50 – 70 | Used in many countries; better energy transfer than donut |
| **Automatic (Trip) Hammer** | 60 – 100 | Most modern; highly repeatable; least operator-dependent |

**Effect on N-value:**
- A hammer with **lower energy ratio** (e.g., donut at 45%) delivers **less kinetic energy** per blow → more blows are required → **N is artificially higher**.
- Conversely, a hammer with **higher energy ratio** (e.g., auto-trip at 90%) delivers more energy → fewer blows → **N is artificially lower**.
- This is why the **$N_{60}$ correction** is mandatory for inter-site and inter-country comparison.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. B. (b): Overburden Correction Table (7 marks)

**Given:**

| Depth | N (measured) | $\sigma'_{vo}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 38 |
| 2 | 11 | 76 |
| 3 | 14 | 114 |
| 4 | 19 | 152 |

**Step 1: Compute $C_N$ using Liao-Whitman (2 marks)**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{100}{\sigma'_{vo}}}
$$

For each depth:
- $C_{N1} = \sqrt{100/38} = \sqrt{2.632} = 1.622$
- $C_{N2} = \sqrt{100/76} = \sqrt{1.316} = 1.147$
- $C_{N3} = \sqrt{100/114} = \sqrt{0.877} = 0.937$
- $C_{N4} = \sqrt{100/152} = \sqrt{0.658} = 0.811$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S PITFALL:** $C_N > 2.0$ triggers the upper limit. For very shallow depths or very loose soil, applying the Liao-Whitman formula without the 2.0 cap can overcorrect N significantly. Always apply the limit $C_N \leq 2.0$.

**Step 2: Compute corrected N (2 marks)**

$$
N_c = N \times C_N
$$

- $N_{c1} = 8 \times 1.622 = 12.98 \approx 13$
- $N_{c2} = 11 \times 1.147 = 12.62 \approx 13$
- $N_{c3} = 14 \times 0.937 = 13.12 \approx 13$
- $N_{c4} = 19 \times 0.811 = 15.41 \approx 15$

**Step 3: Compute relative density $D_r$ (3 marks)**

$$
D_r = \sqrt{\frac{N_c}{0.6 \cdot \sigma'_{vo}}}
$$

- $D_{r1} = \sqrt{12.98 / (0.6 \times 38)} = \sqrt{12.98 / 22.8} = \sqrt{0.569} = 0.754 = 75.4\%$
- $D_{r2} = \sqrt{12.62 / (0.6 \times 76)} = \sqrt{12.62 / 45.6} = \sqrt{0.277} = 0.526 = 52.6\%$
- $D_{r3} = \sqrt{13.12 / (0.6 \times 114)} = \sqrt{13.12 / 68.4} = \sqrt{0.192} = 0.438 = 43.8\%$
- $D_{r4} = \sqrt{15.41 / (0.6 \times 152)} = \sqrt{15.41 / 91.2} = \sqrt{0.169} = 0.411 = 41.1\%$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Depth | N | $C_N$ | $N_c$ | $D_r$ (%) | Density Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | 1.62 | 13 | 75 | Dense |
| 2 | 11 | 1.15 | 13 | 53 | Medium dense |
| 3 | 14 | 0.94 | 13 | 44 | Medium dense |
| 4 | 19 | 0.81 | 15 | 41 | Medium dense |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the **uncorrected N increases with depth** (8 → 19), but the **corrected N is nearly constant (13)**. This shows that the apparent increase in penetration resistance was due to increasing overburden, not increasing soil density. The soil is actually **uniformly medium dense** to a depth of about 4 m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in SPT Questions:**
> 1. **Mistaking N as the total blow count for 450 mm** — Correct: N = blows for last 300 mm only.
> 2. **Using σ in kPa in Peck's original 2000/psf formula** — Convert units or use the metric alternative.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the $C_N \leq 2.0$ upper limit** — Leads to overcorrection at shallow depths.
> 4. **Applying dilatancy correction to clays or gravels** — Only for saturated fine/silty sands.
> 5. **Confusing N, $N_{60}$, $(N_1)_{60}$, and $N_{corr}$** — Each has a specific definition; the order of corrections matters.
> 6. **Omitting the seating drive explanation** — Examiners expect explicit mention of why $N_1$ is discarded.
> 7. **Failing to mention IS codes** — IS 2131, IS 6926, and IS 1498 are mandatory references for Indian context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SPT** is the **most widely used in-situ test worldwide** for soil profiling and density estimation.
- ✅ Standard hammer: **63.5 kg**, drop height: **750 mm**, sampler penetration: **3 × 150 mm**.
- ✅ **N = $N_2 + N_3$** (last 300 mm only); **$N_1$ is discarded** as seating drive.
- ✅ **Refusal** = 50 blows per 150 mm, or 100 blows for 300 mm, or no advance on 10 blows.
- ✅ **IS 2131:1981** is the governing Indian code for the test procedure; **IS 6926:1973** governs the sampler.
- ✅ **Four critical corrections:** overburden, dilatancy, hammer energy, borehole diameter.
- ✅ Peck's $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_{vo})$ uses **psf**; Liao-Whitman $C_N = \sqrt{100/\sigma'_{vo}}$ uses **kPa**.
- ✅ **Dilatancy correction** $N' = 15 + 0.5(N-15)$ applies **only for $N > 15$** in saturated fine sand.
- ✅ **Hammer type** directly affects N: donut (low energy) gives higher N; auto-trip (high energy) gives lower N.
- ✅ **Sample recovered is disturbed** — SPT gives penetration resistance + disturbed sample, NOT an undisturbed sample (use **UD sampler** for that).
- ✅ SPT is **prohibited in gravel, boulder, and rock strata** — use pressuremeter or plate load test instead.
- ✅ Test spacing: typically **1.5 m to 3.0 m** vertically; continuous sampling is sometimes done in critical layers.
- ✅ The **ball check valve** at the top of the sampler prevents air-lock and suction, which would falsely increase N.
- ✅ Correlations from N are **empirical** — they should be validated by **liquefaction, bearing capacity, and pile** design case histories before being used for final design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing SPT Results, Precautions for Reliable Results, Corrections to Observed N Values, Correlations of N Value with Engineering and Index Properties of Soils, Field Study, Field Visit and Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Standard Penetration Test (SPT)?

The **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** is an in-situ dynamic penetration test widely used in geotechnical engineering to obtain a qualitative estimate of the density, strength, and bearing capacity of soils, and to retrieve a disturbed sample for identification and laboratory classification. The test is conducted in a borehole at the desired depth using a standard split-spoon (Raymond) sampler.

The recorded **SPT N-value** is defined as the *number of hammer blows required to drive the standard split-spoon sampler a distance of **300 mm (12 inches)** into the soil at the bottom of the borehole, after an initial seating drive of **150 mm**.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard SPT Configuration (ASTM D1586 / IS 2131):**
> - **Hammer weight:** 63.5 kg (140 lbs)
> - **Drop height:** 750 mm (30 inches)
> - **Sampler:** Standard split-spoon, 50.8 mm OD × 35 mm ID
> - **Blow count (N):** For the last 300 mm of a 450 mm drive
> - **Reference Energy (ER,r):** 60% of theoretical free-fall energy (the "60% energy ratio" benchmark)

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Corrections to SPT N-Values?

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of SPT as a "stress test" for soil — but the test conditions are never the same twice. Imagine measuring someone's sprint time while changing the track surface (overburden), shoe type (sampler), referee stopwatch (operator), and lane width (borehole) all at once. A raw sprint time is meaningless unless normalized to standard conditions. SPT corrections do exactly that — they "normalize" the raw N-value to a common reference (N1,60,cs) so that the result represents the true soil behavior rather than the testing conditions.

The raw SPT blow count (N_measured) is influenced not only by soil properties but also by a multitude of test-execution and equipment variables. Without corrections, the same soil tested under different conditions can give drastically different N-values, leading to erroneous design conclusions.

## 1.3 Categories of Influencing Factors

| Category | Examples | Nature |
|----------|----------|--------|
| **Equipment Factors** | Hammer type, sampler design, rod length, borehole diameter | Mechanical/Procedural |
| **Procedural Factors** | Hammer release, blow rate, drill method, water table | Operational |
| **Soil-State Factors** | Effective overburden stress, density, fines content, saturation | Soil-property related |
| **Human Factors** | Operator skill, interpretation of 50-penetration refusal | Quality-control |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing SPT N-Values

### 2.1.1 Inherent Soil Factors (These cannot be corrected — they ARE the measured signal)

1. **Soil density / relative density (Dr):** Higher Dr → higher N.
2. **Effective overburden pressure (σ'v):** Higher confining stress → higher N (this is why we apply the overburden correction, but the underlying influence is real).
3. **Soil gradation and grain shape:** Angular, well-graded soils give higher N than rounded, uniform soils at the same Dr.
4. **Fines content:** Increasing fines generally reduces N in sands (but increases apparent N in silts due to partial drainage/suction).
5. **Saturation / pore pressure:** Submerged soils give lower N than dry soils at the same relative density (dilatancy effect).
6. **Stress history and OCR:** Overconsolidated sands give higher N.
7. **Soil fabric and aging:** Cementation and aging increase N with time.

### 2.1.2 Test Equipment and Procedure Factors (These CAN and MUST be corrected)

1. **Hammer efficiency / energy delivered (ER):** Donut hammer ≈ 45%, Safety hammer ≈ 50–60%, Automatic hammer ≈ 70–90%.
2. **Drill rod length:** Short rods lose energy through wave reflections → lower N.
3. **Borehole diameter:** Larger diameter → less confinement → lower N.
4. **Sampler type:** Standard vs. non-standard (with or without liner).
5. **Method of driving the casing vs. mud:** Mud-rotary gives slightly higher N.
6. **Hammer release mechanism:** Free-fall vs. cathead + rope slippage.
7. **Frequency of blows:** Standard rate is 30–40 blows/min; faster can artificially increase N.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "Inherent" soil factors cannot be removed — they constitute the engineering information we seek. "Equipment/Procedure" factors must be mathematically removed to obtain a corrected N that represents the soil alone.

## 2.2 Precautions for Reliable SPT Results

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Quality-Control Checklist (from Bowles 1996, IS 2131, and ASTM D1586):**

- **Borehole stability:** Maintain borehole with casing or drilling mud; never test in loose collapsed material.
- **Clean borehole bottom:** Always clean the bottom of the borehole to the test depth before lowering the sampler. Heaved or fallen material falsely increases N.
- **Use standard split-spoon sampler:** Replace damaged shoes and anvils; do not use worn samplers.
- **Correct hammer drop:** 750 mm free-fall; avoid under-drop or jerking the rope.
- **Proper rope-pulley system:** 2 turns of rope on cathead for donut hammer; check rope condition.
- **Drill rod straightness:** Avoid bent rods; use continuous string of standard AW or BW rods.
- **Drive rate:** 30–40 blows per minute (≈ 1.5 sec per blow).
- **Record penetration for each 75 mm:** 0–150 mm (seating) and 150–450 mm (test).
- **Note water table depth** at the time of each test.
- **Document refusal:** If 50 blows advance < 150 mm, or 100 blows < 300 mm, terminate and record as "refusal."
- **Retrieve and label samples** properly in airtight containers.

## 2.3 The Comprehensive SPT Correction Equation

The generally accepted normalized SPT blow count is given by:

$$N_{1,60,cs} = N_{measured} \cdot C_N \cdot C_R \cdot C_S \cdot C_B \cdot C_E \cdot C_T$$

The intermediate step is the energy- and equipment-corrected value:

$$N_{60} = N_{measured} \cdot C_R \cdot C_S \cdot C_B \cdot C_E \cdot C_T$$

And the overburden-corrected, energy-normalized value:

$$N_{1,60} = N_{60} \cdot C_N$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — All Corrections at a Glance

| Symbol | Factor | Standard Reference Value | Formula / Range | Remarks |
|--------|--------|--------------------------|-----------------|---------|
| **C_N** | Overburden correction | σ'v = 100 kPa (≈ 1 atm) | Liao & Whitman: $C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}}$ | Pa = 100 kPa (atmospheric) |
| **C_N (alt.)** | Overburden correction (Seed) | — | $C_N = 1 - 1.25 \log\left(\dfrac{\sigma'_v}{P_a}\right)$ | Older form |
| **C_R** | Rod length correction | Rod ≥ 10 m | 1.00 | See table below |
| **C_S** | Sampler correction | Standard, no liner | 1.00 | See table below |
| **C_B** | Borehole diameter correction | 65–115 mm | 1.00 | See table below |
| **C_E** | Hammer efficiency correction | ER = 60% | $C_E = \dfrac{ER}{60}$ | Auto hammer ≈ 0.8–1.5 |
| **C_T** | Drill method / additional | Mud rotary or dry | 1.0 (most cases) | Seed (1985) |
| **N1,60,cs** | Final normalized value | — | $N_{1,60,cs} = N_{1,60} + \Delta N_{fines}$ | For liquefaction |

### 2.4.1 Rod Length Correction Factor C_R (Skempton, 1986; Youd et al. 2001)

| Rod Length (m) | C_R |
|:--------------:|:---:|
| < 4 | 0.75 |
| 4 – 6 | 0.85 |
| 6 – 10 | 0.95 |
| > 10 | 1.00 |

### 2.4.2 Sampler Correction Factor C_S

| Sampler Type | C_S |
|:------------:|:---:|
| Standard sampler, no liner | 1.00 |
| Standard sampler, with liner (assumed loose) | 1.20 |
| Non-standard sampler | 1.30+ (unreliable) |

### 2.4.3 Borehole Diameter Correction C_B

| Borehole Diameter (mm) | C_B |
|:----------------------:|:---:|
| 65 – 115 | 1.00 |
| 150 | 1.05 |
| 200 | 1.10 |

## 2.5 Fines Correction (ΔN_fines) for Liquefaction

Following Youd et al. (2001) NCEER/NSF guidelines:

$$\Delta N_{fines} = \begin{cases} 0, & FC \leq 5\% \\ \exp\left(1.63 - \dfrac{9.7}{FC} - \left(\dfrac{15.7}{FC}\right)^2\right), & 5\% < FC < 35\% \\ 5.0, & FC \geq 35\% \end{cases}$$

where **FC** = fines content (percentage passing 75 μm sieve).

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Corrected SPT N-values are the primary input for:
- **Liquefaction potential evaluation** (cyclic resistance ratio, CRR)
- **Bearing capacity of shallow and deep foundations** (for sands: qc ≈ k · N1,60)
- **Estimation of friction angle** of granular soils
- **Estimation of undrained shear strength** of clays
- **Relative density** determination
- **Settlement** calculations
- **Empirical correlations** to CPT qc and other in-situ tests

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overburden Correction Factor C_N

The overburden correction arises because the SPT blow count depends on the effective confining stress at the test depth. A "loose" sand at 20 m depth gives a much higher N than a "loose" sand at 2 m depth — not because it is denser, but because of the higher confining stress.

**Liao & Whitman (1986) Approach** — based on dimensional analysis and the assumption that the SPT N-value is proportional to the square root of effective overburden pressure:

$$N_1 = N \cdot C_N \quad \text{where} \quad C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Reference overburden pressure is taken as the atmospheric pressure:
$$P_a = 100 \text{ kPa} \approx 1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 1 \text{ tsf}$$

**Step 2:** At a given depth, the effective vertical stress is:
$$\sigma'_v = \gamma' \cdot z - u \quad (\text{below water table})$$
$$\sigma'_v = \gamma \cdot z \quad (\text{above water table})$$

**Step 3:** For the blow count to be normalized to a reference overburden Pa, we use the power-law form:
$$\dfrac{N_1}{N} = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}\right)^n$$

**Step 4:** Skempton (1986) and Liao & Whitman recommend n = 0.5, leading to:
$$C_N = \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}\right)^{0.5}$$

**Step 5:** Limiting values (Youd et al. 2001): C_N is capped at 1.7 (i.e., minimum σ'v ≈ 35 kPa) to avoid over-correction for shallow depths.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- Depth z = 5 m, water table at 2 m
- Above WT: γ = 17 kN/m³
- Below WT: γ' = 9 kN/m³

Compute:
$$\sigma'_v = (17)(2) + (9)(5-2) = 34 + 27 = 61 \text{ kPa}$$

$$C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{61}} = \sqrt{1.639} = 1.28$$

If measured N = 18:
$$N_1 = 18 \times 1.28 = 23.0$$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Full SPT Correction

**Problem:** A soil investigation at 6 m depth in a borehole of 100 mm diameter gave an SPT N-value of 25. The water table is at 1.5 m. The soil is a sandy clay (γ = 18 kN/m³ above WT, γ' = 9.5 kN/m³ below WT). The hammer is a donut hammer (ER = 45%), rod length = 6 m, and a standard sampler without liner was used. Find the corrected N1,60 value.

**Step 1 — Compute effective overburden stress:**

$$\sigma'_v = (18)(1.5) + (9.5)(6 - 1.5) = 27 + 42.75 = 69.75 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Overburden correction C_N:**

$$C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{69.75}} = \sqrt{1.434} = 1.198$$

**Step 3 — Equipment correction factors (from tables):**
- C_R (rod 6 m, from table) = 0.95
- C_S (standard sampler, no liner) = 1.00
- C_B (borehole 100 mm) = 1.00
- C_E (donut hammer, 45%) = 45/60 = 0.75

**Step 4 — Energy- and equipment-corrected N60:**

$$N_{60} = 25 \times 0.95 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 0.75 = 17.81$$

**Step 5 — Apply overburden correction:**

$$N_{1,60} = 17.81 \times 1.198 = 21.34$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{N_{1,60} \approx 21}$$

## 3.3 Correlations of N-Value with Engineering Properties

### 3.3.1 Friction Angle (φ) of Granular Soils

**(a) Peck, Hanson & Thornburn (1974):**

$$\phi' = 28^\circ + 15^\circ \cdot D_r$$

where Dr is in decimal (0–1).

**(b) Schmertmann (1975) — based on corrected N:**

$$\phi' = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{N_{1,60}}{12} + 25\right] - 5 \quad (\text{fine sand})$$

$$\phi' = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{N_{1,60}}{25} + 20\right] - 5 \quad (\text{coarse sand})$$

**(c) Hatanaka & Uchida (1996) — direct correlation:**

$$\phi' = \sqrt{15.4 \cdot N_{1,60}} + 20 \quad (\text{degrees})$$

**Worked Example:** N1,60 = 21

$$\phi' = \sqrt{15.4 \times 21} + 20 = \sqrt{323.4} + 20 = 17.98 + 20 = 37.98^\circ \approx 38^\circ$$

### 3.3.2 Relative Density (Dr) of Granular Soils

**(a) Skempton (1986) — most widely used:**

$$D_r^2 = \dfrac{N_{1,60}}{C_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{1,60}}{C_p}}$$

| Soil Type | C_p |
|-----------|-----|
| Fine, normally consolidated sand | 60 |
| Coarse, normally consolidated sand | 90 |
| Overconsolidated sand | 120+ |

**Worked Example:** N1,60 = 21, fine NC sand:

$$D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{21}{60}} = \sqrt{0.35} = 0.592 = 59.2\%$$

### 3.3.3 Undrained Shear Strength (cu) of Cohesive Soils

**Terzaghi & Peck (1967) — classic correlation:**

$$c_u \text{ (kPa)} = 4.5 \cdot N \quad \text{(for insensitive clays)}$$

**Stroud (1974) — improved with plasticity index:**

$$c_u \text{ (kPa)} = K_s \cdot N$$

| Plasticity Index, IP (%) | K_s (kPa per blow) |
|:------------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 15 | 7.4 |
| 30 | 5.5 |
| 50 | 4.4 |
| 80 | 3.0 |

**Worked Example:** IP = 30, N = 12:

$$c_u = 5.5 \times 12 = 66 \text{ kPa}$$

### 3.3.4 Correlation with CPT Cone Resistance (qc)

$$q_c \text{ (MPa)} \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 0.5 \times N_{1,60} \quad (\text{sands})$$

### 3.3.5 Elastic Modulus (Es)

$$E_s \text{ (MPa)} = \beta \cdot (N_{1,60} + 15)$$

For sands with silts: β ≈ 0.4 to 0.5 (D'Appolonia), and for clean sands: β ≈ 0.5 to 0.6.

### 3.3.6 SPT N vs Liquefaction (Cyclic Resistance Ratio, CRR)

The simplified Seed-Idriss equation for the cyclic stress ratio is:

$$CSR = 0.65 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_v}{\sigma'_v} \cdot \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} \cdot r_d$$

where r_d is a depth reduction factor (≈ 1.0 at surface, ≈ 0.5 at 30 m).

CRR is read from the (N1,60)cs curve (Seed et al. 1985, modified by Youd et al. 2001). Liquefaction occurs when **CSR > CRR**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — All Correlations at a Glance

| Engineering Property | Soil Type | Correlation | Source |
|----------------------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Friction angle φ' | Sand | φ' = 28° + 15° · Dr | Peck et al. 1974 |
| Friction angle φ' | Sand (clean) | φ' = √(20·N1,60) + 20° | Hatanaka & Uchida |
| Relative density Dr | NC fine sand | Dr² = N1,60 / 60 | Skempton 1986 |
| Relative density Dr | NC coarse sand | Dr² = N1,60 / 90 | Skempton 1986 |
| Undrained strength cu | Clay | cu (kPa) = 4.5 · N | Terzaghi & Peck |
| Undrained strength cu | Clay (IP-based) | cu = Ks · N | Stroud 1974 |
| Modulus Es | Sand | Es = β · (N1,60 + 15) | Various |
| Unit weight γ | Sand | γ = 15 + 0.4 · N (kN/m³) | Bowles (approx.) |
| CPT qc | Sand | qc ≈ 0.5 · N1,60 (MPa) | Robertson |
| Consistency | Clay | SPT N → very soft to hard | IS 1498 classification |
| Liquefaction | Sand | CRR–(N1,60)cs curve | Youd et al. 2001 |

## 3.5 SPT Consistency Classification for Clays (IS 1498 / Terzaghi-Peck)

| N-value | Consistency | cu (kPa) approximate |
|:-------:|:-----------:|:--------------------:|
| < 2 | Very soft | < 12 |
| 2 – 4 | Soft | 12 – 25 |
| 4 – 8 | Medium stiff | 25 – 50 |
| 8 – 15 | Stiff | 50 – 100 |
| 15 – 30 | Very stiff | 100 – 200 |
| > 30 | Hard | > 200 |
