# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

Define the SPT N-value. List any **four** factors that influence the measured SPT blow count.

**Model Answer:**

The **SPT N-value** is the number of blows of a 63.5 kg hammer falling freely through 750 mm required to drive a standard split-spoon sampler the last **300 mm** of a 450 mm penetration, after an initial 150 mm seating drive (as per IS 2131 / ASTM D1586).

**Four Factors Influencing SPT N-value:**

1. **Hammer efficiency / type** — Donut, safety, or automatic hammers deliver different energy ratios (45%, 50–60%, 70–90% respectively).
2. **Effective overburden pressure (σ'v)** — Higher confining stress at greater depth increases N.
3. **Drill rod length** — Short rods cause higher energy loss; correction factor C_R varies from 0.75 to 1.00.
4. **Borehole diameter** — Larger diameters (150–200 mm) reduce confinement and give lower N.

*(Any four valid factors accepted; e.g., also: sampler type, fines content, water table, operator technique, soil gradation.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

What is the overburden correction factor C_N? State the Liao and Whitman (1986) expression and explain why overburden correction is necessary.

**Model Answer:**

The **overburden correction factor (C_N)** is a multiplier applied to the SPT N-value to normalize it to a standard effective overburden pressure of 100 kPa (≈ 1 atm). It is necessary because the raw SPT blow count depends on the in-situ confining stress, which varies with depth and groundwater conditions. Without correction, the same sand tested at different depths would yield different N-values purely because of depth — not because of a difference in density or strength.

The Liao and Whitman (1986) expression is:

$$C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}}$$

where **P_a = 100 kPa** is the reference atmospheric pressure and **σ'v** is the effective vertical stress at the test depth. The factor is usually capped at **1.7** to avoid over-correction in very shallow deposits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **factors influencing the SPT N-value**, classifying them into equipment, procedural, and soil-state factors. State at least three precautions for obtaining reliable SPT results in the field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Standard Penetration Test was conducted at a depth of 8 m in a 100 mm diameter borehole. The water table is at 3 m below ground level. The soil profile consists of fine sand with γ = 17 kN/m³ above the water table and γ_sat = 20 kN/m³ below. The measured SPT N-value is 30. The test was conducted using a **safety hammer** (ER = 60%), **drill rod length 9 m**, and a **standard split-spoon sampler without liner**. Compute the corrected N1,60 value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Factors Influencing SPT N-Value and Precautions:**

**Equipment Factors:**
- **Hammer type and efficiency** (donut ≈ 45%, safety ≈ 60%, automatic ≈ 80–90%): The energy actually delivered to the rod differs from theoretical energy, affecting the blow count significantly.
- **Drill rod length:** Energy loss due to wave reflection is greater for short rods (< 4 m), giving lower N. Correction factor C_R ranges from 0.75 to 1.0.
- **Sampler type:** Standard (no liner) gives C_S = 1.0; with liner the friction on the inner wall raises N (C_S = 1.2).
- **Borehole diameter:** Diameters 65–115 mm give C_B = 1.0; larger diameters (150, 200 mm) require C_B = 1.05, 1.10.

**Procedural Factors:**
- **Hammer release mechanism** (free-fall vs. cathead-and-rope).
- **Blow rate** (standard 30–40 blows/min; faster rates increase N).
- **Method of borehole support** (casing vs. drilling mud).
- **Cleanliness of borehole bottom** (heaved material falsely increases N).
- **Operator skill** in counting and recording.

**Soil-State Factors (Inherent):**
- **Effective overburden pressure (σ'v):** The dominant factor — deeper, more confined soil gives higher N.
- **Relative density (Dr) and density:** Higher Dr → higher N.
- **Fines content:** Fines generally reduce N in clean sands; in silts, partial drainage may cause apparent increase.
- **Grain shape and gradation:** Angular, well-graded soils give higher N.
- **Stress history and aging:** OCR, cementation, and aging raise N.

**Three Precautions for Reliable Results:**
1. Always clean the bottom of the borehole to test depth before lowering the sampler, and maintain the borehole with mud rotary or casing.
2. Use a properly calibrated hammer system; for donut/safety hammers, ensure 2 turns of rope on the cathead and a free-fall drop of 750 mm.
3. Use straight, continuous drill rods of standard AW or BW type, and replace worn sampler shoes and anvils before each test.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Effective overburden stress at z = 8 m, WT at 3 m:**

$$\sigma'_v = (17)(3) + (20 - 9.81)(8 - 3) = 51 + (10.19)(5) = 51 + 50.95 = 101.95 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Overburden correction factor C_N:**

$$C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{101.95}} = \sqrt{0.9809} = 0.9904 \approx 0.99$$

**Step 3 — Equipment correction factors:**
- C_R (rod = 9 m, from table 6–10 m) = **0.95**
- C_S (standard, no liner) = **1.00**
- C_B (borehole = 100 mm) = **1.00**
- C_E (safety hammer, ER = 60%) = **60/60 = 1.00**

**Step 4 — Energy- and equipment-corrected N60:**

$$N_{60} = 30 \times 0.95 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 \times 1.00 = 28.5$$

**Step 5 — Overburden-corrected N1,60:**

$$N_{1,60} = 28.5 \times 0.99 = 28.22$$

$$\boxed{N_{1,60} \approx 28}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the various **corrections applied to the observed SPT N-value**, with the formulae for each correction. Briefly explain the fines correction used in liquefaction analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SPT conducted at 6 m depth gave a corrected N1,60 = 24 in a clean fine sand (fines content < 5%) and N1,60 = 18 in a silty sand (fines content = 25%). Estimate:
- (i) The relative density Dr of both soils.
- (ii) The friction angle φ' of both soils using Hatanaka & Uchida's correlation.
- (iii) Comment on which soil is more susceptible to liquefaction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Corrections to Observed SPT N-Value:**

The corrected normalized blow count is:

$$N_{1,60,cs} = N_{measured} \cdot C_N \cdot C_R \cdot C_S \cdot C_B \cdot C_E \cdot C_T$$

| Correction | Formula / Value | Purpose |
|------------|-----------------|---------|
| C_N (overburden) | $C_N = \sqrt{P_a / \sigma'_v}$ | Normalize to σ'v = 100 kPa |
| C_R (rod length) | 0.75 – 1.00 | Compensate for short-rod energy loss |
| C_S (sampler) | 1.0 (std no liner) / 1.2 (with liner) | Account for inner-wall friction |
| C_B (borehole) | 1.0 – 1.10 | Adjust for loss of confinement |
| C_E (hammer) | C_E = ER / 60 | Normalize to 60% reference energy |
| C_T (drill method) | ≈ 1.0 | Mud rotary vs. dry |

**Fines Correction (ΔN_fines) for Liquefaction (Youd et al. 2001):**

$$\Delta N_{fines} = \begin{cases} 0, & FC \leq 5\% \\ \exp\left(1.63 - \dfrac{9.7}{FC} - \left(\dfrac{15.7}{FC}\right)^2\right), & 5\% < FC < 35\% \\ 5.0, & FC \geq 35\% \end{cases}$$

The clean-sand equivalent blow count is:

$$N_{1,60,cs} = N_{1,60} + \Delta N_{fines}$$

This correction is applied because the standard CRR–N1,60 curve was developed for **clean sands**; silty sands appear denser than they are, and the correction compensates for this apparent over-strength.

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**(i) Relative Density (Skempton 1986, fine sand C_p = 60):**

Clean fine sand: 
$$D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{24}{60}} = \sqrt{0.4} = 0.632 = 63.2\%$$

Silty sand (FC = 25%): First, find ΔN_fines:
$$\Delta N_{fines} = \exp\left(1.63 - \dfrac{9.7}{25} - \left(\dfrac{15.7}{25}\right)^2\right) = \exp(1.63 - 0.388 - 0.394) = \exp(0.848) = 2.335$$

$$N_{1,60,cs} = 18 + 2.335 = 20.34$$
$$D_r = \sqrt{\dfrac{20.34}{60}} = \sqrt{0.339} = 0.582 = 58.2\%$$

**(ii) Friction Angle (Hatanaka & Uchida 1996):**

$$\phi' = \sqrt{15.4 \cdot N_{1,60}} + 20$$

Clean fine sand: 
$$\phi' = \sqrt{15.4 \times 24} + 20 = \sqrt{369.6} + 20 = 19.22 + 20 = 39.22°$$

Silty sand: 
$$\phi' = \sqrt{15.4 \times 20.34} + 20 = \sqrt{313.2} + 20 = 17.70 + 20 = 37.70°$$

**(iii) Liquefaction Susceptibility:**

The clean fine sand has a higher N1,60 (24 vs. 20.34) and higher φ' (39.2° vs. 37.7°), so it is **denser and more resistant** to liquefaction. The silty sand, despite being more "dilative" in the raw N-value, is **more susceptible to liquefaction** because (a) the clean-sand equivalent is lower, and (b) silty sands have higher pore-pressure buildup potential under cyclic loading.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use γ' below the water table.** Many students incorrectly use γ_sat instead of γ' = γ_sat − γ_w. This causes a major error in σ'v and propagates to all subsequent corrections.
> 2. **Confusing C_R and C_N roles.** C_R is *equipment* correction (rod length); C_N is *soil-state* correction (overburden). They are applied in different order and have different sources.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.7 cap on C_N.** For very shallow tests (σ'v < 35 kPa), C_N is capped at 1.7 to avoid unrealistically high corrected N.
> 4. **Using raw N in correlations.** All φ', Dr, cu correlations require either N (with caveats) or N1,60 (preferred). Always state which N is being used.
> 5. **Sign error in fines correction.** Fines correction is *added* (N1,60,cs = N1,60 + ΔN_fines), not multiplied.
> 6. **Skipping units in answers.** Always write kPa, MPa, or degrees explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam prep):**

- **SPT N-value definition:** Blows for last 300 mm of 450 mm drive; 63.5 kg hammer; 750 mm drop; standard split-spoon.
- **Reference energy:** 60% of theoretical free-fall energy.
- **Equipment factors to correct:** C_E (hammer), C_R (rod), C_S (sampler), C_B (borehole).
- **Soil-state correction:** C_N (overburden) — use Liao & Whitman √(Pa/σ'v), cap at 1.7.
- **Final form:** N1,60 = N_measured × C_E × C_R × C_S × C_B × C_N.
- **For liquefaction:** Add fines correction ΔN_fines to get N1,60,cs.
- **Key correlations to remember:**
   - φ' = 28° + 15° · Dr (Peck et al. 1974)
   - Dr² = N1,60 / 60 for fine NC sand (Skempton 1986)
   - cu (kPa) = 4.5 · N for insensitive clays (Terzaghi & Peck)
   - φ' = √(15.4·N1,60) + 20° (Hatanaka & Uchida 1996)
- **SPT refusal:** 50 blows for < 150 mm penetration.
- **Borehole maintenance:** Mud rotary or casing; clean bottom before each test.
- **Fines correction (ΔN):** 0 for FC ≤ 5%, 5 for FC ≥ 35%, exponential in between.
- **Field visit deliverables:** Bore log, N profile, samples, water table, drilling notes.
- **Always compute γ' = γ_sat − 9.81 kN/m³** below the water table.
- **Report values with N1,60 (not raw N)** when comparing to correlations from modern literature.
- **Liquefaction criterion:** Compare CSR (Seed-Idriss) with CRR (from N1,60,cs chart); liquefaction if CSR > CRR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Cone Penetration Test – Procedure, Merits and Drawbacks, Correlation of Static CPT Results with Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static Cone Penetration Test – Procedure, Merits and Drawbacks, Correlation of Static CPT Results with Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Static Cone Penetration Test (SCPT / CPT)** is an *in-situ* geotechnical investigation method in which a standardized conical tip (apex angle **60°**, base diameter **35.7 mm**, base area **10 cm²**) attached to a series of push rods is hydraulically or mechanically pushed into the ground at a **constant rate of penetration of 20 mm/sec**, and the resistance offered by the soil to the penetration of the cone and the friction acting on a friction sleeve are continuously measured and recorded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The standard penetration rate of **20 mm/s** (≈ 1.2 m/min) is the universally accepted quasi-static rate governed by **IS 4968 (Part 3) – 1976** and **ASTM D5778**. Any deviation from this rate makes the test result non-comparable with empirical correlations.

The test directly yields two fundamental parameters:
- **Cone Tip Resistance ($q_c$)** — the force per unit area required to push the conical tip through the soil.
- **Sleeve Friction ($f_s$)** — the unit frictional resistance developed along the surface of the cylindrical friction sleeve located immediately above the cone tip.

A derivative parameter, the **Friction Ratio ($R_f$)**, is computed as:
$$R_f = \frac{f_s}{q_c} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction:** Unlike the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) which is *dynamic* and measures N-values (blows per 300 mm), the Static CPT is a *quasi-static* continuous test that provides near-continuous soil profile data — making it one of the most reliable and repeatable in-situ tests in modern geotechnical practice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a sharpened, pencil-shaped rod steadily into a tray of soft butter versus a tray of compacted sand, both at the **same slow speed**:

- The **butter** offers very little resistance, and the rod slides in smoothly. The "tip resistance" is low, and the side friction along the rod is also low. This simulates **soft clay**.
- The **sand** resists the rod with high force, and the grains press hard against the side of the rod, creating high friction. This simulates **dense sand**.

Now imagine doing this in a layered dessert (a layer of cake, then custard, then chocolate). As you push the rod down, you *feel* the resistance change continuously — high in the cake, low in the custard. The **Static Cone Penetration Test** works on exactly this principle, but instead of your hand, a **hydraulic ram** pushes the rod, and instead of your feeling, **precision load cells** measure the resistance. The continuous record essentially gives a "fingerprint" of the underground soil profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Static"?** The word *static* emphasizes that there is **no dynamic impact or hammering** involved. The rod is *pushed* (compressed) at a slow, steady rate, as opposed to the SPT where a hammer *strikes* the rod dynamically.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference Code |
|---|---|---|
| Cone apex angle | **60°** | IS 4968 / ASTM D5778 |
| Cone base diameter | **35.7 mm** | IS 4968 / ASTM D5778 |
| Cone base area ($A_c$) | **10 cm²** (1000 mm²) | IS 4968 / ASTM D5778 |
| Sleeve surface area ($A_s$) | **150 cm²** (15,000 mm²) | IS 4968 / ASTM D5778 |
| Standard penetration rate | **20 mm/sec** | IS 4968 / ASTM D5778 |
| Maximum test depth (typical truck) | **30–50 m** | Field practice |
| Tip load capacity (electric) | **100 kN** typical | Manufacturer spec |
| Sleeve load capacity (electric) | **15 kN** typical | Manufacturer spec |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Static Cone Penetrometers

The evolution of CPT equipment has produced three major families. KTU students must know the operational differences because exam questions frequently test this distinction.

#### A. Mechanical Cone (Begemann Friction-Cone Mechanism)
- The simplest, oldest design.
- A **telescopic rod system** inside the push rod allows the cone tip to be pushed ahead of the friction sleeve.
- The cone is advanced by **10 cm** first, and $q_c$ is measured.
- Then the cone and sleeve are pushed together by another **10 cm**, and the combined resistance is measured.
- The sleeve friction is obtained by **subtraction**: $f_s = (Q_{total} - Q_c) / A_s$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the mechanical cone gives $f_s$ by *subtraction*, which compounds measurement errors. This is its biggest drawback.

#### B. Electric Cone (Most Commonly Used)
- **Load cells and strain gauges** are built directly into the cone and sleeve.
- The cone and sleeve are pushed **simultaneously** (no telescopic motion).
- $q_c$ and $f_s$ are measured **independently and continuously** using electrical signals transmitted through a cable inside the push rod.
- Far more accurate and reliable than mechanical cones.

#### C. Piezocone (CPTU)
- An advanced version of the electric cone with an **additional porous filter element** and pressure transducer mounted just behind the cone tip.
- Simultaneously measures **cone tip resistance ($q_c$), sleeve friction ($f_s$), and pore water pressure ($u$)** at three standard positions: $u_1$ (at the tip face), $u_2$ (behind the tip), or $u_3$ (just above the sleeve).
- The measured $q_c$ is corrected to obtain the **total cone resistance ($q_t$)**:
$$q_t = q_c + u_2 \cdot (1 - a_n)$$
where $a_n$ is the **net area ratio** of the cone (typically 0.55–0.85).

### 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure (As per IS 4968 Part 3)

The KTU examiner expects an exhaustive, ordered procedure. Memorize the following 10-step sequence:

1. **Site Preparation & Boring** (if required): A starter borehole of 5–10 cm diameter is often advanced to a depth of about 1.5 m to bypass any hard surface crust or fill. Alternatively, a small pre-bore permits the cone to start cleanly.
2. **Positioning the Penetrometer Truck**: The CPT rig is leveled using built-in inclinometers and leveling jacks. The reaction force (typically 100–200 kN) is provided by ballast weight, dead-weight anchors, or the rig's own mass.
3. **Attaching the Cone**: The cleaned, calibrated cone is attached to the bottom of the first push rod. The cone geometry is verified — apex angle **60°** ± 5°, base diameter **35.7 mm** ± 0.35 mm.
4. **Initializing Data Acquisition**: For electric/piezocone, the data logger is zeroed; the cone is held at the surface and baseline readings of $q_c$, $f_s$, and (for CPTU) $u$ are recorded.
5. **Pushing the Cone**: The hydraulic ram pushes the cone-and-rod string into the ground at a **constant rate of 20 mm/sec** (± 5 mm/sec). Deviations from this rate invalidate empirical correlations.
6. **Continuous Data Recording**: Readings are taken every **20 mm** (or every 10 mm in high-resolution mode) for mechanical cones, and continuously (sampled at ~25 Hz) for electric/piezocones.
7. **Adding Push Rods**: As penetration progresses, additional 1-meter push rods are added. The hydraulic stroke is typically 1 m, requiring a pause of ~30 seconds every meter.
8. **Termination Criteria**: The test is stopped when one of the following occurs: (a) the cone refuses (reaches refusal, usually $q_c > 50$ MPa in dense sand or $q_c > 100$ MPa in gravel), (b) maximum thrust is reached, (c) maximum stroke is exhausted, (d) the rod inclination exceeds 15°.
9. **Withdrawal and Final Readings**: The rods are withdrawn in stages. For CPTU, **dissipation tests** may be performed by halting penetration and recording the decay of pore pressure with time (used to estimate consolidation parameters).
10. **Data Processing**: A continuous profile of $q_c$, $f_s$, $R_f$, and (if applicable) $u$ versus depth is plotted, which forms the basis of soil stratigraphy and parameter correlations.

### 2.3 Merits of the Static CPT

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners love framing these as "Compare CPT with SPT" or "Discuss advantages of CPT" questions. Memorize this list.

- **Continuous Profile:** Provides a near-continuous record of soil stratification (every 20 mm), unlike SPT which samples at 1.5 m intervals.
- **Repeatability and Reproducibility:** Standardized procedure and constant penetration rate make results highly repeatable between operators and sites.
- **Economical:** Faster than drilling and sampling — typically **100–200 m of penetration per day** with a single operator.
- **No Borehole Required (in some cases):** Self-contained pushing action; minimal soil disturbance and no spoil to dispose of.
- **Operator-Independent:** Once calibrated, results are not subjective (unlike SPT, where hammer energy efficiency varies).
- **Multiple Parameters:** $q_c$, $f_s$, and $R_f$ are obtained simultaneously; for CPTU, $u$ is also obtained.
- **Excellent for Soft Clays and Sands:** Particularly effective in normally consolidated clays and loose-to-medium dense sands.
- **Stratigraphic Profiling:** The $q_c$–$f_s$–$R_f$ triple enables soil behavior type classification (Robertson chart).

### 2.4 Drawbacks / Limitations of the Static CPT

- **No Soil Sample Recovered:** Soil is not retrieved, so **no visual identification or laboratory testing** is possible. This is the single most cited limitation.
- **Cannot Penetrate Dense Gravel / Hard Strata:** Cone tip wears rapidly and may refuse prematurely in dense gravel, boulder zones, or cemented layers.
- **Reaction Force Required:** A heavy reaction (typically 100–200 kN) is needed, which requires a heavy rig — unsuitable for sites with weak access roads.
- **Limited Use Above Groundwater for Some Correlations:** Several correlations are valid only for saturated soils.
- **Equipment Cost:** Electric/piezocone equipment is significantly more expensive than SPT equipment.
- **Hole Closure in Soft Clays:** The hole may close behind the rod, but this is a minor issue.
- **Lateral Stress Influences:** Results are sensitive to in-situ horizontal stress and stress history (OCR), which complicates correlations.
- **Calibration Sensitive:** Strain gauge drift, temperature effects, and cone wear require frequent recalibration.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` character is forbidden inside markdown table cells. All absolute-value and conditional notation has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` below.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_c = \dfrac{Q_c}{A_c}$ | Cone tip resistance | kPa or MPa | All CPT |
| 2 | $f_s = \dfrac{F_s}{A_s}$ | Unit sleeve friction | kPa | All CPT |
| 3 | $R_f = \dfrac{f_s}{q_c} \times 100$ | Friction Ratio | % | All CPT |
| 4 | $q_t = q_c + u_2(1 - a_n)$ | Total cone resistance (CPTU) | kPa | Piezocone |
| 5 | $q_E = q_t - \sigma_{v0}$ | Effective cone resistance | kPa | CPTU |
| 6 | $q_{net} = q_t - \sigma_{v0}$ | Net cone resistance | kPa | CPTU |
| 7 | $B_q = \dfrac{u_2 - u_0}{q_t - \sigma_{v0}}$ | Pore pressure parameter | dimensionless | CPTU |
| 8 | $I_c = \sqrt{(3.47 - \log Q_{tn})^2 + (\log F_r + 1.22)^2}$ | Soil Behavior Type Index | dimensionless | Robertson (2010) |
| 9 | $D_r \approx -98 + 66 \log_{10}\dfrac{q_{c1N}}{\sqrt{\sigma'_{v0}}}$ | Relative density from CPT (Jamiolkowski) | Decimal | Sands |
| 10 | $D_r \approx 68 \left[\log\left(\dfrac{q_c}{(\sigma'_{v0})^{0.5}}\right) - 1\right]$ | Relative density (Baldi et al.) | Decimal | Sands |
| 11 | $\phi' \approx \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \log\left(\dfrac{q_c}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)\right]$ | Effective friction angle (Kulhawy & Mayne) | degrees | Sands |
| 12 | $\phi' \approx 17 + 2.5 \cdot \sqrt{q_{c1N}}$ | Friction angle (Robertson & Campanella) | degrees | Sands |
| 13 | $S_u = \dfrac{q_c - \sigma_{v0}}{N_k}$ | Undrained shear strength | kPa | Clays (CPT) |
| 14 | $S_u = \dfrac{q_t - \sigma_{v0}}{N_{kt}}$ | Undrained shear strength (CPTU) | kPa | Clays (CPTU) |
| 15 | $N_{kt} \approx 10$ to 18 (typically 14) | Cone factor for CPTU | dimensionless | Clays |
| 16 | $N_k \approx 15$ to 20 (typically 19) | Cone factor for CPT | dimensionless | Clays |
| 17 | $q_{c1N} = \dfrac{q_c}{P_{a}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{0.5}$ | Normalized cone resistance (Liao-Whitman) | dimensionless | Sands |
| 18 | $OCR \approx 0.25 \cdot \left(\dfrac{q_t - \sigma_{v0}}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{1.25}$ | Overconsolidation ratio (Mayne) | Decimal | Clays |
| 19 | $k \approx \dfrac{0.215 \cdot c_v \cdot \gamma_w}{t_{50} \cdot a^2 \cdot I_r}$ | Hydraulic conductivity (parallel method, approximate) | cm/s | Clays (dissipation) |
| 20 | $\dfrac{q_c}{N} \approx 5$ to 6 (clay), 4 to 5 (silt), 3 to 4 (sand) | Approximate $q_c$–N correlation | MPa | All soils (rough) |

**Where:**
- $Q_c$ = force on cone tip
- $F_s$ = force on friction sleeve
- $A_c$ = cone base area (**10 cm²**)
- $A_s$ = sleeve surface area (**150 cm²**)
- $a_n$ = net area ratio of cone (typically 0.55–0.85, calibrated)
- $\sigma_{v0}$ = total vertical overburden stress
- $\sigma'_{v0}$ = effective vertical overburden stress
- $u_0$ = hydrostatic pore water pressure
- $P_a$ = atmospheric pressure = **100 kPa** (reference)
- $Q_{tn}$ = normalized $Q_t$ (Robertson, 2010)
- $F_r$ = normalized friction ratio = $f_s / (q_t - \sigma_{v0}) \times 100\%$
- $t_{50}$ = time for 50% dissipation of excess pore pressure
- $c_v$ = coefficient of consolidation
- $I_r$ = rigidity index
- $N$ = SPT N-value
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Measured Force to Engineering Parameters

The CPT directly measures raw forces. The conversion to engineering parameters follows a logical four-stage transformation.

#### Stage 1 — From Raw Force to Unit Resistance

The cone tip resistance is the **force required to push the cone tip** divided by the **projected area of the cone base**:
$$q_c = \frac{Q_c}{A_c}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Force (kN) divided by area (m²) yields stress (kPa or MPa). This is essentially a "pressure" of the soil on the cone face.

**Numerical Example:** If $Q_c = 7.5$ kN and the standard cone area $A_c = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.001 \text{ m}^2$:
$$q_c = \frac{7.5}{0.001} = 7{,}500 \text{ kPa} = 7.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Similarly, sleeve friction is the force on the sleeve divided by the sleeve's lateral surface area:
$$f_s = \frac{F_s}{A_s}$$

#### Stage 2 — Friction Ratio Calculation (Step-by-Step)

Suppose at a depth of 8 m in a sandy deposit, $q_c = 8.0$ MPa and $f_s = 80$ kPa. Then:
$$R_f = \frac{f_s}{q_c} \times 100\% = \frac{0.080}{8.0} \times 100\% = 1.0\%$$

**Interpretation:** A low $R_f$ (≈ 0.5–1.5%) is typical of clean sands. A high $R_f$ (≈ 4–8%) is typical of clays. This single ratio is the **first discriminator** of soil type in CPT interpretation.

#### Stage 3 — Derivation of $q_t$ from Piezocone Readings

In a piezocone, the measured $q_c$ is influenced by pore water pressure acting on the cone shoulder (the annular area behind the tip). The correction is:

**Step 1:** Recognize that the load cell "sees" the water pressure acting on the shoulder area:
$$A_{shoulder} = A_c - A_{tip-projection}$$
This is captured by the **net area ratio** $a_n$, defined as:
$$a_n = \frac{A_{shoulder,\text{effective}}}{A_c}$$

**Step 2:** The true total cone resistance is the measured $q_c$ plus a correction:
$$q_t = q_c + u_2 \cdot (1 - a_n)$$

**Why the factor $(1 - a_n)$?** Because part of the shoulder area (the fraction $a_n$) is already balanced by internal stress in the cone, leaving only the unbalanced portion $(1 - a_n)$ that needs correction.

**Numerical Example:** Suppose at 12 m depth, $q_c = 1.5$ MPa, $u_2 = 250$ kPa, $a_n = 0.75$:
$$q_t = 1.500 + 0.250 \cdot (1 - 0.75) = 1.500 + 0.250 \cdot 0.25 = 1.5625 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Stage 4 — Net and Effective Cone Resistance

The "net" cone resistance removes the in-situ overburden effect:
$$q_{net} = q_t - \sigma_{v0}$$
and the "effective" version further removes the hydrostatic pore pressure:
$$q_E = q_t - \sigma_{v0} \quad \text{(same as } q_{net} \text{ in many texts)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some authors define $q_E = q_t - u_0$ to remove only the hydrostatic component. The KTU syllabus treats $q_{net} = q_t - \sigma_{v0}$ as the standard engineering parameter for normalization.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Robertson (2010) Soil Behavior Type Index $I_c$

The **Soil Behavior Type (SBT)** classification has evolved from the 1986 Robertson chart to the 2010 normalized version. The latter is now the **default reference** in modern CPT practice.

**Step 1 — Normalized Cone Resistance (dimensionless):**
$$Q_{tn} = \left(\dfrac{q_t - \sigma_{v0}}{P_a}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{n}$
where $n = 0.381 \cdot I_c + 0.05 \cdot (\sigma'_{v0}/P_a) - 0.15$, and $n \le 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Normalized Friction Ratio (dimensionless):**
$$F_r = \dfrac{f_s}{q_t - \sigma_{v0}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Iterative Computation of $I_c$:** The Soil Behavior Type Index is then:
$$I_c = \sqrt{\left(3.47 - \log_{10} Q_{tn}\right)^2 + \left(\log_{10} F_r + 1.22\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration Logic:** Because $n$ depends on $I_c$, the solution requires iteration. The standard iterative procedure is:
- **Step 4a:** Assume $n = 1.0$ initially.
- **Step 4b:** Compute $Q_{tn}$ with assumed $n$.
- **Step 4c:** Compute $I_c$ using the assumed $Q_{tn}$.
- **Step 4d:** Update $n = 0.381 \cdot I_c + 0.05 \cdot (\sigma'_{v0}/P_a) - 0.15$.
- **Step 4e:** Repeat until $I_c$ converges (typically 3–4 iterations).

**SBT Classification (Robertson, 2010):**

| $I_c$ Range | SBT Zone | Soil Behavior Type |
|---|---|---|
| $I_c < 1.31$ | 1 | Gravelly sand to dense sand |
| $1.31 \le I_c < 2.05$ | 2 | Sands: clean sand to silty sand |
| $2.05 \le I_c < 2.60$ | 3 | Sand mixtures: silty sand to sandy silt |
| $2.60 \le I_c < 2.95$ | 4 | Silt mixtures: clayey silt to silty clay |
| $2.95 \le I_c < 3.60$ | 5 | Clays: silty clay to clay |
| $I_c \ge 3.60$ | 6 | Organic soils: peats |

### 3.3 Correlation: Relative Density of Sands (Jamiolkowski et al., 1985)

**Step 1 — Normalize $q_c$ for overburden:**
$$q_{c1N} = \dfrac{q_c}{P_a} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)^{0.5}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Jamiolkowski correlation:**
$$D_r \approx -98 + 66 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q_{c1N}}{\sqrt{\sigma'_{v0}/P_a}}\right) \text{ [%]}$$

The simpler form, more commonly used in KTU numerical problems, is the **Baldi et al. (1982)** expression:
$$D_r \approx 68 \left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q_c}{\sqrt{\sigma'_{v0}}}\right) - 1\right] \text{ [%]}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
Given: $q_c = 12$ MPa, depth = 6 m, water table at 2 m, soil = medium sand, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ above WT and $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ below WT.

**Step a — Compute total stress:**
$$\sigma_{v0} = 18 \cdot 2 + 20 \cdot 4 = 36 + 80 = 116 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step b — Compute pore pressure:**
$$u_0 = 9.81 \cdot 4 \approx 40 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step c — Compute effective stress:**
$$\sigma'_{v0} = 116 - 40 = 76 \text{ kPa} = 0.076 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step d — Apply Baldi correlation:**
$$D_r = 68 \left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{0.076}}\right) - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \left[\log_{10}(43.55) - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \left[1.6392 - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \times 0.6392 = 43.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** The sand is in a **medium-dense** state.

### 3.4 Correlation: Undrained Shear Strength of Clays

The classical bearing capacity analogy gives:
$$S_u = \dfrac{q_c - \sigma_{v0}}{N_k}$$
where $N_k$ is the **cone factor** (empirically back-calculated from laboratory tests on clay samples). Typical values:

| $N_k$ Value | Source / Reference |
|---|---|
| 15–20 | IS Code / Lunne & Eide (1976) |
| 19 (commonly used) | Skempton (1951) general recommendation |
| 10–18 for $N_{kt}$ (CPTU) | Lunne et al. (1997) |

**Worked Numerical Example:** A normally consolidated clay at 8 m depth has $q_c = 1.0$ MPa. $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³, water table at surface. Take $N_k = 19$.

**Step a:** $\sigma_{v0} = 19 \times 8 = 152$ kPa = 0.152 MPa

**Step b:**
$$S_u = \dfrac{1.000 - 0.152}{19} = \dfrac{0.848}{19} = 0.0446 \text{ MPa} = 44.6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step c (CPTU refinement):** For CPTU with $q_t = 1.05$ MPa, $N_{kt} = 14$:
$$S_u = \dfrac{1.050 - 0.152}{14} = \dfrac{0.898}{14} = 0.0641 \text{ MPa} = 64.1 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The difference between $S_u$ from CPT ($N_k = 19$) and CPTU ($N_{kt} = 14$) is **44.6 vs 64.1 kPa** — a 44% variation! This shows why CPTU is preferred for clays: $N_{kt}$ has **less scatter** than $N_k$.

### 3.5 Correlation: Effective Friction Angle of Sands (Kulhawy & Mayne, 1990)

$$\phi' \approx \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q_c}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)\right]$$

**Numerical Example:** $q_c = 10$ MPa, $\sigma'_{v0} = 100$ kPa = 0.1 MPa:
$$\phi' = \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{10}{0.1}\right)\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}(100)\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot 2.0\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.76\right] = \arctan(0.86)$$
$$= 40.7°$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check:** A dense NC sand typically gives $\phi' \approx 38°$–$42°$. If your correlation gives 25° or 55°, you have made a unit conversion error — re-check whether $q_c$ is in **MPa** and $\sigma'_{v0}$ is in the **same unit**.

### 3.6 Approximate $q_c$ to SPT-N Correlation

A frequently asked KTU question is the approximate conversion between CPT and SPT. Recommended values:

| Soil Type | $q_c / N$ (MPa per blow count) |
|---|---|
| Clay, silty clay | **5 to 6** |
| Silt, sandy silt | **4 to 5** |
| Fine to medium sand | **3 to 4** |
| Coarse sand, gravelly sand | **2 to 3** |

**Worked Example:** A sandy silt has SPT $N = 20$. Estimate $q_c$.
$$q_c \approx 4.5 \times 20 = 90 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 9 \text{ MPa}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Static CPT System

The diagram below maps the **interaction between the major subsystems** of a modern electric/piezocone setup, illustrating the data flow from the in-ground sensors to the engineer's plotted profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Ground Reaction System]
        R1[Ballast / Dead Weight Anchors — Provides Reaction Force 100 to 200 kN]
        R2[Hydraulic Push Rams — Piston Stroke 1 m, Rate 20 mm per second]
        R3[Reaction Frame and Levelling Jacks]
    end
    subgraph B[Push String Assembly]
        P1[Surface Data Logger and Control Unit]
        P2[Cable Through Rods — Signal and Power]
        P3[1 m Push Rods — Threaded Couplings]
        P4[Standard Cone Tip — 60 degree, 35.7 mm]
        P5[Friction Sleeve — Surface Area 150 cm squared]
        P6[Pore Pressure Filter — Porous Plastic or Sintered Bronze]
    end
    subgraph C[Sensor Subsystem]
        S1[Tip Load Cell — Strain Gauge Bridge]
        S2[Sleeve Load Cell — Strain Gauge Bridge]
        S3[Inclination Sensor — Tilt Meter 0 to 15 degree]
        S4[PP Transducer — Pore Pressure u2]
        S5[Temperature Sensor — Drift Compensation]
    end
    subgraph D[Data Acquisition and Output]
        D1[Analog to Digital Converter — 24 bit, 25 Hz Sampling]
        D2[Real Time Plot — qc, fs, Rf vs Depth]
        D3[Storage Module — Raw and Processed Data]
        D4[Post Processing — Robertson 2010 Ic, SBT, Correlations]
    end
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> S1
    P5 --> S2
    P4 --> S6
    S6 --> S4
    P3 --> S3
    P4 --> S5
    S1 --> P2
    S2 --> P2
    S4 --> P2
    S3 --> P2
    S5 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
    P1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5[Engineering Parameters — Dr, phi dash, Su, OCR, k]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of the CPT Procedure (State-Transition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Step 1: Site Reconnaissance and Rig Setup] --> Q2[Step 2: Calibration and Zeroing]
    Q2 --> Q3[Step 3: Pre Bore if Needed to 1.5 m]
    Q3 --> Q4[Step 4: Position Cone at Surface]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 5: Push at 20 mm per second Continuously]
    Q5 --> Q6{Decision: Refusal or Target Depth?}
    Q6 -- Not Reached --> Q7[Step 6: Add 1 m Rod and Resume]
    Q7 --> Q5
    Q6 -- Reached --> Q8[Step 7: Termination and Withdrawal]
    Q8 --> Q9[Step 8: Optional Dissipation Test for CPTU]
    Q9 --> Q10[Step 9: Data Logging and Plotting]
    Q10 --> Q11[Step 10: Apply Correlations and Report]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Mechanical vs. Electric vs. Piezocone

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph X1[Mechanical Cone — Begemann]
        XA1[Inner Rod pushes cone ahead 10 cm] --> XA2[Measure Qc]
        XA2 --> XA3[Outer Rod and sleeve follow 10 cm] --> XA4[Measure Qtotal]
        XA4 --> XA5[Subtract to get Fs]
    end
    subgraph X2[Electric Cone]
        XB1[Single Push — Cone and sleeve together] --> XB2[Load cells give qc and fs directly]
        XB2 --> XB3[Continuous analog signal via cable]
    end
    subgraph X3[Piezocone CPTU]
        XC1[Add porous filter and PP transducer] --> XC2[Measure qc, fs, u2 simultaneously]
        XC2 --> XC3[Apply correction qc to qt]
    end
    X1 --> Y[Output: Soil Profile]
    X2 --> Y
    X3 --> Y
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Static Cone Penetration Test. List any four standard specifications of the cone as per IS 4968 (Part 3).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Static Cone Penetration Test (SCPT) is an in-situ test in which a 60° conical tip with a base area of 10 cm², attached to a series of push rods, is hydraulically pushed into the ground at a constant rate of 20 mm/sec, and the resistance offered by the soil to the cone penetration and the friction acting on a friction sleeve are continuously measured.

Standard Specifications of the Cone (IS 4968 Part 3):
1. **Apex angle of cone tip = 60°** ± 5°
2. **Base diameter of cone = 35.7 mm** ± 0.35 mm
3. **Base area of cone = 10 cm²** (1000 mm²)
4. **Friction sleeve area = 150 cm²** (15,000 mm²)
5. Standard penetration rate = **20 mm/sec** ± 5 mm/sec

*(Any four → 0.5 mark each = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is the Friction Ratio ($R_f$) in CPT? State the typical $R_f$ range for clean sand and for soft clay.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Friction Ratio ($R_f$)** is the ratio of unit sleeve friction to cone tip resistance, expressed as a percentage:
$$R_f = \frac{f_s}{q_c} \times 100\%$$

Typical values:
- **Clean sand:** $R_f \approx 0.5\% - 1.5\%$ (low, because $q_c$ is high in sand)
- **Soft sensitive clay:** $R_f \approx 4\% - 8\%$ or higher (high, because $f_s$ is comparable to $q_c$)

The friction ratio is the **primary discriminator** in the Robertson soil behavior type chart and is used in conjunction with $q_c$ for soil classification.

*(Definition with formula = 1 mark; sand range = 1 mark; clay range = 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every 14-mark question has an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below with complete model solutions.

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the procedure for conducting the Static Cone Penetration Test in the field as per IS 4968 (Part 3). (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the merits and drawbacks of the Static Cone Penetration Test in comparison with the Standard Penetration Test. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Site preparation and rig positioning (1 mark):** The site is cleared, the CPT rig is positioned, leveled using built-in inclinometers and leveling jacks, and the reaction force (100–200 kN) is provided by ballast weight, dead-weight anchors, or the rig's own mass.
2. **Calibration and cone inspection (1 mark):** The cone is inspected for wear and damage; apex angle (60°), base diameter (35.7 mm), and surface finish are verified. Load cells are zeroed using a data logger.
3. **Pre-bore (if required) (0.5 mark):** A starter borehole of 5–10 cm diameter may be advanced to ~1.5 m to bypass hard surface crust.
4. **Initial penetration (0.5 mark):** The cone is positioned at the ground surface; baseline readings are taken.
5. **Continuous pushing (1.5 marks):** The hydraulic ram pushes the cone at a **constant rate of 20 mm/sec**. Readings are taken at intervals (20 mm for mechanical, continuous for electric). Additional 1-m rods are added as penetration progresses.
6. **Termination criteria (1 mark):** The test is terminated on: (a) refusal — $q_c > 50$ MPa or hard stratum; (b) maximum thrust reached; (c) maximum stroke; (d) rod inclination > 15°.
7. **Withdrawal and post-test (0.5 mark):** For CPTU, dissipation tests may be performed. Rods are withdrawn; the cone is cleaned and inspected.
8. **Data processing (1 mark):** A continuous profile of $q_c$, $f_s$, $R_f$ (and $u$ for CPTU) vs depth is plotted. Correlations are applied to estimate $D_r$, $\phi'$, $S_u$, etc.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Merits of CPT over SPT (3.5 marks):**
- **Continuous profile** vs. SPT's discrete 1.5 m intervals.
- **Higher repeatability** due to standardized procedure and constant penetration rate.
- **No soil sample** is better than a *disturbed* SPT sample.
- **Faster** execution (100–200 m/day).
- **Multiple parameters** ($q_c$, $f_s$, $R_f$, $u$) from a single test.
- **Lower operator dependence**; not affected by hammer energy variability.

**Drawbacks of CPT compared to SPT (3.5 marks):**
- **No soil sample** is recovered (cannot visually identify soil).
- **Cannot penetrate dense gravel/boulders** — refuses prematurely.
- **Heavy reaction** required (100–200 kN) — not suitable for sites with poor access.
- **Higher equipment cost** (especially electric/piezocone).
- **Sensitive to calibration** drift; needs frequent recalibration.
- **Limited applicability** in some cemented or residual soils.
- **Lateral stress sensitivity** complicates correlations.

---

**Question B (14 Marks – Alternative): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the difference between a Mechanical Cone, an Electric Cone, and a Piezocone. (7 marks)

**(b)** The Static CPT gives $q_c = 12$ MPa at a depth of 6 m in a sandy deposit. The water table is at 2 m below ground level. Take $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ above water table and $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ below water table. Estimate (i) the relative density $D_r$ of the sand using Baldi et al. correlation, and (ii) the effective friction angle $\phi'$ using the Kulhawy & Mayne correlation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Mechanical Cone (Begemann type) (2.5 marks):**
- Uses telescopic rods.
- The cone is pushed **10 cm ahead** of the sleeve first; $Q_c$ is measured.
- Then cone + sleeve pushed together for another **10 cm**; combined resistance measured.
- $f_s$ is obtained by **subtraction** → error-prone.
- No continuous record; intermittent readings.

**Electric Cone (2.5 marks):**
- Cone and sleeve have **independent load cells** with strain gauges.
- Pushed **simultaneously** (no telescopic action).
- $q_c$ and $f_s$ measured **independently and continuously**.
- Electrical signal transmitted via cable inside the push rod.
- Much more accurate than mechanical; standard in modern practice.

**Piezocone (CPTU) (2 marks):**
- Electric cone + **porous filter element** + pore pressure transducer.
- Adds measurement of **pore water pressure ($u$)** at $u_1$, $u_2$, or $u_3$ location.
- Allows correction of $q_c$ to $q_t$.
- Enables estimation of consolidation parameters via dissipation tests.
- Best suited for clay characterization and OCR estimation.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $q_c = 12$ MPa, depth $z = 6$ m, water table at 2 m
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³ (above WT), $\gamma_{sat} = 20$ kN/m³ (below WT)

**Step 1 — Total vertical stress (0.5 mark):**
$$\sigma_{v0} = 18 \times 2 + 20 \times 4 = 36 + 80 = 116 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Pore water pressure (0.5 mark):**
$$u_0 = 9.81 \times 4 \approx 40 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Effective vertical stress (0.5 mark):**
$$\sigma'_{v0} = 116 - 40 = 76 \text{ kPa} = 0.076 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Relative density using Baldi et al. correlation (3 marks):**
$$D_r = 68 \left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q_c}{\sqrt{\sigma'_{v0}}}\right) - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{0.076}}\right) - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \left[\log_{10}(43.55) - 1\right]$$
$$= 68 \times 0.6392$$
$$\boxed{D_r \approx 43.5\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Medium-dense sand (loose-to-dense threshold is 35%).

**Step 5 — Effective friction angle using Kulhawy & Mayne (2.5 marks):**
$$\phi' = \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{q_c}{\sigma'_{v0}}\right)\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{12}{0.076}\right)\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \cdot \log_{10}(157.89)\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.38 \times 2.198\right]$$
$$= \arctan\left[0.1 + 0.8353\right)$$
$$= \arctan(0.9353)$$
$$\boxed{\phi' \approx 43.1°}$$

**Verification:** $\phi' = 43°$ for medium-dense sand is reasonable (range 38°–42° for medium-dense, up to 45° for dense). ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in CPT Problems (Cumulative Loss up to 5–7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in $\sigma'$:** Converting $q_c$ from MPa to kPa (or vice versa) while leaving $\sigma'_{v0}$ in different units. **Always** keep $q_c$ and $\sigma'_{v0}$ in the **same units** (both MPa or both kPa) before applying any logarithmic correlation.
> 2. **Wrong $N_k$/$N_{kt}$ value:** Hardcoding $N_k = 19$ for clays without acknowledging that for **CPTU**, $N_{kt} = 14$ is more appropriate. Examiner deducts 1 mark if you don't specify which cone you are using.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\sigma_{v0}$ subtraction:** In $S_u = (q_c - \sigma_{v0})/N_k$, students often write $S_u = q_c / N_k$, ignoring the overburden correction. This can give a 20–30% over-estimation of $S_u$ and is a **favourite deduction** by examiners.
> 4. **Confusing Mechanical vs. Electric $f_s$:** In mechanical cone, $f_s$ is obtained by **subtraction** of two measured quantities. Writing it as "directly measured" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating the empirical reference:** In correlations like $D_r = 68[\log(...) - 1]$, students often write "Baldi's formula" without specifying the **Baldi et al. (1982)** reference. KTU expects the citation.
> 6. **Ignoring OCR in clay correlations:** Using the $S_u = (q_c - \sigma_{v0})/N_k$ formula for **overconsolidated clays** without OCR correction gives incorrect results; the Mayne (2001) OCR relation should be used for OC clays.
> 7. **Not converting the answer to conventional units:** Final answers in unconventional units (e.g., MN/m² instead of MPa) lose 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Cone Penetration Test**

- **Definition:** Push a standard 60° cone (10 cm² area) at **20 mm/sec**; record $q_c$ and $f_s$.
- **Cone geometry:** Apex 60°, base 35.7 mm, area 10 cm², sleeve area 150 cm².
- **Penetration rate:** **20 mm/sec** is the **only** standard rate; deviation invalidates correlations.
- **Three types:** Mechanical (subtraction method), Electric (continuous), Piezocone/CPTU (with pore pressure).
- **Three primary outputs:** $q_c$ (MPa), $f_s$ (kPa), $R_f$ (%) = ($f_s / q_c$) × 100.
- **CPTU correction:** $q_t = q_c + u_2(1 - a_n)$ where $a_n \approx 0.55$–$0.85$.
- **Friction Ratio $R_f$:** Low ($< 1.5\%$) → sand; high ($> 4\%$) → clay.
- **Relative density (sand) — Baldi:** $D_r = 68[\log(q_c/\sqrt{\sigma'_{v0}}) - 1]$ %.
- **Relative density (sand) — Jamiolkowski:** Uses $q_{c1N}$ normalized by $P_a$ and $\sigma'_{v0}$.
- **Friction angle (sand) — Kulhawy & Mayne:** $\phi' = \arctan[0.1 + 0.38 \log(q_c/\sigma'_{v0})]$.
- **Shear strength (clay):** $S_u = (q_c - \sigma_{v0})/N_k$, $N_k \approx 19$; CPTU: $N_{kt} \approx 14$.
- **OCR (clay, Mayne):** $OCR \approx 0.25 \cdot [(q_t - \sigma_{v0})/\sigma'_{v0}]^{1.25}$.
- **SBT Index (Robertson 2010):** $I_c = \sqrt{(3.47 - \log Q_{tn})^2 + (\log F_r + 1.22)^2}$.
- **SBT zones:** $I_c < 1.31$ (sand) → $I_c > 3.60$ (organic/peat).
- **Approx. $q_c$–N correlation:** $q_c \approx 5N$ (clay), $4N$ (silt), $3N$ (sand) — units: **MPa**.
- **Maximum depth:** ~30–50 m; refusal at $q_c > 50$ MPa in dense sand/gravel.
- **Biggest advantage:** Continuous stratigraphic profile + repeatability + multiple parameters.
- **Biggest limitation:** **No soil sample** is recovered; cannot penetrate dense gravel.
- **IS Code:** IS 4968 (Part 3) – 1976 governs Indian practice; ASTM D5778 is the international equivalent.
- **Reaction force:** 100–200 kN provided by ballast, dead-weight anchors, or rig mass.
- **Hydraulic conductivity:** Estimated from CPTU dissipation tests using parallel method.
- **Important correlations to memorize for KTU exam:**
  1. Baldi's $D_r$ (most frequently asked)
  2. Kulhawy & Mayne's $\phi'$ (most frequently asked)
  3. Skempton's $S_u$ with $N_k = 19$
  4. Robertson 2010 SBT chart
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Cone Penetration Test – Procedure, Merits and Drawbacks, Critical Comparison of SPT, Static CPT, and Dynamic CPT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Cone Penetration Test – Procedure, Merits and Drawbacks, Critical Comparison of SPT, Static CPT, and Dynamic CPT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT)** is an *in-situ* subsurface investigation technique in which a standardized conical point attached to the lower end of a drill rod is driven into the ground by means of a free-falling hammer of standard mass dropped from a standard height. The resistance of the soil is measured in terms of the number of blows required to achieve a specified penetration (or alternatively, the penetration achieved per blow).

As per **IS 4968 (Part 1) : 1976**, the standard DCPT configuration uses:
- Hammer mass: **8 kg**
- Drop height: **575 mm (0.575 m)**
- Cone apex angle: **60°**
- Cone base diameter: **50 mm**
- Rod: **16 mm dia. connector rods**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DCPT is the third member of the in-situ penetration test family (after SPT and Static CPT). While SPT samples the soil, and Static CPT pushes a cone hydraulically, **DCPT dynamically drives a cone without a borehole**, making it uniquely suited for shallow investigations of granular and cohesive soils up to about **8–10 m** depth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hammering a sharp nail into the ground with a small mallet. Every time you strike it, the nail sinks a little. If the soil is loose sand, the nail sinks easily (fewer blows). If the soil is dense clay or gravel, the nail barely moves (more blows). The **DCPT works on exactly this principle**, but in a scientific, standardized way.

- A **standardized "nail"** (the cone) is hammered into the ground.
- A **standardized mallet** (the 8 kg hammer) is dropped from a fixed height.
- We **count the blows** needed to push the cone a fixed distance.
- The harder the soil resists, the more blows it takes — this gives us an indirect measure of **soil strength and density**.

Think of it as a "soil stethoscope" for civil engineers: instead of cutting the ground open, we listen to how the ground *responds* to a controlled impact, and interpret that response in terms of bearing capacity, relative density, and stiffness.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Standard |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| Hammer mass (M) | **8 kg** | IS 4968 (Part 1) |
| Drop height (h) | **575 mm** | IS 4968 (Part 1) |
| Cone apex angle | **60°** | IS 4968 (Part 1) |
| Cone base diameter | **50 mm** | IS 4968 (Part 1) |
| Standard penetration interval | **100 mm** | Common practice |
| Blow count notation | **DCPI or N_dc** | — |
| Typical depth range | **Up to 8–10 m** | Field practice |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Dynamic Cone Penetration Index (DCPI)** is defined as the penetration in mm per blow, i.e., $\text{DCPI} = \dfrac{\text{Depth of penetration (mm)}}{\text{Number of blows}}$. A *low* DCPI = strong/dense soil; a *high* DCPI = weak/loose soil.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

The DCPT is executed in a sequential, highly standardized manner. The following stepwise breakdown mirrors the exact field procedure expected in KTU practical examinations.

### **Step 1 — Site Preparation & Equipment Calibration**
- Survey the test location, clear vegetation, and level the ground.
- Verify the **hammer mass (8 kg)**, **drop height (575 mm)**, and **cone dimensions** against IS 4968 (Part 1).
- Ensure the anvil, guide rod, and connector rods are vertical and properly engaged.

### **Step 2 — Initial Setup**
- Place the **cone tip** at the ground surface. Some variants use a small starter hole (~50 mm) to seat the cone vertically.
- Attach the **first drill rod** (16 mm dia.) above the cone and tighten securely.
- Mount the **anvil** and **hammer guide assembly** at the top of the rod.

### **Step 3 — Driving the Cone**
- Raise the 8 kg hammer to the standard drop height of **575 mm** using a free-release mechanism.
- Allow the hammer to fall freely and strike the anvil.
- **Count the number of blows** required to achieve each **100 mm** of penetration (or 300 mm in some versions).
- Record the cumulative blows for every penetration interval in the field logbook.

### **Step 4 — Continuous Profiling**
- Add successive rods (typically 1.0 m or 1.5 m lengths) as penetration proceeds.
- Continue driving until the desired depth, refusal (≥ 50 blows for 100 mm), or the maximum rod length is reached.

### **Step 5 — Withdrawal & Data Interpretation**
- Extract the rod and cone by jacking or pulling.
- Plot the **blow count vs. depth** profile (DCPT log).
- Correlate DCPI with SPT-N, relative density, and allowable bearing pressure using empirical charts (e.g., the **Sowers (1961)** or **IS 2131** correlations).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| Standardizing hammer mass & drop height | Ensures the **driving energy** imparted to the cone is constant — this is the *fundamental* requirement for any dynamic test. Without it, results from different sites cannot be compared. |
| Recording blows per 100 mm | Converts the continuous penetration into a **discrete, plottable resistance profile**. |
| Adding rods continuously | Allows the test to reach the required investigation depth (often 5–10 m) for foundation design. |
| Plotting DCPI vs. depth | Produces a **stratigraphic signature** of the soil — soft layers, dense layers, water table effects all appear as changes in slope. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DCPI} = \dfrac{\text{Penetration (mm)}}{\text{Number of blows}}$ | Penetration index | mm/blow | Primary test output |
| 2 | $E = W \cdot h$ | Hammer energy per blow | J (N·m) | W = 8 kg, h = 0.575 m → **E = 45.08 J** |
| 3 | $E = M \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hammer energy (with g) | J | M = mass (kg), g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 4 | $q_d = \dfrac{K \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h}{A \cdot s}$ | Dynamic point resistance | kN/m² | K = energy efficiency factor, A = cone area, s = penetration/blow |
| 5 | $A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Cone base area | m² | d = 50 mm → **A_c = 1.963 × 10⁻³ m²** |
| 6 | $N_{\text{SPT}} \approx (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot N_{dc}$ | Empirical correlation | — | Convert DCPT blow count to equivalent SPT-N |
| 7 | $\phi = 27.1 + 0.30 \cdot N_{dc}$ | Empirical relation | degrees | For granular soils (Sowers-type) |
| 8 | $q_a = f(N_{dc})$ | Allowable bearing pressure | kN/m² | Read from IS/standard charts |

> **Important Numerical Constant (memorize):**
> $$\boxed{E_{\text{DCPT}} = M \cdot g \cdot h = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.575 \approx 45.13 \text{ J per blow}}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DCPT is widely used in:

1. **Highway and railway embankment investigations** — rapid profiling of subgrade strength along alignments.
2. **Rural road design** under PMGSY and other low-volume road programs where budget precludes deep boreholes.
3. **Liquefaction assessment** in preliminary seismic microzonation studies.
4. **Compaction control** — comparing pre- and post-compaction DCPI values to evaluate densification effectiveness.
5. **Shallow foundation design** for residential buildings, where depth rarely exceeds 5 m.
6. **Correlation-based estimation** of SPT-N where borehole access is difficult (e.g., inside existing buildings, on slopes).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hammer Driving Energy

The hammer delivers gravitational potential energy to the cone. This is the *input* to the soil-cone system.

**Step 1 — Identify the form of energy.**

The hammer is raised to height $h$ against gravity. The potential energy stored is converted to kinetic energy on release (ignoring air drag).

**Step 2 — Write the potential energy expression.**

$$E = M \cdot g \cdot h$$

where
- $M$ = mass of hammer (kg)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $h$ = drop height (m)

**Step 3 — Substitute KTU/IS standard values.**

$$E = 8 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 0.575 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.575 \\
&= 78.48 \times 0.575 \\
&= 45.126 \text{ J} \\
&\approx 45.13 \text{ J per blow}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with SPT for perspective.**

For SPT: $E_{\text{SPT}} = 63.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.76 = 473.4$ J.

Therefore the **DCPT hammer delivers only about 9.5% of the SPT hammer energy**, which is why the DCPT is limited to shallower, softer deposits unless very dense conditions exist.

## 3.2 Derivation of Dynamic Point Resistance (Dutch Formula Variant)

The dynamic point resistance relates the driving energy to the cone's penetration and cross-sectional area.

**Step 1 — Energy balance concept.**

The hammer energy $E$ is partially transferred to the cone-soil system. Define an efficiency factor $K$ (typically 0.5–0.75 for DCPT rigs).

**Step 2 — Express the work done by the cone.**

Work done against soil resistance = $q_d \cdot A_c \cdot s$

where
- $q_d$ = dynamic point resistance (kN/m²)
- $A_c$ = cone base area (m²)
- $s$ = penetration per blow (m)

**Step 3 — Equate available energy to work done (with efficiency).**

$$K \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h = q_d \cdot A_c \cdot s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $q_d$.**

$$q_d = \dfrac{K \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h}{A_c \cdot s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical constants.**

With $M = 8$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h = 0.575$ m, $A_c = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m², $K = 0.6$ (typical):

$$q_d = \dfrac{0.6 \times 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.575}{1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times s}$$

$$q_d = \dfrac{27.076}{1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times s} = \dfrac{13{,}794}{s} \text{ kN/m}^2$$

where $s$ is in metres. For example, if $s = 0.025$ m (i.e., 25 mm per blow), then:

$$q_d = \dfrac{13{,}794}{0.025} = 551{,}760 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation logic:** A *small* $s$ (low DCPI, high blow count) → *large* $q_d$ → *stronger/denser* soil. A *large* $s$ (high DCPI) → *small* $q_d$ → *weaker/looser* soil.

## 3.3 Worked Example: DCPT to SPT-N Correlation

**Problem:** A DCPT yields 18 blows for a 100 mm penetration at a depth of 3.0 m. Estimate the equivalent SPT-N value and the angle of internal friction using the Sowers-type correlation.

**Given:**
- $N_{dc} = 18$ blows / 100 mm
- Use $N_{\text{SPT}} \approx 1.75 \cdot N_{dc}$
- $\phi = 27.1 + 0.30 \cdot N_{dc}$ (for granular soils)

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{SPT}} &= 1.75 \times 18 \\
&= 31.5 \approx 32
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= 27.1 + 0.30 \times 18 \\
&= 27.1 + 5.4 \\
&= 32.5^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $N_{\text{SPT}} = 32$ corresponds to a **medium-dense to dense** sand stratum. The shear strength angle $\phi = 32.5°$ is typical of well-graded medium sand. This data is suitable for designing a shallow isolated footing at 1.0 m depth with an allowable bearing pressure of approximately **150–200 kN/m²** as per IS 6403.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SPT vs. Static CPT vs. Dynamic CPT

This is the **core KTU high-yield matrix** for Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize the contrasts.

| Feature / Parameter | **SPT (Standard Penetration Test)** | **Static CPT (Dutch Cone)** | **Dynamic CPT (DCPT)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driving mechanism | Dynamic (63.5 kg hammer, 760 mm drop) | Static hydraulic push at 20 mm/s | Dynamic (8 kg hammer, 575 mm drop) |
| Energy per blow | **473.4 J** | N/A (no hammer) | **45.13 J** |
| Penetration element | Split-spoon sampler (50.8 mm OD, 35 mm ID) | 60° cone, 10 cm² or 15 cm² base area | 60° cone, 50 mm dia. (~19.6 cm²) |
| Borehole required? | **Yes** (must be done inside a borehole) | **No** | **No** (some versions may need starter hole) |
| Soil sample recovered? | **Yes** (disturbed sample) | **No** | **No** |
| Continuous profile? | No (every 1.5 m or at change of stratum) | **Yes** (continuous) | **Yes** (continuous) |
| Parameters measured | N-value (blows/300 mm) | $q_c$ (tip), $f_s$ (sleeve), $u$ (pore pressure in piezocone) | Blow count per 100 mm or DCPI |
| Maximum practical depth | ~30–45 m (with extension rods) | ~30–50 m (limited by reaction force) | ~8–10 m (light hammer) |
| Suitability in gravel/boulders | Poor (refusal common) | **Very poor** (refusal) | Poor (refusal common) |
| Suitability in soft clays | Moderate (low N) | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Suitability in dense sand | Good | **Excellent** (best for sand) | Good |
| Operator dependence | Moderate–High | **Low** (automated) | Moderate–High |
| Equipment cost & complexity | Moderate | **High** (heavy reaction frame) | **Low** (light, portable) |
| Standard reference | **IS 2131 : 1981**, IS 2132 | **IS 4968 (Part 3)**, ASTM D5778 | **IS 4968 (Part 1) : 1976** |
| Output correlation | Direct use in many design codes | Empirical correlations to φ, su, OCR | Correlated to SPT-N via $N_{dc}$ relations |
| Disturbance to soil | High (impact + sampler) | **Negligible** (pushed) | Moderate (impact, no sampler) |
| Pore pressure effects? | Affects N in saturated sands | Measured directly in piezocone | Affects blow count (partially) |
| Typical use in Kerala laterite/gravel | Moderate (refusal common) | Poor (refusal on laterite) | **Good for shallow profiling** |

## 3.5 Critical Engineering Takeaways from the Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **No single test is universal.** The choice between SPT, Static CPT, and DCPT depends on soil type, depth, project budget, and required parameters.
> 2. **For granular (sandy) soils:** Static CPT is *superior* because it provides continuous $q_c$ and $f_s$ data with minimum disturbance.
> 3. **For cohesive (clayey) soils:** Static CPT with pore-pressure measurement (CPTU) is the gold standard; SPT gives disturbed samples for classification.
> 4. **For shallow, economical investigations:** DCPT is the most cost-effective, especially in remote or rugged terrain.
> 5. **For projects needing actual soil samples:** Only SPT provides disturbed samples — the others are *blind* (no sample) tests.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DCPT Equipment Assembly — Mermaid Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of the DCPT equipment chain and the energy flow path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["HAMMER ASSEMBLY 8 kg mass free fall 575 mm drop height"]
    nodeB["ANVIL TOP CONNECTOR distributes impact load to rod"]
    nodeC["GUIDE TUBE ensures vertical hammer drop no friction loss"]
    nodeD["DRILL ROD 16 mm diameter connects cone to surface"]
    nodeE["CONE TIP 60 degree apex 50 mm base diameter"]
    nodeF["SOIL MEDIUM resistance generates blow count"]
    nodeG["DATA LOGGING blows per 100 mm penetration"]
    nodeH["INTERPRETATION DCPI vs depth profile correlate to SPT-N"]
    nodeI["FOUNDATION DESIGN bearing capacity phi estimation"]

    nodeA -->|kinetic energy impact| nodeB
    nodeB -->|axial stress wave| nodeD
    nodeC -.->|ensures alignment of| nodeA
    nodeD -->|drives into soil| nodeE
    nodeE -->|displaces soil| nodeF
    nodeF -->|resistance| nodeE
    nodeE -->|reaction transmitted up| nodeD
    nodeD -->|measured at surface as| nodeG
    nodeG -->|plotted as| nodeH
    nodeH -->|feeds into| nodeI
```

## 4.2 Comparative Test Selection Logic — Mermaid Decision Tree

The following decision tree helps KTU students *justify* the choice of in-situ penetration test for a given project scenario — a typical 14-mark question requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["IN-SITU PENETRATION TEST REQUIRED"] --> q1{"Soil sample needed?"}
    q1 -->|YES| spt["CHOOSE SPT IS 2131 split-spoon sampler disturbed sample recovered"]
    q1 -->|NO| q2{"Soil type predominantly clayey with pore pressure interest?"}
    q2 -->|YES| cptu["CHOOSE PIEZOCONE CPTU measures qc fs and pore pressure u"]
    q2 -->|NO| q3{"Soil type predominantly sandy and need continuous profile?"}
    q3 -->|YES| cpt["CHOOSE STATIC CPT IS 4968 Part 3 continuous qc and fs measurement"]
    q3 -->|NO| q4{"Shallow depth less than 10 m and budget constrained?"}
    q4 -->|YES| dcpt["CHOOSE DCPT IS 4968 Part 1 8 kg hammer 575 mm drop"]
    q4 -->|NO| q5{"Access difficult or reaction frame not feasible?"}
    q5 -->|YES| dcpt2["CHOOSE DCPT lightweight portable no reaction frame needed"]
    q5 -->|NO| spt2["CHOOSE SPT most universally applicable in Indian practice"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Blow Count vs. Depth Profile — Conceptual Block

A typical DCPT profile shows blow count increasing with depth due to overburden stress. Students should be able to interpret the *shape* of this curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layer1["DEPTH 0.0 to 1.0 m FILL loose DCPI 8 to 10 mm per blow"] --> layer2["DEPTH 1.0 to 3.0 m MEDIUM SAND DCPI 4 to 6 mm per blow"]
    layer2 --> layer3["DEPTH 3.0 to 5.0 m DENSE SAND DCPI 2 to 3 mm per blow"]
    layer3 --> layer4["DEPTH 5.0 to 6.0 m STIFF CLAY DCPI 1 to 2 mm per blow"]
    layer4 --> layer5["DEPTH beyond 6.0 m REFUSAL greater than 50 blows per 100 mm test terminated"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the profile:** A *decreasing* DCPI with depth = soil is getting *stronger* (denser/stiffer). A *sudden jump* in DCPI often indicates a *soft* layer (e.g., a clay seam). *Refusal* marks the practical end of investigation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT). State the standard hammer weight, drop height, and cone dimensions as per IS 4968 (Part 1).**

**Model Answer:**

The Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT) is an in-situ field test used to assess the resistance of soil to dynamic penetration by a standard cone driven by a free-falling hammer. The penetration resistance is measured as the number of blows required for a fixed penetration (usually 100 mm) or as the depth of penetration per blow (DCPI).

**As per IS 4968 (Part 1) : 1976:**
- Hammer mass: **8 kg**
- Drop height: **575 mm**
- Cone apex angle: **60°**
- Cone base diameter: **50 mm**
- Connecting rod diameter: **16 mm**

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for *all four* numerical values. A common error is quoting the SPT hammer (63.5 kg) values — *do not confuse* the two.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four advantages and three limitations of the Dynamic Cone Penetration Test.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. Equipment is **simple, light, and portable** — no heavy reaction frame required.
2. **No borehole is needed** in most versions, reducing time and cost.
3. Provides a **continuous profile** of soil resistance with depth.
4. **Economical** for shallow investigations (up to 8–10 m) — ideal for rural roads and small structures.
5. Quick execution — typically a 6 m profile in less than 2 hours.

**Limitations (any 3):**
1. **Limited depth** — practical limit is about 8–10 m due to the light hammer.
2. **Refusal in hard strata** — cannot penetrate dense gravel, boulders, or rock.
3. **No soil sample** is recovered — soil identification relies on correlations.
4. **Operator-dependent** — incorrect drop height or hammer mass invalidates the data.
5. Results are **disturbed** by the dynamic nature of the test, especially in sensitive clays.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the IS code reference** in any comparative question (e.g., IS 4968 Part 1 for DCPT, IS 2131 for SPT) costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating the energy of DCPT (45 J) with that of SPT (473 J)** is a common error — examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
> 3. **In a comparison question, students often list features without a comparative sentence** (e.g., "SPT gives a sample. Static CPT does not."). Always write *which is better in which scenario*.
> 4. **Skipping the units** of blow count, DCPI, or energy in derivations leads to mark deductions.
> 5. **Writing "DCPT is the best test"** is a fatal flaw — no single test is best; the answer must be *context-specific*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Describe in detail the procedure for conducting the Dynamic Cone Penetration Test in the field. What are the standard specifications of the equipment as per IS 4968 (Part 1)? (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the merits and drawbacks of the DCPT. How does it compare with the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) in terms of equipment, energy, depth, and sample recovery? (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The procedure for conducting the DCPT in the field involves the following sequential steps:

**1. Site Preparation:**
The test location is cleared of vegetation, debris, and any loose surface material. The ground is leveled. A sketch of the test point is made relative to a benchmark.

**2. Equipment Inspection:**
Verify the **hammer mass (8 kg)**, **drop height (575 mm)**, **cone dimensions (60° apex, 50 mm base)**, and the **16 mm dia. drill rods**. Ensure the hammer guide tube is vertical and free of friction.

**3. Initial Setup:**
The cone tip is seated at the ground surface. In stiff soils, a small starter hole (≈ 50 mm deep) may be made to seat the cone vertically. The first connecting rod is screwed into the cone's top thread and tightened.

**4. Driving the Cone:**
The 8 kg hammer is raised manually (or with a trip mechanism) to a height of 575 mm and released to free-fall onto the anvil. Each blow corresponds to one impact of standard energy.

**5. Recording Blows:**
The number of blows required to achieve **each 100 mm of penetration** is recorded. The cumulative blow count vs. depth is logged in a field sheet. Some variants record blows for 300 mm penetration intervals.

**6. Adding Extension Rods:**
As the cone penetrates, additional 1.0 m or 1.5 m rods are added at the top to extend the assembly to the required depth. The connection is tightened using pipe wrenches.

**7. Termination Criteria:**
The test is stopped when any one of the following occurs:
- The required design depth is reached.
- **Refusal** is encountered (typically ≥ 50 blows for 100 mm of penetration).
- The maximum rod length is reached (practical limit ~ 8–10 m).

**8. Withdrawal and Data Plotting:**
The rods and cone are withdrawn using a jack or puller. The blow count data is plotted as **blows per 100 mm vs. depth** (or DCPI vs. depth) to obtain the DCPT resistance profile.

**Standard Specifications as per IS 4968 (Part 1):**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Hammer mass | 8 kg |
| Drop height | 575 mm |
| Cone apex angle | 60° |
| Cone base diameter | 50 mm |
| Connecting rod | 16 mm dia. steel rod |
| Penetration recording | 100 mm intervals |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Merits of DCPT:**
1. **Simple and economical** — minimal equipment, no borehole rig required.
2. **Portable** — the entire setup can be carried by 2–3 persons into remote sites.
3. **Continuous profile** — unlike SPT, the DCPT provides an *uninterrupted* record of soil resistance with depth.
4. **Rapid** — a 5–6 m profile can be completed in 1–2 hours.
5. **Suitable for shallow foundations** — ideal for road subgrade and small building projects.

**Drawbacks of DCPT:**
1. **Shallow depth** — limited to 8–10 m due to light hammer energy (45 J per blow).
2. **No soil sample** — only resistance is measured; soil identification is via correlation.
3. **Refusal in hard strata** — cannot penetrate dense gravel, laterite, or rock (a major issue in Kerala lateritic formations).
4. **Operator-dependent** — variability in drop height and hammer release introduces error.
5. **Disturbance** — dynamic loading alters the in-situ stress state, especially in sensitive clays.

**Critical Comparison with SPT:**

| Parameter | **SPT** | **DCPT** | Comparative Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hammer mass | 63.5 kg | 8 kg | SPT hammer is ~8× heavier |
| Drop height | 760 mm | 575 mm | SPT drop is higher |
| Energy per blow | 473.4 J | 45.1 J | SPT delivers ~10× more energy |
| Cone/Sampler | 50.8 mm OD split-spoon | 50 mm solid cone | SPT recovers sample; DCPT does not |
| Borehole needed? | **Yes** | **No** | DCPT is more economical |
| Sample recovery | **Yes** (disturbed) | **No** | SPT is advantageous for classification |
| Continuous profile? | No (at 1.5 m intervals) | **Yes** | DCPT gives finer resolution |
| Maximum depth | 30–45 m | 8–10 m | SPT reaches much deeper |
| Standard | IS 2131 | IS 4968 Part 1 | — |

**Conclusion:** SPT is preferable when **depth exceeds 10 m**, when a **soil sample is needed** for classification, or when **deep foundations** are considered. DCPT is preferable for **shallow, economical, continuous profiling** in remote or budget-constrained sites. The choice is governed by the project's depth, soil type, and information requirements.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) With the help of a neat comparison table, critically compare the Standard Penetration Test (SPT), the Static Cone Penetration Test (Static CPT), and the Dynamic Cone Penetration Test (DCPT) with respect to: (i) principle of operation, (ii) equipment used, (iii) parameters measured, (iv) sample recovery, (v) depth of investigation, and (vi) suitability in different soil types. (7 marks)**

**(b) A DCPT was conducted at a site and the following readings were obtained. The test used an 8 kg hammer with 575 mm drop. Estimate the equivalent SPT-N value and the angle of internal friction. Use the empirical correlations $N_{\text{SPT}} = 1.75 \cdot N_{dc}$ and $\phi = 27.1 + 0.30 \cdot N_{dc}$. (7 marks)**

| Depth (m) | Blows for 100 mm |
|---|---|
| 0.5 | 4 |
| 1.0 | 6 |
| 1.5 | 8 |
| 2.0 | 10 |
| 2.5 | 12 |
| 3.0 | 15 |
| 3.5 | 18 |
| 4.0 | 22 |

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Critical Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | **SPT** | **Static CPT** | **DCPT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Principle** | Dynamic — a hammer drives a sampler into the soil at the bottom of a borehole | Static — a cone is pushed into the ground at a constant rate (20 mm/s) using hydraulic pressure | Dynamic — a light hammer drives a cone into the ground *without* a borehole |
| **(ii) Equipment** | 63.5 kg hammer, 760 mm drop, split-spoon sampler (50.8 mm OD, 35 mm ID), drill rods, borehole rig | Hydraulic pushing machine, reaction frame, 60° cone (10 or 15 cm²), friction sleeve, pore-pressure sensor (optional) | 8 kg hammer, 575 mm drop, 60° cone (50 mm base), 16 mm rods, anvil, guide tube |
| **(iii) Parameters** | N-value (blows per 300 mm) | $q_c$ (tip resistance), $f_s$ (sleeve friction), $R_f = f_s/q_c$, $u$ (in piezocone) | $N_{dc}$ (blows per 100 mm) or DCPI (mm/blow) |
| **(iv) Sample recovery** | **Yes** — disturbed sample for soil classification, moisture content, Atterberg limits | **No** — blind test; soil type inferred from $q_c$, $f_s$, and $R_f$ | **No** — blind test; correlations only |
| **(v) Depth** | 30–45 m (with extension rods and borehole) | 30–50 m (limited by reaction force) | 8–10 m (limited by light hammer energy) |
| **(vi) Soil suitability** | General — works in sand, silt, clay; refusal in gravel/boulders | **Best in clays and sands**; refusal in dense gravel, rock | **Best in soft to medium soils**; refusal in hard strata |

**Critical Observations:**

1. **Static CPT provides the most detailed and continuous data** ($q_c$, $f_s$, $u$), making it the *gold standard* for modern site characterization — but it requires a heavy reaction frame.
2. **SPT remains the most universally used test in India** because it combines resistance measurement with sample recovery, satisfying both geotechnical and geological logging needs.
3. **DCPT is a niche but valuable tool** for shallow, rapid, low-cost profiling, particularly in remote or rugged terrain where borehole rigs cannot operate.
4. For Kerala's lateritic formations, **SPT and DCPT often encounter refusal** on laterite hardpan; **Static CPT is also limited** by reaction force. In such cases, the **Shear Wave Velocity (Vs) test (MASW)** or **Pressuremeter test** may be preferred.
5. The **modern trend** is to use *multiple complementary tests* on the same site to cross-validate results (e.g., SPT + Static CPT in a hybrid program).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $M = 8$ kg, $h = 575$ mm
- $N_{\text{SPT}} = 1.75 \cdot N_{dc}$
- $\phi = 27.1 + 0.30 \cdot N_{dc}$

**Hammer energy:**
$$E = M \cdot g \cdot h = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.575 = 45.13 \text{ J}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{dc}$ at each depth (cumulative blows):**

| Depth (m) | $N_{dc}$ (blows/100 mm) |
|---|---|
| 0.5 | 4 |
| 1.0 | 6 |
| 1.5 | 8 |
| 2.0 | 10 |
| 2.5 | 12 |
| 3.0 | 15 |
| 3.5 | 18 |
| 4.0 | 22 |

**Step 2 — Compute equivalent SPT-N at each depth:**

Using $N_{\text{SPT}} = 1.75 \cdot N_{dc}$:

| Depth (m) | $N_{dc}$ | $N_{\text{SPT}} = 1.75 \cdot N_{dc}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | 4 | 7.0 |
| 1.0 | 6 | 10.5 |
| 1.5 | 8 | 14.0 |
| 2.0 | 10 | 17.5 |
| 2.5 | 12 | 21.0 |
| 3.0 | 15 | 26.3 |
| 3.5 | 18 | 31.5 |
| 4.0 | 22 | 38.5 |

**Step 3 — Compute $\phi$ at the design depth (say, 2.5 m, the typical shallow foundation level):**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{dc} &= 12 \\
N_{\text{SPT}} &= 21.0 \\
\phi &= 27.1 + 0.30 \times 12 \\
&= 27.1 + 3.6 \\
&= 30.7^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- The soil is **medium-dense sand** (N = 21 corresponds to medium-dense classification per IS 6403 / Terzaghi-Peck).
- The **angle of internal friction** $\phi = 30.7°$ is consistent with a medium sand.
- The allowable bearing pressure for a 1.0 m wide strip footing at 1.0 m depth on medium-dense sand (using IS 6403) is approximately **150 kN/m²**.
