- The **DCPT profile shows a steady increase in blow count with depth**, indicating normal consolidation with no weak seams or soft pockets — a favorable result for shallow foundation design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

### **Core Concepts**
- [ ] DCPT is a *dynamic, borehole-less* penetration test for *shallow* investigations.
- [ ] Standards: **IS 4968 (Part 1) : 1976**.
- [ ] Hammer: **8 kg, 575 mm drop** (NOT 63.5 kg / 760 mm — that is SPT).
- [ ] Cone: **60° apex, 50 mm base** (smaller than the typical 10 cm² / 15 cm² Static CPT cone).
- [ ] Energy per blow: **≈ 45.13 J** (about 1/10 of SPT energy).
- [ ] Practical depth: **8–10 m**.

### **Procedure (Memorize the 6 Steps)**
- [ ] Site prep → Equipment check → Setup → Drive & record → Add rods → Terminate & plot.
- [ ] Termination: **Refusal** (≥ 50 blows / 100 mm) or design depth reached.

### **Merits (4 must-know)**
- [ ] Simple, portable, economical.
- [ ] No borehole needed.
- [ ] Continuous profile.
- [ ] Rapid execution.

### **Drawbacks (3 must-know)**
- [ ] Shallow depth only.
- [ ] No soil sample.
- [ ] Refusal in hard strata (laterite, gravel, rock — common in Kerala!).

### **Formulas (Memorize with units)**
- [ ] $E = M \cdot g \cdot h = 45.13$ J.
- [ ] $\text{DCPI} = s / n$ (mm/blow).
- [ ] $A_c = (\pi/4) d^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m².
- [ ] $N_{\text{SPT}} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot N_{dc}$.
- [ ] $\phi = 27.1 + 0.30 \cdot N_{dc}$ (granular soils).
- [ ] $q_d = \dfrac{K \cdot M \cdot g \cdot h}{A_c \cdot s}$ (dynamic point resistance).

### **SPT vs. Static CPT vs. DCPT (Master the matrix)**
- [ ] **SPT** = sample + blow count + borehole + deep.
- [ ] **Static CPT** = continuous $q_c, f_s$ + no sample + heavy rig.
- [ ] **DCPT** = light, portable, shallow, no sample, no borehole.
- [ ] No single test is "best" — the choice is *context-driven*.

### **High-Yield Traps to Avoid**
- [ ] ❌ Don't confuse DCPT hammer (8 kg) with SPT hammer (63.5 kg).
- [ ] ❌ Don't say "DCPT gives a soil sample" — only SPT does.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget to mention the IS code.
- [ ] ❌ Don't write "DCPT is the best" — write "DCPT is preferred *when* [condition]."
- [ ] ❌ Don't skip units in derivation (J, m, mm, kN/m²).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate Load Test – Procedure, Uses, Limitations, Design of Foundation from Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plate Load Test – Procedure, Uses, Limitations, Design of Foundation from Data Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (As per KTU 2024 Syllabus) The **Plate Load Test (PLT)**, classified under *in-situ* bearing capacity tests as per **IS 1888:1982 (Reaffirmed 2020)** and **ASTM D1194**, is a semi-empirical field test conducted at the proposed foundation level to evaluate the **ultimate bearing capacity** and the **settlement / load-deformation behaviour** of the soil by applying a known incremental load through a rigid steel plate and recording the corresponding settlement. The test essentially produces a **Pressure (Load Intensity) versus Settlement (Settlement Curve)**, from which the safe bearing capacity and the modulus of subgrade reaction are determined directly without resorting to extensive laboratory testing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET753, Module 2):** The plate load test is grouped under the broader theme of *"In-situ Testing for Bearing Capacity and Settlement Prediction."* Students are expected to know the procedure, the design interpretations (Terzaghi's and IS code extrapolation), and the well-documented limitations of the test. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are testing the "stiffness" of a mattress before buying it. You place a small wooden disc (the *plate*) on the mattress and press it down with known weights, observing how much the disc sinks. By understanding how the small disc sinks, you predict how a heavier person (the *actual footing*) will sink when sleeping on it. In geotechnical terms: - The **wooden disc** = the *Plate Load Test plate* (standard size of **30 cm × 30 cm** or **60 cm × 60 cm** square, or a circular plate of equivalent area). - The **mattress** = the *in-situ soil* (truly representative of field conditions, unlike a disturbed sample). - The **sinking amount** = the *settlement* (recorded using sensitive dial gauges of **0.01 mm** least count). - The **press weights** = the *hydraulic jack load* (transferred from a kentledge/anchor truss reaction system). The fundamental assumption is that the **stress bulb** beneath the plate mimics a portion of the stress bulb beneath the actual footing, and we **extrapolate** the small-scale results to the full-size foundation using **scale-effect relationships**. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Reference | |---|---|---| | Standard plate size (square) | **30 cm × 30 cm** (most common), 45 cm, 60 cm, 75 cm | IS 1888:1982 | | Equivalent circular plate diameter | **30 cm**, 45 cm, 60 cm | IS 1888:1982 | | Plate thickness | **25 mm (minimum)** rigid steel plate | IS 1888:1982 | | Load increment | **1/5th of estimated ultimate load** per stage | IS 1888:1982 | | Settlement dial gauge LC | **0.01 mm** | IS 1888:1982 | | Test pit depth | **Equal to actual foundation depth** | IS 1888:1982 | | Test pit width | **≥ 4 to 5 times plate width** | IS 1888:1982 | | Poisson's ratio (μ) assumed | **0.3 to 0.5** (clay), **0.25 to 0.35** (sand) | Terzaghi & Peck | > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (Board favourite):** *"Plate Load Test is a field test carried out to determine the ultimate bearing capacity of the soil and the probable settlement of the proposed foundation by loading a plate of standard size and observing its settlement."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic – The "Why" Behind the "How" The test rests on three engineering pillars: 1. **Direct measurement** of soil response (settlement) at the actual foundation depth, avoiding sample disturbance. 2. **Load-Settlement curve interpretation** using the *Biot's* and *Terzaghi's* extrapolation rules for prototype footing design. 3. **Scale-effect correction**, because the stress influence zone under a small plate is shallower than under a real footing. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Test Procedure (IS 1888:1982) ### Stage A: Site Preparation 1. Excavate a test pit of size **at least 5 times the plate width** in plan and up to the **proposed foundation depth** (D_f). 2. Carefully level the bottom of the pit. Place a thin layer of **plaster of Paris / sand cushion** (≤ 5 mm) for proper seating of the plate. 3. Set up the reaction frame: either a **kentledge platform** (loaded with heavy weights like sandbags, concrete blocks) or a **ground anchor / truss system** with reaction beams. ### Stage B: Instrumentation & Loading 4. Position the **rigid steel plate** centrally. Place a **hydraulic jack** of adequate capacity (typically 100–500 kN) between the plate and the reaction system. 5. Install **at least 2 (preferably 4) dial gauges** on a reference beam (independent of the setup) to measure plate settlement. Gauge stems should rest on small lugs welded to the plate. 6. Apply a **seating load** of about **7 kPa** to remove bedding errors and reset dial gauges to zero. ### Stage C: Loading & Recording 7. Apply load in **increments of 1/5th of the expected ultimate load** (or as per IS 1888: about 1/10th to 1/15th of estimated safe load). 8. Maintain each load increment until the rate of settlement becomes **less than 0.02 mm/minute** (or per IS 1888) — this defines the *stabilized settlement* for that load step. 9. Record cumulative settlement at each stage. Continue until **shear failure or yielding** of soil is observed (a clear "break" in the load-settlement curve), or up to a target settlement (often 25 mm or 1 inch = 25.4 mm). ### Stage D: Unloading 10. Unload in **equal decrements**, recording rebound settlement for each stage (helps estimate the elastic component). ## 2.3 The Load-Settlement Curve (P-S Curve) The most critical output is the **Pressure vs. Settlement Curve** plotted from test data. | Zone | Behaviour | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **Zone I (Initial Straight Portion)** | Linear elastic | Soil behaves elastically; useful for computing $k_s$ and $E_s$. | | **Zone II (Curved Portion)** | Non-linear, plastic strains develop | Local shear failure initiated. | | **Zone III (Steep / Failure Branch)** | Settlement increases rapidly with little load | General shear failure reached. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Point:** For dense sand or stiff clay, the failure point (P_ult) is read at the *point where the curve becomes vertical or near-vertical*. For loose sand or soft clay where no clear break appears, **Terzaghi & Peck (1948)** recommend reading P_ult at a settlement equal to **1/5th to 1/10th of the plate width**, or the point where the initial straight line intersects the rebound-corrected curve (the **Biot's method** or **double-tangent method**). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k_s = \dfrac{q}{s} = \dfrac{P}{A \cdot s}$ | **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction** from initial linear portion of P-S curve | kN/m/m² or kN/m³ | | 2 | $E_s = \dfrac{q \cdot B}{s} \cdot I \cdot (1 - \mu^2)$ | **Equivalent Modulus of Elasticity** using Boussinesq's elastic solution with Influence Factor I (or $I_w$) | kN/m² or kPa | | 3 | $S_f = S_p \cdot \dfrac{B_f}{B_p}$ | **Settlement of footing (granular soil)** by Terzaghi's linear scaling | mm | | 4 | $S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^2$ | **Settlement of footing (cohesive soil)** by Terzaghi's parabolic scaling | mm | | 5 | $q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)}$ | **Bearing pressure** (clay — undrained) carried over directly | kPa | | 6 | $q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)} \cdot \dfrac{B_p}{B_f}$ | **Bearing pressure** (sand — drained) reduced by width ratio (Terzaghi) | kPa | | 7 | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_{ult}}{FOS}$ | **Safe Bearing Pressure** | FOS = **3 (general)** as per IS 1904 | | 8 | $q_{safe} = \dfrac{q_s \;\text{(at 25 mm)}}{FOS}$ | **Safe Bearing Pressure** from settlement criterion (S = 25 mm) | FOS = 2 to 3 | | 9 | $I_w = 0.82 \;(square), \;0.88\;(rect.),\;1.0\;(circle)$ | **Influence Factor (Shape)** for flexible plate (Biot) | Dimensionless | | 10 | $k_{s1} = k_{s2} \cdot \dfrac{B_2}{B_1}$ | **Modulus correction** for plate size (Bowles) | kN/m³ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The symbol $k_s$ (modulus of subgrade reaction) is **NOT** a soil property — it depends on the **plate/footing width**. Always report $k_s$ with the corresponding plate size, e.g., $k_{s(30)} = 12000 \; kN/m^3$. For prototype footing use $k_{s(f)} = k_{s(p)} \cdot B_p / B_f$ (Bowles correction). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Direct design input** for **shallow foundations** of buildings, bridge piers, and **raft foundations** where settlement governs. - **Calibration tool** for correlating in-situ test results (like SPT N-values) with bearing capacity. - **Back-analysis** of footing performance and forensic investigation of failed foundations. - **Verification** of laboratory soil parameters against actual field behaviour — particularly useful for **expansive soils**, **loose cohesionless fills**, and **weathered rock** profiles. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulus of Subgrade Reaction ($k_s$) The modulus of subgrade reaction represents the **pressure required to produce a unit settlement**. By definition, for the initial elastic (linear) portion of the P-S curve: $$ k_s = \frac{q}{s} = \frac{P/A}{s} $$ Where: - $P$ = applied load on the plate - $A$ = area of the plate - $s$ = settlement recorded - $q$ = applied pressure This ratio is a constant in the linear elastic zone. From the test data, take **two arbitrary points** $(s_1, q_1)$ and $(s_2, q_2)$ on the straight portion: $$ k_s = \frac{q_2 - q_1}{s_2 - s_1} \quad \left[ \frac{kN}{m^2} \Big/ m = \frac{kN}{m^3} \right] $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Modulus of Elasticity ($E_s$) Boussinesq's solution for the elastic settlement of a *flexible* loaded area on a semi-infinite, isotropic, elastic half-space gives: $$ s = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}{E_s} \cdot I_w $$ Rearranging to solve for the **equivalent modulus of elasticity** $E_s$: $$ E_s = \frac{q \cdot B \cdot I_w \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}{s} $$ **Step-by-step numerical evaluation (Illustrative Example):** > Given: Plate size $B_p = 0.30\;m$ (square), recorded settlement $s = 5\;mm = 0.005\;m$ at $q = 200\;kPa$, $\mu = 0.30$ (silty sand), $I_w = 0.82$ (square plate). $$ E_s = \frac{200 \times 0.30 \times 0.82 \times (1 - 0.30^2)}{0.005} $$ $$ = \frac{200 \times 0.30 \times 0.82 \times (1 - 0.09)}{0.005} $$ $$ = \frac{200 \times 0.30 \times 0.82 \times 0.91}{0.005} $$ $$ = \frac{44.772}{0.005} = 8954.4 \; kPa \approx 8.95 \; MPa $$ This $E_s$ is then used with the **footing width** $B_f$ to predict the **actual foundation settlement**: $$ S_f = \frac{q_f \cdot B_f \cdot I_{wf} \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}{E_s} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Scale Correction for Granular Soils (Terzaghi's Linear Law) Terzaghi assumed that in **cohesionless (sandy) soils**, the settlement is approximately **proportional to the footing width** because the failure zone depth scales with $B$. Therefore: $$ \frac{S_f}{B_f} = \frac{S_p}{B_p} $$ $$ \boxed{S_f = S_p \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_p}} $$ **Worked Example:** > Plate test data: $B_p = 0.30\;m$, $S_p = 10\;mm$ at $q_p = 200\;kPa$. Find settlement of a square footing of $B_f = 1.5\;m$ at the same pressure intensity. $$ S_f = 10 \cdot \frac{1.5}{0.30} = 10 \times 5 = 50 \; mm $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scale Correction for Cohesive Soils (Parabolic Law) In **cohesive (clay) soils**, Terzaghi suggested the settlement scales with the **square of the width** because the consolidation-driven settlement depends on the stress influence volume: $$ \frac{S_f}{B_f^2} = \frac{S_p}{B_p^2} $$ $$ \boxed{S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^2} $$ **Worked Example:** > $B_p = 0.30\;m$, $S_p = 8\;mm$ at $q_p = 150\;kPa$. Square footing $B_f = 1.2\;m$, same intensity. $$ S_f = 8 \cdot \left(\frac{1.2}{0.30}\right)^2 = 8 \cdot (4)^2 = 8 \times 16 = 128 \; mm $$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Bearing Capacity Translation (Drained, Sand) For granular soils, **Terzaghi** established (verified by model tests) that the **ultimate bearing pressure decreases as footing width increases** in the same deposit, because failure surfaces become relatively shallower in wider footings: $$ q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)} \cdot \frac{B_p}{B_f} $$ > **Why the inverse relationship?** Wider footings mobilize a larger, more distributed failure mass. The peak unit pressure per unit area reduces. The **30 cm plate** experiences a much higher unit bearing pressure at failure than a **3 m footing** in the same sand. **For undrained clay (φ = 0 condition)**, the bearing capacity is **independent of width** (Terzaghi's $q_{ult} = 5.7 c_u$ for strip, and $5.14 c_u$ to $6.0 c_u$ for square/circular): $$ q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)} \quad (\text{clay, undrained}) $$ ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading Safe Bearing Capacity from PLT | Condition Observed in Test | Soil Type | Method to Find $q_{safe}$ | FOS | |---|---|---|---| | **Distinct failure** in P-S curve | Any | $q_{safe} = q_{ult} / 3$ | 3 | | **No clear failure** (P-S curve monotonic) | Sand / Soft clay | **Biot's method** — extrapolate tangent of initial straight line till it cuts the curve; the corresponding pressure is $q_{ult}$ | 3 | | Settlement-controlled design | All | $q_{safe} = q$ at $S = 25\;mm$ divided by 2 to 3 | 2 to 3 | | Differential settlement concern | All | **Van der Veen's method** — hyperbolic extrapolation | 2 to 3 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students blindly use $q_{safe} = q_{ult}/3$ even when the curve shows no clear failure. For PLT in **loose sand**, a failure may not be reached within 25 mm settlement. Always state the method adopted (Biot / settlement criterion / Van der Veen) and justify it. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Plate Load Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Step 0: Site Reconnaissance & Soil Profile Study"] --> A1["Step 1: Excavate Test Pit
Depth = Foundation Depth
Width >= 5B_p"] A1 --> A2["Step 2: Level Pit Floor &
Apply Plaster of Paris Bedding"] A2 --> A3["Step 3: Set up Reaction Frame
Kentledge or Anchor Truss"] A3 --> A4["Step 4: Position Rigid Plate 30cm
Place Hydraulic Jack above Plate"] A4 --> A5["Step 5: Install Dial Gauges
on Independent Reference Beam"] A5 --> A6["Step 6: Apply Seating Load
~7 kPa; Reset Dial Gauges to Zero"] A6 --> A7["Step 7: Apply Load in Increments
1/5 of Expected Ultimate Load"] A7 --> A8["Step 8: Record Settlement at
Each Stage until Stabilization"] A8 --> A9{"Settlement >= 25 mm
OR
Failure Reached?"} A9 -- No --> A7 A9 -- Yes --> A10["Step 9: Unload in Stages
Record Rebound"] A10 --> A11["Step 10: Plot Pressure vs
Settlement Curve"] A11 --> A12["Step 11: Interpret q_ult and
k_s; Extrapolate to Actual Footing"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Test Setup Cross-Section (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Load_Reaction["Load Reaction System (Top)"] K["Kentledge Platform
Concrete Blocks / Sandbags
Total Reaction >= 2x Test Load"] end subgraph Load_Transfer["Load Transfer Assembly"] B1["Reaction Beam / Truss Girder"] J["Hydraulic Jack
Capacity 100 to 500 kN"] P["Pressure Gauge / Load Cell
Calibrated"] end subgraph Test_Assembly["Test Assembly (Bottom)"] SP["Rigid Steel Plate
B_p = 30 cm, 25 mm thick"] DG["Dial Gauges x 4
LC 0.01 mm
on Reference Beam"] end subgraph Subsoil["In-situ Soil"] S["Test Pit Soil
Depth = D_f
Width >= 5B_p"] end K --> B1 B1 --> J J --> P P --> SP SP --> S DG -.measures.-> SP ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Interpretation Logic for P-S Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Recorded P-S Data Points"] --> B["Plot Pressure vs
Settlement Curve"] B --> C{"Is There a Clear
Failure Break?"} C -- Yes --> D["q_ult = Pressure at
Vertical Tangent Point"] C -- No --> E["Apply Biots Method
Draw Initial Tangent
Extrapolate to Cut Curve"] E --> F["q_ult = Pressure at
Intersection Point"] D --> G["Compute q_safe = q_ult / FOS"] F --> G G --> H["Apply Scale Correction
S_f = S_p x B_f / B_p for sand
S_f = S_p x B_f² / B_p² for clay"] H --> I["Final Design Recommendation
for Actual Footing"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note for Students:** The Mermaid blocks above abstract the physical 3D test setup. In the actual field, the **kentledge** sits *above* the plate, and the load travels *downward* through the jack onto the plate into the soil. The dial gauges measure the *vertical* settlement of the plate relative to a far-field reference beam (often a steel I-section resting on supports 2-3 m away from the plate edges). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / PECET753 Model Paper]** > **List any six limitations of the Plate Load Test.** **Model Answer (6 points, in brief):** 1. The test results represent the **soil response in the upper 2B to 4B** depth only — beyond this, properties are unknown. 2. **Scale effect:** A 30 cm plate cannot simulate a 2–3 m footing accurately; extrapolation is empirical. 3. The test **does not reflect long-term consolidation settlement** in clay (it is essentially undrained / short-term). 4. The reaction system requires a **massive kentledge** (often 2× to 3× the test load), making it expensive. 5. The test does **not capture the effects of submergence** of the water table on bearing capacity. 6. The test is **time-consuming** (3–5 days minimum including setup, loading, and rebound reading). --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / PECET753]** > **What is the modulus of subgrade reaction? How is it determined from the Plate Load Test?** **Model Answer:** The **modulus of subgrade reaction ($k_s$)** is defined as the **ratio of the applied pressure (q) to the corresponding settlement (s)** on the foundation soil. It is a measure of the **stiffness of the supporting soil** per unit area, expressed in **kN/m³** (or kPa/m). $$ k_s = \frac{q}{s} = \frac{P / A_p}{s} $$ **Determination from PLT:** - Plot the **Pressure (q) vs Settlement (s) curve** from the test data. - Identify the **initial straight-line (linear elastic) portion** of the curve. - Take the **slope** of this linear segment: $k_s = \Delta q / \Delta s$. - Standard plate size must be **reported** alongside (e.g., $k_{s(30)} = 15000$ kN/m³), and a **width correction** applied for actual footing using **Bowles' relation**: $k_{s(f)} = k_{s(p)} \cdot B_p / B_f$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / PECET753]** > **(a)** Describe the **procedure of conducting a Plate Load Test** in detail as per **IS 1888:1982**, with a neat schematic of the test setup. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A Plate Load Test was conducted using a **30 cm × 30 cm square plate** on a sandy soil. The failure was observed at a **load of 4050 kgf** at a settlement of **25 mm**. Design a **square footing of size 1.5 m × 1.5 m** to carry a **load of 800 kN** at the same site. Use **FOS = 3**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Q A (a): Procedure 1. **Pit Preparation:** Excavate a test pit of width at least 5B_p (≥ 1.5 m) and depth equal to the **proposed foundation depth**. Carefully level the bottom and apply a thin layer of plaster of Paris (≤ 5 mm). 2. **Plate Placement:** A 25 mm thick rigid steel plate of size 30 cm × 30 cm is placed centrally on the bedding. A hydraulic jack is positioned over the plate, and a reaction system (kentledge or anchor truss) is set up to take the reaction. 3. **Instrumentation:** At least two dial gauges (LC 0.01 mm) are mounted on an independent reference beam and connected to lugs on the plate. 4. **Seating Load:** A small seating load (~7 kPa) is applied to ensure proper contact and dial gauges are set to zero. 5. **Loading Stages:** Load is applied in **increments of 1/5th of estimated ultimate load**. Each increment is maintained until settlement stabilizes (rate < 0.02 mm/min). 6. **Recording:** Settlement readings are taken at each stage. Loading is continued until **failure** or settlement exceeds 25 mm. 7. **Unloading:** Unload in stages; record rebound for elastic modulus estimation. 8. **Output:** Plot Pressure (q) vs Settlement (s) curve; determine $q_{ult}$, $k_s$, and $E_s$. #### Model Solution — Q A (b): Design of Footing **Step 1 — Convert failure load to pressure:** $$ P_{ult(p)} = 4050 \; kgf = 4050 \times 9.81 = 39730.5 \; N \approx 39.73 \; kN $$ $$ A_p = 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.09 \; m^2 $$ $$ q_{ult(p)} = \frac{39.73}{0.09} = 441.5 \; kPa $$ **Step 2 — Translate to footing-scale (sandy soil — Terzaghi's correction):** $$ q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)} \cdot \frac{B_p}{B_f} = 441.5 \times \frac{0.30}{1.50} = 441.5 \times 0.20 = 88.3 \; kPa $$ **Step 3 — Apply Factor of Safety:** $$ q_{safe} = \frac{q_{ult(f)}}{FOS} = \frac{88.3}{3} = 29.43 \; kPa $$ **Step 4 — Check footing capacity against applied load:** $$ A_{req} = \frac{P}{q_{safe}} = \frac{800}{29.43} = 27.18 \; m^2 $$ For a square footing: $$ B_{req} = \sqrt{27.18} = 5.21 \; m $$ **Step 5 — Settlement prediction for 1.5 m × 1.5 m footing:** $$ S_f = S_p \cdot \frac{B_f}{B_p} = 25 \times \frac{1.50}{0.30} = 25 \times 5 = 125 \; mm $$ **Result:** A footing of size **1.5 m × 1.5 m is grossly inadequate** for an 800 kN load in this soil. Provide a footing of size approximately **5.25 m × 5.25 m** to satisfy bearing, and check settlement (which will be substantially larger than 125 mm — a **raft or pile foundation** should be considered). --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / PECET753]** > **(a)** Discuss the **various uses and limitations** of the Plate Load Test as a site investigation tool. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** The following data was obtained from a Plate Load Test on a **45 cm diameter circular plate** in a clayey soil: > > | Load (kN) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| > | Settlement (mm) | 2.0 | 4.0 | 6.5 | 9.5 | 14.0 | 22.0 | 38.0 | > > Determine (i) the **modulus of subgrade reaction**, (ii) the **equivalent modulus of elasticity** (use $\mu = 0.4$, $I_w = 1.0$), and (iii) predict the **settlement of a 2.0 m × 2.0 m square footing** at the same pressure intensity as the **last load step** (70 kN). Use clay scaling law. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Q B (a): Uses and Limitations **Uses (5 key points):** 1. Direct determination of **ultimate and safe bearing capacity** at the actual foundation depth. 2. Direct estimation of **probable settlement** of a prototype footing. 3. Evaluation of **modulus of subgrade reaction** ($k_s$) and **equivalent modulus of elasticity** ($E_s$). 4. Provides **calibration data** for SPT, CPT, and laboratory test correlations. 5. **Forensic tool** for back-analysis of failed foundations and load tests on existing footings. **Limitations (5 key points):** 1. Limited to **shallow depths (up to ~2B_p to 4B_p)**; cannot investigate deeper strata. 2. **Scale effect** – small plate cannot model deep failure surfaces of large footings. 3. Does **not reflect consolidation settlement** (long-term) of clay soils. 4. **Costly and time-consuming** due to the heavy kentledge requirement. 5. **No control over water table fluctuations** during the test, which may alter results. #### Model Solution — Q B (b): Numerical Analysis **Given:** - Circular plate, $B_p = 0.45\;m$, $A_p = \pi/4 \times 0.45^2 = 0.159\;m^2$ - $\mu = 0.40$, $I_w = 1.0$ (circular flexible) - $S_f = S_p \cdot (B_f / B_p)^2$ (clay scaling) **(i) Modulus of Subgrade Reaction ($k_s$):** Use the initial linear portion: between (10 kN, 2.0 mm) and (30 kN, 6.5 mm): $$ q_1 = \frac{10}{0.159} = 62.89 \; kPa, \quad q_2 = \frac{30}{0.159} = 188.68 \; kPa $$ $$ k_s = \frac{q_2 - q_1}{s_2 - s_1} = \frac{188.68 - 62.89}{(6.5 - 2.0) \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{125.79}{0.0045} $$ $$ \boxed{k_s \approx 27953 \; kN/m^3} $$ **(ii) Equivalent Modulus of Elasticity ($E_s$):** Using $q = 188.68$ kPa, $s = 6.5$ mm = 0.0065 m, $B_p = 0.45$ m: $$ E_s = \frac{q \cdot B_p \cdot I_w \cdot (1 - \mu^2)}{s} $$ $$ = \frac{188.68 \times 0.45 \times 1.0 \times (1 - 0.16)}{0.0065} $$ $$ = \frac{188.68 \times 0.45 \times 0.84}{0.0065} = \frac{71.32}{0.0065} $$ $$ \boxed{E_s \approx 10972 \; kPa \approx 10.97 \; MPa} $$ **(iii) Settlement of 2.0 m × 2.0 m Square Footing:** At $P = 70$ kN: $$ q = \frac{70}{0.159} = 440.25 \; kPa, \quad s_p = 38.0 \; mm $$ Using clay scaling (parabolic): $$ S_f = S_p \cdot \left(\frac{B_f}{B_p}\right)^2 = 38.0 \times \left(\frac{2.0}{0.45}\right)^2 $$ $$ = 38.0 \times (4.444)^2 = 38.0 \times 19.75 = 750.6 \; mm $$ $$ \boxed{S_f \approx 750 \; mm} $$ This is an **unacceptably large settlement** — the design must be revised (larger footing area, lower net pressure, or a **pile foundation**). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong unit conversion of load:** Convert kgf to kN consistently (multiply by 9.81, not 10). > 2. **Confusing scaling laws:** Granular soil → linear ($B_f/B_p$); Cohesive soil → parabolic ($(B_f/B_p)^2$). Mixing these is a guaranteed 3-mark deduction. > 3. **Forgetting to report $B_p$ with $k_s$:** $k_s$ is a *plate-specific* parameter — never report it as a soil property without the corresponding width. > 4. **Not checking settlement criterion:** A footing can pass bearing capacity but fail on settlement. Always verify both. > 5. **Neglecting depth factor & water table correction:** IS 1888 / IS 6403 corrections are frequently skipped by students. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **PLT = IS 1888:1982** field test for bearing capacity & settlement at the foundation depth. - [x] **Standard plate size = 30 cm × 30 cm** (most common); alternatives 45 cm, 60 cm. - [x] **Load increment = 1/5th of estimated ultimate load**; record at stabilized settlement. - [x] **Test pit width ≥ 5B_p**; depth = proposed footing depth. - [x] **Modulus of subgrade reaction:** $k_s = q/s$ — read from the *initial linear* portion of the P-S curve. - [x] **Equivalent modulus of elasticity:** $E_s = q \cdot B \cdot I_w \cdot (1-\mu^2) / s$. - [x] **Terzaghi's scaling laws:** Sand → $S_f = S_p \cdot B_f/B_p$; Clay → $S_f = S_p \cdot (B_f/B_p)^2$. - [x] **Bearing capacity scaling:** Sand → $q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)} \cdot B_p/B_f$; Clay → $q_{ult(f)} = q_{ult(p)}$. - [x] **Biot's method** used when no clear failure is observed on the P-S curve. - [x] **Settlement criterion:** $q_{safe} = q$ at $S = 25\;mm$ divided by FOS = 2 to 3. - [x] **Limitations:** limited stress bulb depth (2B–4B), scale effect, no consolidation effect, costly. - [x] **Bowles' correction:** $k_{s(f)} = k_{s(p)} \cdot B_p/B_f$. - [x] **Plate test is NOT suitable for very soft clays, deep foundations, or layered soils** with weak strata below the influence zone. - [x] **Always verify both** *bearing capacity* AND *settlement* criteria in design — never use one alone.

topics.title: Geophysical Methods – Seismic Refraction Method: Procedure and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geophysical Methods – Seismic Refraction Method: Procedure and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Seismic Refraction Method** is a non-destructive geophysical exploration technique that determines subsurface stratigraphy, bedrock depth, and elastic wave velocities by measuring the travel times of seismic waves refracted at the interface between geological layers of contrasting acoustic impedance. The method relies on the principle of **Snell's Law of refraction**, wherein a compressional (P-wave) traveling from a lower-velocity medium to a higher-velocity medium bends towards the normal and travels along the interface as a **head wave** before returning to the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Seismic Refraction Method is classified as an **ACTIVE** geophysical method (energy is artificially introduced) and is governed by the condition that the seismic wave velocity must **INCREASE with depth** ($V_1 < V_2 < V_3 \ldots V_n$) for the refracted signal to be the first arrival at the geophones.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine shouting into a canyon. The echo takes time to return. If you shout at a friend standing in a flat field, the sound reaches them almost instantly. Now, picture shouting down at the ground where a hard rocky layer lies hidden under soft soil. The sound wave hits this hard layer, races along the rock surface at high speed, and comes back up — *arriving BEFORE* the direct wave that plodded through the soft soil. By placing many "ears" (geophones) in a line and timing when each hears the sound, we can **map the hidden rocky layer** just like timing echoes to map a canyon floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The **harder (denser, more elastic) the layer**, the **faster** the seismic wave travels. Typical field velocities: Soft soil $V_1 = 300$–$800$ m/s, Weathered rock $V_2 = 1000$–$2500$ m/s, Competent bedrock $V_3 = 3000$–$5500$ m/s. Standard field unit is **metres/second (m/s)**.

### 1.3 Standard Field Configuration Metrics

| Parameter | Standard KTU Reference Value |
|---|---|
| Seismic source | Explosive charge (gelignite) or 5–10 kg hammer drop |
| Geophone spacing | 2 m to 10 m (for shallow investigations) |
| Geophone natural frequency | 8 Hz to 14 Hz (P-wave detection) |
| Recording system | 12 to 24 channel signal enhancement seismograph |
| Recording time window | 100 ms to 500 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — First Arrival:** The *first arrival* is the earliest seismic energy detected by a geophone. It corresponds to either the **direct wave** (short offset) or the **refracted (head) wave** (long offset). Plotting these arrival times versus geophone offset yields the **Time-Distance (T–X) graph** — the cornerstone of seismic refraction interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Refraction Theory

The refracted wave phenomenon is governed by the following sequential logic:

- **Step 1 — Wave Generation:** A seismic source (hammer/blast) generates a compressional P-wave that radiates spherically from the impact point.
- **Step 2 — Incident Ray Path:** The downward-traveling wave strikes the interface between Layer 1 (velocity $V_1$) and Layer 2 (velocity $V_2$) at an **angle of incidence** $i$.
- **Step 3 — Snell's Law Application:** Refraction obeys the law $\frac{\sin i}{V_1} = \frac{\sin \theta}{V_2}$. For $V_2 > V_1$, there exists a **critical angle** $i_c$ such that $\sin i_c = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$, at which the refracted ray travels **parallel to the interface** ($\theta = 90^\circ$).
- **Step 4 — Head Wave Formation:** Beyond $i_c$, the wave is critically refracted and propagates along the top of Layer 2 at velocity $V_2$, continuously radiating energy back into Layer 1 (the **head wave**).
- **Step 5 — Return to Surface:** The head wave emerges at the surface, where it is detected by geophones. At a sufficient offset, this refracted energy **outpaces the direct wave** and becomes the *first arrival*.
- **Step 6 — Layered Interpretation:** Each successive interface produces a new refracted segment on the T–X graph. From slopes and intercepts, velocities and depths are computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why Behind Snell's Law:** Energy conservation at a boundary demands that the tangential component of wave velocity must be preserved across the interface. Since $V_2 > V_1$, the wave must refract *away* from the normal to maintain this continuity — eventually grazing the interface at $i_c$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value and division notations in the table below use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` macros to prevent markdown table breakage. The vertical bar `\|` character is **strictly prohibited** inside table cells.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sin i_c = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Critical (refraction) angle condition | $V_1, V_2$ in m/s |
| 2 | $V_1 = \dfrac{D_1}{t_1}$ | Velocity of first layer (direct wave) | $D_1$ = offset (m), $t_1$ = time (s) |
| 3 | $V_2 = \dfrac{\Delta D}{\Delta t}$ | Velocity of refractor (slope of refracted segment) | Slope of T–X curve (s) |
| 4 | $D_c = 2\,h_1\,\sqrt{\dfrac{V_2 + V_1}{V_2 - V_1}}$ | **Critical distance** (crossover) | $h_1$ = depth to interface (m) |
| 5 | $t_i = \dfrac{2\,h_1\,\cos i_c}{V_1}$ | **Intercept time** (y-intercept of refracted line) | $t_i$ in seconds |
| 6 | $h_1 = \dfrac{V_1\,V_2\,t_i}{2\,\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}$ | Depth to first interface | $h_1$ in metres |
| 7 | $h_1 = \dfrac{t_i}{2}\,\dfrac{V_1\,V_2}{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}$ | Alternative form of depth | Same as above |
| 8 | $h_n = \dfrac{V_n}{\cos i_{c_n}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{i_n} - t_{i_{n-1}}}{2}$ | Generalized multi-layer depth formula (using intercept times) | $h_n$ = depth to $n$-th interface |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dam and reservoir site investigation:** Locating the depth to competent bedrock for foundation design.
- **Highway and tunnel alignment studies:** Detecting buried channels, weak zones, and fault planes.
- **Bridge abutment studies:** Identifying soil profile and rippability of rock.
- **Environmental geophysics:** Mapping the water table (often a strong seismic refractor) for groundwater studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity of the First Layer (Direct Wave)

The direct wave travels along the surface from source $S$ to geophone $G$ at a distance $D$ in a straight line at velocity $V_1$.

$$
t_{\text{direct}} = \frac{D}{V_1}
$$

This equation is the linear equation of the first segment of the T–X graph, passing through the origin. The **inverse of its slope** yields $V_1$.

$$
V_1 = \frac{1}{\text{slope of direct wave segment}} = \frac{D_1}{t_1}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Slope of T–X plot is $\Delta t / \Delta D$, so its reciprocal is $\Delta D / \Delta t$, which is the velocity.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Travel Time of the Refracted (Head) Wave in a Two-Layer Case

Consider Layer 1 of thickness $h_1$ and velocity $V_1$ overlying Layer 2 of velocity $V_2$ (where $V_2 > V_1$). A wave travels from source $S$ to geophone $G$ via the path $S \to A \to B \to G$:

- $S \to A$ : travels downward in Layer 1 at angle $i_c$
- $A \to B$ : travels along the top of Layer 2 at velocity $V_2$
- $B \to G$ : travels upward in Layer 1 at angle $i_c$

The path lengths and travel times are computed as follows.

**Step (a):** Distance $S \to A = \dfrac{h_1}{\cos i_c}$

**Step (b):** Horizontal projection of $S \to A = h_1 \tan i_c$

**Step (c):** By symmetry, horizontal projection of $B \to G$ is also $h_1 \tan i_c$

**Step (d):** Total horizontal distance between source and geophone:

$$
D = 2\,h_1 \tan i_c + \overline{AB}
$$

**Step (e):** Travel time in $S \to A$:

$$
t_{SA} = \frac{h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c}
$$

**Step (f):** Travel time in $A \to B$:

$$
t_{AB} = \frac{\overline{AB}}{V_2} = \frac{D - 2\,h_1 \tan i_c}{V_2}
$$

**Step (g):** Travel time in $B \to G$ (equal to $t_{SA}$ by symmetry):

$$
t_{BG} = \frac{h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c}
$$

**Step (h):** Total refracted wave travel time:

$$
t_{\text{refracted}} = t_{SA} + t_{AB} + t_{BG} = \frac{2\,h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c} + \frac{D - 2\,h_1 \tan i_c}{V_2}
$$

**Step (i):** Rearranging and using $\tan i_c = \dfrac{\sin i_c}{\cos i_c} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2 \cos i_c}$:

$$
t_{\text{refracted}} = \frac{D}{V_2} + \frac{2\,h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c} - \frac{2\,h_1 \tan i_c}{V_2}
$$

$$
t_{\text{refracted}} = \frac{D}{V_2} + \frac{2\,h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c} - \frac{2\,h_1}{V_1 \cos i_c}
$$

**Step (j):** The two correction terms cancel because $\tan i_c = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2 \cos i_c}$, leaving the elegant final form:

$$
t_{\text{refracted}} = \frac{D}{V_2} + \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

This is a **linear equation** in $D$ with:
- Slope $= \dfrac{1}{V_2}$ (so the inverse slope gives $V_2$)
- y-intercept $= t_i = \dfrac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical (Crossover) Distance $D_c$

At the **critical distance** $D_c$, the direct wave and refracted wave arrive **simultaneously**:

$$
t_{\text{direct}} = t_{\text{refracted}}
$$

$$
\frac{D_c}{V_1} = \frac{D_c}{V_2} + \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
D_c \left( \frac{1}{V_1} - \frac{1}{V_2} \right) = \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

$$
D_c \left( \frac{V_2 - V_1}{V_1 V_2} \right) = \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $V_1$:

$$
D_c \left( \frac{V_2 - V_1}{V_2} \right) = 2\,h_1 \cos i_c
$$

$$
D_c = \frac{2\,h_1 V_2 \cos i_c}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

Substituting $\cos i_c = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}{V_2}$:

$$
D_c = \frac{2\,h_1 V_2}{V_2 - V_1} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}{V_2}
$$

$$
D_c = \frac{2\,h_1 \sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

Factoring $V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2} = (V_2 - V_1)(V_2 + V_1)$:

$$
D_c = \frac{2\,h_1 \sqrt{(V_2 - V_1)(V_2 + V_1)}}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_c = 2\,h_1 \sqrt{\frac{V_2 + V_1}{V_2 - V_1}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** Beyond $D_c$, the refracted wave (racing through the deeper, faster layer) overtakes the direct wave. Hence $D_c$ is also called the **crossover distance** in many KTU references.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Depth to Interface from Intercept Time

Starting from the intercept time definition:

$$
t_i = \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

Solving for $h_1$:

$$
h_1 = \frac{V_1 t_i}{2 \cos i_c}
$$

Substituting $\cos i_c = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}{V_2}$:

$$
h_1 = \frac{V_1 t_i}{2} \cdot \frac{V_2}{\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_1 = \frac{V_1 V_2\, t_i}{2\,\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** In a two-layer seismic refraction survey, the first layer has $V_1 = 600$ m/s and the second layer has $V_2 = 1800$ m/s. The intercept time of the refracted wave on the T–X graph is $t_i = 0.025$ s. Compute the depth to the interface and the crossover distance.

**Step (a):** Compute the critical angle:

$$
\sin i_c = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{600}{1800} = \frac{1}{3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; i_c = 19.47^\circ
$$

**Step (b):** Compute the cosine of the critical angle:

$$
\cos i_c = \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{1}{9}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{8}{9}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} \approx 0.9428
$$

**Step (c):** Compute the depth using the intercept-time formula:

$$
h_1 = \frac{V_1 V_2\, t_i}{2\,\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}} = \sqrt{1800^2 - 600^2} = \sqrt{3\,240\,000 - 360\,000} = \sqrt{2\,880\,000} \approx 1697.06 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
h_1 = \frac{600 \times 1800 \times 0.025}{2 \times 1697.06} = \frac{27\,000}{3394.12} \approx 7.96 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (d):** Compute the crossover distance:

$$
D_c = 2\,h_1 \sqrt{\frac{V_2 + V_1}{V_2 - V_1}} = 2 \times 7.96 \times \sqrt{\frac{1800 + 600}{1800 - 600}}
$$

$$
D_c = 15.92 \times \sqrt{\frac{2400}{1200}} = 15.92 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 22.51 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** Depth $h_1 \approx 7.96$ m; Crossover distance $D_c \approx 22.5$ m.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Velocity Ranges for Common Materials

| Material | P-Wave Velocity $V_p$ (m/s) | Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| Dry loose sand | 200 – 600 | 1500 – 1700 |
| Saturated clay | 1100 – 1600 | 1700 – 2000 |
| Soft sedimentary rock (shale) | 1500 – 2500 | 2000 – 2500 |
| Sandstone | 2000 – 3500 | 2200 – 2700 |
| Limestone | 3000 – 5500 | 2400 – 2800 |
| Granite (fresh) | 4500 – 6000 | 2600 – 2800 |
| Water-saturated weathered zone | 400 – 1200 | 1800 – 2000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Seismic Refraction Field Procedure

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`). No reserved keywords are used as node names. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting characters.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1 SITE RECONNAISSANCE"] --> nodeB["STEP 2 LAYOUT GEOPHONE SPREAD"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3 DRILL SHOT HOLE OR PLACE HAMMER PLATE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4 CONNECT GEOPHONES TO SEISMOGRAPH"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5 GENERATE SEISMIC IMPULSE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6 RECORD TRAVEL TIMES AT EACH GEOPHONE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 7 PLOT TIME DISTANCE GRAPH"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 8 IDENTIFY DIRECT AND REFRACTED WAVE SEGMENTS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 9 COMPUTE V1 V2 V3 FROM SLOPES"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["STEP 10 COMPUTE INTERCEPT TIMES TI"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["STEP 11 COMPUTE DEPTHS H1 H2 HN"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["STEP 12 PREPARE SUBSURFACE PROFILE REPORT"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["STEP 13 QUALITY CONTROL AND REVERSED PROFILE CHECK"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["STEP 14 FINAL INTERPRETATION AND CROSS VERIFICATION"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Refracted Wave Ray Path in a Two-Layer Model

The diagram below models the ray-path geometry as a functional flow block, abstracting the physics into labelled paths to comply with the Mermaid block-level rendering rule.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["SOURCE POINT S"] --> path1["PATH SA DOWNWARD IN LAYER 1 V1"]
    path1 --> ifaceA["INTERFACE AT DEPTH H1"]
    ifaceA --> path2["HEAD WAVE PATH ALONG LAYER 2 V2 FASTER"]
    path2 --> ifaceB["INTERFACE AT DEPTH H1"]
    ifaceB --> path3["PATH BG UPWARD IN LAYER 1 V1"]
    path3 --> geoA["GEOPHONE G AT OFFSET D"]
    srcA --> pathD["DIRECT WAVE PATH ALONG SURFACE V1"]
    pathD --> geoA
```

### 4.3 Time-Distance Graph Topology (Schematic)

A typical multi-layer T–X graph contains three (or more) linear segments originating at the source point:

- **First segment** (origin to $D_c$): Direct wave with slope $1/V_1$.
- **Second segment** ($D_c$ to $D_{c2}$): Refracted wave from interface 1, slope $1/V_2$, with y-intercept $t_{i1}$.
- **Third segment** (beyond $D_{c2}$): Refracted wave from interface 2 (deeper, faster), slope $1/V_3$, y-intercept $t_{i2}$.

The intersection of consecutive segments defines the **crossover distances** $D_c$, $D_{c2}$, … from which interface depths are derived.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the seismic refraction method and state the fundamental condition for its applicability.**

**Model Answer:** The seismic refraction method is a geophysical exploration technique in which artificially generated seismic waves are refracted at subsurface interfaces between layers of contrasting velocities, and the travel times of the first-arriving refracted (head) waves are recorded at the surface to determine the depth and velocity of the underlying strata.

The fundamental condition is that the **seismic wave velocity must increase with depth** ($V_1 < V_2 < V_3 < \ldots$). If a low-velocity layer is concealed beneath a high-velocity layer, the refracted wave from the deeper interface cannot overtake the wave travelling in the upper layer and therefore cannot be detected — this is known as the **blind-zone problem**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the "critical distance" in seismic refraction surveying? Write the expression for the crossover distance for a two-layer case.**

**Model Answer:** The **critical distance (or crossover distance)** $D_c$ is the minimum offset from the seismic source at which the refracted (head) wave arrives at the geophone **simultaneously with** (or earlier than) the direct wave. Beyond $D_c$, the refracted wave becomes the first arrival.

For a two-layer horizontal case:

$$
D_c = 2\,h_1 \sqrt{\frac{V_2 + V_1}{V_2 - V_1}}
$$

where $h_1$ is the thickness of Layer 1, and $V_1$ and $V_2$ are the seismic wave velocities in Layer 1 and Layer 2 respectively.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe, with a neat schematic, the **field procedure** for conducting a seismic refraction survey to determine the depth to bedrock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-layer seismic refraction survey yields the following data: $V_1 = 500$ m/s, $V_2 = 2000$ m/s, intercept time $t_i = 0.030$ s. Calculate the **depth to the interface** and the **crossover distance**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — Field Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Site reconnaissance** to identify surface conditions, accessibility, and cultural noise sources.
2. **Layout of geophone spread:** A linear array of 12 to 24 geophones is laid out along a straight line with uniform spacing (typically 2 m to 5 m for shallow investigations and 5 m to 10 m for deeper targets).
3. **Source preparation:** A shallow shothole is drilled (or a hammer-strike plate is positioned) at one end of the spread, at a known offset from the nearest geophone.
4. **Cable connection:** Each geophone is connected via seismic cable to a multi-channel seismograph with a trigger geophone near the source.
5. **Energy generation:** A seismic impulse is generated either by detonating a small explosive charge or by dropping a sledgehammer. The instant of impact triggers the recording.
6. **Recording:** The arrival time of the first P-wave energy at each geophone is captured and stored.
7. **Plotting T–X graph:** Travel times are plotted against geophone offsets to produce a piecewise-linear T–X curve.
8. **Velocity analysis:** The inverse slope of each linear segment yields the velocity of the corresponding layer.
9. **Intercept-time calculation:** The y-intercept of the refracted segment gives $t_i$.
10. **Depth computation:** Using $h_1 = \dfrac{V_1 V_2 t_i}{2 \sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}$, the depth to the interface is computed.
11. **Quality control:** A **reverse profile** is run (source and geophones swapped) to confirm results and detect dipping interfaces.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_1 = 500$ m/s, $V_2 = 2000$ m/s, $t_i = 0.030$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}} = \sqrt{2000^2 - 500^2} = \sqrt{4\,000\,000 - 250\,000} = \sqrt{3\,750\,000}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3\,750\,000} \approx 1936.49 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute depth $h_1$:**

$$
h_1 = \frac{V_1 V_2 t_i}{2 \sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}} = \frac{500 \times 2000 \times 0.030}{2 \times 1936.49}
$$

$$
h_1 = \frac{30\,000}{3872.98} \approx 7.746 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute crossover distance $D_c$:**

$$
D_c = 2\,h_1 \sqrt{\frac{V_2 + V_1}{V_2 - V_1}} = 2 \times 7.746 \times \sqrt{\frac{2000 + 500}{2000 - 500}}
$$

$$
D_c = 15.492 \times \sqrt{\frac{2500}{1500}} = 15.492 \times \sqrt{1.6667}
$$

$$
D_c = 15.492 \times 1.2910 \approx 20.00 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $h_1 \approx 7.75$ m; $D_c \approx 20.0$ m.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **limitations of the seismic refraction method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Time-Distance (T–X) graph method** for a two-layer horizontal interface and derive the expression for the **travel time of the refracted wave**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — Limitations (7 marks):**

1. **Velocity inversion limitation (Blind Zone):** The method fails when a low-velocity layer lies beneath a high-velocity layer. Refracted waves from below the low-velocity zone cannot overtake the wave in the high-velocity layer and remain undetected.
2. **Hidden layer problem:** Thin layers sandwiched between two thick layers may not produce a distinct refracted arrival, causing depth interpretation errors.
3. **Requires contrast in velocities:** The method demands that each successive layer has a higher velocity than the one above; if velocities are nearly equal ($V_2 \approx V_1$), the refracted wave cannot outpace the direct wave and no usable signal is recorded.
4. **Shallow water-table interference:** The water table often acts as a strong refractor, masking deeper refractions and producing misleading "two-layer" interpretations of more complex geology.
5. **Cultural noise and vibrations:** Traffic, machinery, and wind-induced vibrations contaminate first arrivals, especially in urban sites.
6. **Costly and labour-intensive:** Requires explosive permits, drilling, geophone arrays, and skilled operators. Hammer-source surveys are limited to shallow depths ($\le 30$ m).
7. **Ambiguous interpretation:** Multiple subsurface models can produce identical T–X graphs, leading to **non-unique solutions** unless auxiliary borehole data are available.
8. **Poor detection of cavities and steep dips:** The method gives unreliable results for cavernous terrain, vertical fault faces, or steeply dipping interfaces.
9. **Lateral inhomogeneity:** Abrupt lateral velocity changes (e.g., at geological contacts) are poorly resolved.

---

**(b) Model Solution — T–X Graph Method & Derivation (7 marks):**

The T–X graph is a plot of **first-arrival travel time** (y-axis) versus **geophone offset** $D$ (x-axis), measured along a straight surface line.

**For a two-layer horizontal model:**

The direct wave equation is $t = D / V_1$ (a straight line through the origin with slope $1/V_1$).

The refracted wave equation is:

$$
t = \frac{D}{V_2} + \frac{2\,h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}
$$

(derived in Section 3.2, Steps a–j of this note).

**Key features of the T–X graph:**

- **Origin (0, 0):** Both lines pass through the source point.
- **Crossover point at $D_c$:** Where the two lines intersect, the direct and refracted waves arrive simultaneously. For offsets $< D_c$, the direct wave is first; for offsets $> D_c$, the refracted wave is first.
- **Slope of direct segment** = $1/V_1$, giving $V_1$.
- **Slope of refracted segment** = $1/V_2$, giving $V_2$.
- **y-intercept of refracted segment** = $t_i = \dfrac{2 h_1 \cos i_c}{V_1}$, used to compute $h_1$.

The intercept time $t_i$ is read by **extrapolating the refracted line back** to $D = 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Velocities in m/s, time in seconds, distance in metres — mixing units causes cascading calculation errors.
> 2. **Sign error in the denominator:** $\sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}$ requires $V_2 > V_1$. Reversing gives imaginary numbers.
> 3. **Misreading the intercept time:** Students often quote the y-intercept incorrectly; it must be extrapolated from the **refracted segment only**, not read off the original data.
> 4. **Omitting the blind-zone limitation:** A 3-mark question on "limitations" without mentioning the velocity-inversion problem typically loses 1 full mark.
> 5. **Confusing crossover and critical distance:** Both terms are used interchangeably in KTU papers, but the numerical value must always be the same.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core principle:** Seismic refraction measures the travel time of **refracted (head) waves** at layer interfaces; valid **only when $V_1 < V_2 < V_3 \ldots$**
- **Snell's Law:** $\sin i_c = V_1 / V_2$ — the foundation equation of all refraction theory.
- **Time-Distance graph:** Slope of the direct segment gives $1/V_1$; slope of the refracted segment gives $1/V_2$.
- **Intercept time $t_i$:** y-intercept of the refracted segment, used to compute interface depth.
- **Crossover distance $D_c$:** Distance beyond which the refracted wave is the first arrival; equals $2 h_1 \sqrt{(V_2 + V_1)/(V_2 - V_1)}$.
- **Depth formula:** $h_1 = \dfrac{V_1 V_2 t_i}{2 \sqrt{V_2^{\,2} - V_1^{\,2}}}$.
- **Multi-layer extension:** Each additional layer adds a new linear segment with a new $V_n$ and intercept time $t_{i_n}$.
- **Velocity ranges to memorize (m/s):** Soil 200–800, Weathered rock 1000–2500, Competent rock 3000–6000.
- **Blind-zone limitation:** A low-velocity layer beneath a high-velocity layer is **invisible** to this method.
- **Reverse profile:** Essential quality-control procedure to detect dipping interfaces and validate results.
- **Reverse profile dip-correction:** If the two intercept times differ, use the average $\bar{t_i}$ for depth calculation, and the dip angle $\alpha$ from the difference in apparent velocities.
- **Hidden layer:** A thin, low-velocity stratum sandwiched between thick layers may go undetected, leading to overestimation of bedrock depth.
- **Cultural noise:** Mitigation requires stacking multiple shots, using signal-enhancement seismographs, and choosing quiet early-morning hours.
- **Typical field equipment:** 12–24 channel seismograph, 8–14 Hz vertical geophones, 5 kg hammer or 100–500 g explosive charge, 50–100 m cable spread.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Resistivity Method – Electrical Profiling and Electrical Sounding: Procedure, Uses, and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Resistivity Method – Electrical Profiling and Electrical Sounding: Procedure, Uses, and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Electrical Resistivity Method (ERM)

The **Electrical Resistivity Method** is a non-destructive, surface-based geophysical technique in which a controlled direct (or low-frequency alternating) electrical current is introduced into the ground through a pair of **current electrodes (C₁ & C₂)**, and the resulting drop in electrical potential is measured across a second pair called **potential electrodes (P₁ & P₂)**. The ratio of the measured potential difference to the injected current, multiplied by a configuration-dependent **geometric factor K**, yields the **apparent resistivity (ρₐ)** of the subsurface — a property that depends on the lithology, porosity, fluid content, salinity, and degree of saturation of the soils/rocks present.

Mathematically, for a general four-electrode collinear array:

$$\rho_a = K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I} \quad [\Omega \cdot m]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *"The electrical resistivity method measures the resistance of subsurface materials to the flow of an electric current, providing information on the type, porosity, water content, and stratification of subsurface formations."*

The **measured quantity is *apparent* resistivity** — it represents the resistivity of a hypothetical, electrically homogeneous half-space that would produce the observed (V/I) ratio. The actual layered earth must be interpreted (inverted) from this apparent response.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Soil as an Electrical Sieve"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Leaky Battery" Analogy:**
> Imagine the subsurface as a giant, multi-layered **leaky battery pack**. Different materials (wet clay, dry sand, fractured rock, voids) act like different resistors. **Wet clay is a poor resistor** (low resistivity, ~1–10 Ω·m) because ions in pore water conduct easily. **Dry crystalline rock or air-filled cavities are excellent resistors** (high resistivity, > 1000 Ω·m). By "poking" the ground with current and "listening" with potential electrodes, we map where the easy and hard paths are — much like a doctor uses electrical conductivity (ECG/EKG) to "see" inside the body.

In essence:
- **Low ρₐ** → Conductive layer (clay, salty water, metallic ore, contaminated soil)
- **Medium ρₐ** → Saturated sand/gravel, weathered rock
- **High ρₐ** → Dry sand, dense rock, air-filled cavity, fresh bedrock

The **resistivity (ρ)** is the fundamental material property (in **Ω·m**), while the **resistance (R)** is the measured circuit quantity (in **Ω**). The geometric factor **K** (in **m**) converts the two for a given electrode arrangement.

---

## 1.3 Why Use This Method in Geotechnical Engineering?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for *Advanced Geotechnical Investigation*, ERM is a vital **preliminary / reconnaissance tool** because it:

1. Covers **large lateral areas quickly** (unlike boreholes which are point measurements).
2. Detects **anomalies** (cavities, faults, contaminated zones) **non-invasively**.
3. Helps **optimize borehole locations**, reducing investigation cost and time.
4. Provides a **continuous subsurface profile** between boreholes.

Two operational variants — **Profiling** and **Sounding** — answer two fundamentally different questions and are treated as separate KTU sub-topics.

| Variant | Question Answered | What is Varied | What is Held Fixed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Profiling** | *"How does resistivity vary horizontally at a chosen depth?"* | Horizontal position of the array | Electrode spacing (a) |
| **Electrical Sounding (VES)** | *"How does resistivity vary with depth at a chosen point?"* | Electrode spacing (a) | Center of the array |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physics — Ohm's Law in a Hemispherical Earth

When a single current electrode injects current **I** into a homogeneous, isotropic half-space, current flows radially outward. At a distance **r** from the electrode, current passes through a hemispherical shell of area **2πr²**, so the current density is:

$$J(r) = \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$

The corresponding electric field intensity is:

$$E(r) = \rho \cdot J(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r^2}$$

The potential at distance **r** (with reference at infinity) is:

$$V(r) = \int_{\infty}^{r} E\,dr = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

This is the **fundamental potential equation** for a point current source in a homogeneous ground. **All four-electrode configurations are built on this.**

---

## 2.2 The General Apparent Resistivity Equation

For two current sources (C₁ at +I, C₂ at –I) and two potential electrodes (P₁, P₂), superposition gives:

$$V_{P_1} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_1}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_1}}\right)$$

$$V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_2}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_2}}\right)$$

$$\Delta V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left[\frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_1}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_1}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_2}} + \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_2}}\right]$$

Solving for **ρ** (or **ρₐ** for a non-homogeneous earth):

$$\boxed{\;\rho_a = 2\pi \left[\frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_1}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_1}} - \frac{1}{r_{C_1 P_2}} + \frac{1}{r_{C_2 P_2}}\right]^{-1} \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I} = K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I}\;}$$

where **K** is the **geometric factor** (configuration constant, units: **metres**).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA:** $\rho_a = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$, where K depends **only** on electrode geometry.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Remarks (KTU focus) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent Resistivity | ρₐ | $\rho_a = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | Ω·m | Master equation |
| 2 | Geometric Factor (general) | K | $K = 2\pi \left[\dfrac{1}{r_{C_1 P_1}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{C_2 P_1}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{C_1 P_2}} + \dfrac{1}{r_{C_2 P_2}}\right]^{-1}$ | m | Depends only on electrode distances |
| 3 | **Wenner Array** Geometric Factor | $K_W$ | $K_W = 2\pi a$ | m | Equal spacing: $C_1 P_1 = P_1 P_2 = P_2 C_2 = a$ |
| 4 | **Schlumberger Array** Geometric Factor | $K_S$ | $K_S = \dfrac{\pi (a^2 - b^2/4)}{b}$, where C₁C₂ = 2a, P₁P₂ = b | m | Typically $b \ll 2a$ |
| 5 | **Dipole-Dipole** Geometric Factor | $K_{DD}$ | $K_{DD} = \pi n(n+1)(n+2)a$ | m | n = dipole separation index |
| 6 | **Pole-Pole** Geometric Factor | $K_{PP}$ | $K_{PP} = 2\pi a$ | m | Single C at infinity, single P at infinity |
| 7 | Depth of Investigation (approx.) | $d_i$ | $d_i \approx 0.5\,a$ (Wenner); $d_i \approx 0.25\,(C_1 C_2)$ (Schlumberger) | m | Rule of thumb |
| 8 | True Resistivity (single layer) | ρ | $\rho = \rho_a$ | Ω·m | Only if subsurface is homogeneous |
| 9 | Longitudinal Conductance | S | $S = \dfrac{h}{\rho}$ | S (Siemens) | For a layer of thickness h |
| 10 | Transverse Resistance | T | $T = h \cdot \rho$ | Ω·m² | Used in Dar Zarrouk parameters |

---

## 2.4 Standard Resistivity Values of Common Materials (KTU Memory Bank)

| Material | Typical Resistivity (Ω·m) |
|---|---|
| Clay (saturated) | 1 – 100 |
| Silt / Loam | 10 – 150 |
| Sand (saturated) | 50 – 500 |
| Sand (dry) | 200 – 5 000 |
| Gravel (saturated) | 100 – 1 000 |
| Weathered Rock | 50 – 1 000 |
| Sound Crystalline Rock | 1 000 – 10 000 |
| Fresh Granite / Basalt | > 10 000 |
| Air-filled Cavity | > 10 000 (infinite) |
| Sea Water | ~ 0.2 |
| Pure Water | > 1 000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Wet & salty = low ρ; Dry & massive = high ρ."*

---

## 2.5 Why This Works in Geotechnical Practice

- **Conduction in soils/rocks is predominantly electrolytic** (via pore-fluid ions), not electronic. Therefore ρ depends on:
  1. Porosity (n)
  2. Degree of saturation (Sᵣ)
  3. Pore-fluid salinity / TDS
  4. Temperature
  5. Clay content (high surface conductance)
- **Archie's Law** (for clay-free sediments):

$$\rho = a \cdot \rho_w \cdot \phi^{-m} \cdot S_r^{-n}$$

where ρw = pore-water resistivity, ϕ = porosity, a ≈ 1, m ≈ 2 (cementation exponent), n ≈ 2 (saturation exponent).

This relationship allows indirect estimation of **porosity, saturation, and groundwater salinity** — making ERM a powerful hydrological and contamination-mapping tool in addition to geotechnical reconnaissance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Factor for the Wenner Array

**Array Layout** (collinear, equal spacing *a*):

$$C_1 \quad\bullet \quad P_1 \quad\bullet \quad P_2 \quad\bullet \quad C_2$$
$$\;\;a\;\;\;\;a\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;a$$

Hence the inter-electrode distances are:

$$r_{C_1 P_1} = a, \quad r_{C_2 P_1} = 2a, \quad r_{C_1 P_2} = 2a, \quad r_{C_2 P_2} = a$$

**Step 1** — Substitute into the master geometric-factor equation:

$$K = 2\pi \left[\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{2a} - \frac{1}{2a} + \frac{1}{a}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 2** — Simplify the bracketed term:

$$\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{2a} - \frac{1}{2a} = \frac{2}{a} - \frac{1}{a} = \frac{1}{a}$$

**Step 3** — Take the inverse and multiply by 2π:

$$K_W = 2\pi \left(\frac{1}{a}\right)^{-1} = 2\pi a$$

$$\boxed{\;K_W = 2\pi a \quad\Rightarrow\quad \rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometric Factor for the Schlumberger Array

**Array Layout** (collinear, symmetric):

$$C_1 \quad\bullet \;\;\;\; P_1 \quad\bullet \quad P_2 \;\;\;\; \bullet \quad C_2$$
$$\;\;\;\; a \;\;\;\;\;\; b/2 \;\;\;\; b/2 \;\;\;\;\; a$$

Thus $C_1 C_2 = 2a$ and $P_1 P_2 = b$, with the centre of P₁P₂ coinciding with the centre of C₁C₂. The inter-electrode distances are:

$$r_{C_1 P_1} = a - \frac{b}{2}, \quad r_{C_2 P_1} = a + \frac{b}{2}, \quad r_{C_1 P_2} = a + \frac{b}{2}, \quad r_{C_2 P_2} = a - \frac{b}{2}$$

**Step 1** — Substitute into the master equation:

$$K = 2\pi \left[\frac{1}{a - b/2} - \frac{1}{a + b/2} - \frac{1}{a + b/2} + \frac{1}{a - b/2}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 2** — Group like terms:

$$K = 2\pi \left[2 \cdot \frac{1}{a - b/2} - 2 \cdot \frac{1}{a + b/2}\right]^{-1} = 2\pi \left[2 \left(\frac{1}{a - b/2} - \frac{1}{a + b/2}\right)\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 3** — Combine the fractions inside:

$$\frac{1}{a - b/2} - \frac{1}{a + b/2} = \frac{(a + b/2) - (a - b/2)}{(a - b/2)(a + b/2)} = \frac{b}{a^2 - b^2/4}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute back:

$$K = 2\pi \left[2 \cdot \frac{b}{a^2 - b^2/4}\right]^{-1} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{a^2 - b^2/4}{2b}$$

$$\boxed{\;K_S = \frac{\pi (a^2 - b^2/4)}{b} = \frac{\pi}{b}\left(a^2 - \frac{b^2}{4}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting case:** If $b \ll a$ (typical field practice: b = 1 m, a = 5–100 m):

$$K_S \approx \frac{\pi a^2}{b}$$

This shows **why Schlumberger is more sensitive to shallow noise** when b is small — the geometric factor magnifies small voltage errors.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth of Investigation (Approximate)

Empirically, for any collinear four-electrode array, the **effective depth of investigation** correlates with the **largest current-electrode separation**. Using potential-theory arguments, the depth where the contribution function peaks is approximately:

**For Wenner:**
$$d_W \approx 0.5\,a$$

**For Schlumberger:**
$$d_S \approx 0.25\,(C_1 C_2) = 0.5\,a$$

> **Implication:** By simply doubling the electrode spacing, we *approximately* double the depth probed. This is the operational basis of **Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES)**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem (Mock):** A Schlumberger sounding is conducted at a site. Current I = 50 mA is injected, the measured potential difference ΔV = 280 mV, half-current-electrode-spacing a = 25 m, and half-potential-spacing b/2 = 0.5 m (so b = 1 m). Calculate the apparent resistivity.

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration:**

Schlumberger: $K_S = \dfrac{\pi (a^2 - b^2/4)}{b}$

**Step 2 — Compute K:**

$$K_S = \frac{\pi (25^2 - 1^2/4)}{1} = \pi (625 - 0.25) = 624.75\,\pi \approx 1962.96 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute V/I ratio:**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{I} = \frac{280 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}}{50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}} = 5.6\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute apparent resistivity:**

$$\rho_a = K_S \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I} = 1962.96 \times 5.6 \approx 10\,992.6\,\Omega \cdot m$$

$$\boxed{\;\rho_a \approx 10\,993\,\Omega \cdot m \;\approx\; 11\,000\,\Omega \cdot m\;}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:** This is a high value, consistent with **dry sand or crystalline rock** at this electrode spacing/depth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Profiling vs Sounding at a Glance

| Attribute | Electrical Profiling | Electrical Sounding (VES) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Detect **lateral** variations at constant depth | Detect **vertical** (depth-wise) variations at a fixed point |
| **Parameter Varied** | Position of array (x) | Electrode spacing (a) |
| **Parameter Fixed** | Electrode spacing (a) | Center of array (x = constant) |
| **Depth of Probe** | Constant (≈ 0.5 a) | Increases with a (≈ 0.5 a) |
| **Output Graph** | ρₐ vs. horizontal distance (X-axis) | ρₐ vs. half-spacing (L = a or C₁C₂/2) |
| **Type of Result** | Qualitative map of anomalies | Quantitative 1-D layered model |
| **Common Configurations** | Wenner (most common) | Schlumberger (most common) |
| **Field Time** | Fast (single a, multiple stations) | Slower (many a at one station) |
| **Interpretation** | Visual inspection, anomaly mapping | Curve matching / computer inversion |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Locating buried utilities, cavity mapping | Groundwater table depth, bedrock depth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Schematic — ERM Field Setup (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Source: Battery or Generator] --> B[Resistivity Meter: Tx and Rx unit]
    B -->|Current I via cable| C1[Current Electrode C1 in ground]
    B -->|Current return| C2[Current Electrode C2 in ground]
    B -->|Voltage sensing| P1[Potential Electrode P1 in ground]
    B -->|Voltage sensing| P2[Potential Electrode P2 in ground]
    C1 -.Current flows through.-> Sub1[Subsurface Volume]
    C2 -.Current flows through.-> Sub1
    Sub1 --> L1[Layer 1: Topsoil]
    Sub1 --> L2[Layer 2: Weathered Rock]
    Sub1 --> L3[Layer 3: Bedrock or Water Table]
    P1 -->|Reads potential| B
    P2 -->|Reads potential| B
    B --> D[Digital Readout: V and I]
    D --> E[Compute rho_a = K times V divided by I]
    E --> F[Plot / Invert / Interpret]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a typical resistivity field survey, from current injection through the layered subsurface to data interpretation. **C₁ & C₂** carry the current; **P₁ & P₂** measure the resulting potential drop; the meter computes ρₐ using the appropriate K.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — When to Use Profiling vs Sounding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Geotechnical Investigation Objective] --> Q1{What do you need to know?}
    Q1 -->|Lateral variation at one depth| Prof[Electrical Profiling]
    Q1 -->|Vertical stratification at one location| Sound[Vertical Electrical Sounding VES]
    Q1 -->|Both lateral and vertical| Both[Combined: Profiling + Sounding]
    Prof --> P1[Fix electrode spacing a]
    P1 --> P2[Move entire array along traverse]
    P2 --> P3[Plot rho_a vs distance]
    P3 --> P4[Identify anomalies, contacts, cavities]
    Sound --> S1[Fix center of array]
    S1 --> S2[Progressively increase spacing a]
    S2 --> S3[Plot rho_a vs half spacing L]
    S3 --> S4[Match curve with master curves or invert]
    S4 --> S5[Obtain layer thicknesses and resistivities]
    Both --> B1[Conduct soundings at key points]
    B1 --> B2[Conduct profiling between soundings]
    B2 --> B3[Build pseudo 3D resistivity model]
```

---

## 4.3 Schlumberger Sounding — Schematic of Increasing Spacing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Step1[Step 1: a equals 1.5 m, shallow probe] --> Step2[Step 2: a equals 5 m, intermediate]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: a equals 15 m, deeper]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: a equals 50 m, deep probe]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5: a equals 150 m, deepest]
    Step1 -.Same center point O.-> Step2
    Step2 -.Same center point O.-> Step3
    Step3 -.Same center point O.-> Step4
    Step4 -.Same center point O.-> Step5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Concept:** The center "O" of the array remains stationary; the **outer current electrodes (C₁, C₂)** march outward symmetrically. As a result, the **current penetration depth grows progressively**, allowing the operator to "see" deeper layers at the *same* lateral location.

---

## 4.4 Wenner Profiling — Schematic of Array Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    St1[Station 1: x equals 0 m] --> St2[Station 2: x equals 5 m]
    St2 --> St3[Station 3: x equals 10 m]
    St3 --> St4[Station 4: x equals 15 m]
    St4 --> St5[Station 5: x equals 20 m]
    St1 -.Same spacing a.-> St2
    St2 -.Same spacing a.-> St3
    St3 -.Same spacing a.-> St4
    St4 -.Same spacing a.-> St5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Concept:** In profiling, the four electrodes march *together* along a traverse line like a **measuring stick of fixed length a**. The constant spacing fixes the depth of investigation, while the translation maps **horizontal** changes — ideal for tracing faults, contacts, and buried objects.

---

## 4.5 Typical VES Curve — Layer Response Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Curve[Apparent Resistivity Curve rho_a vs L] --> Type1{Type of Curve}
    Type1 -->|Two-layer H type: rho2 greater than rho1| H[H-type: rises then plateaus]
    Type1 -->|Two-layer K type: rho2 less than rho1| K[K-type: falls then plateaus]
    Type1 -->|Three-layer Q type: rises then falls| Q[Q-type: rho1 less than rho2 greater than rho3]
    Type1 -->|Three-layer A type: falls then rises| A[A-type: rho1 greater than rho2 less than rho3]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip — Master Curve Codes:** The letter code reflects the *visual* shape of the curve. **H** = high-resistivity basement; **K** = low-resistivity basement; **A** = alternate rising/falling; **Q** = reverse A. This is a high-yield topic for short questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q1.** Define **apparent resistivity**. Why is it called "apparent" rather than "true"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **apparent resistivity (ρₐ)** is the resistivity of a hypothetical, electrically homogeneous and isotropic half-space that would produce the same (V/I) ratio as the actual heterogeneous, layered subsurface, for a given electrode configuration.

$$\rho_a = K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I}$$

It is called *"apparent"* because in a real, layered earth the current does **not** flow through a uniform medium — it is channelled differently by each layer depending on its true resistivity and thickness. Hence ρₐ is a **weighted average** of the resistivities of all layers contributing to the measurement, and does not represent any single layer's true resistivity. Only in a **perfectly homogeneous ground** does ρₐ equal the true resistivity.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]`

**Q2.** Differentiate between **electrical profiling** and **electrical sounding** in geophysical investigation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Electrical Profiling | Electrical Sounding (VES) |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Maps **lateral (horizontal)** variation of resistivity at a chosen depth | Maps **vertical (depth-wise)** variation of resistivity at a chosen point |
| Parameter varied | Position of the array along a traverse | Electrode spacing (a) |
| Parameter held constant | Electrode spacing (a) | Centre of the array |
| Output | ρₐ vs. horizontal distance | ρₐ vs. half-spacing (L) |
| Common use | Detecting buried objects, cavities, faults, contacts | Determining layer thicknesses, water table, bedrock depth |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**Q.A)** **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **Wenner electrode configuration** for an electrical resistivity survey. Derive its geometric factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **field procedure for carrying out a Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) using the Schlumberger array**, including data acquisition, plotting, and interpretation steps. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Wenner Configuration & Geometric Factor (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Wenner array** is a collinear, four-electrode, equal-spacing configuration in which the two potential electrodes (P₁, P₂) are placed symmetrically between the two current electrodes (C₁, C₂), with all adjacent electrodes separated by an equal distance *a*.

**Sketch (1 mark) — Array Layout:**

$$C_1 \;\;\bullet\;\;\; P_1 \;\;\bullet\;\;\; P_2 \;\;\bullet\;\;\; C_2$$
$$\;\;\;\;a\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;a\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;a$$

**Derivation of Geometric Factor (4 marks):**

Inter-electrode distances:

$$r_{C_1 P_1} = a, \quad r_{C_2 P_1} = 2a, \quad r_{C_1 P_2} = 2a, \quad r_{C_2 P_2} = a$$

From the master equation:

$$K = 2\pi \left[\frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{2a} - \frac{1}{2a} + \frac{1}{a}\right]^{-1}$$

$$K = 2\pi \left[\frac{2}{a} - \frac{1}{a}\right]^{-1} = 2\pi \left[\frac{1}{a}\right]^{-1} = 2\pi a$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{I}\;}$$

**Advantages of Wenner (1 mark):** Simple, robust, minimal cable layout, good signal strength, suitable for profiling.

**Limitation (1 mark):** Requires all four electrodes to be moved together for profiling; bulkier in field than Schlumberger.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — VES Field Procedure with Schlumberger Array (7 marks)

**1. Site Selection & Reconnaissance (1 mark):**
Identify the objective (e.g., water-table depth, bedrock depth). Choose a flat, accessible traverse. Mark the central sounding point (O).

**2. Array Layout (1 mark):**
- Place C₁ and C₂ symmetrically on either side of O at half-spacing *a*.
- Place P₁ and P₂ at half-spacing *b/2* from O (b is small and constant; typically b = 1 m).
- Total spread: $C_1 C_2 = 2a$, $P_1 P_2 = b$.

**3. Electrode Installation (0.5 mark):**
Drive four steel electrodes firmly into the ground, ensuring good contact (use salt water / bentonite slurry in dry, resistive soils).

**4. Equipment Setup (0.5 mark):**
Connect to a digital resistivity meter (e.g., Terrameter, SuperSting). Use insulated cables to avoid leakage. Set frequency (typically ~ 1 Hz to suppress telluric noise).

**5. Data Acquisition (1 mark):**
- Inject current I, measure ΔV.
- Compute $\rho_a = K_S \cdot \Delta V / I$ where $K_S = \pi(a^2 - b^2/4)/b$.
- **Progressively increase *a*** (e.g., 1.5, 2.5, 4, 6, 10, 15, 25, 40, 65, 100, 150 m …) while keeping the centre O fixed.
- At each step, check that ΔV is within instrument range; reduce b if ΔV becomes too small.

**6. Plotting the Sounding Curve (1 mark):**
Plot **ρₐ (Y-axis, log scale)** vs. **AB/2 = a (X-axis, log scale)** on a bilogarithmic graph. This is the **field curve**.

**7. Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Qualitative:** Identify curve type (H, K, A, Q, HA, HK, etc.) — corresponds to a layered model.
- **Quantitative:**
