# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define "Pile Load Test" and mention the two main types per IS 2911 (Part 4) – 2013.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Pile Load Test (PLT)** is an in-situ full-scale test performed on a deep foundation pile to determine its **axial compressive load-carrying capacity** and **load-settlement behaviour** by applying a known, incremental load at the pile head and measuring the resulting settlement.

As per **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 2013**, there are two main types:
1. **Initial Test (Preliminary Test):** Conducted on a designated test pile *before* the foundation design is finalized. The pile is loaded to **at least 2.5 times the estimated design working load** or to failure, whichever occurs earlier. Used to establish the *true* ultimate load.
2. **Routine Test (Working Test):** Conducted on a *working pile* (already part of the foundation) at a load equal to **1.5 times the design working load**. Used to verify that the constructed pile meets the design specifications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Many students incorrectly state "Initial Test = 1.5 WL" and "Routine Test = 2.5 WL" — these are **reversed**! Memorize: *Initial = More Load (Explore Limits); Routine = Standard Verification (1.5 WL).*

---

### Question 2: What is "Davisson's Offset Method" for determining the ultimate load from a pile load test?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Davisson's Offset Method (1972)** determines the ultimate pile load by establishing an **offset elastic compression line** on the load-settlement (Q-s) curve.

**Procedure:**
1. Calculate the **elastic compression** of the pile under load $Q$:
$$s_e = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A_p \cdot E_p}$$
2. Add an **offset** $\Delta$ to this elastic line:
- $\Delta = 4 \text{ mm}$ (0.15 inch) for piles in soil,
- $\Delta = 1.25 \text{ mm}$ (0.05 inch) for piles bearing on rock.
3. The **ultimate load ($Q_u$)** is the load at the intersection of this **offset line** with the **actual measured Q-s curve**.

The method provides a **clear, unambiguous failure criterion** and is particularly useful in soils where the Q-s curve does not show a sharp peak.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert units. With $L$ in metres, $A_p$ in m², $E_p$ in kN/m² (note: 1 GPa = $10^6$ kN/m²), and $Q$ in kN, the result $s_e$ comes in **metres**. Convert to **mm** for plotting on the Q-s curve.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

### Question A: (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A:** A **500 mm diameter, 18 m long** RCC bored pile is to be tested by a **Maintained Load (ML) test** in a clayey soil deposit. The concrete has $E_p = 27 \times 10^6$ kN/m², and the design working load is **600 kN**.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe in detail the **procedure** of conducting a Maintained Load (ML) pile load test as per IS 2911 (Part 4) – 2013, with neat sketches of the test setup. Clearly mention the loading sequence, hold criteria, and reference beam support requirements.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following data was recorded during the test. Using the **Davisson's Offset Method** and the **IS 2911 criterion (s = 0.1 D)**, determine the **ultimate load** and the **allowable working load** (F_s = 2.5).

| Load (kN) | Settlement (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 |
| 300 | 1.2 |
| 600 | 2.5 |
| 900 | 4.5 |
| 1200 | 7.5 |
| 1500 | 12.0 |
| 1800 | 18.5 |
| 2100 | 28.0 |
| 2400 | 41.0 |

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Procedure of ML Test (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer (Detailed — Worth 7 marks):**

**1. Test Pile Preparation (1 mark):**
- The test pile should be installed at least **5 pile diameters (5D)** away from any other pile (to avoid group interaction).
- The pile head is brought to a convenient working level, **at least 0.5 m above ground**, and capped with a thin layer (10–20 mm) of high-strength plaster or cement mortar to ensure a smooth, level bearing surface.

**2. Reaction System — Kentledge Method (1 mark):**
- A **reaction beam** (welded steel I-section or built-up girder) is placed above the pile.
- **Kentledge** (dead weight: sand bags, concrete blocks, or pig iron) is stacked symmetrically on the beam.
- The total kentledge weight must be **at least 1.2 times the maximum anticipated test load** to prevent overturning.
- *Alternative:* Use **4 to 6 anchor (tension) piles** at a minimum centre-to-centre spacing of 3D, designed to resist 1.2–1.5× the maximum test load in tension.

**3. Jack and Load Measurement (1 mark):**
- A **calibrated hydraulic jack** (capacity 200–500 tonnes) is placed centrally between the pile head and the reaction beam.
- Load measurement: Use a **pressure gauge** (for quick readings) and a **load cell** (more accurate, ±1% accuracy) — both calibrated against a **proving ring** before each test.

**4. Dial Gauges and Reference Beam (1 mark):**
- A minimum of **2 dial gauges** (preferably 4) of **0.01 mm least count** and **100 mm travel** are placed diametrically opposite on a flat, polished **glass plate** fixed to the pile head.
- The dial gauges rest on a **reference beam**, which in turn is supported on **immovable supports** placed at a distance of **at least 3D (1.5 m for D = 0.5 m)** from the test pile and **5D** from any anchor pile.
- This prevents the reference supports from being affected by soil disturbance.

**5. Loading Sequence (2 marks):**
- Apply load in **increments of 20% of the estimated working load** (e.g., 120 kN for a 600 kN working load).
- Each load increment is **maintained constant** until settlement stabilizes:
  - In **clayey soil**: rate of settlement ≤ **0.1 mm per 20 minutes**.
  - In **sandy soil**: rate of settlement ≤ **0.05 mm per 20 minutes**.
- **Minimum hold period** per increment: 1 hour (or until stabilization), and **2 hours** for the final load.
- **Maximum test load (Initial Test):** at least **2.5 × design working load** = 2.5 × 600 = 1500 kN (or up to failure).

**6. Unloading and Records (1 mark):**
- Unload in decrements of 20% of the maximum test load, with **2-hour observation periods** to capture elastic rebound.
- Final residual settlement is observed for **12 hours** after complete unloading.
- **Records:** Plot **Load vs. Settlement (Q-s)** and **Time vs. Settlement (T-s)** curves at every load step.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 Marks)

**Given:** $D = 500$ mm = 0.5 m, $L = 18$ m, $E_p = 27 \times 10^6$ kN/m²

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute Pile Cross-Sectional Area:**
$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.5)^2}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute Slope of Elastic Line:**
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{L}{A_p \cdot E_p} = \dfrac{18}{0.1963 \times 27 \times 10^6}$$
$$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{18}{5.301 \times 10^6} = 3.395 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/kN}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Construct Davisson's Offset Line:**

| $Q$ (kN) | $s_{elastic} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A_p \cdot E_p}$ (mm) | $s_{Davisson} = s_{elastic} + 4$ mm (mm) | $s_{measured}$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 4.00 | 0.0 |
| 300 | 1.02 | 5.02 | 1.2 |
| 600 | 2.04 | 6.04 | 2.5 |
| 900 | 3.06 | 7.06 | 4.5 |
| 1200 | 4.07 | 8.07 | 7.5 |
| 1500 | 5.09 | 9.09 | 12.0 |
| 1800 | 6.11 | 10.11 | 18.5 |
| 2100 | 7.13 | 11.13 | 28.0 |
| 2400 | 8.15 | 12.15 | 41.0 |

**Step 4 (1 mark): Identify Intersection:**

Between $Q = 900$ kN and $Q = 1200$ kN, the Davisson's offset line crosses the measured Q-s curve:
- At $Q = 900$ kN: $s_{Davisson} = 7.06$ mm, $s_{measured} = 4.5$ mm → Davisson line *above* measured curve.
- At $Q = 1200$ kN: $s_{Davisson} = 8.07$ mm, $s_{measured} = 7.5$ mm → Davisson line *above* measured curve.
- At $Q = 1500$ kN: $s_{Davisson} = 9.09$ mm, $s_{measured} = 12.0$ mm → Davisson line *below* measured curve.

**Linear Interpolation between $Q = 1200$ kN and $Q = 1500$ kN:**
$$Q_u = 1200 + \dfrac{(0 - (8.07 - 7.5))}{((9.09 - 12.0) - (8.07 - 7.5))} \times (1500 - 1200)$$
$$Q_u = 1200 + \dfrac{-0.57}{-1.92} \times 300$$
$$Q_u = 1200 + 89.1 = 1289.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Davisson's Ultimate Load $Q_u \approx 1289$ kN.**

**Step 5 (1 mark): Apply IS 2911 Criterion (s = 0.1D):**

Limiting settlement:
$$s_{lim} = 0.1 \times D = 0.1 \times 500 = 50 \text{ mm}$$

Interpolating the measured Q-s curve at $s = 50$ mm (between $Q = 2100$ at 28 mm and $Q = 2400$ at 41 mm — but 50 mm exceeds 41 mm, so we extrapolate):

Using the last two data points $(Q_1, s_1) = (2100, 28)$ and $(Q_2, s_2) = (2400, 41)$:
$$\text{Slope (in this range)} = \dfrac{2400 - 2100}{41 - 28} = \dfrac{300}{13} = 23.08 \text{ kN/mm}$$

At $s = 50$ mm:
$$Q_u = 2400 + (50 - 41) \times 23.08 = 2400 + 207.7 = 2607.7 \text{ kN}$$

**IS 2911 Ultimate Load $Q_u \approx 2608$ kN.**

**Step 6 (1 mark): Allowable Working Load (F_s = 2.5):**

For **conservative design**, use the lower of the two ultimate loads: **$Q_u = 1289$ kN** (Davisson's method):
$$Q_a = \dfrac{1289}{2.5} = 515.6 \text{ kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #1:** Using $\Delta = 0.15$ inch = 3.81 mm instead of 4 mm is acceptable. Using $\Delta = 1.25$ mm (the rock value) for a soil-bearing pile is a major error — the entire offset line will be too low, leading to an **unreasonably high $Q_u$** and an unsafe design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #2:** In Step 5, students often forget to convert 0.1 × D. **0.1 × 500 mm = 50 mm**, NOT 50 cm or 0.5 m. Always express settlement in the **same unit as $D$** in the formula.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #3:** In Step 6, students often use $F_s = 2.0$ instead of 2.5. Per IS 2911 (Part 1) for compression piles in clay with static analysis, the **minimum $F_s$ = 2.5** unless an initial test is conducted and the soil is verified (then $F_s$ = 2.0 is allowed). Read the question carefully.

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice for Internal Option (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Alternative Set]`

**Question B:** A 600 mm diameter, 25 m long precast concrete pile is load-tested in sandy soil. The pile concrete has $E_p = 30 \times 10^6$ kN/m², and the design working load is 800 kN.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Chin-Kondner (Hyperbolic) Method** and the **Tangent Intersection Method** for determining the ultimate load from a pile load test. Compare their relative merits.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the test data below, determine the **ultimate load** by both methods and compute the **allowable working load** (F_s = 2.5).

| Load (kN) | Settlement (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 |
| 400 | 1.0 |
| 800 | 2.5 |
| 1200 | 4.5 |
| 1600 | 7.5 |
| 2000 | 12.0 |
| 2400 | 18.5 |
| 2800 | 28.0 |
| 3200 | 42.0 |
| 3600 | 60.0 |

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory of Two Methods (7 Marks)

**1. Chin-Kondner Hyperbolic Method (1970) — 3.5 marks:**

The Chin-Kondner method models the Q-s curve as a **hyperbola**:
$$Q = \dfrac{s}{m \cdot s + c}$$

Rearranging:
$$\dfrac{s}{Q} = m \cdot s + c$$

This is a **linear equation** in the variables $(s, s/Q)$ with slope $m$ and intercept $c$.

**Plotting Procedure:**
- Plot $\dfrac{s}{Q}$ (vertical axis) vs. $s$ (horizontal axis).
- Fit a straight line through the data points.
- Compute the slope $m$.
- The **ultimate load** is $Q_u = \dfrac{1}{m}$.

**Merits:**
- Useful when only a portion of the Q-s curve (before full failure) is available.
- Provides an objective, mathematical estimate of $Q_u$ without subjective tangent drawing.
- Well-suited for **sandy soils** where the Q-s curve is smooth and gradual.

**Limitations:**
- Sensitive to the data points near failure (a single bad data point can shift the slope significantly).
- May **overestimate** $Q_u$ in clays where the curve is more sharply curved.

**2. Tangent Intersection Method (Mazurkiewicz, 1972) — 3.5 marks:**

This method uses a **graphical construction** on the Q-s curve.

**Procedure:**
- On the Q-s curve, draw a **tangent** along the initial straight (steeper) portion of the curve (this represents the shaft-friction-dominated range).
- Draw a **second tangent** along the final straight (less steep) portion of the curve (this represents the base-resistance-dominated range).
- The **intersection of the two tangents** defines the **yield load (Q_y)**, which is taken as the **ultimate load (Q_u)**.

**Merits:**
- Simple, **graphical** — no complex calculations.
- Clearly identifies the **transition** from shaft-dominated to base-dominated behaviour.

**Limitations:**
- **Subjective** — the result depends on where the student draws the tangents.
- Cannot be applied if the Q-s curve is highly irregular (e.g., for piles in layered soils).

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Chin-Kondner** | **Tangent Intersection** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Analytical | Graphical |
| Subjectivity | Low (mathematical) | High (drawing dependent) |
| Suitability | Sand, stiff clay | Cohesive soils with clear bi-linear Q-s |
| Sensitivity to Data | Moderate | Low |
| Data Beyond Failure Needed? | No | Yes |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Apply Chin-Kondner Method:**

Compute $\dfrac{s}{Q}$ for each data point:

| $Q$ (kN) | $s$ (mm) | $s/Q$ (mm/kN) |
|---|---|---|
| 400 | 1.0 | 0.0025 |
| 800 | 2.5 | 0.003125 |
| 1200 | 4.5 | 0.003750 |
| 1600 | 7.5 | 0.004688 |
| 2000 | 12.0 | 0.006000 |
| 2400 | 18.5 | 0.007708 |
| 2800 | 28.0 | 0.010000 |
| 3200 | 42.0 | 0.013125 |
| 3600 | 60.0 | 0.016667 |

**Linear Regression (Least Squares):**

Let $x = s$ (mm) and $y = s/Q$ (mm/kN). Use the standard regression formula:
$$m = \dfrac{N \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$$

Compute summations ($N = 9$):
- $\sum x = 1.0 + 2.5 + 4.5 + 7.5 + 12.0 + 18.5 + 28.0 + 42.0 + 60.0 = 176.0$
- $\sum y = 0.0025 + 0.003125 + 0.003750 + 0.004688 + 0.006000 + 0.007708 + 0.010000 + 0.013125 + 0.016667 = 0.067563$
- $\sum xy$:
  - (1.0)(0.0025) = 0.0025
  - (2.5)(0.003125) = 0.007813
  - (4.5)(0.003750) = 0.016875
  - (7.5)(0.004688) = 0.035160
  - (12.0)(0.006000) = 0.072000
  - (18.5)(0.007708) = 0.142598
  - (28.0)(0.010000) = 0.280000
  - (42.0)(0.013125) = 0.551250
  - (60.0)(0.016667) = 1.000020
  - $\sum xy = 2.108216$
- $\sum x^2 = 1.0 + 6.25 + 20.25 + 56.25 + 144.0 + 342.25 + 784.0 + 1764.0 + 3600.0 = 6718.0$

Apply formula:
$$m = \dfrac{9 \times 2.108216 - 176.0 \times 0.067563}{9 \times 6718.0 - (176.0)^2}$$
$$m = \dfrac{18.974 - 11.891}{60462.0 - 30976.0}$$
$$m = \dfrac{7.083}{29486.0}$$
$$m = 2.402 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kN}^{-1}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_u = \dfrac{1}{m} = \dfrac{1}{2.402 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4163 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Apply Tangent Intersection Method:**

- **Tangent 1 (Initial Steep Portion):** Through points (400, 1.0) and (1200, 4.5).
  - Slope: $\dfrac{4.5 - 1.0}{1200 - 400} = \dfrac{3.5}{800} = 0.004375$ mm/kN
  - Equation: $s - 1.0 = 0.004375 \cdot (Q - 400)$ → $s = 0.004375 Q - 0.75$

- **Tangent 2 (Final Portion):** Through points (2400, 18.5) and (3600, 60.0).
  - Slope: $\dfrac{60.0 - 18.5}{3600 - 2400} = \dfrac{41.5}{1200} = 0.03458$ mm/kN
  - Equation: $s - 18.5 = 0.03458 \cdot (Q - 2400)$ → $s = 0.03458 Q - 64.49$

- **Intersection:**
$$0.004375 Q - 0.75 = 0.03458 Q - 64.49$$
$$63.74 = 0.03021 Q$$
$$Q_u = \dfrac{63.74}{0.03021} = 2109.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute Allowable Load:**

Using the more conservative Tangent Intersection result:
$$Q_a = \dfrac{Q_u}{F_s} = \dfrac{2109.9}{2.5} = 844 \text{ kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #1:** In the Chin-Kondner method, students often **forget to invert** the slope $m$. The ultimate load is $Q_u = 1/m$ — it is the **inverse** of the slope, not the slope itself. A slope of $2.4 \times 10^{-4}$ gives $Q_u \approx 4167$ kN, NOT 0.00024 kN!

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #2:** In the Tangent Intersection method, students often choose the **wrong points** to define the two tangents. The first tangent should be along the **initial linear-elastic portion** (very small settlements), and the second tangent along the **final steep portion** near failure. Choosing mid-range points will give incorrect $Q_u$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall #3:** Always check the **dimensional consistency** of $m$. The slope of $(s/Q)$ vs. $s$ has units of $(mm/kN) / mm = 1/kN$. The inverse of this slope is in kN, which is the unit of $Q_u$. This is a quick way to verify your answer is correct.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist for "Pile Load Tests – Procedure and Analysis of Results"**

- **Pile Load Test (PLT)** is the most reliable method to determine the *true* ultimate load and load-settlement behaviour of a pile, conducted per **IS 2911 (Part 4) – 2013**.

- **Two Main Types:** *Initial Test* (2.5 × WL or to failure) and *Routine Test* (1.5 × WL). Reversing these is a common exam error.

- **Two Loading Methods:** *Maintained Load (ML)* — slow, step-by-step, with hold periods. *Constant Rate of Penetration (CRP)* — fast, strain-controlled.

- **Reaction System:** Either **Kentledge** (≥ 1.2 × max load) or **Anchor Piles** (≥ 3D spacing, 1.2–1.5 × max load in tension). **Reference beam** supports must be **≥ 3D** from the test pile and **≥ 5D** from anchor piles.

- **Dial Gauges:** Minimum 2 (preferably 4), least count **0.01 mm**, **100 mm travel**, diametrically opposite on a glass plate on the pile head.

- **Load Increments:** **20% of working load** per IS 2911, applied in 2–5 minutes, held until settlement rate stabilizes (clay: **0.1 mm/20 min**; sand: **0.05 mm/20 min**).

- **Ultimate Load Definition (IS 2911):** Load at which the pile **fails to sustain further load**, OR load at settlement $s = 0.1D$, whichever is **less**.

- **Five Major Analysis Methods:**
  1. **IS 2911 Criterion:** $s = 0.1D$
  2. **Davisson's Offset:** Elastic line + 4 mm offset (soil) or 1.25 mm (rock)
  3. **Chin-Kondner:** Hyperbolic fit; $Q_u = 1/m$ from $s/Q$ vs. $s$ plot
  4. **Tangent Intersection:** Intersection of initial and final tangents
  5. **Brinch Hansen:** Bilinear in $s/(1+ks)$ plot

- **Allowable Load:** $Q_a = Q_u / F_s$ with $F_s = 2.0$ to $3.0$ (initial test) and $1.5$ to $2.0$ (routine test). Standard minimum per IS 2911: **F_s = 2.5**.

- **Separation of Shaft and Base Resistance:** Possible via *instrumented piles* (strain gauges at multiple levels) or *O-Cell tests*. Shaft resistance mobilizes at small displacements (2–5 mm in clay, 5–10 mm in sand), base resistance at large displacements (5–10% of D).

- **Pile Compressibility Formula (most-used):** $s_e = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A_p \cdot E_p}$ — required for Davisson's method.

- **Cemented vs. Driven vs. Bored Piles:** The test procedure is similar, but **bored piles** often have a more gradual failure, while **driven piles** in dense sand may show a sharp failure peak.

- **Exam Strategy:** Always show **3 things** in numerical problems — (1) the **formula** with proper units, (2) the **substitution** of values, and (3) the **final answer with units**. Use a **sketch** of the Q-s curve whenever possible.

- **Most-tested Methods in KTU Exams:** IS 2911 (10%D) and Davisson's Offset (numerical), Chin-Kondner and Tangent Intersection (theory + numerical). Cyclic tests and O-cell tests appear in **Part A** (3 marks) more often.

- **ASTM Codes (for reference):** ASTM D1143 (compression), D3689 (tension), D3966 (lateral). These may be asked in MCQ-based exams.

> **Final Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners expect students to **plot the Q-s curve** (or describe it) before applying any analysis method. Always include a clearly labelled **sketch** of the Q-s curve with the **failure point** and the **offset line** (if applicable) to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Sampling – Disturbed and Undisturbed Samples, Representative and Non-representative Samples, Chunk and Tube Samples
topics.content:
# 1. Soil Sampling — Conceptual Foundation ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Soil Sampling** is the controlled, scientific process of extracting a portion of soil from its in-situ (natural) location using standardized tools, samplers, and techniques, for the purpose of laboratory testing, classification, and determination of engineering properties relevant to foundation design, slope stability analysis, and earthwork construction. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):** > *Soil sampling is the procurement of a quantitatively and qualitatively adequate volume of subsurface soil, in either a disturbed or undisturbed state, such that the sample tested in the laboratory is truly representative of the in-situ stratum from which it is drawn.* The four master classification pairs recognized by **IS 1892 (Subsurface Investigation)** and **IS 2131 (Sampling from Boreholes)** are: | Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **Structure preservation** | Disturbed sample | Undisturbed sample | | **Statistical fidelity** | Representative sample | Non-representative sample | | **Geometry of recovery** | Chunk (block) sample | Tube sample | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cake Test" Imagine you are asked to judge the **taste, texture, moisture, and ingredient distribution** of a large multi-layered wedding cake without eating the whole cake. - **Disturbed Sample** → You are given a *spoonful* scooped randomly. You can tell the flavours, the ingredients, the *type of cake*, but you can no longer judge the **layering**, the **air-pocket count**, or the **load-bearing crumb structure**. → Best for **identification and classification**. - **Undisturbed Sample** → A *cylindrical core* is cut carefully with a sharp tube, preserving the original layering, density, moisture, and air voids exactly as in the cake. → Best for **strength, consolidation, and permeability tests**. - **Representative Sample** → The spoonful was taken from a zone that *genuinely reflects* the average cake (e.g., middle layer). → Reliable. - **Non-Representative Sample** → The spoonful was taken only from the *icing on top* or just the *bottom crust*. → Misleading. - **Chunk Sample** → A *cubical block* carved out neatly with a knife — preserves all three-dimensional structure. - **Tube Sample** → A *thin cylindrical straw* pushed in to extract a long core — preserves vertical stratification. ## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Exam-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Numerical Constants & Code References for KTU Board Exams:** > - **Area Ratio (Ar)** of a good undisturbed sampler: **≤ 10%** for soft sensitive clays, **≤ 15%** for stiff clays. > - **Inside Clearance Ratio (ICR)** of a good sampler: **1% to 3%**. > - **Outside Clearance Ratio (OCR)** of a good sampler: **≤ 3%**. > - **Recovery Ratio (RR)** for a perfect sample: **> 95%** (KTU benchmark: ≥ 90% acceptable). > - Governing code: **IS 1892:2021** (Subsurface Investigation), **IS 2131:1981 (Reaffirmed 2017)** (Sampling), **IS 2720 Series** (Laboratory Testing). > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick — "A-I-O-R"** for the four sampler quality ratios: > **A**rea → **I**nside → **O**utside → **R**ecovery. Asked in almost every KTU Board paper. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Soil Sampling Geotechnical engineers cannot economically test the *entire* soil mass beneath a structure. Hence, the philosophy of sampling rests on the principle of **"Informed Inference"**: test a small specimen, infer the behavior of the whole mass. This makes the **fidelity of the sample** the most critical link in the design chain. $$ \text{Design Reliability} \;\propto\; f(\text{Sample Quality}) $$ ## 2.2 Classification of Samples — A Logic Tree The decision logic to classify any sample follows a four-step hierarchy: 1. **Step 1 — Did the sampler disturb the natural structure?** - **YES** → Disturbed Sample - **NO** → Undisturbed Sample 2. **Step 2 — Is the sample's composition a fair statistical image of the parent stratum?** - **YES** → Representative - **NO** → Non-representative 3. **Step 3 — What is the geometric form of the extracted specimen?** - **Block-shaped** → Chunk Sample - **Cylindrical/Linear** → Tube Sample 4. **Step 4 — Match the sample type to the test type.** (Detailed in Section 2.5) ## 2.3 Disturbed Samples — Operational Logic **Definition:** A disturbed sample is one in which the *natural structure*, *void ratio*, *density*, *stress history*, and *particle packing* have been altered, although the *mineralogical composition* and *grain-size distribution* remain essentially intact. **Operational Use Cases:** - Grain-size analysis (Sieve & Hydrometer) - Atterberg Limits (LL, PL, SL) - Specific gravity - Compaction tests (Proctor, Modified Proctor) - Chemical analysis (pH, sulphate, chloride) - Identification & Classification (IS, USCS, AASHTO) **Methods of Recovery (KTU Board favorites):** - Hand-auger cuttings - Power-auger cuttings - Chopping bits and chopping samples from the bottom of boreholes - Wash-bore samples from drilling mud returns - Cuttings from the *cutting shoe* of a *split-spoon* (SPT) sampler - Bag samples from trial pits > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pearl:** A *disturbed sample* can still be *representative* (e.g., a properly bagged cutting from the middle of a clay layer). Conversely, an *undisturbed sample* can be *non-representative* if taken from a localized sand lens inside a clay matrix. The two classifications are **orthogonal**, not mutually exclusive. ## 2.4 Undisturbed Samples — The Engineering Gold Standard **Definition:** An undisturbed sample preserves the **in-situ structure, density, moisture content, stress state, and fabric** of the soil, allowing laboratory measurement of the soil's natural engineering response (strength, deformation, permeability). **Operational Use Cases:** - Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) test - Triaxial Shear Test (UU, CU, CD) - Consolidation Test (Oedometer) - Permeability Test (constant/falling head) - Direct Shear Test (for cohesive soils) - Swell pressure & swell index tests **Sampler Types (KTU Module 4 Focus):** | Sampler | Area Ratio | Best For | Limitations | |---|---|---|---| | **Shelby Tube (Thin-walled)** | < 10% | Soft to medium stiff clays | Cannot cut dense soils or gravel | | **Piston Sampler** | < 10% | Soft sensitive clays below water table | Complex, needs rods | | **Denison Sampler** | 12–15% | Stiff clays, weak rocks | Requires drill rig | | **Pitcher Sampler** | 10–12% | Stiff-to-hard clays | Spring-loaded inner tube | | **Laval Sampler** | 6–10% | Very soft sensitive clays | University/Research sampler | | **Continuous-Flight Auger** | N/A | Disturbed sampling only | Not for undisturbed | ## 2.5 The Match-Making Table — Test vs. Sample Type | Engineering Test | Minimum Required Sample | |---|---| | Grain-size analysis | Disturbed (representative) | | Atterberg Limits | Disturbed (representative) | | Specific gravity | Disturbed | | Proctor Compaction | Disturbed | | UCS | Undisturbed (chunk or tube) | | Triaxial UU / CU / CD | Undisturbed | | Consolidation | Undisturbed | | Permeability | Undisturbed | | Chemical tests | Disturbed | | Swell pressure | Undisturbed | ## 2.6 Representative vs. Non-Representative Samples A **representative sample** is one whose mineralogy, gradation, plasticity, and overall character *mirror the parent stratum from which it is taken*. A **non-representative sample** is biased — it captures only a localized zone, a particular layer, or a biased portion of the stratum. **Common Causes of Non-Representative Sampling:** - Tube sampler passes through a sand lense sandwiched in clay → biased sampling. - Wash-boring returns mix soil from multiple strata. - Loss of fines in drilling mud → underestimates silt/clay content. - Auger flight carries material down from upper layers → contamination. - Sampler jammed in gravel layer → only matrix is recovered. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this table — it accounts for ~70% of numericals on this topic in KTU exams.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Ideal Value (KTU) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Area Ratio (Ar)** | $A_r = \dfrac{D_w^2 - D_e^2}{D_e^2} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 10% (soft clay), ≤ 15% (stiff clay) | | 2 | **Inside Clearance Ratio (ICR)** | $ICR = \dfrac{D_i - D_e}{D_e} \times 100$ | % | 1% – 3% | | 3 | **Outside Clearance Ratio (OCR)** | $OCR = \dfrac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100$ | % | ≤ 3% | | 4 | **Recovery Ratio (RR)** | $RR = \dfrac{L_r}{L_s} \times 100$ | % | > 95% | | 5 | **Length Ratio (LR)** | $LR = \dfrac{L_r}{D_e}$ | — | 5 – 10 (soft clay), 2 – 5 (stiff clay) | | 6 | **Soil Retention Factor** | $SRF = \dfrac{D_i - D_e}{2 \cdot t}$ | — | High for fine soils | | 7 | **Volume of Sample** | $V = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_e^2 \cdot L_r$ | m³ or cm³ | — | | 8 | **Mass of Sample** | $M = \rho_b \cdot V$ | g or kg | — | > [!NOTE] > **Nomenclature** (used in all KTU Board exam diagrams): > - $D_w$ = **Outside (cutting) diameter** of the sampler cutting shoe > - $D_o$ = **Outside diameter** of the sampler tube body > - $D_i$ = **Inside diameter** of the sampler tube body > - $D_e$ = **Inside diameter** at the **cutting edge** (= sample diameter) > - $t$ = Wall thickness of the tube > - $L_s$ = Length of sampler drive (penetration) > - $L_r$ = Length of soil actually recovered > - $\rho_b$ = Bulk density of soil ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Highway & Runway Design** → Undisturbed samples of clay subgrade are tested in oedometer to predict long-term settlement. - **Offshore Oil Platforms** → Piston samplers recover soft marine clay from 100+ m depth. - **Dam Foundations** → Undisturbed tube samples determine shear strength parameters ($c$, $\phi$) for slope stability. - **Liquefaction Studies** → Disturbed but representative samples are reconstituted and tested cyclically. - **Landfill Liners** → Undisturbed samples verify permeability of compacted clay liners. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Area Ratio of a Sampler (Theoretical Foundation) **Geometric Setup:** A thin-walled tube sampler is a hollow cylinder with an outer wall and an inner bore. When pushed into the ground, soil is displaced and compressed *outside* the cutting shoe. The fraction of the cross-section occupied by the displaced soil relative to the recovered soil area is the **Area Ratio (Ar)**. **Geometric Sketch (Mental Model):** ``` Outside annular area = (π/4)(Dw² − De²) ┌──────────────────────┐ ← Outer wall (diameter Dw) │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ RECOVERED │ │ ← Inside sample (diameter De) │ │ SOIL AREA │ │ │ │ = (π/4)De² │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────┘ ``` **Derivation:** $$ A_r \;=\; \frac{\text{Area of soil displaced (annulus)}}{\text{Area of soil recovered (inner core)}} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\bigl(D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}\bigr)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 $$ The $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ cancels out (Step 2 of simplification): $$ A_r \;=\; \frac{D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}}{D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 $$ **Why this matters:** A *high* Ar means more soil is being remoulded and pushed aside during sampling, causing greater disturbance. A *low* Ar implies minimal disturbance → higher quality sample. Hence, **IS 2131 specifies Ar ≤ 10% for soft sensitive clays** to preserve their natural structure. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inside Clearance Ratio (ICR) The ICR prevents **wall friction** from compressing the sample as it enters the tube. A small inside clearance ensures the sample is not dragged along the wall. $$ ICR \;=\; \frac{D_i - D_e}{D_e} \times 100 $$ **Logical Reasoning:** - $D_i > D_e$ by design (a small step is machined at the cutting edge). - If ICR = 0 → sample is gripped by the wall → severe disturbance. - If ICR > 3% → sample slides loosely → disturbance during retrieval. - **Sweet spot = 1% to 3%** (IS 2131). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Outside Clearance Ratio (OCR) $$ OCR \;=\; \frac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100 $$ **Logical Reasoning:** A small external relief prevents soil from gripping the *outside* of the tube as the sampler is withdrawn. Typical value: **< 3%**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recovery Ratio (RR) $$ RR \;=\; \frac{L_r}{L_s} \times 100 $$ where - $L_r$ = Length of soil actually recovered in the tube. - $L_s$ = Length of sampler penetration (drive length). **Interpretation Matrix:** | RR (%) | Quality | Likely Cause of Loss | |---|---|---| | > 95 | Excellent | None — ideal sampling | | 85 – 95 | Good | Minor friction | | 70 – 85 | Fair | Wall friction, partial entry | | < 70 | Poor | Sampler jammed, gravel obstruction, loss of cohesion | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Trick:** If a question gives you $L_s = 600\text{ mm}$ and $L_r = 510\text{ mm}$, the recovery is **85%**, which is *acceptable* but indicates slight disturbance — not "excellent". ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Length Ratio (LR) $$ LR \;=\; \frac{L_r}{D_e} $$ This is the **slenderness ratio** of the recovered core. Too long → sample buckles inside the tube. Too short → not enough material for testing. - **Soft clays:** LR = 5 to 10 (long, slender samples) - **Stiff clays:** LR = 2 to 5 (shorter to avoid crumbling) ## 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Pattern > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A Shelby tube sampler has outside diameter $D_w = 101.6\text{ mm}$, inside diameter $D_i = 99.0\text{ mm}$, and cutting-edge diameter $D_e = 98.4\text{ mm}$. The sampler was driven 600 mm and recovered 560 mm of soil. The bulk density of the recovered soil is $1.85\text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate: > 1. Area Ratio (Ar) > 2. Inside Clearance Ratio (ICR) > 3. Outside Clearance Ratio (OCR) — assume body OD = 100 mm > 4. Recovery Ratio (RR) > 5. Mass of the sample (in grams) > > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern, 14-mark question type]** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Area Ratio:** $$ A_r = \frac{D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}}{D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{(101.6)^2 - (98.4)^2}{(98.4)^2} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{10322.56 - 9682.56}{9682.56} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{640.00}{9682.56} \times 100 \;\approx\; 6.61\% $$ **Step 2 — Inside Clearance Ratio:** $$ ICR = \frac{D_i - D_e}{D_e} \times 100 = \frac{99.0 - 98.4}{98.4} \times 100 = \frac{0.6}{98.4} \times 100 \;\approx\; 0.61\% $$ **Step 3 — Outside Clearance Ratio:** $$ OCR = \frac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100 = \frac{101.6 - 100.0}{100.0} \times 100 = 1.6\% $$ **Step 4 — Recovery Ratio:** $$ RR = \frac{L_r}{L_s} \times 100 = \frac{560}{600} \times 100 \;\approx\; 93.33\% $$ **Step 5 — Mass of the Sample:** $$ V = \frac{\pi}{4} D_e^{\,2} \cdot L_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (9.84)^2 \times 56.0 \text{ cm}^3 $$ $$ V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 96.83 \times 56.0 = 0.7854 \times 96.83 \times 56.0 \;\approx\; 4258.7 \text{ cm}^3 $$ $$ M = \rho_b \cdot V = 1.85 \times 4258.7 \;\approx\; 7878.6 \text{ g} \;\approx\; 7.88 \text{ kg} $$ **Final Answer Table:** | Quantity | Value | Verdict (per IS 2131) | |---|---|---| | Ar | 6.61% | Excellent (≤ 10% for soft clays) | | ICR | 0.61% | Slightly low (1–3% ideal) | | OCR | 1.6% | Excellent (≤ 3%) | | RR | 93.33% | Good (> 90%) | | Mass | 7.88 kg | Sufficient for lab testing | ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Sample-Type Selection Heuristic | Soil Type | Recommended Sampler | Sample Category | Test Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Soft marine clay | Piston / Laval | Undisturbed tube | Consolidation, strength | | Medium stiff clay | Shelby tube | Undisturbed tube | Triaxial, UCS, Oedometer | | Stiff fissured clay | Denison / Pitcher | Undisturbed tube | Shear strength | | Loose sand below WT | Split-spoon (SPT) | Disturbed | Relative density (N-value) | | Dense gravelly sand | Bulk auger cuttings | Disturbed | Gradation, Proctor | | Organic peat | Piston with large ICR | Undisturbed tube | Compressibility | | Residual laterite | Chunk / hand-carved | Undisturbed chunk | Triaxial with relict structure | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Q:** Distinguish between *disturbed* and *undisturbed* soil samples. Give two examples each of laboratory tests performed on them. **Model Answer (Board-Examiner Wording):** A **disturbed sample** is one in which the *natural structure* of the soil has been altered during sampling, but its mineralogical composition and grain-size distribution remain intact. Such samples are obtained using augers, chopping bits, and wash-bore returns. They are used for **grain-size analysis (sieve/hydrometer)**, **Atterberg limits (LL, PL)**, **specific gravity**, and **compaction tests**. An **undisturbed sample** preserves the *in-situ density, moisture content, structure, and stress history* of the soil. These are obtained using Shelby tubes, piston samplers, or by carving chunk samples. They are used for **Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS)**, **Triaxial Shear Test (UU, CU, CD)**, **Consolidation (Oedometer)**, and **Permeability tests**. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Q:** Define *Area Ratio* of a soil sampler. What is its significance in sampling? **Model Answer:** The **Area Ratio (Ar)** of a soil sampler is defined as the ratio of the *annular cross-sectional area of soil displaced by the sampler walls* to the *cross-sectional area of the soil sample actually recovered*, expressed as a percentage: $$ A_r = \frac{D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}}{D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 $$ **Significance:** A high area ratio indicates excessive remoulding and disturbance of the soil during sampling, leading to unreliable strength and compressibility measurements. As per **IS 2131**, the area ratio should be **≤ 10% for soft sensitive clays** and **≤ 15% for stiff clays** to ensure sample integrity. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]` **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the classification of soil samples into *disturbed* and *undisturbed* categories. Discuss the engineering tests for which each type is suitable, with reference to IS 1892 and IS 2131. **Q (b)** [7 Marks] A thin-walled Shelby tube sampler has $D_w = 102\text{ mm}$, $D_o = 100\text{ mm}$, $D_i = 99\text{ mm}$, and $D_e = 98.4\text{ mm}$. The sampler is driven 600 mm into a soft clay stratum and recovers 552 mm of soil. Comment on the quality of the sample using all four standard ratios. Take bulk density $\rho_b = 1.78\text{ g/cm}^3$. --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Classification of Soil Samples (3 marks):** Soil samples are broadly classified into two categories based on the preservation of their natural structure: 1. **Disturbed Samples:** These samples experience a change in the natural structure, density, void ratio, and stress state of the soil during extraction. The mineral composition and grain-size distribution, however, remain unchanged. They are collected using hand augers, mechanical augers, chopping bits, split-spoons (SPT), or by wash-boring. The recovered soil is typically stored in airtight bags or jars and labelled with depth, borehole number, and project details. 2. **Undisturbed Samples:** These samples retain the *in-situ* fabric, density, moisture content, pore-water pressure, and stress history of the parent soil. They are obtained by carefully pushing thin-walled tubes (Shelby), piston samplers, or by hand-carving chunk samples from trial pits. **Suitability of Tests (3 marks):** | Sample Type | Tests Performed | Reason | |---|---|---| | Disturbed | Sieve analysis, Hydrometer, Atterberg Limits, Specific gravity, Proctor Compaction, Chemical tests | Tests require only the soil's compositional and index properties | | Undisturbed | UCS, Triaxial (UU, CU, CD), Direct Shear (cohesive), Consolidation (Oedometer), Permeability, Swell pressure | Tests require in-situ structure, density, and stress state | **Code References (1 mark):** **IS 1892:2021** prescribes the procedure for subsurface investigation. **IS 2131:1981 (Reaffirmed 2017)** prescribes the method of sampling from boreholes. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution **Given:** - $D_w = 102\text{ mm}$ (cutting-edge outer diameter) - $D_o = 100\text{ mm}$ (tube body outer diameter) - $D_i = 99\text{ mm}$ (tube body inner diameter) - $D_e = 98.4\text{ mm}$ (cutting-edge inner diameter) - $L_s = 600\text{ mm}$ (sampler drive length) - $L_r = 552\text{ mm}$ (recovered length) - $\rho_b = 1.78\text{ g/cm}^3$ **Step 1 — Area Ratio (Ar):** $$ A_r = \frac{D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}}{D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{(102)^2 - (98.4)^2}{(98.4)^2} \times 100 = \frac{10404 - 9682.56}{9682.56} \times 100 $$ $$ A_r = \frac{721.44}{9682.56} \times 100 \;\approx\; 7.45\% $$ **Verdict:** Ar = 7.45% **≤ 10% → Excellent** for soft clay (per IS 2131). No remoulding concern. (1.5 marks) **Step 2 — Inside Clearance Ratio (ICR):** $$ ICR = \frac{D_i - D_e}{D_e} \times 100 = \frac{99 - 98.4}{98.4} \times 100 = 0.61\% $$ **Verdict:** ICR = 0.61% is **slightly below the 1–3% ideal** but acceptable; the sample may experience minor wall friction. (1 mark) **Step 3 — Outside Clearance Ratio (OCR):** $$ OCR = \frac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100 = \frac{102 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 2.0\% $$ **Verdict:** OCR = 2.0% **< 3% → Excellent**, no external soil gripping. (0.5 mark) **Step 4 — Recovery Ratio (RR):** $$ RR = \frac{L_r}{L_s} \times 100 = \frac{552}{600} \times 100 = 92.0\% $$ **Verdict:** RR = 92% **> 90% → Good**; minor loss possibly due to friction or end-bulging. (1 mark) **Step 5 — Mass of the Sample (Supplementary):** $$ V = \frac{\pi}{4} (9.84\text{ cm})^2 \times 55.2\text{ cm} \;\approx\; 4197.5\text{ cm}^3 $$ $$ M = 1.78 \times 4197.5 \;\approx\; 7471.5\text{ g} \;\approx\; 7.47\text{ kg} $$ **Overall Verdict (1 mark):** The sample is of **GOOD quality** and suitable for laboratory tests such as UCS, Triaxial, and Consolidation. Minor improvement possible by increasing ICR to 1% via re-design of the cutting shoe. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]` **Q (a)** [7 Marks] What are *representative* and *non-representative* samples? With the help of neat sketches, describe the construction and working of (i) a Shelby tube sampler and (ii) a Piston sampler. Indicate the soil types for which each is best suited. **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the concept of *chunk samples* in detail. Compare the relative merits and demerits of chunk samples versus tube samples for laboratory testing of cohesive soils. --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Representative vs Non-Representative Samples (2 marks):** A **representative sample** is one whose composition, gradation, plasticity, and engineering character faithfully reflect the parent stratum from which it is drawn. A **non-representative sample** is biased — it captures only a localized or atypical portion of the stratum (e.g., a wash-bore return mixing sand and clay from multiple depths). **(i) Shelby Tube Sampler (3 marks):** A Shelby tube is a **thin-walled seamless steel tube** (typically 75–100 mm diameter, 600–900 mm long) with a sharpened cutting edge at the bottom and a drive head at the top. The tube is pushed into the soil at a slow, uniform rate using a hydraulic jack or the dead weight of the drilling rig — **no hammering is used** because dynamic force would disturb sensitive soils. As the tube advances, a continuous cylindrical core enters the inside of the tube. After penetration, the tube is withdrawn, the ends are sealed with molten wax, and the sample is transported vertically to the laboratory. - **Best suited for:** Soft to medium stiff cohesive soils (clays, silts) above the water table. - **Area Ratio:** < 10% (ideal). - **Drawback:** Cannot penetrate dense sand, gravel, or stiff clays. **(ii) Piston Sampler (2 marks):** The piston sampler consists of a Shelby-type tube with a **piston (plug) initially positioned at the cutting tip**. The piston is held stationary at the bottom of the borehole by a rod extending to the surface. The tube is then pushed *down past the stationary piston*, sucking the soil into the tube. The piston prevents the entry of drilling fluid and the loss of fine particles, and provides continuous hydrostatic support at the cutting face — eliminating "suck-back" disturbance. - **Best suited for:** Very soft, sensitive, normally consolidated clays *below* the water table. - **Example:** Hvorslev piston sampler, Norwegian Geotechnical Institute sampler. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Chunk Samples — Concept (2 marks):** A **chunk sample** is a block of soil carved carefully by hand from the base or wall of a trial pit, or from the bottom of a borehole using a specialized ring. The block is typically **20–40 cm on each side** and preserves the *three-dimensional in-situ fabric, fissures, joints, roots, and macro-features* of the soil. The sample is immediately wrapped in muslin cloth, coated with paraffin wax, and transported in a padded box to prevent vibration damage. **Comparison: Chunk vs Tube Samples (5 marks):** | Criterion | Chunk Sample | Tube Sample | |---|---|---| | **Sample Size** | Large (20–40 cm block) | Small cylinder (75–100 mm dia, 600 mm long) | | **In-situ structure preserved** | Excellent — full 3D | Good — 1D vertical core | | **Fissures & macro-features** | Fully preserved | May be cut through | | **Disturbance risk** | Low if careful | Moderate (wall friction) | | **Cost & effort** | High (trial pit + skilled labour) | Low (drill rig + tube) | | **Depth limitation** | Shallow (< 5–6 m) | Any depth with drill rig | | **Best suited for** | Stiff fissured clays, residual soils, weathered rocks | Soft-to-medium clays, silts | | **Larger laboratory specimens** | Yes (multiple tests from one block) | Limited by tube length | | **Standardization** | Less standardized | Highly standardized (IS 2131) | | **Transportation** | Difficult (heavy, fragile) | Easy (sealed tube) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:** > 1. **Confusing "disturbed" with "non-representative".** These are *orthogonal* classifications. A sample can be disturbed yet fully representative (e.g., a properly bagged auger cutting from a uniform clay layer). > 2. **Forgetting to express Area Ratio in percentage.** Students write the decimal value (0.0745) instead of 7.45%. Always multiply by 100. > 3. **Mixing up ICR and OCR.** ICR uses $D_i$ and $D_e$ (inside diameters). OCR uses $D_w$ and $D_o$ (outside diameters). This is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in numerical problems. > 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to cm** (or vice versa) before computing volume. A 1 cm mistake gives 30% error in mass. > 5. **Claiming "undisturbed" means "no disturbance at all".** A truly undisturbed sample is an *idealization*; in practice we say "minimum disturbance". Use this phrasing to earn full credit. > 6. **Omitting the code reference** (IS 1892, IS 2131, IS 2720). Examiners award at least 0.5–1 mark for citing the relevant code. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Save this section — it is your one-page summary before walking into the KTU exam hall.** ✅ **Soil Sampling** is the procurement of a soil specimen (disturbed or undisturbed) such that lab tests yield meaningful inference about the parent stratum. ✅ **Disturbed Sample** = structure altered, composition intact → use for *index properties* (sieve, Atterberg, SG, Proctor). ✅ **Undisturbed Sample** = structure, density, moisture, stress preserved → use for *engineering properties* (UCS, Triaxial, Oedometer, Permeability). ✅ **Representative Sample** = faithful image of parent stratum. **Non-Representative** = biased, localized, or contaminated. ✅ **Chunk Sample** = hand-carved block (20–40 cm) from trial pit, preserves 3D fabric, fissures, and macro-features; best for stiff/residual soils. ✅ **Tube Sample** = cylindrical core from Shelby, Piston, Denison, or Pitcher sampler; best for soft-to-medium clays at any depth. ✅ **Four Critical Sampler Ratios** to memorize: $$ A_r = \frac{D_w^{\,2} - D_e^{\,2}}{D_e^{\,2}} \times 100 \;\leq\; 10\% \text{ to } 15\% $$ $$ ICR = \frac{D_i - D_e}{D_e} \times 100 = 1\% \text{ to } 3\% $$ $$ OCR = \frac{D_w - D_o}{D_o} \times 100 \;\leq\; 3\% $$ $$ RR = \frac{L_r}{L_s} \times 100 \;\geq\; 90\% $$ ✅ **Code References (must cite in KTU exam):** IS 1892 (Subsurface Investigation), IS 2131 (Sampling), IS 2720 Series (Lab Testing). ✅ **Sampler–Soil Matching Rule of Thumb:** - Soft clay below WT → **Piston** (Ar < 10%) - Medium clay → **Shelby tube** (Ar < 10%) - Stiff clay / weak rock → **Denison** or **Pitcher** (Ar 10–15%) - Sand & gravel → **Auger cuttings** (disturbed) or **SPT** (N-value) - Residual / fissured soil → **Chunk sample** ✅ **Memory Aid — "A-I-O-R"** = Area → Inside → Outside → Recovery. State in this order in numericals. ✅ **Golden Phrase for KTU answers:** *"Sample quality is judged by the Area Ratio, Inside Clearance Ratio, Outside Clearance Ratio, and Recovery Ratio, all in accordance with IS 2131."*

topics.title: Area Ratio, Clearance, Outside Clearance, and Recovery Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Area Ratio, Clearance, Outside Clearance, and Recovery Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Sampler Quality Parameters

In advanced geotechnical investigation, the quality of a soil sample retrieved from the subsurface directly governs the reliability of laboratory test results used for design of foundations, slopes, and earth-retaining structures. To quantify how *disturbed* or *undisturbed* a sample is, KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with IS 2132 and ASTM D1587) prescribes four critical geometric performance indices for any cylindrical tube sampler:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Sample Quality Assessment**
> 1. **Area Ratio (Ar)** — quantifies *wall friction* and *compaction* disturbance during penetration.
> 2. **Inside Clearance (Ci)** — accommodates *elastic expansion* of the soil after sampling.
> 3. **Outside Clearance (Co)** — reduces *wall adhesion* and friction along the outer surface.
> 4. **Recovery Ratio (Rr)** — measures *physical completeness* of the sample retrieved.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

### A. Area Ratio (Ar)
The **Area Ratio** is defined as the ratio of the *maximum cross-sectional area of the sampler wall* (the annular soil displaced during penetration) to the *cross-sectional area of the soil sample at the cutting edge*, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{Area Ratio} = \frac{\text{Area of sampler wall}}{\text{Area of sample}} \times 100\%$$

### B. Inside Clearance Ratio (Ci)
The **Inside Clearance Ratio** is the percentage ratio of the *inside clearance volume allowance* to the *cross-sectional area of the sample*, designed to relieve elastic rebound and prevent sample compression inside the tube.

### C. Outside Clearance Ratio (Co)
The **Outside Clearance Ratio** is the percentage ratio of the *outside clearance gap* to the *outside diameter of the sample*, intended to reduce frictional resistance and adhesion on the outer wall of the sampler.

### D. Recovery Ratio (Rr)
The **Recovery Ratio** is the percentage of the *actual length of sample recovered* relative to the *length of sampler penetration* into the soil.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw and Cake" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pushing a straw vertically into a soft cake.
> - The **Area Ratio** is like how *thick* the straw wall is compared to the cake column inside — the thicker the wall, the more cake you crush and *disturb* as the straw enters.
> - The **Inside Clearance** is the *tiny gap* you leave inside the straw so that, when the cake is pushed upward, it can *expand slightly sideways* without jamming the straw.
> - The **Outside Clearance** is the *slight taper* on the straw's exterior so it slides in smoothly without scraping the surrounding cake against the wall.
> - The **Recovery Ratio** is simply *how much cake actually came up* with the straw versus how deep you pushed it in.

If you push a thick-walled straw too far, the cake is crushed (high Ar). If the inside gap is too small, the cake gets *squeezed* and distorted. If too large, the cake falls apart. Hence, every parameter must lie in a *balanced design range* prescribed by IS 2132.

---

## 1.4 Key Geometric Parameters of a Sampler

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Unit |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| $D_o$ | Outside diameter of sampler cutting edge | mm |
| $D_i$ | Inside diameter of sampler cutting edge | mm |
| $D_c$ | Inside diameter of sampling tube (at top) | mm |
| $D_t$ | Outside diameter of sample (after recovery) | mm |
| $L_r$ | Length of sample recovered | mm |
| $L_p$ | Length of penetration | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Benchmark (IS 2132 / ASTM D1587):**
> - Area Ratio for soft sensitive clays: **< 10%**
> - Area Ratio for stiff clays: **< 15%**
> - Inside Clearance: **0.5% – 3%**
> - Recovery Ratio target: **> 95%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Reasoning Behind Each Ratio

### 2.1.1 Why Area Ratio Matters — The "Compaction & Disturbance" Logic

When a sampler is driven into the ground, soil in front of the cutting tip is displaced *radially outward* and *axially downward*. The thicker the wall, the greater the volume of soil that gets remoulded. This remoulded zone forms a *disturbed annulus* around the sample, altering the in-situ stress state, fabric, and structure.

A high $A_r$ causes:
- Excessive compaction in cohesive soils
- Loss of natural moisture content
- Smearing along the wall
- Altered shear strength parameters

A low $A_r$ (achieved by thin walls and sharp cutting edges) minimizes:
- Remoulding
- Volume change
- Friction-induced heating

---

### 2.1.2 Why Inside Clearance is Required — The "Elastic Rebound" Logic

Once the soil enters the sampler tube, it experiences *stress relief*. The effective stress drops from in-situ to atmospheric. Clay samples tend to *swell elastically* (especially saturated clays). If no clearance is provided:
- The sample is compressed axially
- Lateral expansion is restricted
- The sample may be remoulded by friction along the inner wall

A small inside clearance (typically 0.5%–3%) provides a *reservoir* of space for the sample to expand radially without distress, while still maintaining lateral confinement.

---

### 2.1.3 Why Outside Clearance is Required — The "Friction Reduction" Logic

The outside wall of the sampler experiences significant *skin friction* during penetration and extraction. Without outside clearance:
- Soil adheres to the outer wall
- Extraction force increases dramatically
- Sample may be sheared during withdrawal

A small outside taper (typically 0.5%–2%) reduces this adhesion and ensures smooth retrieval.

---

### 2.1.4 Why Recovery Ratio Matters — The "Sample Completeness" Logic

A recovery ratio less than 100% indicates that the sampler did *not capture* the full column of soil it penetrated. This can occur due to:
- Sample compression (high Ar, low Ci)
- Loss of cohesion in loose sands
- Plugging of the sampler tip in stiff clays
- Jamming during extraction

A low recovery ratio invalidates the sample for laboratory tests such as consolidation, unconfined compression, or triaxial testing.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA BOX** — Memorize these four equations. They are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Typical Range |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Area Ratio** | $A_r = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\%$ | $D_o$ = outside diameter, $D_i$ = inside diameter (at cutting edge) | **< 10%** (soft clay), **< 15%** (stiff clay) |
| 2 | **Inside Clearance** | $C_i = \dfrac{D_i - D_c}{D_c} \times 100\%$ | $D_c$ = diameter of recovered sample (or top of tube) | **0.5% – 3%** |
| 3 | **Outside Clearance** | $C_o = \dfrac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\%$ | $D_t$ = outside diameter of sample | **0.5% – 2%** |
| 4 | **Recovery Ratio** | $R_r = \dfrac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\%$ | $L_r$ = length recovered, $L_p$ = length penetrated | **> 95%** (acceptable), **> 100%** (heave) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Trap:** All diameters must be in the **same unit** (typically mm) before substitution. KTU examiners frequently use mixed units to test unit-awareness.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Critical Parameter | Consequence of Violation |
|-------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Offshore site investigation | Area Ratio | Erratic consolidation settlement in clay |
| Liquefaction assessment | Recovery Ratio | Underestimation of fines content |
| Slope stability analysis | Inside Clearance | Overestimation of shear strength |
| Earth dam foundation | Outside Clearance | Sample loss during extraction |
| Tunnel design | All four | Misleading soil profile & parameters |

---

## 2.4 Concept Map — How the Ratios Interact

- **Ar ↓** → less compaction → **Rr ↑**
- **Ci ↑** (within range) → less compression → **Rr ↑**
- **Co ↑** → easier extraction → **Rr ↑**
- **Ar ↑** (excessive) → sample compression → **Rr ↓**
- **Ci = 0** → sample jammed → **Rr ↓**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU frequently tests whether students understand that *all four ratios are interdependent*. A poorly designed sampler with high Ar will never achieve high Rr, regardless of clearance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Area Ratio (Ar)

### Starting Concept
Consider a cylindrical sampler with **outside diameter $D_o$** and **inside diameter $D_i$** at the cutting edge. The soil sample has a circular cross-section of diameter $D_i$.

### Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Sample

$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_i^2$$

*This is the area of soil inside the sampler that becomes the sample.*

### Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area of the Sampler Wall

The wall is the annular region between $D_o$ and $D_i$:

$$A_w = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D_o^2 - D_i^2)$$

*This is the area of soil that is *displaced and remoulded* during penetration.*

### Step 3 — Forming the Ratio

By definition, Area Ratio is the wall area to sample area:

$$A_r = \frac{A_w}{A_s} \times 100\%$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D_i^2} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \left[\left(\frac{D_o}{D_i}\right)^2 - 1\right] \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note: The factor $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ cancels out cleanly because both areas share the same geometric constant.*

### Step 5 — Worked Example

> **Problem:** A sampler has $D_o = 100\,\text{mm}$ and $D_i = 95\,\text{mm}$. Compute the Area Ratio and comment on its suitability for soft sensitive clay.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{100^2 - 95^2}{95^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10000 - 9025}{9025} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{975}{9025} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 10.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Comment:** $A_r = 10.80\%$ **exceeds** the 10% limit prescribed for soft sensitive clays. This sampler is **unsuitable** for soft sensitive clays but **acceptable** for stiff clays (limit < 15%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Inside Clearance Ratio (Ci)

### Step 1 — Identify Geometric Components
- $D_i$ = inside diameter of the sampler cutting edge
- $D_c$ = inside diameter at the top of the sample tube (or diameter of sample at the top)

### Step 2 — Express the Clearance Gap

The gap available for elastic expansion:

$$\Delta D = D_c - D_i$$

*(Note: In some texts, $D_c$ refers to the inside diameter of the *sample*, post-recovery, in which case the gap is $D_i - D_c$ depending on sign convention. The KTU convention is shown below.)*

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio Relative to Sample Diameter

Using the sample diameter $D_c$ as the reference (IS 2132 convention):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \frac{D_c - D_i}{D_c} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{D_i}{D_c}\right) \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

> **Problem:** A thin-wall Shelby tube has cutting edge inside diameter $D_i = 70.0\,\text{mm}$ and the tube's inside diameter at the top is $D_c = 70.5\,\text{mm}$. Compute $C_i$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \frac{70.5 - 70.0}{70.0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.5}{70.0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 0.714\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $C_i = 0.714\%$ falls within the prescribed range of **0.5% – 3%**, confirming that the tube design is appropriate for undisturbed sampling of soft clays.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Outside Clearance Ratio (Co)

### Step 1 — Identify Geometric Components
- $D_o$ = outside diameter of the sampler at the cutting edge
- $D_t$ = outside diameter of the sample after recovery (or outside diameter at the top of the drive shoe)

### Step 2 — Express the Gap

$$\Delta D_o = D_o - D_t$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_o &= \frac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{D_t}{D_o}\right) \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

> **Problem:** A piston sampler has $D_o = 80\,\text{mm}$ and the outside diameter at the top of the drive shoe is $D_t = 79.2\,\text{mm}$. Compute $C_o$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_o &= \frac{80 - 79.2}{80} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.8}{80} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 1.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $C_o = 1.00\%$ lies within the typical range of **0.5% – 2%**, indicating adequate outside clearance for reducing wall friction during extraction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Recovery Ratio (Rr)

### Step 1 — Identify Length Parameters
- $L_p$ = length of penetration of the sampler into the soil
- $L_r$ = length of soil sample actually recovered inside the tube

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example

> **Problem:** A sampler is driven 600 mm into a soft clay layer. Upon extraction, the recovered sample length is measured as 585 mm. Compute $R_r$ and judge sample acceptability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= \frac{585}{600} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 97.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $R_r = 97.5\% > 95\%$, so the sample is **acceptable** for laboratory testing. If $R_r > 100\%$, it indicates *heave* (sample swelling) — also valid for sensitive clays.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sample Classification Based on Recovery Ratio

| Recovery Ratio (%) | Quality | Action Required |
|-------------------|---------|-----------------|
| $R_r < 70\%$ | **Poor** — significant loss | Reject sample, re-bore |
| $70\% \leq R_r < 90\%$ | **Fair** — partial loss | Note disturbance in report |
| $90\% \leq R_r \leq 105\%$ | **Good** — acceptable | Proceed with lab tests |
| $R_r > 105\%$ | **Heave / Swelling** | Acceptable for soft sensitive clays; report heave value |

---

## 3.6 Combined Problem — Design Verification

> **Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Standard):**
> A thin-walled tube sampler for soft sensitive clay has the following dimensions:
> - Outside diameter $D_o = 75.0\,\text{mm}$
> - Inside diameter at cutting edge $D_i = 72.5\,\text{mm}$
> - Inside diameter at top of tube $D_c = 73.0\,\text{mm}$
> - Outside diameter at top of drive shoe $D_t = 74.0\,\text{mm}$
> - Penetration depth $L_p = 450\,\text{mm}$
> - Recovered sample length $L_r = 442\,\text{mm}$
>
> Compute all four ratios and judge whether the sampler is suitable for soft sensitive clay sampling.

**Solution:**

### (a) Area Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{75.0^2 - 72.5^2}{72.5^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5625 - 5256.25}{5256.25} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{368.75}{5256.25} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 7.015\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** $A_r = 7.02\% < 10\%$ — **PASSES** for soft sensitive clay.

### (b) Inside Clearance

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \frac{D_c - D_i}{D_c} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{73.0 - 72.5}{72.5} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.5}{72.5} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 0.690\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** $C_i = 0.69\%$ — within **0.5%–3%** — **PASSES**.

### (c) Outside Clearance

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_o &= \frac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{75.0 - 74.0}{75.0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.0}{75.0} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 1.333\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** $C_o = 1.33\%$ — within **0.5%–2%** — **PASSES**.

### (d) Recovery Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{442}{450} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 98.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** $R_r = 98.22\% > 95\%$ — **PASSES**.

**Final Conclusion:** The sampler is *fully suitable* for soft sensitive clay sampling. All four design criteria are satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sampler Cross-Section — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *functional geometry* of a standard thin-walled tube sampler, mapping the dimensional parameters that govern the four ratios.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SOIL SURFACE - GROUND LEVEL"] --> B["DRIVE CAP"]
    B --> C["SAMPLER BODY - TUBE WALL"]
    C --> D["SAMPLER TIP - CUTTING EDGE"]
    D --> E["SOIL SAMPLE - RECOVERED CORE"]
    D --> F["REMOVED SOIL - DISPLACED ANNULUS"]

    G["PARAMETER Do - OUTSIDE DIAMETER"] -.-> C
    H["PARAMETER Di - INSIDE DIAMETER"] -.-> C
    I["PARAMETER Dc - TUBE INSIDE TOP"] -.-> C
    J["PARAMETER Dt - DRIVE SHOE OUTSIDE"] -.-> D
    K["PARAMETER Lp - PENETRATION LENGTH"] -.-> D
    L["PARAMETER Lr - RECOVERED LENGTH"] -.-> E

    M["AREA RATIO Ar - WALL TO SAMPLE"] --> N["DISTURBANCE INDICATOR"]
    O["INSIDE CLEARANCE Ci - ELASTIC SWELL"] --> P["SAMPLE INTEGRITY"]
    Q["OUTSIDE CLEARANCE Co - WALL FRICTION"] --> R["EXTRACTION QUALITY"]
    S["RECOVERY RATIO Rr - SAMPLE COMPLETENESS"] --> T["LABORATORY VALIDITY"]

    F --> M
    H --> M
    G --> M
    H --> O
    I --> O
    G --> Q
    J --> Q
    E --> S
    K --> S
    L --> S

    M --> N
    O --> P
    Q --> R
    S --> T

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```

---

## 4.2 Cylindrical Geometry Schematic — Wall vs Sample Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OUTER CIRCLE - Do"] --> B["ANNULAR WALL ZONE"]
    B --> C["INNER CIRCLE - Di"]
    C --> D["SAMPLE CORE ZONE"]

    E["ANNULAR AREA = pi/4 x Do squared minus Di squared"] -.-> B
    F["SAMPLE AREA = pi/4 x Di squared"] -.-> D
    G["Ar = ANNULAR AREA divided by SAMPLE AREA x 100"] -.-> B
    H["MINIMIZE Ar FOR LESS DISTURBANCE"] -.-> B

    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style B fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style C fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — How Ratios Influence Sample Quality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SAMPLER DESIGN INPUTS - Do, Di, Dc, Dt"] --> B["COMPUTE FOUR RATIOS"]
    B --> C{"AR LESS THAN 10 PERCENT"}

    C -->|NO| E["REJECT - HIGH DISTURBANCE RISK"]

    B --> F{"Ci IN 0.5 TO 3 PERCENT"}
    F -->|YES| G["ADEQUATE ELASTIC RELIEF"]
    F -->|NO| H["ADJUST TUBE DIMENSIONS"]

    B --> I{"Co IN 0.5 TO 2 PERCENT"}
    I -->|YES| J["SMOOTH EXTRACTION EXPECTED"]
    I -->|NO| K["MODIFY DRIVE SHOE TAPER"]

    B --> L{"Rr GREATER THAN 95 PERCENT"}
    L -->|YES| M["SAMPLE ACCEPTED FOR LAB TESTS"]
    L -->|NO| N["SAMPLE REJECTED - RE-BORE REQUIRED"]

    J --> O
    M --> O

    K --> P

```

---

