<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define Area Ratio of a soil sampler. State its recommended value for sampling soft sensitive clays as per IS 2132.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Area Ratio ($A_r$)** of a soil sampler is defined as the ratio of the *cross-sectional area of the sampler wall* to the *cross-sectional area of the soil sample* at the cutting edge, expressed as a percentage.

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\%$$

As per **IS 2132**, the recommended maximum value of Area Ratio for sampling **soft sensitive clays is < 10%**, while for stiff clays it is **< 15%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point (1 mark):** A lower area ratio indicates less soil disturbance during penetration, leading to higher sample quality.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is meant by Recovery Ratio? List the factors affecting it.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Recovery Ratio ($R_r$)** is defined as the percentage ratio of the *actual length of soil sample recovered* ($L_r$) to the *length of sampler penetration* ($L_p$) into the ground.

$$R_r = \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\%$$

**Factors affecting Recovery Ratio:**
1. **Area Ratio of the sampler** — higher $A_r$ causes sample compression → lower $R_r$.
2. **Inside Clearance** — inadequate $C_i$ causes sample jamming.
3. **Soil type and consistency** — loose sands and soft sensitive clays behave differently.
4. **Method of driving** — hammer weight, drop height, and number of blows.
5. **Sampler extraction rate** — sudden extraction can cause sample loss.
6. **Groundwater table position** — affects effective stress and sample cohesion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the geometry of a thin-walled tube sampler and define the terms *Area Ratio*, *Inside Clearance*, and *Outside Clearance*. Derive expressions for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A piston sampler has the following specifications:
- Outside diameter of cutting edge $D_o = 102\,\text{mm}$
- Inside diameter of cutting edge $D_i = 98\,\text{mm}$
- Inside diameter at top of tube $D_c = 99\,\text{mm}$
- Outside diameter of drive shoe at top $D_t = 100.5\,\text{mm}$
- Length of penetration $L_p = 500\,\text{mm}$
- Length of sample recovered $L_r = 490\,\text{mm}$

Compute (i) Area Ratio, (ii) Inside Clearance, (iii) Outside Clearance, and (iv) Recovery Ratio. State whether this sampler is suitable for soft sensitive clay. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Theory and Derivations (7 marks)

**Sampler Geometry:**
A thin-walled tube sampler consists of a *drive cap* at the top, a *sampling tube* with a *cutting edge* at the bottom, and a *drive shoe* to protect the cutting edge during penetration. The sample enters the tube as the sampler is driven or pushed into the soil.

**Area Ratio (Ar):**
It is the ratio of the *area of the sampler wall* to the *area of the sample*, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{A_{\text{wall}}}{A_{\text{sample}}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
A_{\text{wall}} &= \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \\[4pt]
A_{\text{sample}} &= \frac{\pi}{4}D_i^2 \\[4pt]
\therefore A_r &= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inside Clearance (Ci):**
It is the ratio of the *inside clearance gap* to the *inside diameter of the sample*, providing space for elastic rebound.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \frac{D_c - D_i}{D_c} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Outside Clearance (Co):**
It is the ratio of the *outside clearance gap* to the *outside diameter of the sample*, reducing outer wall friction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_o &= \frac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Area Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{102^2 - 98^2}{98^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{10404 - 9604}{9604} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{800}{9604} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 8.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Inside Clearance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_i &= \frac{D_c - D_i}{D_c} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{99 - 98}{98} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{98} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 1.02\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Outside Clearance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_o &= \frac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{102 - 100.5}{102} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.5}{102} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 1.47\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Recovery Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{490}{500} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 98.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability Assessment:**

| Parameter | Computed | Required (Soft Sensitive Clay) | Verdict |
|-----------|----------|-------------------------------|---------|
| $A_r$ | 8.33% | < 10% | **PASS** |
| $C_i$ | 1.02% | 0.5% – 3% | **PASS** |
| $C_o$ | 1.47% | 0.5% – 2% | **PASS** |
| $R_r$ | 98.0% | > 95% | **PASS** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** The piston sampler is **suitable** for sampling soft sensitive clays. All four design criteria are satisfied as per IS 2132.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Recovery Ratio. Discuss its importance in assessing sample quality. What is the significance of $R_r > 100\%$? (7 marks)

**(b)** A thin-walled Shelby tube sampler has:
- $D_o = 76.2\,\text{mm}$, $D_i = 73.0\,\text{mm}$
- Sample recovered length $L_r = 595\,\text{mm}$ from a penetration $L_p = 600\,\text{mm}$

Compute the Area Ratio and Recovery Ratio. If the maximum permissible Area Ratio is 10% and the minimum acceptable Recovery Ratio is 95%, comment on the sample quality. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Discussion (7 marks)

**Definition:**
The **Recovery Ratio ($R_r$)** is the percentage of soil sample length retrieved relative to the depth of penetration of the sampler.

$$R_r = \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\%$$

**Importance in Sample Quality Assessment:**

1. **Indicator of Sampling Disturbance:** A low $R_r$ suggests that the sample was compressed, lost, or remoulded during retrieval.
2. **Validity for Laboratory Testing:** Most geotechnical tests (consolidation, triaxial, unconfined compression) require samples with $R_r > 95\%$ to be considered representative.
3. **Correlation with Soil Behavior:** Recovery ratio provides indirect evidence of in-situ soil consistency, stratification, and groundwater conditions.
4. **Quality Control Tool:** Enables the engineer to reject defective samples and re-bore at the same location, ensuring design reliability.

**Significance of $R_r > 100\%$:**

When $R_r > 100\%$, the recovered sample is *longer* than the depth of penetration. This phenomenon is called **"sample heave"** or **"swelling"** and occurs primarily in:
- Soft sensitive clays (high plasticity, low overburden pressure)
- Organic soils with high compressibility
- Soils with significant stress relief after sampling

A value of $R_r$ between **100% and 105%** is generally considered acceptable, but the engineer must report the heave value and account for it in subsequent laboratory index correction procedures.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Area Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_r &= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{76.2^2 - 73.0^2}{73.0^2} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5806.44 - 5329.00}{5329.00} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{477.44}{5329.00} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 8.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovery Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_r &= \frac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \frac{595}{600} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 99.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with Acceptance Criteria:**

| Parameter | Computed | Required Limit | Status |
|-----------|----------|----------------|--------|
| Area Ratio $A_r$ | 8.96% | < 10% | **SATISFACTORY** |
| Recovery Ratio $R_r$ | 99.17% | > 95% | **SATISFACTORY** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** Both criteria are satisfied. The sample is of **acceptable quality** and may be used for laboratory testing of soft sensitive clays. The sampler design is also deemed appropriate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing cm and mm in formula substitution. Always convert to a *single consistent unit* before plugging values.
>
> 2. **Confusing $D_i$ and $D_c$:** Many students use the *same* value for both diameters, yielding $C_i = 0$. Recall that $D_i$ is at the *cutting edge* and $D_c$ is at the *top of the tube*.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Percentage Multiplier:** The Area Ratio, Clearances, and Recovery Ratio are *all expressed in percentage*. Forgetting to multiply by 100% is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Misinterpreting $R_r > 100\%$:** Students often wrongly conclude that a recovery ratio > 100% means the sample is invalid. In reality, it indicates *elastic heave* and is acceptable for sensitive clays.
>
> 5. **Insufficient Justification:** KTU examiners award marks for *commenting on suitability*, not just numerical values. Always state the prescribed limit and compare.
>
> 6. **Sign Error in $C_i$:** If $D_c < D_i$, the inside clearance becomes *negative* — this is a physical impossibility and indicates a *manufacturing defect*. Always verify the sign.
>
> 7. **Formula Recitation Without Derivation:** Simply stating the formula scores only 1–2 marks. KTU requires the *derivation* from the area definitions, particularly for Area Ratio.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**
>
> - **Area Ratio ($A_r$)** measures *disturbance* due to wall thickness. Formula: $A_r = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100\%$. Limit: **< 10%** (soft clay), **< 15%** (stiff clay).
> - **Inside Clearance ($C_i$)** accommodates *elastic expansion*. Formula: $C_i = \dfrac{D_c - D_i}{D_c} \times 100\%$. Range: **0.5% – 3%**.
> - **Outside Clearance ($C_o$)** reduces *wall friction* on extraction. Formula: $C_o = \dfrac{D_o - D_t}{D_o} \times 100\%$. Range: **0.5% – 2%**.
> - **Recovery Ratio ($R_r$)** measures *sample completeness*. Formula: $R_r = \dfrac{L_r}{L_p} \times 100\%$. Limit: **> 95%** (acceptable).
> - **All four ratios are interdependent** — high $A_r$ reduces $R_r$; appropriate $C_i$ and $C_o$ enhance $R_r$.
> - **IS 2132 and ASTM D1587** are the governing standards for these design parameters in India and internationally.
> - **Sample heave** ($R_r > 100\%$) is common in soft sensitive clays and is reported, not rejected.
> - **Thin-walled Shelby tubes** typically have $A_r \approx 7$–$10\%$, while *thick-walled split spoons* may have $A_r > 30\%$ (unsuitable for undisturbed sampling).
> - **Always state the prescribed limit AND compare** in your answer — KTU awards marks for this justification step.
> - **Units must be consistent** — typically all diameters in mm.
> - The **$\pi/4$ factor cancels** in Area Ratio derivation — students often incorrectly retain it.
> - **Inside Clearance sign convention:** $D_c > D_i$ (tube widens upward) gives positive clearance; reverse gives negative (invalid).
> - For **offshore and sensitive clay investigation**, a low $A_r$ sampler (e.g., Shelby tube) is *mandatory*; split-spoon samplers are inadequate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling and Transportation of Samples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handling and Transportation of Samples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample Handling and Transportation** refers to the systematic sequence of procedures employed between the moment a soil/rock specimen is extracted from the ground (via boreholes, trial pits, or sampling tubes) and the moment it is received at the geotechnical testing laboratory, with the primary objective of **preserving the in-situ properties** (moisture content, density, structure, stress state) intact and free from mechanical disturbance, thermal changes, or chemical alteration.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET753 – Advanced Geotechnical Investigation), this topic is positioned under **Module 4: Subsurface Investigation and Sampling**, and is critically evaluated because improper handling is the single largest contributor to **"bad data → unsafe design"** failures in geotechnical engineering.

### Key Terminology Anchors (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Undisturbed Sample (UD)** | A sample that retains the in-situ structure, moisture content, and stress state of the soil. Used for shear, consolidation, and permeability tests. |
| **Disturbed Sample (DS)** | A sample whose natural structure has been altered, but the mineralogical composition is preserved. Used for classification, index properties, and compaction tests. |
| **Sample Disturbance** | Any change in the soil's natural state caused by mechanical impact, stress relief, temperature change, moisture loss, or vibration. |
| **Sealing Wax/Paraffin** | A molten wax mixture applied to sample ends to prevent moisture loss. |
| **Sample Tube Liner** | A thin metal or plastic sleeve inside the sampling tube used to extract the sample with minimal friction. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ice-Cream Transport Problem"**

Imagine you order a premium handmade ice-cream tub from a famous parlour 50 km away. If the delivery boy:
- Tilts the tub (mechanical disturbance),
- Removes the lid (moisture loss),
- Drives over a pothole (vibration),
- Parks the bike in the sun (temperature change),
- Drops it once (shock),

…you will receive a melted, lumpy, recrystallised mess. The **flavour** (mineralogy) is intact, but the **texture** (structure) is destroyed.

A geotechnical sample is **exactly the same**. The soil's *flavour* is its grain mineralogy (preserved even in disturbed samples), but the *texture* is its in-situ density, fabric, and pore-water pressure (preserved only in *undisturbed* samples). **Handling and transportation is the "delivery system"** of geotechnical engineering — and just like ice-cream, the rules are strict, time-bound, and unforgiving.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- **Standard Sample Tube Diameter (Shelby Tube):** **76.2 mm (3 inches)** outer, **73 mm** inner.
- **Standard Sample Tube Length:** **600 mm** (typical), with extensions up to **1000 mm** for soft clays.
- **Maximum Acceptable Storage Temperature:** **4 °C to 18 °C** (to suppress microbial activity without freezing).
- **Maximum Storage Duration (Undisturbed Samples):** **4 weeks** before testing (per IS 2132 and IS 2720 guidelines).
- **Paraffin Wax Sealing Thickness:** Minimum **10 mm** coating on each end.
- **Freezing Temperature Threshold:** Below **0 °C** is strictly forbidden (ice expansion alters fabric).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module specifically expects students to differentiate between handling of *disturbed bulk samples* (e.g., from auger cuttings) and *undisturbed tube/shelby/piston samples*, including the sealing, labelling, packaging, and transport orientation protocols.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters (Engineering Reality)

In a real KTU-aligned geotech consultancy workflow:
1. Sample drilled at site (e.g., Kochi Metro, Metro Rail alignment).
2. Sealed, labelled, packed in wooden/thermocol boxes with cushioning.
3. Transported vertically (if shelby tube) or horizontally (if rock cores) in a **dedicated geotech vehicle** (no harsh braking, no sun exposure).
4. Received at lab with a **chain-of-custody form** and **field log**.
5. Stored in a climate-controlled room (18–22 °C, ~95% RH for clays).

A single missing step — say, transporting a shelby tube **horizontally** — can cause the soil core to slide, remould, and produce a triaxial test result showing **50% lower shear strength** than the true in-situ value. This translates directly to:
- Oversized pile foundations (cost overrun).
- Unstable slope design (safety hazard).
- Unreliable settlement predictions (serviceability failure).

**This is why KTU examiners love this topic — one careless answer, and a 14-mark question is lost.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 4-Stage Chain of Custody

The entire process can be modelled as a **4-stage linear workflow** (this is exactly how KTU questions are framed):

### **STAGE 1: Field Extraction → Initial Handling**
1. **At the borehole mouth**, the sampling tube (e.g., Shelby, Piston, Denison) is withdrawn slowly at a **constant penetration rate of 20 mm/s** (or as per sampler type).
2. **Excess soil** protruding from each end is carefully trimmed flush with a clean wire saw or spatula — *never* with a knife (knife creates shear planes and remoulds the periphery).
3. The exposed soil ends are **immediately sealed** to prevent moisture loss and exposure to air.
4. A **field identification label** is attached (waterproof paper, indelible ink) showing:
   - Borehole number (e.g., BH-05)
   - Depth of sample (e.g., 6.00 m – 6.60 m)
   - Date and time of sampling
   - Sample type (UD / DS)
   - Project name & chain-of-custody ID

### **STAGE 2: Sealing the Sample**
- **Method A (Wax Sealing):** Used for tube samples and bulk samples. Steps:
  1. Trim ends flush.
  2. Place a **wax-impregnated wooden disc** or **plastic cap** over each end.
  3. Pour **molten paraffin wax (melting point 55–65 °C)** over the cap, building a minimum **10 mm thick seal**.
  4. Allow wax to cool and solidify (avoid direct sunlight).
- **Method B (Plastic Cap + Tape + Wax):** A two-stage seal:
  1. Place a tight-fitting plastic cap on each end.
  2. Wrap with **electrical tape / PVC tape** spirally.
  3. Apply a wax coat over the taped joint.
- **Method C (For Rock Cores):** Wrap each core piece in **cling film**, place in a **core box** (cardboard or wooden), separated by foam inserts. Label the box with depth intervals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The examiner expects you to state **why** wax is used — *not* just *that* it is used. The answer: paraffin wax is **hydrophobic, flexible, and has a low melting point**, allowing it to mould around irregularities and provide an airtight seal without generating heat damage to the soil.

### **STAGE 3: Packaging and Cushioning**
- The sealed tube is placed in a **rigid wooden or PVC box** lined with:
  - **Thermocol / polystyrene beads** (loose fill, ideal for shock absorption), or
  - **Foam rubber inserts** (pre-cut to tube diameter), or
  - **Sawdust** (cheaper, used in rural projects — acceptable for disturbed samples only).
- Multiple tubes from the same borehole are **packed vertically, side-by-side**, never stacked on top of each other.
- Each tube is separated by a **cardboard divider** to prevent tube-to-tube contact.

### **STAGE 4: Transportation and Storage**
- **Transport Orientation:**
  - **Shelby / Piston tubes (clay samples):** **Always transported vertically**, capped ends up. This prevents the soil core from sliding due to gravity and remoulding.
  - **Rock cores:** **Horizontal**, in core boxes, with depth markers.
  - **Bulk disturbed samples:** **Sealed plastic / gunny bags**, transported in ventilated crates.
- **Transport Vehicle Requirements:**
  - Closed vehicle (no direct sunlight).
  - Soft suspension preferred.
  - **Avoid bumpy roads** — if unavoidable, drive at **≤ 30 km/h**.
  - No co-transport with chemicals, fuels, or perishable goods.
- **Lab Storage:**
  - Temperature: **18–22 °C** (for tropical KTU regions like Kerala, AC storage is mandatory).
  - Humidity: **> 90% RH** (achieved by covering tubes with wet burlap inside a closed bin).
  - **Do NOT freeze** samples (ice crystals damage the clay fabric irreversibly).
  - **Storage duration limit:** Test within **4 weeks**; beyond that, pore water chemistry may change.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While the topic is largely **procedural**, a few quantitative relationships are testable in KTU exams. They are tabulated below.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Purpose / KTU Application |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Sample Recovery Ratio $R = \dfrac{L_{recovered}}{L_{pushed}}$ | $R$ (dimensionless) | If $R < 0.95$, sample is disturbed; reject for UD tests. |
| 2 | Area Ratio $A_r = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100$ | $A_r$ in % | For Shelby: $A_r \le 10\%$; higher $A_r$ means more disturbance. |
| 3 | Inside Clearance Ratio $C_i = \dfrac{D_i - D_{e}}{D_e} \times 100$ | $C_i$ in % | Recommended: $0.5\% \le C_i \le 3\%$. |
| 4 | Outside Clearance Ratio $C_o = \dfrac{D_o - D_s}{D_s} \times 100$ | $C_o$ in % | Recommended: $0\% \le C_o \le 2\%$. |
| 5 | Standard Penetration Test (SPT) – indirectly: $N$ value | blows/300 mm | Used to *correlate* sample quality, not a direct handling formula. |
| 6 | Moisture Loss Limit | $\le 1\%$ of in-situ $w$ | For "undisturbed" status, moisture content at lab must equal field value within **1%**. |
| 7 | Wax Seal Thickness | $t_{wax} \ge 10$ mm | Minimum acceptable to ensure vapour barrier. |
| 8 | Sample Storage Time Limit | $t \le 28$ days | Maximum delay before triaxial/consolidation testing. |

> **Notation Note:** $D_o$ = outer diameter of cutting edge, $D_i$ = inner diameter of cutting edge, $D_e$ = diameter at the cutting tip, $D_s$ = diameter of the sample inside the tube, $L_{recovered}$ = length of soil recovered, $L_{pushed}$ = length of tube pushed into the soil.

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** Vertical pipe symbols `|` are not used inside any table row above; instead, $\vert$ or $\mid$ would be used in any inline absolute-value expression to avoid breaking the table.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Proper Handling |
|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Highway / Metro Rail Embankments** | Ensures oedometer test data is reliable, preventing post-construction settlement. |
| **Offshore Platform Design** | Marine clay samples must maintain pore pressure — improper sealing causes gas exsolution and fabric loss. |
| **Tailings Dams** | Liquefaction studies need truly undisturbed sand samples; vibration during transport can densify or loosen them. |
| **Landslide Investigation (Kerala Context)** | Laterite / clay samples from the Western Ghats need humidity-controlled transport to retain peak vs. residual strength. |
| **Nuclear Power Plant Foundations** | Zero tolerance for disturbance; samples are often tested in **mobile labs at site** to avoid transport entirely. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sample Recovery Ratio (R) and Disturbance Threshold

The recovery ratio quantifies how much of the *intended* sample length was actually retained in the tube. KTU examiners love asking: *"A sample tube is pushed 600 mm into the ground. After retrieval, only 540 mm of soil is recovered. Comment on sample quality."*

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Given:**
- $L_{pushed} = 600 \text{ mm}$ (length of tube pushed into the soil stratum)
- $L_{recovered} = 540 \text{ mm}$ (length of soil column actually inside the tube on retrieval)

**Step 1:** Write the definition of Recovery Ratio.

$$R = \frac{L_{recovered}}{L_{pushed}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$R = \frac{540 \text{ mm}}{600 \text{ mm}} = 0.90$$

**Step 3:** Interpret using the standard KTU/IS threshold table (memorise this — guaranteed 3-mark question!):

| Recovery Ratio $R$ | Sample Quality (KTU Classification) |
|---------------------|-------------------------------------|
| $R \ge 0.98$ | Excellent — suitable for all UD tests |
| $0.95 \le R < 0.98$ | Good — acceptable for most UD tests |
| $0.90 \le R < 0.95$ | Fair — usable for index tests, marginal for shear/consolidation |
| $R < 0.90$ | Poor — Reject for UD tests; use only as disturbed sample |

**Step 4:** Apply the rule.

$$R = 0.90 \rightarrow \text{Boundary case, classified as "Fair"}$$

**Conclusion:** The sample is at the lower limit of acceptability. It **may be used for index and classification tests**, but for triaxial or consolidation tests, a fresh sample must be obtained. This is a typical 3-mark KTU question — answer must include both the *calculation* and the *interpretation*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Area Ratio ($A_r$) of a Sampling Tube

The area ratio determines the *volume of soil* that is displaced (and thus disturbed) during sampling. A high $A_r$ means more soil is pushed aside, producing a more disturbed sample.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Given (Shelby Tube Standard Dimensions):**
- $D_o = 76.2$ mm (outer diameter of the cutting edge)
- $D_i = 73.0$ mm (inner diameter of the cutting edge)

**Step 1:** State the formula for area ratio.

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100 \;\;(\%)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator (cross-sectional area of the tube wall).

$$D_o^2 - D_i^2 = (76.2)^2 - (73.0)^2$$

Compute each term:

$$(76.2)^2 = 5806.44 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

$$(73.0)^2 = 5329.00 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

Subtract:

$$D_o^2 - D_i^2 = 5806.44 - 5329.00 = 477.44 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$D_i^2 = 5329.00 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio.

$$A_r = \frac{477.44}{5329.00} \times 100$$

$$A_r = 0.0896 \times 100 = 8.96 \;\%$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the standard threshold.

Standard recommendation (per IS 2132 / ASTM D1587): **$A_r \le 10\%$** for undisturbed sampling.

**Conclusion:** Since $A_r = 8.96\% < 10\%$, the Shelby tube is **acceptable** for undisturbed sampling. If $A_r$ had exceeded 10%, the sampler would be rejected for UD work and used only for disturbed sampling.

### **Alternative Quick Approximation (KTU Shortcut)**

For a thin-walled Shelby tube, an approximate closed-form is:

$$A_r \approx \frac{2t}{D_i} \times 100 \;\;(\%)$$

where $t = \dfrac{D_o - D_i}{2}$ is the wall thickness. For our case:

$$t = \frac{76.2 - 73.0}{2} = 1.6 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$A_r \approx \frac{2 \times 1.6}{73.0} \times 100 = \frac{3.2}{73.0} \times 100 = 4.38\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *linear* approximation. The exact value is **8.96%** because the area ratio uses squared diameters. Always use the exact formula in KTU exams — the examiner knows the shortcut and will deduct if you misuse it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Moisture Loss Calculation (Quantitative Threshold)

The "undisturbed" status of a sample is conditional on **moisture content preservation**. KTU sometimes asks: *"In-situ moisture content was 42%. Lab-measured moisture content after transport was 39.8%. Is the sample acceptable for UD testing?"*

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Given:**
- $w_{field} = 42.0\%$
- $w_{lab} = 39.8\%$

**Step 1:** Compute the absolute moisture loss.

$$\Delta w = w_{field} - w_{lab} = 42.0 - 39.8 = 2.2\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute the *relative* moisture loss (a dimensionless ratio).

$$\Delta w_{rel} = \frac{\Delta w}{w_{field}} = \frac{2.2}{42.0} = 0.0524 = 5.24\%$$

**Step 3:** Apply the KTU/IS acceptance criterion.

The standard tolerance is **$\Delta w \le 1\%$ absolute** OR **$\Delta w_{rel} \le 5\%$**.

**Step 4:** Conclude.

Since $\Delta w = 2.2\% > 1\%$ (absolute), the sample **fails the absolute criterion**, even though the relative loss is borderline (5.24%). The sample is **rejected for UD tests**. Likely causes:
- Wax seal was too thin or cracked.
- Storage time exceeded 4 weeks.
- Transport vehicle was exposed to direct sunlight.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often only check $\Delta w_{rel}$ and forget the absolute criterion. Always check **both** thresholds in KTU answers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Procedure Applies?"

This is the **highest-yield KTU content** for this topic. Examiners love: *"Describe the procedure for handling and transporting (a) an undisturbed Shelby tube sample, (b) a bulk disturbed sample, (c) a rock core sample."*

| Parameter | Shelby Tube (UD) | Bulk Disturbed (DS) | Rock Core |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| End preparation | Trim flush, no knife | Not applicable (loose soil) | Trim to length, wrap in cling film |
| Sealing | Wax (≥ 10 mm) + plastic cap | Sealed plastic bag / airtight drum | Core box with foam separators |
| Cushioning | Thermocol-lined wooden box | Gunny bag inside ventilated crate | Cardboard / wooden core box |
| Orientation in transport | **Vertical**, cap-up | Any (preferably upright) | Horizontal |
| Vehicle | Closed, AC preferred | Open truck acceptable | Closed, vibration-controlled |
| Storage temp | 18–22 °C | Room temp, dry | Room temp, dry |
| Storage duration | ≤ 28 days | ≤ 90 days (inert soils) | Indefinite (after drying) |
| Test timing | ASAP, max 4 weeks | Any time before 90 days | Anytime |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Workflow Diagram — From Borehole to Laboratory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Borehole Drilling Complete] --> B[Select Sampler Type]
    B --> C[Shelby Tube]
    B --> D[Split Spoon]
    B --> E[Piston Sampler]
    B --> F[Bulk Auger Cuttings]
    C --> G1[Push at 20 mm per sec]
    D --> G2[Drive with 63.5 kg hammer]
    E --> G3[Push with hydraulic piston]
    F --> G4[Collect in bucket]
    G1 --> H[Withdraw Tube Slowly]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I1[Trim Ends Flush]
    H --> I2[Bag and Tag]
    I1 --> J1[Apply Plastic Cap]
    J1 --> K1[Pour Molten Paraffin Wax]
    K1 --> L1[Wax Seal Minimum 10 mm]
    L1 --> M1[Attach Waterproof Label]
    M1 --> N1[Place in Thermocol Lined Box]
    N1 --> O1[Transport Vertically Closed Vehicle]
    O1 --> P1[Lab Storage 18 to 22 deg C]
    I2 --> J2[Seal in Plastic Bag]
    J2 --> K2[Pack in Wooden Crate]
    K2 --> L2[Attach Field Label]
    L2 --> M2[Transport in Open Truck]
    M2 --> N2[Lab Storage Dry Room Temp]
    P1 --> Q[Geotechnical Testing Lab]
    N2 --> Q
    Q --> R[Index Tests and Strength Tests]
```

> **Reading Note:** The diagram shows two parallel workflows — one for undisturbed (left branch) and one for disturbed (right branch) — converging at the laboratory.

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Sealed Shelby Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Sealed Shelby Tube Cross Section]
        direction TB
        TopEnd[Top Plastic Cap] --> WaxTop[Paraffin Wax Layer 10 mm]
        WaxTop --> SoilCore[Soil Core 540 mm to 600 mm]
        SoilCore --> WaxBot[Paraffin Wax Layer 10 mm]
        WaxBot --> BotEnd[Bottom Plastic Cap]
        SoilCore -.-> Label[Waterproof Label Attached to Tube Wall]
    end
    TubeWall[Shelby Tube Wall 76.2 mm OD 73 mm ID]
    A --- TubeWall
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Chain of Custody System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Site[Site Sampling Engineer] -->|Field Log| Log1[Field Logbook]
    Log1 --> Custody1[Chain of Custody Form Part A]
    Custody1 --> Trans[Transport Driver Signs]
    Trans --> Custody2[Chain of Custody Form Part B]
    Custody2 --> Lab[Lab Receives Sample]
    Lab --> Custody3[Chain of Custody Form Part C]
    Custody3 --> Test[Test Engineer Begins Testing]
    Test --> Report[Final Report with Provenance]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Sample Accept / Reject

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Sample Arrives at Lab] --> Check1{Recovery Ratio R greater than 0.95?}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Check2{Area Ratio Ar less than 10 percent?}
    Check1 -->|No| Reject1[Reject for UD Use]
    Check2 -->|Yes| Check3{Moisture Loss less than 1 percent absolute?}
    Check2 -->|No| Reject2[Reject for UD Use]
    Check3 -->|Yes| Check4{Storage Time less than 28 days?}
    Check3 -->|No| Reject3[Reject for UD Use]
    Check4 -->|Yes| Accept[Accept as Undisturbed Sample]
    Check4 -->|No| Reject4[Reject for UD Use]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **What is meant by "undisturbed sample"? List any four precautions to be taken while transporting an undisturbed sample from the site to the laboratory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **undisturbed sample** is a soil specimen retrieved from the ground using a suitable sampler (e.g., Shelby tube) in such a way that its **in-situ structure, density, moisture content, and stress state are preserved** to a degree sufficient for laboratory strength and compressibility tests.

**Four precautions during transportation:**
1. **Seal the sample ends** with molten paraffin wax (≥ 10 mm thick) immediately after retrieval to prevent moisture loss.
2. **Pack the tube vertically in a rigid box** lined with thermocol/cushioning material to prevent mechanical shock and sliding of the soil core.
3. **Transport in a closed vehicle**, avoiding direct sunlight and excessive vibration; drive at moderate speed.
4. **Maintain chain of custody** with proper labelling (BH number, depth, date) and store the sample in a humidity-controlled lab (18–22 °C) within 28 days.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four well-explained precautions.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Why is molten paraffin wax (and not water-based paint or epoxy) used for sealing the ends of soil samples?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Molten paraffin wax is preferred because:

1. **Hydrophobic and airtight:** It forms an effective moisture-vapour barrier, preventing moisture loss or gain that would alter the in-situ water content.
2. **Low melting point (55–65 °C):** It does not generate excessive heat during application, so the soil's structure and pore water are not thermally damaged.
3. **Flexible and conformable:** It moulds around surface irregularities, producing a tight seal even on rough sample ends.
4. **Removable in lab:** It can be melted or peeled off easily without contaminating the soil specimen.

In contrast, water-based paint would re-introduce moisture, and epoxy would generate heat during curing and may chemically contaminate the sample.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester, Module 4 Choice Question]**

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure for handling and transporting an undisturbed soil sample** obtained using a Shelby tube, from the moment it is withdrawn from the borehole until it is placed in the laboratory storage room. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Shelby tube sampler has an **outer diameter of 76.2 mm** and an **inner diameter of 73.0 mm**. The tube is pushed **600 mm** into a soft clay stratum, and on retrieval, the **recovered soil length is 570 mm**. The in-situ moisture content was **45%**, and the laboratory moisture content measured 24 hours later was **43.2%**. Determine: **(i)** the area ratio of the sampler, **(ii)** the recovery ratio, and **(iii)** the relative moisture loss. Comment on the suitability of the sample for an **unconsolidated undrained (UU) triaxial test**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The procedure is divided into four sequential stages:

**Stage 1: Withdrawal and Initial Handling (2 marks)**
- After the Shelby tube has been pushed to the required depth, it is withdrawn slowly to avoid suction disturbance.
- At the borehole mouth, the **excess soil protruding from each end** is carefully trimmed flush using a **wire saw or spatula** (never a knife, which would remould the periphery).
- A **field label** (waterproof paper, indelible ink) is attached showing borehole number, depth interval, date, and sample type.

**Stage 2: Sealing (2 marks)**
- A **tight-fitting plastic cap** is placed on each end of the tube.
- **Molten paraffin wax (55–65 °C)** is poured over each cap to form a seal of **minimum 10 mm thickness**.
- The wax is allowed to cool and solidify in shade.
- A second layer of **PVC tape** is wrapped spirally over the waxed joint for added security.

**Stage 3: Packaging and Cushioning (1.5 marks)**
- The sealed tube is placed vertically in a **rigid wooden or PVC box** lined with thermocol beads or foam rubber inserts.
- Multiple tubes from the same borehole are placed side-by-side, separated by cardboard dividers, **never stacked on top of each other**.

**Stage 4: Transportation and Storage (1.5 marks)**
- The box is transported in a **closed, preferably air-conditioned vehicle** at moderate speeds, avoiding bumps and direct sunlight.
- The tube is kept **vertical, capped ends up**, to prevent the soil core from sliding under gravity.
- At the laboratory, the tube is stored in a **humidity-controlled room (18–22 °C, > 90% RH)** and tested within **28 days**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $D_o = 76.2$ mm
- $D_i = 73.0$ mm
- $L_{pushed} = 600$ mm
- $L_{recovered} = 570$ mm
- $w_{field} = 45\%$
- $w_{lab} = 43.2\%$

**(i) Area Ratio:**

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100$$

$$A_r = \frac{(76.2)^2 - (73.0)^2}{(73.0)^2} \times 100$$

$$A_r = \frac{5806.44 - 5329.00}{5329.00} \times 100 = \frac{477.44}{5329.00} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{A_r = 8.96\%}$$

Since $A_r = 8.96\% < 10\%$, the sampler is **acceptable** for undisturbed sampling.

**(ii) Recovery Ratio:**

$$R = \frac{L_{recovered}}{L_{pushed}} = \frac{570}{600} = \boxed{0.95 \; (95\%)}$$

Since $R = 0.95$, the sample is at the **lower boundary of "Good" quality** (per the KTU classification table).

**(iii) Relative Moisture Loss:**

$$\Delta w_{rel} = \frac{w_{field} - w_{lab}}{w_{field}} \times 100 = \frac{45 - 43.2}{45} \times 100$$

$$\Delta w_{rel} = \frac{1.8}{45} \times 100 = \boxed{4.0\%}$$

**Comment on Suitability for UU Triaxial Test:**

| Criterion | Value | Threshold | Verdict |
|-----------|-------|-----------|---------|
| $A_r$ | 8.96% | < 10% | Pass |
| $R$ | 0.95 | ≥ 0.95 | Pass (boundary) |
| $\Delta w_{rel}$ | 4.0% | < 5% | Pass |
| $\Delta w$ absolute | 1.8% | < 1% | **Fail** |

> **Conclusion:** Although the area ratio and recovery ratio are acceptable, the **absolute moisture loss of 1.8% exceeds the 1% threshold**. The sample is therefore **not strictly "undisturbed"** but can be used for **approximate UU triaxial testing** with a note in the report. For high-stakes projects (e.g., metro rail), a fresh sample should be retrieved.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester, Module 4 Choice Question]**

> **(a)** Explain the **"Chain of Custody"** concept in geotechnical sampling. What documents are typically included, and why is it critical for forensic and contractual projects? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **bulk disturbed sample** of 25 kg is collected from a trial pit at 3 m depth. Discuss in detail the **handling, packaging, labelling, and transportation procedure** for this sample from the field to a laboratory located 250 km away, where it will be used for **compaction and CBR tests**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Chain of Custody (CoC) is the **unbroken, documented sequence of possession** of a geotechnical sample from the moment of its extraction at the site to its final disposal after testing. Each transfer of possession is **signed, dated, and timed** by the receiving party.

**Documents Included in a CoC Form (3 marks):**
1. **Field Log** — borehole/trial pit ID, GPS coordinates, depth, date/time, sampler type, recovery ratio.
2. **Sample Identification Label** — unique sample ID, project name, type (UD/DS), chain-of-custody seal number.
3. **Transfer Receipts** — signatures of the field engineer, transport in-charge, lab receiver.
4. **Storage Record** — date of receipt, storage conditions, date of test initiation, name of testing engineer.
5. **Test Report Reference** — links the sample ID to the final laboratory test report.

**Critical Importance (2 marks):**
- **Forensic projects** (e.g., landslide failure investigations): The CoC proves the sample was not tampered with after collection, ensuring the data is admissible as evidence.
- **Contractual / Arbitration projects** (e.g., metro rail claims): The CoC protects the contractor and client against allegations of sample substitution or contamination.
- **Quality assurance:** It enables traceability if test results are questioned later.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Stage 1: Field Handling (1.5 marks)**
- The 25 kg of soil collected from the trial pit is mixed homogeneously in a clean, dry, non-metallic tray.
- Foreign materials (roots, gravel > 40 mm, organic debris) are removed if the sample is intended for fine-grained compaction tests.
- The sample is **air-dried in shade** (not under direct sun) until it reaches a workable moisture content.

**Stage 2: Packaging (2 marks)**
- The sample is divided into **two equal portions of 12.5 kg each** and packed in **heavy-duty polythene bags** (200 µm thickness minimum).
- Each bag is **sealed with a heat sealer or strong PVC tape**, with excess air pressed out before sealing.
- The bags are placed inside a **second polythene liner** for double-sealing against moisture ingress.
- The doubled bags are placed in a **ventilated wooden crate or PVC drum** with sawdust cushioning at the bottom and sides.

**Stage 3: Labelling (1.5 marks)**
- A **waterproof tag** is attached to each bag showing:
  - Project name and location
  - Trial pit (TP) number and depth
  - Date and time of collection
  - Sample weight and intended tests (Compaction + CBR)
  - Sampler's signature
- A **duplicate copy of the label** is placed inside the crate (visible through a clear window) for redundancy.

**Stage 4: Transportation (2 marks)**
- The crate is transported in a **covered vehicle** (open trucks acceptable, but cover with tarpaulin).
- Cushion the crate with **shock-absorbing material** (foam, old tyres) to minimise vibration.
- For the 250 km journey, ensure the vehicle is **not co-loaded** with chemicals, fuel, or perishable goods.
- Maintain **chain-of-custody documentation** signed at three points: (1) site engineer → driver, (2) driver → lab receiver, (3) lab receiver → test engineer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Disturbed" with "Undisturbed":** Many students write that disturbed samples also need wax sealing. *No* — bulk disturbed samples are sealed in plastic bags; wax is reserved for tube/core samples.
>
> 2. **Wrong Transport Orientation:** Carrying a Shelby tube *horizontally* is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. It must be **vertical**, capped ends up.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Chain of Custody:** Examiners award marks for explicitly mentioning the CoC form. Omitting it can cost 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Stating "Freeze the sample" for preservation:** This is **fatal**. Freezing creates ice lenses that destroy the clay fabric. The correct answer is **refrigeration at 4–18 °C, never below 0 °C**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Area Ratio and Recovery Ratio formulas:** These are the only quantitative tools examiners test. Memorise them — they appear in nearly every Part B question.
>
> 6. **Not justifying "why wax and not paint":** Examiners want the *reason* — hydrophobicity, low melting point, removability. Writing only "wax is used" is a half-answer.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam!**

- [ ] **Undisturbed vs. Disturbed:** UD = structure preserved; DS = structure altered, mineralogy preserved.
- [ ] **Shelby Tube Specs:** OD = 76.2 mm, ID = 73 mm, length = 600 mm.
- [ ] **Area Ratio $A_r \le 10\%$** for UD sampling; formula uses **squared diameters**.
- [ ] **Recovery Ratio $R \ge 0.95$** for "Good" sample; below 0.90 = reject.
- [ ] **Moisture Loss Limits:** $\Delta w \le 1\%$ (absolute) **AND** $\Delta w_{rel} \le 5\%$.
- [ ] **Wax Seal:** Molten paraffin, melting point 55–65 °C, **minimum 10 mm thick**.
- [ ] **Transport Orientation:** Shelby tubes → **vertical**; rock cores → **horizontal**; bulk samples → upright in crates.
- [ ] **Storage:** 18–22 °C, > 90% RH, **never freeze**, test within **28 days**.
- [ ] **Chain of Custody:** Site → Driver → Lab → Test Engineer; signed at every transfer.
- [ ] **Cushioning:** Thermocol / foam rubber / sawdust (latter only for DS).
- [ ] **Vehicle:** Closed, AC preferred, ≤ 30 km/h on rough roads, no co-transport with chemicals.
- [ ] **Penalty for Errors:** A remoulded core gives **up to 50% lower** shear strength — leading to overdesigned foundations and project cost overruns.
- [ ] **Syllabus Tag:** This topic directly addresses **CO4 (Plan and execute subsurface investigation programs)** of PECET753.
- [ ] **Bloom's Level Tested:** Mostly **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyse)**; numerical problems test **L5 (Evaluate)**.
- [ ] **Mark Distribution Pattern:** Part A = 3 marks (definition + precautions); Part B = 14 marks (procedure + numerical, split 7+7).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: TYPES OF SOIL SAMPLERS

## 1.1 Introduction to Soil Sampling

Soil sampling is the process of obtaining representative specimens of subsurface strata for laboratory testing and engineering classification. The quality of the sample retrieved directly dictates the reliability of the geotechnical parameters derived for foundation design, slope stability analysis, and earthwork specifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A *soil sampler* is a specialized mechanical device attached to the bottom of a drill string or pushed hydraulically into the ground to retrieve soil specimens with minimal disturbance to their in-situ structure, density, moisture content, and stress state.

### 1.2 Classification of Soil Samplers

| Sampler Type | Category | Typical Soil | Disturbance Level |
|--------------|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Thin Walled (Shelby) | Undisturbed | Soft to medium cohesive clays, silts | Lowest |
| Piston Sampler | Undisturbed | Very soft clays, sensitive soils | Very Low |
| Split Spoon | Disturbed (SPT) | Sands, silts, clays, weak rocks | High |
| Sand Sampling Tools | Disturbed/Compacted | Sands below water table | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **Undisturbed Samplers:** Preserve in-situ structure (fabric, void ratio, water content) for strength and consolidation tests.
> - **Disturbed Samplers:** Preserve mineralogical composition and gradation but lose natural structure; used for classification tests (sieve, hydrometer, Atterberg limits).

---

## 1.3 Thin Walled Sampler (Shelby Tube)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Thin Walled Sampler* is a seamless steel tube (commonly called a *Shelby Tube*) with a sharp cutting edge, a constant inside diameter, and an area ratio of less than 10%, designed to retrieve relatively undisturbed cohesive soil samples by mechanical or hydraulic pushing.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine pushing a hollow soda straw straight down into a layer of soft butter on a slice of bread. If the straw walls are razor-thin and sharp at the bottom, the butter column trapped inside retains its layered structure perfectly. That is exactly how a Shelby tube works on soft clay—but the "straw" must withstand **15 m to 30 m** of overburden pressure and abrasive contact with the soil.

### Engineering Specifications
- **Wall thickness:** 1.24 mm to 3.25 mm
- **Standard diameters:** 50.8 mm (2"), 76.2 mm (3"), 101.6 mm (4")
- **Length:** 600 mm to 900 mm typical
- **Area ratio:** < 10% (preferably < 10% for undisturbed samples)
- **Material:** Cold-drawn seamless steel tubing

---

## 1.4 Piston Sampler

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Piston Sampler* (e.g., the Hvorslev or Osterberg type) is an advanced thin-walled sampler equipped with an internal piston that remains stationary at the bottom of the borehole while the sample tube advances past it, thereby creating a vacuum seal and preventing the entry of disturbed material, drilling fluid, or collapse of soft soils.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of inserting a drinking straw into a glass of thick milkshake with your thumb tightly sealing the top. When you release your thumb only after the straw tip is at the desired depth, the milkshake rushes up the straw under a controlled vacuum. The piston in a soil sampler performs this exact "thumbs-up" sealing function—preventing soil from sloughing into the tube prematurely.

### Advantages over Standard Thin Walled Samplers
1. Achieves **100% recovery** in soft, sensitive, or cohesionless soils.
2. Vacuum effect eliminates washout from drilling fluid.
3. Eliminates piston lag and sample compression.
4. Suitable for depths exceeding **30 m** in soft marine clays.

---

## 1.5 Split Spoon Sampler (Standard Penetration Test Sampler)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Split Spoon Sampler* is a **51 mm OD × 35 mm ID** two-piece steel barrel split longitudinally into halves, fitted with a driving shoe at the bottom and a check valve at the top, used to obtain disturbed samples while simultaneously measuring the *Standard Penetration Resistance (N-value)* — the number of hammer blows required to drive the sampler **300 mm** into the soil.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine hammering a hollow cookie-cutter into a tray of firm cookie dough. The dough that gets pinched into the cookie-cutter is broken, compressed, and remolded—but you can still tell whether it was chocolate chip or oatmeal by looking at the contents. The split spoon works the same way: it "remolds" the soil but provides an empirical measure of how hard it was to drive in (the famous **N-value**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Note:**
> The Split Spoon Sampler is **the cornerstone of SPT testing**. You must remember the standard dimensions: **51 mm OD, 35 mm ID, 457 mm length**, with a **63.5 kg hammer dropped from 760 mm**.

---

## 1.6 Methods for Collection of Sand Samples from Beneath the Water Table

Sampling granular soils below the water table is one of the most challenging tasks in geotechnical engineering because loose sand tends to **flow, boil, or wash out** when the borehole is dewatered or when a sampler is pushed in. Several specialized methods are used:

| Method | Core Principle | Typical Use |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Borehole Method | Hollow-stem auger + clean water column | Sands with low fines |
| Piston Sampler Method | Vacuum-assisted piston | Medium dense sands |
| Sand Pumping Method | Suction through perforated tip | Loose submerged sands |
| Bailer Method | Percussion of a hollow tube with check valve | Coarse gravels, dense sands |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this difficult?**
> When a borehole penetrates the water table, the hydrostatic head difference between the water inside the borehole and the surrounding sand can cause a **quick condition** (liquefaction-like boiling), washing fines into the borehole. Sampler design must counteract this with check valves, pistons, or water head maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Quantitative Measures of Sample Disturbance

The quality of any soil sample is assessed by three dimensionless ratios. These ratios appear routinely in KTU exam questions (both 3-mark direct and 14-mark derivations).

### 2.1.1 Area Ratio (Ar)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *Area Ratio* is the ratio of the cross-sectional area of soil displaced by the sampler wall to the cross-sectional area of the sample, expressed as a percentage.

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

where:
- $D_o$ = Outside diameter of the cutting edge (mm)
- $D_i$ = Inside diameter of the cutting edge (mm)

**Acceptance Criteria (IS 2132 & KTU References):**
- $A_r < 10\%$ → Undisturbed (clayey soils)
- $A_r = 10\% \text{ to } 15\%$ → Slightly disturbed
- $A_r > 15\%$ → Highly disturbed

### 2.1.2 Inside Clearance Ratio (Cr)

$$C_r = \frac{D_i - D_{cutting\;tip}}{D_{cutting\;tip}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

- **Ideal range:** $C_r = 0.5\% \text{ to } 3\%$
- **Purpose:** Allows slight lateral expansion of the sample to relieve wall friction, preventing compression and internal disturbance.

### 2.1.3 Outside Clearance Ratio

$$\text{Outside Clearance} = \frac{D_o - D_{body}}{D_o} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

- **Ideal:** Outside clearance should be **zero or slightly negative** to avoid soil inward displacement.

### 2.1.4 Recovery Ratio (Rr)

$$R_r = \frac{L_{sample}}{L_{penetration}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

- $R_r = 100\%$ → Perfect recovery
- $R_r < 100\%$ → Sample compression
- $R_r > 100\%$ → Sample swelling (rebound) — indicates overstressed clay

---

## 2.2 Thin Walled Sampler – Operational Sequence

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Drill to clean hole:** Advance borehole to a depth **less than the sampler length** (typically one sampler length above the sampling depth) using augers.
2. **Clean the base:** Use a cleanout bucket or bailer to remove loose cuttings; maintain drilling fluid to stabilize.
3. **Attach sampler to rods:** Connect Shelby tube to drill rod string with a check valve head.
4. **Push the sampler:** Apply **continuous, smooth hydraulic pressure** (not hammer blows) at a rate of approximately **25 mm/sec** for a distance equal to the sampler length minus **75 mm** (to avoid over-pushing).
5. **Rotate and extract:** After 5 minutes, rotate 1-2 turns to shear the sample at the base; withdraw slowly.
6. **Seal ends:** Wax the ends immediately, label with depth, date, and project ID.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The Shelby tube is indispensable for *unconfined compression*, *triaxial (CU/CD/UU)*, *consolidation*, and *permeability* tests on cohesive soils. Without it, the laboratory parameters (c, φ, Cc, Cv) would be unreliable.

---

## 2.3 Piston Sampler – Hydraulic Mechanics

### 2.3.1 Hvorslev Piston Sampler Geometry

The Hvorslev sampler consists of:
- A thin-walled sampling tube
- A piston that fits tightly inside the tube
- A piston rod that extends up to the surface
- A check valve above the piston

### 2.3.2 Operating Principle (Why it Works)

When the piston is held fixed at the borehole base:
- The sample tube advances *past* the piston.
- Vacuum develops above the piston as it is held stationary.
- Atmospheric pressure plus the vacuum assists in retaining the sample.
- Soil cannot enter the tube from the top (sealed by piston).
- Soil cannot slough from the borehole sides (tube tip is closed by the piston position).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Vacuum Effect:**
> Consider a syringe: hold the plunger fixed and pull the barrel down. Negative pressure (vacuum) develops inside. Atmospheric pressure pushes fluid up. In a piston sampler, the *soil* is the fluid being pushed up by the hydrostatic head plus vacuum, ensuring full recovery.

### 2.3.3 Osterberg Sampler Variation

A modification uses compressed gas instead of a mechanical piston rod—particularly useful for **deep-water offshore sampling** where long piston rods become impractical.

---

## 2.4 Split Spoon Sampler – SPT Mechanics

### 2.4.1 Standard Dimensions (IS 2131 / IS 2132 / ASTM D1586)

| Component | Dimension |
|-----------|-----------|
| Outside diameter | **51 mm** |
| Inside diameter | **35 mm** |
| Length of barrel | **457 mm** (or 450 mm) |
| Cutting shoe length | **50 mm** (tapered) |
| Sampler weight (empty) | ~7.2 kg |
| Hammer mass | **63.5 kg** |
| Drop height | **760 mm** |

### 2.4.2 Driving Procedure

1. Lower the split spoon to the bottom of the cleaned borehole.
2. Record the blows for the **initial 150 mm seating drive** (this is **discarded**).
3. Drive the sampler **300 mm further**; count the blows.
4. The **SPT N-value** = blows for the 300 mm penetration.
5. Extract the spoon, separate the two halves, and retrieve the disturbed sample for visual classification and moisture content.

### 2.4.3 Overburden Correction (Kicking the N-value Up)

$$N_{corrected} = N \times C_N = N \times 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_v}\right)$$

where:
- $C_N$ = Overburden correction factor
- $\sigma'_v$ = Effective vertical overburden pressure (kPa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students apply the correction *after* correlating N to bearing capacity. The correct sequence is: **(1) Correct N → (2) Correlate to φ or qc → (3) Compute allowable bearing capacity**.

---

## 2.5 Methods for Sampling Sand Below the Water Table

### 2.5.1 Method A – Borehole Method (Open Borehole with Water Head)

**Principle:** Maintain the water level inside the borehole **at or above** the natural water table at all times. Lower a thin-walled or piston sampler to the bottom of the hole; push the sampler into the undisturbed sand.

**Critical Step:** If the water level inside the borehole drops below the natural water table, the effective stress becomes negative, the sand **boils** (quick condition), and no sample can be obtained.

**Analogy:** Like keeping a swimming pool water level above a hose outlet—otherwise air enters the hose and water flow stops.

### 2.5.2 Method B – Piston Sampler Method

Already described in §2.3. Particularly suitable for **loose to medium dense submerged sands**, achieving recovery ratios of 90–100%.

### 2.5.3 Method C – Sand Pumping Method

**Principle:**
- A perforated tip is lowered to the sampling depth.
- Water is pumped up through a hollow stem, creating suction.
- Sand is sucked up into a collection chamber at the surface.
- The sand is recompacted in the lab to estimate **relative density**.

**Limitation:** The sample is fully disturbed and re-molded; only gradation and density estimates are reliable.

### 2.5.4 Method D – Bailer Method (Wash-Boring + Bailer)

**Principle:**
- Use rotary or wash boring to advance the hole.
- Lower a **bailer** (a hollow tube with a check valve at the bottom) on a wireline.
- The check valve opens on impact, allowing sand-water slurry to enter.
- Pull up; the valve closes, retaining the sand.

**Use:** Coarse sands, gravelly sands, and bouldery strata where pushing a Shelby tube is impossible.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $A_r = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2}\times 100$ | Sampler quality check | % |
| 2 | $C_r = \dfrac{D_i - D_{tip}}{D_{tip}}\times 100$ | Inside clearance | % |
| 3 | $R_r = \dfrac{L_{sample}}{L_{drive}}\times 100$ | Recovery check | % |
| 4 | $N_{cor} = N \cdot 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_v)$ | SPT overburden correction | — |
| 5 | $\phi \approx 27.1 + 0.3 N_{cor} - 0.00054 (N_{cor})^2$ | Schmertmann correlation for sand | degrees |
| 6 | $q_{c,ult} \approx 0.4 N_{cor}$ (kPa) | Approx. static cone from SPT | kPa |
| 7 | $S_{u,field} \approx f \cdot N$ | Undrained shear (clay, $f$=4–15) | kPa |
| 8 | $\gamma_{d,field} = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{1+e}$ | Used post-lab recompaction | kN/m³ |
| 9 | $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | Void ratio from density | — |
| 10 | $D_r \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{N_{cor}}{C \cdot \sigma'_v}}$ | Relative density (Skempton) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall — Pipe Symbol in Tables:**
> In your written exams, never use the vertical bar `|` in equation cells. Use parentheses or write "absolute" instead. (Our table already complies.)

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundation Design:** Sampler-derived parameters feed into bearing capacity (Terzaghi, Meyerhof) and settlement (Schmertmann) equations.
- **Liquefaction Assessment:** SPT N-values corrected for overburden and fines content are the *primary input* for the *Seed-Idriss simplified procedure*.
- **Slope Stability:** Undisturbed Shelby tube samples provide c and φ for limit-equilibrium analysis.
- **Earthquake Engineering:** Vs (shear wave velocity) is often correlated to N via empirical relations in seismic site classification.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Area Ratio Formula

The area ratio represents the fraction of soil cross-section that is *displaced* (compressed sideways) when the sampler is pushed in. A higher Ar means more disturbance.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a thin-walled sampler in cross-section. Let:
- $D_o$ = outside diameter
- $D_i$ = inside diameter
- $A_o$ = full outside cross-sectional area = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_o^2$
- $A_i$ = inside hollow cross-section = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2$

The *annular area* of the wall (the displaced soil zone) is:

$$A_{wall} = A_o - A_i = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)$$

The *effective sample area* (the cylindrical plug of soil entering the tube) is:

$$A_{sample} = A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^2$$

### 3.1.2 Definition of Area Ratio

By definition:

$$A_r = \frac{A_{wall}}{A_{sample}}$$

Substituting the geometric expressions:

$$A_r = \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_i^2}$$

The factor $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ cancels out:

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2}$$

To express as a percentage (standard KTU convention):

$$\boxed{\;A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100 \;\; (\%)\;}$$

### 3.1.3 Engineering Interpretation

- If $A_r \to 0$, the sampler is *infinitely thin* — idealized no disturbance.
- If $A_r > 15\%$, the wall is too thick, displacing a large soil annulus that disturbs adjacent soil.
- For a standard Shelby tube, $D_o = 50.8$ mm, $D_i = 47.6$ mm → $A_r \approx 14.3\%$ (borderline, hence preferred only for soft clays).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inside Clearance Ratio

### 3.2.1 Setup

The cutting tip of a sampler may have a slightly *larger* inside diameter than the body of the tube. This intentional enlargement allows the soil to swell laterally as wall friction is released, reducing sample compression.

Let:
- $D_i$ = inside diameter of the body
- $D_e$ = inside diameter of the cutting edge (sometimes called $D_{tip}$)

### 3.2.2 Definition

$$C_r = \frac{D_e - D_i}{D_i} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

A small positive $C_r$ (typically 0.5–3%) provides relief for the soil to expand slightly.

### 3.2.3 KTU Worked Example

**Problem:** A Shelby tube has a body inside diameter of 50.0 mm and a cutting edge inside diameter of 51.0 mm. Calculate the inside clearance ratio.

**Solution:**

$$C_r = \frac{51.0 - 50.0}{50.0} \times 100 = 2.0\%$$

This falls within the ideal 0.5–3% range — **acceptable**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Recovery Ratio

### 3.3.1 Setup

Let $L_p$ be the length of sampler penetration into the soil. Let $L_s$ be the length of sample actually recovered in the tube.

### 3.3.2 Definition

$$\boxed{\;R_r = \frac{L_s}{L_p} \times 100 \;\; (\%)\;}$$

### 3.3.3 KTU Worked Example

**Problem:** A Shelby tube is pushed **600 mm** into a clay layer. Upon extraction, the sample length is measured as **570 mm**. Compute the recovery ratio and comment.

**Solution:**

$$R_r = \frac{570}{600} \times 100 = 95.0\%$$

**Comment:** $R_r < 100\%$ indicates sample **compression**, suggesting the clay is overconsolidated or the inside clearance is inadequate. The sample still meets KTU acceptance criteria (≥ 90% for clays).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: SPT N-value Correction

### 3.4.1 Empirical Basis

Standard Penetration Test results are sensitive to overburden pressure. Liao & Whitman (1986) proposed the overburden correction factor $C_N$:

$$C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{\sigma'_v}\right)$$

where $\sigma'_v$ is the effective vertical stress in kPa, bounded such that $0.5 \leq C_N \leq 2.0$.

### 3.4.2 The Corrected N-value

$$N_{cor} = N \cdot C_N$$

### 3.4.3 Numerical Example (Worked)

**Problem:** SPT conducted at 10 m depth in saturated sand with water table at 2 m. Unit weight above water table = 17 kN/m³; below water table = 19 kN/m³. Recorded N = 18. Calculate corrected N and estimate friction angle using Schmertmann's correlation.

**Step 1: Compute $\sigma'_v$ at 10 m depth.**

$$\sigma'_v = 17 \times 2 + (19 - 9.81) \times 8$$

$$= 34.0 + (9.19)(8) = 34.0 + 73.5 = 107.5 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_N$.**

$$C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{107.5}\right)$$

$$= 0.77 \times \log_{10}(18.605)$$

$$= 0.77 \times 1.2698 = 0.9778$$

**Step 3: Apply bounds.** $0.5 \leq 0.9778 \leq 2.0$ — **acceptable as-is**.

**Step 4: Corrected N.**

$$N_{cor} = 18 \times 0.9778 = 17.6 \approx 18$$

**Step 5: Friction angle (Schmertmann, 1975).**

$$\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 N_{cor} - 0.00054 (N_{cor})^2$$

$$= 27.1 + 0.3(17.6) - 0.00054(17.6)^2$$

$$= 27.1 + 5.28 - 0.00054 \times 309.76$$

$$= 27.1 + 5.28 - 0.1673 = 32.21^\circ$$

**Result:** $\phi \approx 32^\circ$ — typical for medium-dense sand.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Sampler Selection

| Soil Type | Best Sampler | Why? | Key Parameter |
|-----------|--------------|------|---------------|
| Soft, sensitive clay | Piston (Hvorslev) | Prevents loss, full recovery | $A_r < 10\%$, $R_r \approx 100\%$ |
| Medium stiff clay | Thin walled (Shelby) | Adequate, cheaper | $A_r \leq 12\%$ |
| Stiff, fissured clay | Denison or Pitcher | Double-tube, drilling mud | Cores large clasts |
| Loose sand below WT | Piston + water head | Vacuum-assisted recovery | $C_N$ applied |
| Medium-dense sand | SPT (Split spoon) | Empirical N-value | $N_{cor}$ → $D_r$ |
| Gravelly sand | Bailer / wash boring | Cannot push thin wall | Sample recompacted |
| Sensitive marine clay | Osterberg (gas piston) | Long-stroke, no piston rod | High recovery |
| Coarse boulder fill | Bulk sampling (excavation) | No drilling method works | Visual classification |

---

## 3.6 KTU Board Problem Worked Out — Piston Sampler Area Ratio

**Problem:** A piston sampler has an outside diameter of 75 mm and a wall thickness of 2.0 mm. Determine whether it qualifies as an "undisturbed" sampler per IS 2132.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute inside diameter.

$$D_i = D_o - 2t = 75 - 2(2.0) = 71 \; \text{mm}$$

Step 2: Compute area ratio.

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{75^2 - 71^2}{71^2} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{5625 - 5041}{5041} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{584}{5041} \times 100 = 11.58\%$$

Step 3: Comment.

Since $A_r = 11.58\%$ is **just above 10%**, the sampler is on the borderline. For *sensitive* clays, this is **not acceptable**; for *medium stiff* clays, it can be used. Engineers typically prefer $A_r \leq 10\%$ for undisturbed sampling.

---

## 3.7 KTU Board Problem — Piston Sampler Recovery Mechanics

**Problem:** A piston sampler advances 800 mm into a soft marine clay. After extraction, the sample measures 792 mm. The effective stress at the sampling depth is 50 kPa. Calculate:
(a) Recovery ratio
(b) Comment on whether the piston sampler is operating effectively

**Solution (a):**

$$R_r = \frac{792}{800} \times 100 = 99.0\%$$

**Solution (b):**

With $R_r = 99\%$, the piston sampler is **operating almost ideally**. The minor 1% loss can be attributed to:
- Slight adhesion of clay to the cutting tip
- Lubrication effect of the piston seal
- Elastic rebound after withdrawal

The result confirms the piston sampler's suitability for soft, sensitive marine clays where undisturbed sampling is critical.
