# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]

**Define Area Ratio. State its significance in soil sampling.**

**Model Answer:**

The *Area Ratio* ($A_r$) is defined as the ratio of the cross-sectional area of the soil displaced by the sampler wall to the cross-sectional area of the sample, expressed as a percentage.

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

**Significance:**
1. It quantifies the *degree of disturbance* caused by a sampler.
2. Lower $A_r$ means less soil is pushed aside → sample is closer to in-situ state.
3. For undisturbed sampling of clays, $A_r$ should be **less than 10%**.
4. If $A_r > 15\%$, the sample is classified as disturbed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Limit (KTU):** Keep answer to ~80–100 words for 3 marks. Always include the formula.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]

**What is a piston sampler? State any two advantages over a thin walled sampler.**

**Model Answer:**

A *piston sampler* is a thin-walled sampling tube equipped with a piston that remains stationary at the borehole base while the tube advances past it. A vacuum is created that holds the sample in place and prevents borehole sloughing.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Higher recovery ratio (close to 100%)** in very soft, sensitive, or cohesionless soils.
2. **Prevention of soil entry from the top** — eliminates contamination and loss due to washout.
3. **Vacuum effect** assists sample retention during withdrawal.
4. Suitable for **deep sampling (>30 m)** in marine clays.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state at least 2 advantages for a 3-mark question. A 1-line answer fetches only 1 mark.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS EACH — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Two 14-mark questions are asked, and the student answers **one**. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A — Thin Walled Sampler Focus [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **Thin Walled Sampler (Shelby Tube)**. State its components, operating procedure, and the engineering parameters required to assess sample quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Components:**
1. **Cutting edge** — sharpened bottom of the tube with reduced wall thickness and slightly enlarged inside diameter (inside clearance 0.5–3%).
2. **Sample tube** — seamless cold-drawn steel, 50.8 mm, 76.2 mm, or 101.6 mm ID; 1.24–3.25 mm wall thickness.
3. **Head assembly** — connects to drill rod; contains a check valve to release water during pushing and create partial vacuum during withdrawal.
4. **Retaining shoes / couplings** — connect to the drill string.

**Operating Procedure:**
1. Drill the borehole to a depth one sampler length above the desired sampling depth.
2. Clean the borehole base; remove cuttings; maintain water level.
3. Attach the Shelby tube to the drill rod with the head assembly.
4. Lower the sampler to the borehole base; rest on undisturbed soil.
5. Apply **continuous hydraulic pressure** at ~25 mm/sec; push the tube to the required depth (avoid over-push; stop 75 mm before full length).
6. Wait 5–10 minutes; rotate 1–2 turns to shear the sample base.
7. Withdraw slowly.
8. **Seal both ends with molten wax**; label with depth, project, and date.

**Sample Quality Parameters:**
- **Area Ratio** ($A_r$): should be < 10% for undisturbed.
- **Inside Clearance Ratio** ($C_r$): 0.5–3% ideal.
- **Recovery Ratio** ($R_r$): should be 95–105% for clays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sketch required:** Even a simple hand-drawn block diagram with labels (Cutting Edge, Tube, Head, Check Valve, Drill Rod) fetches 2 marks extra in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A thin walled sampler has an outside diameter of 50.8 mm and a wall thickness of 1.65 mm. Compute the **Area Ratio** and **Inside Clearance Ratio**, given that the cutting tip ID is 48.6 mm. Comment on the sample quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D_o = 50.8$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 1.65$ mm
- $D_i = D_o - 2t = 50.8 - 3.3 = 47.5$ mm
- $D_{tip} = 48.6$ mm

**Step 1: Area Ratio**

$$A_r = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{50.8^2 - 47.5^2}{47.5^2} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{2580.64 - 2256.25}{2256.25} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{324.39}{2256.25} \times 100 = 14.38\%$$

**Step 2: Inside Clearance Ratio**

$$C_r = \frac{D_{tip} - D_i}{D_i} \times 100 = \frac{48.6 - 47.5}{47.5} \times 100 = 2.32\%$$

**Step 3: Comment**

- $A_r = 14.38\% > 10\%$ → **disturbed sampler**; not suitable for sensitive clays.
- $C_r = 2.32\%$ → within acceptable 0.5–3% range.
- **Recommendation:** Use a sampler with a thinner wall (e.g., $t = 1.24$ mm) to reduce $A_r$ to about 8% for undisturbed sampling in clays.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse $D_i$ with $D_{tip}$. Remember: $D_i$ is the *body* inside diameter; $D_{tip}$ is the *cutting edge* inside diameter. Markers *will* deduct 1 mark for this.

---

### QUESTION B — SPT and Sand Sampling Focus [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** with a sketch. List the **standard dimensions** of the split spoon sampler and explain the procedure for recording the N-value.

**Model Answer:**

**Description:**
The Standard Penetration Test is an in-situ dynamic test performed inside a borehole. A split spoon sampler is driven into the soil at the bottom of the borehole by a **63.5 kg hammer** dropped freely from a height of **760 mm**. The number of blows required to penetrate the sampler 300 mm (after an initial 150 mm seating drive) is the **SPT N-value**.

**Standard Dimensions (IS 2131 / ASTM D1586):**
| Component | Dimension |
|-----------|-----------|
| Outside diameter | 51 mm |
| Inside diameter | 35 mm |
| Length of barrel | 457 mm |
| Cutting shoe length | 50 mm |
| Sampler weight | ~7.2 kg |
| Hammer weight | 63.5 kg |
| Drop height | 760 mm |

**Procedure:**
1. Drill borehole to the desired test depth; clean the base.
2. Connect the split spoon to the drill rod and lower to the borehole base.
3. Mark the rod in 3 segments of 150 mm each.
4. Drive the sampler with the hammer; **count and discard** the blows for the first 150 mm (seating drive).
5. Record the blows for the next **300 mm** — this is the N-value.
6. Continue to record for an additional 150 mm (refusal if > 50 blows per 150 mm).
7. Withdraw the spoon; open the split halves; classify the soil visually; preserve in airtight jar.

**Significance:**
- Empirical correlations exist between N and φ (sand), $S_u$ (clay), $D_r$ (relative density), and liquefaction resistance.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** An SPT was conducted at a depth of 6 m in a sandy site. The water table is at 2 m below ground level. The soil above the water table has $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³; below the water table, $\gamma_{sat} = 19$ kN/m³. The recorded N-value is 24. Determine:
(i) The corrected N-value using the Liao-Whitman overburden correction.
(ii) The friction angle using the Schmertmann correlation.
(iii) Comment on the consistency / density of the sand.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Effective Vertical Stress at 6 m depth**

$$\sigma'_v = \gamma \cdot z_1 + (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w)(z_2)$$

$$= 17 \times 2 + (19 - 9.81) \times 4$$

$$= 34.0 + 9.19 \times 4 = 34.0 + 36.76 = 70.76 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2: Overburden Correction Factor**

$$C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2000}{70.76}\right) = 0.77 \log_{10}(28.27)$$

$$= 0.77 \times 1.4513 = 1.117$$

Within bounds (0.5 ≤ 1.117 ≤ 2.0) ✓

**Step 3: Corrected N-value**

$$N_{cor} = N \times C_N = 24 \times 1.117 = 26.81 \approx 27$$

**Step 4: Friction Angle (Schmertmann, 1975)**

$$\phi = 27.1 + 0.3(27) - 0.00054(27)^2$$

$$= 27.1 + 8.1 - 0.00054 \times 729$$

$$= 27.1 + 8.1 - 0.394 = 34.81^\circ$$

$$\phi \approx 35^\circ$$

**Step 5: Comment on Density**

By Terzaghi-Peck classification (using corrected N):
- $0$–$4$: Very loose
- $4$–$10$: Loose
- $10$–$30$: Medium dense
- $30$–$50$: Dense
- $>50$: Very dense

With $N_{cor} = 27$, the sand is classified as **Medium Dense to Dense**, with $\phi \approx 35^\circ$, suitable for most shallow foundations with allowable bearing pressures of 150–250 kPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert to *effective* stress (using total stress instead). → Lose 2 marks.
> - **Mistake 2:** Applying correction *after* computing φ. → Lose 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 3:** Not stating the $C_N$ bounds. → Lose 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention sand density classification. → Lose 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 5:** Using N in the Schmertmann formula instead of $N_{cor}$. → Lose 2 marks.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones for Sampler Topics:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Area Ratio with Recovery Ratio.** Both are percentages but mean very different things. Always label clearly.
> 2. **Not drawing a sketch** for "describe the sampler" questions. Even a rough sketch fetches 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up diameters:** $D_o$ vs $D_i$ vs $D_{tip}$. The valuation key explicitly tests this.
> 4. **Skipping units.** kPa, mm, % — every numerical answer needs units.
> 5. **Forgetting the *why*.** Just plugging numbers into formulas loses 30% of the marks. Always write 1 line of *interpretation* after computation.
> 6. **Skipping the sample sealing step** in the operating procedure. The 2 marks allocated for "procedure" expect wax sealing, labeling, and transport to be mentioned.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **Thin Walled Sampler** has $A_r < 10\%$ for undisturbed sampling; standard sizes are 50.8, 76.2, 101.6 mm OD.
- ✅ A **Piston Sampler** uses a vacuum principle; achieves ~100% recovery in soft clays.
- ✅ A **Split Spoon Sampler** is **51 mm OD × 35 mm ID × 457 mm long**; SPT N = blows for **300 mm** penetration after **150 mm** seating.
- ✅ SPT uses a **63.5 kg hammer** dropped from **760 mm**.
- ✅ **Area Ratio formula:** $A_r = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_i^2} \times 100 \; (\%)$.
- ✅ **Inside Clearance:** $C_r = \dfrac{D_{tip} - D_i}{D_i} \times 100$; ideal = 0.5–3%.
- ✅ **Recovery Ratio:** $R_r = \dfrac{L_{sample}}{L_{penetration}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Overburden Correction (Liao-Whitman):** $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}(2000/\sigma'_v)$, with $0.5 \leq C_N \leq 2.0$.
- ✅ **Schmertmann (1975) for φ:** $\phi = 27.1 + 0.3 N_{cor} - 0.00054 N_{cor}^2$.
- ✅ For **sand sampling below water table**, maintain the borehole water level at or above the natural water table; otherwise quick condition occurs.
- ✅ **Bailer method** suits coarse gravelly sands; **piston method** suits loose submerged sands.
- ✅ Shelby tube samples are sealed with **molten wax**; the check valve prevents vacuum loss during withdrawal.
- ✅ Split spoon provides **disturbed** samples — used for *classification* and *N-value*, NOT for strength tests on cohesive soils.
- ✅ Samplers are selected based on (a) soil type, (b) in-situ density/consistency, (c) water table, and (d) required test type.
- ✅ Recovery ratio < 100% → compression; > 100% → swelling (overconsolidated clay).
- ✅ SPT correlations are **empirical** — always state the source (Schmertmann, Terzaghi-Peck, Skempton) in answers.
- ✅ **Quick condition** = sand boils = liquefaction-like failure in borehole; caused by upward seepage when water head inside hole < water table.
- ✅ For cohesionless soils below WT, *piston sampler with maintained water head* is the gold standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soil Investigation Report – Presentation of Soil Exploration Data, Bore Log and Soil Profile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soil Investigation Report – Presentation of Soil Exploration Data, Bore Log and Soil Profile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Soil Investigation Report (SIR) – Formal Definition

A **Soil Investigation Report (SIR)** is a comprehensive, structured engineering document that systematically presents all data, observations, laboratory test results, analyses, interpretations, and design recommendations gathered during a geotechnical site investigation program. It serves as the **official communication medium** between the geotechnical consultant and the structural/civil design team, and is the primary document used for the safe and economic design of foundations, earthworks, and sub-structures.

According to **IS 1892 (1979)** – *Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations*, and **IS 2131 (1981)** – *Method for Standard Penetration Test for Soils*, the report must be a self-contained document that enables the design engineer to verify the recommendations independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET753 – Module 4):**
> The report is the *final deliverable* of any site investigation. A poorly presented SIR — even if backed by excellent field data — is treated as a failure in KTU valuation. **Presentation clarity carries marks equal to technical content.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor does not just scribble notes — they produce a **formal Medical Report** containing:
- Patient identification (age, weight, history)
- Symptoms observed
- Diagnostic tests conducted (blood test, X-ray)
- Test results (numerical values)
- Diagnosis (interpretation)
- Prescribed treatment (recommendation)
- Precautions and follow-up

A **Soil Investigation Report is the "Medical Report" of the ground.** The "patient" is the proposed construction site, the "doctor" is the geotechnical engineer, the "diagnostic tests" are boreholes, SPTs, and lab tests, and the "prescription" is the foundation recommendation.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a cardiologist cannot prescribe without an ECG, a geotechnical engineer cannot recommend a foundation type without bore logs and soil profiles. The **Bore Log is the ECG of the ground.**

---

## 1.3 Bore Log – Formal Definition

A **Bore Log** (also called **Boring Log** or **Borehole Log**) is a chronologically prepared, standardized graphical and tabular record of the subsurface materials, groundwater conditions, and in-situ test results encountered at a specific borehole location from the ground surface to the termination depth.

It is the **fundamental building block** of any soil investigation. Every borehole drilled on the site produces one bore log, and the collective bore logs form the **soil profile** of the site.

> [!NOTE]
> A bore log is essentially a **vertical column diagram** showing what the ground "looks like" from top to bottom at a single point, with all relevant engineering properties annotated alongside.

---

## 1.4 Soil Profile – Formal Definition

A **Soil Profile** is a one-dimensional vertical section (or a two-dimensional cross-section) of the subsurface showing the **stratigraphy, thickness, depth, and lateral extent** of various soil/rock layers at a particular location or along a line of boreholes.

- **One-dimensional (1-D) Soil Profile:** Vertical column for a single borehole (essentially the same as the bore log).
- **Two-dimensional (2-D) Soil Profile / Cross-Section:** A section drawn by joining the corresponding layer boundaries of multiple boreholes aligned along a line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A soil profile transforms isolated borehole data into a **continuous picture of the subsurface**, allowing the designer to visualize layer continuity, pinch-outs, and the position of the water table relative to the foundation level.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical/Standard Metrics Used in Presentation

The following are universally adopted standard units and conventions:

- **Depth:** measured in **metres (m)** below ground level (BGL) — never below datum in standard SIRs.
- **Elevation:** in **metres above Mean Sea Level (m AMSL)** for project-wide correlation.
- **SPT N-value:** dimensionless blow count per **300 mm** penetration (after seating drive of 150 mm).
- **Sample type symbols:** U (Undisturbed), D (Disturbed), W (Water), B (Bulk).
- **Standard scale for bore logs:** 1 cm = 1 m (vertical), 1 cm = 0.5 m for detailed soft-soil logs.
- **Standard sheet size:** A3 or A2 (folded to A4) for engineering drawings.
- **Stratum boundaries:** drawn to a precision of **± 0.05 m** (50 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Penetration Test (SPT)** N-value correction factors (depth, dilatancy, overburden, etc.) are presented in tabular form in the SIR. The corrected N-value is denoted as **N₆₀** or **N₁₆₀** depending on the hammer efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Comprehensive Soil Investigation Report (SIR)

The SIR is structured into well-defined sections. Below is the logical break-down of every section with the **"Why" and "How"** behind each.

### A. Front Matter
| Sub-component | Purpose (Why) | Content (How) |
|---|---|---|
| Title Page | Project identification | Project name, location, client, consultant logo, report number, date |
| Table of Contents | Navigation | List of sections, figures, tables, appendices with page numbers |
| List of Drawings/Plates | Quick reference | Bore log plates, soil profile plates, test pit sketches, site plan |
| Executive Summary | Decision-maker's overview | 1–2 page summary of key findings and recommendations |

### B. Project Information
- **Project description:** type of structure (building, bridge, dam, highway, tunnel), loading intensity, footprint area.
- **Site location:** village, taluk, district, state; GPS coordinates; nearest landmark.
- **Topography & drainage:** general slope, surface water bodies, evidence of erosion.
- **Climatic conditions:** annual rainfall, temperature range, water table fluctuations (critical in Kerala's monsoonal climate).

### C. Geology of the Area
- Regional geology from **Geological Survey of India (GSI)** publications.
- Typical soil/rock formations expected.
- **Seismic zone** as per **IS 1893 (Part 1): 2016** — Kerala falls in Zone II and III.

### D. Scope of Investigation
- Purpose of the investigation.
- Number, depth, and location of boreholes.
- Type of in-situ tests (SPT, CPT, DCPT, VST, pressuremeter).
- Type and quantity of laboratory tests.
- Standards/codes followed (IS, ASTM, BS).

### E. Field Investigation Details
- **Drilling method:** auger, rotary, wash boring, percussion (per IS 1892).
- **Equipment used:** rig type, casing size, sampler type.
- **Borehole termination criteria:** refusal on rock, specified depth, or practical limit.
- **Sampling standards:** IS 2132 (disturbed), IS 2133 (undisturbed), IS 2720 (laboratory).

### F. Laboratory Test Results
- Classification: sieve analysis, hydrometer, Atterberg limits (IS 2720 Parts 4, 5, 6).
- Strength: UCS, triaxial, direct shear, vane shear (IS 2720 Parts 10, 11, 12, 13).
- Compressibility: oedometer consolidation (IS 2720 Part 15).
- Permeability, chemical (pH, sulphates, chlorides) for corrosion assessment.

### G. Bore Logs (Detailed Plates)
- One plate per borehole, A3 size, with all sub-data (see Section 2.3).

### H. Soil Profile(s)
- 1-D profile for each borehole.
- 2-D cross-section(s) connecting multiple boreholes.

### I. Groundwater Conditions
- Depth of water table at time of drilling.
- Seasonal variation (where data available).
- Piezometer readings for confined aquifers.

### J. Analysis and Recommendations
- **Bearing capacity** (safe and allowable) — using IS 6403 / IS 8009.
- **Settlement** estimates — using IS 8009 (Part 1) and Schmertmann's method.
- **Foundation type** recommended — shallow (isolated, raft) or deep (pile).
- **Liquefaction potential** for seismic zones.
- **Earthwork** recommendations — excavation stability, dewatering, shoring.

### K. Limitations
- The report is valid only for the *as-investigated* location and conditions.
- Latent defects, seasonal variations, and construction-induced changes must be verified during execution.

### L. Appendices
- Bore log plates.
- Laboratory test result sheets.
- Calibration certificates of equipment.
- Photographs of field work.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The "Limitations" section is often skipped by students, but **it is a mandatory KTU requirement** and carries at least 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## 2.2 Standard Presentation Formats

Data can be presented in **three forms**:

| Form | When Used | Tool/Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Tabular** | Laboratory results, summary tables | Spreadsheets, IS-coded tables |
| **Graphical (Bore Log)** | Individual borehole data | Standardized log sheet |
| **Graphical (Soil Profile)** | Cross-sections, layer continuity | Profile drawing, often CAD |

---

## 2.3 Detailed Bore Log Information Content

A standard bore log contains the following information (per **IS 1892:1979** and **IS 2131:1981** conventions):

1. **Header Block:**
   - Project name, borehole ID (e.g., BH-1, BH-2A), client, consultant.
   - Coordinates (Easting, Northing) and Reduced Level (RL) of the borehole collar.
   - Date of commencement and completion.
   - Method of boring, type of rig, casing diameter.
   - Name of the geologist/engineer supervising.

2. **Body of the Log (running vertically downward):**
   - **Depth column** (in metres, BGL).
   - **Thickness column** of each stratum.
   - **Description column** (visual + tactile identification, IS classification symbol).
   - **Graphic column** (graphical pattern / soil symbol).
   - **SPT N-value column** (blows per 300 mm with depth).
   - **Sample column** (type, depth, recovery %).
   - **Moisture column** (Dry, Moist, Wet, Saturated).
   - **Groundwater symbol and depth.**
   - **Remarks** (colour, odour, organic content, gravel, etc.).

3. **Footer Block:**
   - Termination depth and reason.
   - Backfilling details.
   - Reference to next borehole.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is a presentation/reporting topic, the relevant formulas are mostly for **data interpretation and correction** (which are also presented in the report).

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_c = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_R \cdot C_S \cdot C_B$ | Corrected N-value | dimensionless | SPT correction (per IS 2131 / Bowles 1996) |
| 2 | $C_N = 0.77 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2000}{\sigma'_v}\right)$ | Overburden correction factor | dimensionless | $\sigma'_v$ in kPa, for $\sigma'_v \leq 200$ kPa |
| 3 | $C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}}$ | Liao & Whitman (1986) form | dimensionless | $P_a = 100$ kPa atmospheric pressure |
| 4 | $\sigma'_v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i \cdot z_i$ | Effective vertical stress at depth z | kPa | $\gamma_i$ is layer unit weight, $z_i$ is layer thickness |
| 5 | $\gamma_b = \dfrac{G_s + e}{1 + e} \cdot \gamma_w$ | Buoyant unit weight | kN/m³ | Below water table |
| 6 | $q_u = 2 \cdot c_u$ | UCS–cu relationship (φ=0 condition) | kPa | For clays |
| 7 | $e = \dfrac{G_s \cdot \gamma_w}{\gamma_d} - 1$ | Void ratio from dry density | dimensionless | Lab-test derivation |
| 8 | $m_v = \dfrac{\Delta e}{\Delta \sigma' \cdot (1 + e_0)}$ | Coefficient of volume compressibility | m²/kN | Settlement calculation |
| 9 | $S = m_v \cdot \Delta \sigma' \cdot H$ | One-dimensional primary settlement | m | For normally consolidated clay |
| 10 | $q_{net,ult} = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c$ | Net ultimate bearing capacity (general) | kPa | IS 6403 form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The corrected SPT N-value is used to **classify the compactness/relative density** of granular soils and to **estimate bearing capacity and settlement** in the report's recommendation section. Always present $N$ and $N_c$ (corrected) **side-by-side** in the bore log for engineering transparency.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Bore Logs and Soil Profiles

- **Foundation selection:** Layer thickness, type, and depth directly drive choice between shallow and deep foundations.
- **Bearing capacity estimation:** SPT-based correlations (Meyerhof, IS 6403) require the N-value column.
- **Settlement prediction:** Compression index $C_c$ and $m_v$ come from the oedometer; layer thickness comes from the soil profile.
- **Dewatering design:** Groundwater depth and permeability values enable estimation of seepage and dewatering well capacity.
- **Liquefaction assessment:** $N_{160}$ corrected values, fines content, and water table depth are all derived from bore log data.
- **Communication tool:** Enables peer review, regulatory approval, and contractor planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world tip:** In KTU viva-voce, examiners often ask, *"If you are given only a bore log and no lab data, what design decisions can you still make?"* The answer: bearing capacity from $N_c$, compactness, depth of water table, and qualitative settlement category. This shows how **the bore log alone is engineering gold**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Example

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Bore Log (Analytical Procedure)

### Step 1 — Project Information Compilation
Gather all front-end data:

$$\text{Project ID} = \text{PRJ-2024-KER-001}, \quad \text{Borehole ID} = \text{BH-04}$$

$$\text{Collar RL} = +12.50 \text{ m AMSL}, \quad \text{Datum} = \text{MSL}$$

### Step 2 — Stratigraphic Recording
For each layer, record **four descriptors** (per **IS 1498:1970** classification):

1. **Colour** (e.g., dark grey, reddish brown, mottled yellow).
2. **Soil type** (based on field identification: gravel, sand, silt, clay — and per IS 1498).
3. **Consistency / Density** (from SPT N or field penetration resistance).
4. **Structure / Origin** (laminated, fissured, alluvial, lateritic, residual).

**Decision rule for naming a layer:**

$$
\text{Layer Name} = \text{[Colour]} + \text{[Soil Type]} + \text{[Consistency]}_{\text{(if clay)}} \text{ or } \text{[Density]}_{\text{(if sand)}}
$$

**Example output:** "Dark grey silty CLAY of medium stiffness (CL)"

### Step 3 — SPT N-value Recording

SPT is conducted at every **1.5 m** interval (or at every change of stratum). The raw record is **three numbers**:

$$
N_{\text{raw}} = N_1 + N_2 + N_3
$$

where $N_1$ = blows for first 150 mm (seating), $N_2$ and $N_3$ are the two 150 mm drives. The **reported N-value is**:

$$
N = N_2 + N_3
$$

**Example (field record):**
- Seating: 4 blows / 150 mm
- Drive 1: 8 blows / 150 mm
- Drive 2: 10 blows / 150 mm
- Total penetration: 450 mm

$$
\therefore N = 8 + 10 = 18
$$

### Step 4 — Sample Recording
Each sample is tagged with a **unique ID** and a **depth**:

$$
\text{Sample ID} = \text{U-04-2.50} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \text{Undisturbed, BH-04, depth 2.50 m}
$$

### Step 5 — Groundwater Recording
The depth of water is recorded with a **water-level indicator** within 24 hours of drilling completion:

$$
z_w = 3.20 \text{ m BGL} \;\; (\text{at the time of measurement})
$$

### Step 6 — Drawing the Graphic Column
Use **standard graphical symbols** (per IS 1498):

| Soil Type | Symbol |
|---|---|
| Gravel | Triangular dots (▲) |
| Sand | Dots (•) |
| Silt | Dashes (–) |
| Clay | Horizontal lines (▬) |
| Rock | Cross-hatched blocks (▦) |
| Water table | Inverted triangle (▽) |

The **vertical scale** is fixed: **1 cm = 1 m** (default). For soft soils requiring finer detail, **1 cm = 0.5 m** may be used.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of a Soil Profile

A soil profile combines multiple bore logs into a continuous section.

### Step 1 — Align Boreholes
Place all relevant boreholes on a horizontal axis **scaled to true distances** between them:

$$
\text{Horizontal Scale} : 1 \text{ cm} = 5 \text{ m} \;\; \text{(typical for site scale)}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Depths to RLs
Every depth in each borehole is converted to a Reduced Level (RL) using the collar RL:

$$
\text{RL}_{\text{point}} = \text{RL}_{\text{collar}} - z_{\text{depth}}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Given: BH-1 collar RL = +12.50 m AMSL, sample at depth $z = 2.50$ m BGL.

$$
\text{RL}_{\text{point}} = +12.50 - 2.50 = +10.00 \text{ m AMSL}
$$

### Step 3 — Draw Stratum Boundaries
For each borehole, the **top and bottom of each layer** is plotted as a horizontal line at the correct RL. These points are then **joined** between adjacent boreholes (with a smooth line, since boundaries are rarely perfectly flat in nature).

**Rule of joining:** Adjacent points of the same layer boundary are joined by a **straight or gently curved line**. A "?" is placed where the boundary is **uncertain** between two boreholes.

### Step 4 — Hatch the Layers
Each layer is filled with the standard soil symbol pattern matching the bore log graphic column.

### Step 5 — Annotate
- Add SPT N-values at appropriate depths.
- Add water table line as a connected line through all boreholes.
- Add project name, scale, north arrow, legend.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Layered SPT Correction

A borehole is drilled through the following strata:

| Layer | Depth Range (m BGL) | Soil Description | γ (kN/m³) | $G_s$ | e | SPT N (raw) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.00 – 1.50 | Fill / Topsoil | 16.0 | — | — | 6 |
| 2 | 1.50 – 4.50 | Loose sand | 17.0 (above WT) | 2.65 | 0.75 | 10 |
| 3 | 4.50 – 9.00 | Medium sand (saturated) | 19.0 (saturated) | 2.65 | 0.65 | 18 |

**Water table** is at **3.00 m BGL**.

**Required:** Compute the effective vertical stress at 5.00 m BGL and the corrected SPT N-value at 4.50–5.00 m interval.

---

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — Effective Vertical Stress at z = 5.00 m BGL

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_v &= (\gamma_1 \cdot z_1) + (\gamma_2 \cdot z_2) + \gamma'_3 \cdot (z - z_1 - z_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $z_1 = 1.50$ m (Layer 1 thickness, all above WT)
- $z_2 = 3.00 - 1.50 = 1.50$ m (Layer 2 above WT)
- $z_3 = 5.00 - 3.00 = 2.00$ m (Layer 3 below WT)
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

Buoyant unit weight of Layer 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma'_3 &= \gamma_{\text{sat},3} - \gamma_w \\
&= 19.0 - 9.81 \\
&= 9.19 \;\; \text{kN/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Effective vertical stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_v &= (16.0 \times 1.50) + (17.0 \times 1.50) + (9.19 \times 2.00) \\
&= 24.00 + 25.50 + 18.38 \\
&= 67.88 \;\; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using general formula:**

$$
\sigma'_v = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \gamma_i z_i \;\; \text{with buoyant weights below WT} \;\;\checkmark
$$

#### Part (b) — Corrected SPT N-value

Using Liao & Whitman (1986) correction:

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_a}{\sigma'_v}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{67.88}} = \sqrt{1.4732} = 1.2138
$$

**Limiting rule:** $C_N \leq 2.0$ (Bowles 1996).

$$
N_{\text{corrected}} = C_N \times N = 1.2138 \times 18 = 21.85 \;\; \approx \;\; 22
$$

**Reporting format in the bore log:**

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{raw}} = 18, \quad \sigma'_v = 67.88 \text{ kPa}, \quad C_N = 1.21, \quad N_c = 22}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Note:** When presenting corrected N-values in the bore log, **always show the raw N, the σ'ᵥ, the correction factor, and the final corrected N** in a four-column sub-table. This is what differentiates a "data logger" from a "geotechnical engineer."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Interpretation of Bore Log Data

The following matrix is a rapid interpretation guide for KTU problem-solving:

| SPT N (raw) | Granular Soil Description | Cohesive Soil Description | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$–$4$ | Very loose | Very soft | **NOT acceptable** for foundation; ground improvement needed |
| $4$–$10$ | Loose | Soft | Marginal; only for light loads; deep foundation preferred |
| $10$–$30$ | Medium dense | Firm to stiff | **Suitable** for most shallow foundations |
| $30$–$50$ | Dense | Very stiff | Excellent bearing; low settlement |
| $>50$ | Very dense | Hard | Possible rock-like behaviour; refusal imminent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These bands are **rules of thumb** (Terzaghi & Peck, 1967). KTU expects students to **cite the source** and **note the site-specific override** (e.g., presence of fines, organic content, water table).

---

## 3.5 Standard Bore Log Output – Text Representation

Below is a *textual* representation of a standard bore log (this is exactly the format that gets converted to a graphical plate):

```
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  PROJECT: KTU Campus Building – Ernakulam      BH ID: BH-02          ║
║  CLIENT: KTU Estate Section                 COLLAR RL: +11.20 m AMSL ║
║  DRILL METHOD: Rotary                       DATE: 15/03/2024         ║
╠════╤════╤═══════════════╤════╤═════╤════════╤══════╤════════╤═════════╣
║z(m)│Thk │Description    │Grph│ N   │Sample  │Moist │ WT/Rem │ Remarks ║
╠════╪════╪═══════════════╪════╪═════╪════════╪══════╪════════╪═════════╣
║0.00│1.20│Topsoil, dark  │ ▒▒ │ —   │ D-0.5  │ Moist│        │organic  ║
║    │    │brown, rootmat │    │     │        │      │        │         ║
║1.20│2.30│Silty CLAY     │ ▬▬ │ 6,8,9│ U-2.0 │ Moist│ ▽ WT   │CL, firm ║
║    │    │grey, medium   │    │ N=17│        │      │ z=2.0m │         ║
║    │    │stiff          │    │     │        │      │        │         ║
║3.50│3.00│Fine SAND      │ •• │ 10,12│ D-4.0 │ Wet  │        │SP, med ║
║    │    │grey, medium   │    │N=22 │        │      │        │dense    ║
║    │    │dense          │    │     │        │      │        │         ║
║6.50│2.00│Sandy GRAVEL   │ ▲▲ │ 20,25│ D-6.5 │ Sat. │        │GW, dense║
║    │    │sub-angular,   │    │N=45 │        │      │        │         ║
║    │    │dense          │    │     │        │      │        │         ║
║8.50│ END │Terminated at  │    │     │        │      │        │Refusal  ║
║    │    │8.50 m         │    │     │        │      │        │on gravel║
╚════╧════╧═══════════════╧════╧═════╧════════╧══════╧════════╧═════════╝
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answer sheets, you cannot draw a full graphical bore log. **A neat, dimensioned text-sketches with the standard columns is the accepted alternative** and carries full marks if legibly drawn.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIR Preparation Workflow

The flow chart below maps the entire process from field work to final report submission.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation & Scope Definition] --> B[Desk Study: Toposheets, GSI, Past Reports]
    B --> C[Site Reconnaissance Visit]
    C --> D[Borehole Layout Planning on Site Plan]
    D --> E[Field Investigation: Drilling, SPT, Sampling]
    E --> F[Groundwater Monitoring]
    E --> G[Disturbed and Undisturbed Sample Collection]
    G --> H[Laboratory Testing: Classification, Strength, Consolidation, Chemical]
    H --> I[Data Compilation: Tabular Sheets]
    I --> J[Bore Log Preparation: One Plate per BH]
    J --> K[Soil Profile Drawing: 1D and 2D Cross Sections]
    K --> L[Engineering Analysis: Bearing Capacity, Settlement, Liquefaction]
    L --> M[Foundation Recommendations and Design Parameters]
    M --> N[Report Writing: SIR with All Sections and Appendices]
    N --> O[Internal Review and Quality Check]
    O --> P[Final Submission to Client]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style G fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style H fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style I fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style J fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style K fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000
    style L fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style M fill:#ffc000,color:#000000
    style N fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Standard Bore Log Sheet Layout (Schematic)

The block diagram below shows the typical column arrangement of a graphical bore log plate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HeaderBlock["HEADER BLOCK"]
        H1[Project Name]
        H2[Borehole ID]
        H3[Collar RL and Coordinates]
        H4[Date and Drilling Method]
    end

    subgraph LogBody["LOG BODY (Vertical Columns L to R)"]
        L1[Depth Column m BGL]
        L2[Thickness Column m]
        L3[Soil Description Column]
        L4[Graphic Symbol Column]
        L5[SPT N-Value Column]
        L6[Sample Column]
        L7[Moisture Column]
        L8[Remarks Column]
    end

    subgraph FooterBlock["FOOTER BLOCK"]
        F1[Termination Depth and Reason]
        F2[Groundwater Depth]
        F3[Backfilling Details]
        F4[Reference to Next BH]
    end

    HeaderBlock --> LogBody
    LogBody --> FooterBlock
```

---

## 4.3 Soil Profile Construction (2D Cross-Section) — Functional Flow

This block-level architecture flow shows how multiple bore logs are integrated into a 2D soil profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Multiple Bore Logs BH-1, BH-2, BH-3 ...] --> B[Step 1: Mark True Horizontal Distances Between BHs]
    B --> C[Step 2: Convert All Depths to RLs Using Collar RLs]
    C --> D[Step 3: Plot Collar Points and Stratum Boundaries as Horizontal Markers]
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify Common Stratum Layers Across BHs]
    E --> F[Step 5: Join Corresponding Boundaries with Straight or Curved Lines]
    F --> G[Step 6: Mark Uncertain Boundaries with Question Mark]
    G --> H[Step 7: Add Soil Symbol Hatch Patterns and SPT N Annotations]
    H --> I[Step 8: Add Water Table Line and Project Title Block]
    I --> J[Output: Final 2D Soil Profile Cross Section]
```

---

## 4.4 Visual Schematic: Soil Profile Across Three Boreholes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BH1["BH-01 Collar RL plus 12.50"]
        L1A["Topsoil 0.0 to 1.0 m"]
        L1B["Soft Clay 1.0 to 4.0 m N equals 4"]
        L1C["Medium Sand 4.0 to 8.0 m N equals 18"]
        L1D["Dense Gravel 8.0 to 12.0 m N equals 40"]
    end

    subgraph BH2["BH-02 Collar RL plus 11.80"]
        L2A["Topsoil 0.0 to 0.8 m"]
        L2B["Soft Clay 0.8 to 3.5 m N equals 5"]
        L2C["Medium Sand 3.5 to 7.5 m N equals 20"]
        L2D["Dense Gravel 7.5 to 11.5 m N equals 42"]
    end

    subgraph BH3["BH-03 Collar RL plus 13.20"]
        L3A["Topsoil 0.0 to 1.2 m"]
        L3B["Soft Clay 1.2 to 4.5 m N equals 4"]
        L3C["Medium Sand 4.5 to 9.0 m N equals 17"]
        L3D["Dense Gravel 9.0 to 13.0 m N equals 38"]
    end

    L1A --- L2A --- L3A
    L1B --- L2B --- L3B
    L1C --- L2C --- L3C
    L1D --- L2D --- L3D

    style BH1 fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000
    style BH2 fill:#d9ead3,color:#000000
    style BH3 fill:#cfe2f3,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the schematic above, the corresponding strata are joined by horizontal lines. In a real profile, the joining lines may **dip** or **rise** because geological boundaries are rarely horizontal. The schematic is the *conceptual* template for KTU answers.

---

## 4.5 Bore Log vs Soil Profile — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BoreLogBlock["BORE LOG: One Borehole, 1D Vertical"]
        BL1[Single Location]
        BL2[Vertical Scale 1cm to 1m]
        BL3[Includes SPT, Samples, WT]
        BL4[Plate Format A3 Sheet]
    end

    subgraph SoilProfileBlock["SOIL PROFILE: Multiple Boreholes, 2D Section"]
        SP1[Multiple Locations Aligned]
        SP2[Vertical Scale 1cm to 1m]
        SP3[Horizontal Scale 1cm to 5m]
        SP4[Shows Layer Continuity and Dip]
        SP5[Includes Joined Boundaries and Water Table Line]
    end

    BoreLogBlock -- Combines to form --> SoilProfileBlock
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Expectation:** A *bore log* is for a *single point*; a *soil profile* (cross-section) integrates *multiple bore logs*. Do NOT use these terms interchangeably in the answer sheet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **"List the essential components of a Soil Investigation Report (SIR) as per IS 1892."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Soil Investigation Report (SIR) as per **IS 1892:1979** must contain the following essential components:

1. **Project description, site location, and topography** — providing the context of the investigation.
2. **Field investigation details** — borehole layout, drilling method, in-situ tests conducted (SPT, CPT, etc.), and sampling programme.
3. **Bore logs and soil profiles** — graphical presentation of subsurface conditions at each borehole and along cross-sections.
4. **Groundwater observations** — depth of water table, seasonal variation, and pore-water pressures.
5. **Laboratory test results** — classification, shear strength, consolidation, and chemical tests on collected samples.
6. **Analysis and recommendations** — bearing capacity, settlement estimates, foundation type, depth, and special precautions.
7. **Limitations of the report** — explicitly stating the conditions under which the recommendations are valid.

> [!NOTE]
> Award **½ mark per point** (6 points × ½ = 3 marks). Any 6 valid points from the above list are sufficient.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **"Differentiate between a Bore Log and a Soil Profile. State the standard vertical scale used for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Bore Log | Soil Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definition | A record of subsurface conditions at a **single borehole** | A continuous section combining **multiple boreholes** |
| 2 | Dimension | One-dimensional (vertical) | Two-dimensional (vertical + horizontal) |
| 3 | Purpose | Detailed point-specific stratigraphy | Regional/overall picture of layer continuity |
| 4 | Information | Soil description, SPT, samples, WT, remarks | Layer boundaries, lateral extent, dip, WT line |
| 5 | Vertical scale | **1 cm = 1 m** (standard) | **1 cm = 1 m** (vertical) and **1 cm = 5 m** (horizontal) |
| 6 | Format | A3 plate per borehole | A2/A1 drawing sheet with title block |

> [!TIP]
> The key distinguishing phrase for full marks: **"Bore log is point-specific (1D), soil profile is section-specific (2D)."**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **"Prepare a comprehensive Soil Investigation Report for a proposed multi-storey building in Ernakulam district, Kerala. Discuss the methodology, presentation of data through bore logs and soil profiles, and final recommendations."**

**OR**

> **"Explain in detail the procedure for the preparation of a bore log. With a neat labelled sketch, describe all the information presented in a typical bore log as per IS 2131:1981."**

---

#### Solution to Question A (Sub-part a: 7 marks)

**(a) Methodology of SIR Preparation (7 marks)**

The preparation of a comprehensive SIR proceeds through the following stages:

1. **Desk Study (1 mark):** Collection and review of existing geological maps (GSI), toposheets, past investigation reports, and aerial photographs of the area. For Ernakulam, this involves understanding the typical **alluvial–lateritic** soil sequence along the Kerala coast.

2. **Site Reconnaissance (1 mark):** Visual inspection to identify surface features, access constraints, existing structures, slope, drainage, and signs of distress.

3. **Field Investigation Planning (1 mark):** Deciding borehole spacing (typically **15–30 m** for buildings per IS 1892), depth (minimum 1.5 × width of foundation or refusal, whichever is earlier), and sampling plan.

4. **Field Investigation (2 marks):** Execution of boreholes using rotary or auger methods, with **SPT at every 1.5 m**, collection of disturbed (D) and undisturbed (U) samples, and installation of piezometers for groundwater monitoring.

5. **Laboratory Testing (1 mark):** Index tests (sieve, hydrometer, Atterberg limits), strength tests (UCS, triaxial, vane shear), consolidation tests, and chemical tests (pH, sulphates, chlorides) per IS 2720 series.

6. **Data Presentation and Interpretation (1 mark):** Preparation of bore logs, soil profiles, and analysis of data for bearing capacity, settlement, and foundation type as per IS 6403 and IS 8009.

> [!NOTE]
> Award marks in proportion to coverage. A clear **6-step methodology** with one-line descriptions is the minimum for full marks.

---

#### Solution to Question A (Sub-part b: 7 marks)

**(b) Presentation through Bore Logs and Soil Profiles (7 marks)**

**Bore Log Presentation (3.5 marks):**

Each borehole is presented as a **standard A3 plate** containing:

- **Header:** project name, BH ID, coordinates, collar RL, date, method.
- **Body (left to right):** depth, thickness, soil description (per IS 1498), graphic symbol, SPT N-value, sample ID, moisture, groundwater symbol, remarks.
- **Footer:** termination depth, reason for termination, backfilling record.

**Example (textual form):**

| Depth (m) | Description | N | Sample | WT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00–1.00 | Topsoil, dark brown | — | D-0.5 | — |
| 1.00–4.00 | Soft grey CLAY (CH) | 6 | U-2.0 | ▽ 2.5 m |
| 4.00–9.00 | Medium dense SAND (SP) | 22 | D-5.0, D-7.0 | — |
| 9.00–12.00 | Dense sandy GRAVEL (GW) | 45 | D-10.0 | — |
| 12.00 | Refusal on hard stratum | — | — | — |

**Soil Profile Presentation (3.5 marks):**

The soil profile is a **2D cross-section** drawn by joining corresponding strata of aligned boreholes:

1. Boreholes are placed on a horizontal axis with **true inter-distances** (scale: 1 cm = 5 m).
2. Depths are converted to **RLs** using the collar RL.
3. Corresponding stratum boundaries are joined with straight/curved lines.
4. The water table is shown as a continuous line through all boreholes.
5. Soil symbol patterns, SPT N-values, and a title block are added.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A profile drawing showing **at least 3 boreholes**, the **water table line**, and a **legend** is the KTU benchmark for full 7 marks in this part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

> **"Discuss the various methods of presentation of soil exploration data. With neat sketches, explain the preparation of a 2D soil profile cross-section from multiple boreholes."**

**OR**

> **"A site has three boreholes BH-1, BH-2, and BH-3 spaced at 20 m apart along a straight line. Their collar RLs are +10.50, +10.20, and +9.80 m AMSL. The stratum boundaries (depth in m BGL) and SPT N-values are tabulated. Prepare a 2D soil profile and interpret the subsurface conditions for a shallow foundation at 2.0 m depth."**

---

#### Solution to Question B (Sub-part a: 7 marks)

**(a) Methods of Presentation of Soil Exploration Data (7 marks)**

The data obtained from a soil exploration programme can be presented in the following ways:

1. **Tabular Presentation (1.5 marks):** All field and laboratory data are arranged in structured tables — borehole summary tables, lab test result tables, and chemical analysis tables. This is the most compact form and is used as the data backbone of the SIR.

2. **Bore Log / Boring Log (2 marks):** A graphical record of the subsurface at a single borehole, showing depth, soil description, SPT N-values, samples, and groundwater in standardised columns. **Standard vertical scale: 1 cm = 1 m.**

3. **Soil Profile (2 marks):** A 2D vertical section combining multiple boreholes. The horizontal axis represents distance and the vertical axis represents elevation (RL). It is used to visualise the **continuity, dip, and pinch-out** of strata.

4. **Geotechnical Maps (1 mark):** Plan-view maps showing contours of a particular stratum (e.g., depth to rock, SPT N-value contours, water table contours) for the entire site.

5. **Cross-Sections Along Multiple Lines (0.5 mark):** Multiple 2D profiles are drawn in different directions to give a 3D feel of the subsurface.

> [!NOTE]
> Always mention the **scale convention** (1 cm = 1 m vertical, 1 cm = 5 m horizontal) in the answer; this is the KTU favourite sub-point.

---

#### Solution to Question B (Sub-part b: 7 marks)

**(b) Numerical Problem — 2D Soil Profile Preparation (7 marks)**

**Given data (synthesised for demonstration):**

| BH ID | Collar RL (m AMSL) | Topsoil (0.0–1.0 m) | Soft Clay (1.0–4.0 m) | Medium Sand (4.0–8.0 m) | Dense Gravel (8.0–12.0 m) | Water Table (m BGL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BH-1 | +10.50 | 0.0–1.0 | 1.0–4.0, N=4 | 4.0–8.0, N=18 | 8.0–12.0, N=40 | 2.50 |
| BH-2 | +10.20 | 0.0–0.8 | 0.8–3.8, N=5 | 3.8–7.8, N=20 | 7.8–11.8, N=42 | 2.20 |
| BH-3 | +9.80 | 0.0–1.2 | 1.2–4.5, N=4 | 4.5–9.0, N=17 | 9.0–13.0, N=38 | 2.80 |

**Solution steps:**

**Step 1: Set up horizontal scale (1 mark)**

Distance BH-1 to BH-2 = 20 m, BH-2 to BH-3 = 20 m.
At scale 1 cm = 5 m, spacing = **4 cm** between boreholes.

**Step 2: Plot collar points (1 mark)**

Mark BH-1 at RL +10.50, BH-2 at RL +10.20, BH-3 at RL +9.80 on the vertical scale (1 cm = 1 m).

**Step 3: Convert each layer boundary to RL and plot (2 marks)**

| Layer | BH-1 RL (top–bot) | BH-2 RL (top–bot) | BH-3 RL (top–bot) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topsoil | +10.50 to +9.50 | +10.20 to +9.40 | +9.80 to +8.60 |
| Soft Clay | +9.50 to +6.50 | +9.40 to +6.40 | +8.60 to +5.30 |
| Medium Sand | +6.50 to +2.50 | +6.40 to +2.40 | +5.30 to +0.80 |
| Dense Gravel | +2.50 to −1.50 | +2.40 to −1.60 | +0.80 to −3.20 |

**Step 4: Join corresponding boundaries (1 mark)**

Connect the top of the soft clay across the three boreholes, then the bottom, and so on. The boundaries will be **near-horizontal with slight dip** toward BH-3 (because BH-3 collar is at lower RL and the dense gravel lies deeper there).

**Step 5: Plot water table (1 mark)**

WT RLs: BH-1 → +10.50 − 2.50 = +8.00; BH-2 → +10.20 − 2.20 = +8.00; BH-3 → +9.80 − 2.80 = +7.00.

A connecting line through these points is the water table line.

**Step 6: Annotate and interpret (1 mark)**

**Interpretation for shallow foundation at 2.0 m BGL:**

- The **foundation level at 2.0 m BGL** corresponds to RLs of +8.50 (BH-1), +8.20 (BH-2), +7.80 (BH-3).
- The foundation rests in the **soft clay layer** (CL/CH), which is **not recommended** for direct bearing.
- The corrected SPT N-value at this depth is **~ 4 to 5**, indicating **very soft to soft** consistency.
- **Recommendation:** Either **deepen the foundation to 4.5 m BGL** to rest on medium dense sand, or adopt a **deep foundation (pile)** terminating in the dense gravel layer (8.0–9.0 m BGL).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert depths to RLs** before plotting. Without RL conversion, the profile is dimensionally inconsistent and **loses 2–3 marks outright**. Also, the **water table line must be drawn as a single connected line** through all boreholes — not as separate symbols in each column.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for last-day revision):**

- ✅ **SIR = official deliverable** of any site investigation; must follow **IS 1892:1979**.
- ✅ Bore log is **1D, point-specific**; soil profile is **2D, section-specific**.
- ✅ Standard bore log vertical scale: **1 cm = 1 m**; profile horizontal scale: **1 cm = 5 m**.
- ✅ Bore log columns (left → right): **Depth, Thickness, Description, Graphic, N-value, Sample, Moisture, Remarks**.
- ✅ Soil description must follow **IS 1498:1970** — Colour + Type + Consistency/Density + Origin.
- ✅ SPT N-value = sum of blows for **2nd + 3rd** 150 mm drives (after 150 mm seating).
- ✅ Always report **raw N and corrected N** (with $C_N$ shown) in the bore log.
- ✅ $C_N = \sqrt{P_a / \sigma'_v}$ (Liao & Whitman) with upper limit of **2.0**.
- ✅ Soil profile construction: **align BHs → convert depths to RLs → join corresponding boundaries → mark WT line → hatch soil patterns**.
- ✅ Always include a **"Limitations"** section in the SIR — it is mandatory and mark-yielding.
- ✅ Common SPT correlations: $N_c$ for bearing capacity per **IS 6403**; settlement via **Schmertmann**; liquefaction via **Seed & Idriss**.
- ✅ In KTU answers, **sketch > text**. A neat, dimensioned bore log sketch carries more marks than a 500-word description.
- ✅ For shallow foundations, always recommend the **strongest layer** within 1.5 × width below GL.
- ✅ For pile foundations, terminate piles in the layer with **$N \geq 50$** (refusal-like behaviour).
- ✅ **Kerala-specific reminder:** Alluvial-lateritic sequence, water table typically 1.5–3.0 m BGL in coastal districts, **Kuttanad clays are highly compressible** — special mention in the limitations section.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Soil Investigation Report, Bore Log, and Soil Profile.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geosynthetics – Functions and Applications from Case Studies: Pavements, Embankments, Railways, Erosion Control from Kerala State
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET753 - ADVANCED GEOTECHNICAL INVESTIGATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geosynthetics – Functions and Applications from Case Studies: Pavements, Embankments, Railways, Erosion Control from Kerala State

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geosynthetics** are planar, polymeric (synthetic) engineering materials manufactured primarily from petrochemical-based polymers such as **polypropylene (PP)**, **polyethylene (PE)**, **polyester (PET)**, **polyamide (PA)** and **polyvinyl chloride (PVC)**, used in contact with soil, rock, or any other geotechnical engineering material as an integral part of a civil engineering project, structure, or system.

The American Society for Testing and Materials (**ASTM D4439**) defines geosynthetics as:

> *"A planar product manufactured from polymeric material used with soil, rock, earth, or other geotechnical engineering related material as an integral part of a man-made project, structure, or system."*

## 1.2 Major Types of Geosynthetics

| S.No | Type | Brief Description | Primary Function |
|------|------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Geotextile** | Permeable, woven/non-woven textile | Separation, Filtration, Drainage |
| 2 | **Geogrid** | Polymer mesh with apertures (openings) | Soil Reinforcement |
| 3 | **Geonet** | 3D net-like structure with open apertures | In-plane drainage |
| 4 | **Geomembrane** | Impermeable polymeric sheet | Barrier / Containment |
| 5 | **Geocomposite** | Hybrid of two or more geosynthetics | Multi-functional |
| 6 | **Geocell** | 3D honey-comb cellular confinement system | Reinforcement + Confinement |
| 7 | **Geosynthetic Clay Liner (GCL)** | Bentonite sandwiched between geosynthetics | Hydraulic barrier |
| 8 | **Geofoam** | Lightweight expanded polystyrene block | Lightweight fill |

## 1.3 The Six Primary Functions of Geosynthetics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> These six functions are the **most frequently asked** concept in KTU Module 4. Memorize them with one-word keywords.

1. **Separation** – Prevent mixing of two dissimilar soils.
2. **Reinforcement** – Provide tensile strength to a soil mass.
3. **Filtration** – Allow water passage while retaining soil particles.
4. **Drainage** – Conduct fluids within their own plane (in-plane flow).
5. **Barrier (Containment)** – Prevent fluid/gas migration.
6. **Protection** – Cushion against punctures/stresses (e.g., under geomembranes).

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bodyguard for Soil" Analogy:** Imagine a soft, weak sponge (representing soft clay). If you insert thin, strong plastic sheets inside it, the sponge becomes much stiffer and can carry more load without deforming. Geosynthetics do exactly this—they act as the "skeletal reinforcement" inside a soil mass, similar to how steel rebars reinforce concrete or how our bones reinforce our body.

**Real-world parallel:** When a road is built on soft, marshy clay in Kuttanad (Kerala's rice bowl region), the soft subgrade would normally deform and fail under traffic loads. By placing a geogrid or geotextile layer within the granular sub-base, the composite section behaves **10–100 times stiffer** than the unreinforced section—this is essentially **tensile reinforcement** of soil.

## 1.5 Why Geosynthetics are Critical for Kerala State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Kerala's unique geomorphology presents challenges that make geosynthetics indispensable:
> - **Lateritic soils** with high iron/aluminum oxide content (acidic, variable permeability)
> - **Coastal alluvium** in districts like Alappuzha, Ernakulam, Kozhikode (soft clays, high compressibility)
> - **High rainfall (~3000 mm/yr)** in Western Ghats triggering **slope failures** (Wayanad 2024, Kavalappara 2019)
> - **Post-2018 & 2019 flood reconstruction** demand rapid, durable ground improvement
> - **NH-66 widening** and **SilverLine / Sabari Rail** projects require reinforced embankments

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functions – Mechanism, "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Separation Function

**Mechanism:** A geotextile placed between two dissimilar soil layers (e.g., subgrade and aggregate sub-base) prevents the aggregate from punching into the soft subgrade and subgrade fines from migrating upward into the aggregate voids.

**Why needed:** Without separation, mixing causes **loss of design thickness, rutting, and reduced CBR strength**.

**How:** The geotextile's **apparent opening size (AOS / O₉₅)** must be smaller than the larger soil's $D_{85}$ but larger than the finer soil's $D_{15}$.

**Survivability requirements:** AASHTO M288 specifies minimum grab strength and puncture resistance based on subgrade CBR and aggregate angularity.

---

### 2.1.2 Reinforcement Function

**Mechanism:** Geosynthetics (mainly geogrids and high-strength woven geotextiles) develop **tensile resistance** through:
- **Interface friction** between soil and polymer (Mohr-Coulomb: $\tau = c_a + \sigma_n \tan\delta$)
- **Interlock** (especially for geogrids where aggregate sits in apertures)
- **Membrane effect** (vertical stress reduction due to catenary action)

**Why needed:** Soils are strong in **compression** but weak in **tension**. Geosynthetics provide the missing tensile component, enabling steeper slopes, taller walls, and longer-lasting pavements.

**How:** Through three fundamental mechanisms illustrated by the **Binquet and Lee (1975)** approach for footings:

1. **Shear layer effect** above the reinforcement.
2. **Deep footing effect** below the reinforcement.
3. **Membrane (tensile) effect** when deformation occurs.

---

### 2.1.3 Filtration Function

**Mechanism:** Soil water passes through the geotextile while soil particles are retained, preventing piping and internal erosion.

**Design criteria (per Christopher & Holtz, 1985):**

$$O_{95} \leq (1 \text{ to } 2) \times D_{85} \text{ of base soil}$$

$$\text{Permittivity } \psi = \frac{k_{n}}{t} \geq 0.1 \text{ sec}^{-1}$$

where $k_n$ = cross-plane permeability, $t$ = geotextile thickness.

**Why needed:** Prevents piping in dams, retaining walls, and underdrains.

---

### 2.1.4 Drainage Function

**Mechanism:** In-plane flow (transmissivity, $\theta$) within the plane of the geosynthetic.

**Why needed:** Accelerates consolidation of soft clays in Kerala's coastal belts (e.g., **Kochi Metro construction**).

**How:** Transmissivity must exceed the design flow rate:

$$\theta \geq Q_{\text{design}} \times L_{\text{drain}} \times \frac{1}{i}$$

where $i$ = hydraulic gradient.

---

### 2.1.5 Barrier Function

**Mechanism:** Geomembranes and GCLs prevent fluid or gas migration.

**Why needed:** Landfill liners, mining heap-leach pads, canal lining (Kerala's **Bhavani and Peruvannamuzhi dams** rehabilitation projects).

**Critical Property:** Permeability $k \leq 1 \times 10^{-13}$ cm/s for HDPE geomembranes.

---

### 2.1.6 Protection Function

**Mechanism:** Geotextile cushions (typically non-woven, mass $\geq 270 \text{ g/m}^2$) absorb impact energy from aggregate placed on top of geomembranes.

**Why needed:** Prevents puncture during installation of protective soil cover on geomembrane liners.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and aligned with KTU Module 4 syllabus outcomes.

### Table 2.1: Core Equations for Geosynthetic Engineering

| S.No | Function / Application | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Units |
|------|------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Mohr-Coulomb Interface Friction** | $\tau = c_a + \sigma_n \tan\delta$ | $\tau$ = interface shear, $c_a$ = adhesion, $\sigma_n$ = normal stress, $\delta$ = interface friction angle | kPa, kPa, kPa, ° |
| 2 | **Reinforced Bearing Capacity (Binquet-Lee)** | $q_{u(R)} = q_u \cdot (1 + \frac{N_c \cdot b \cdot h}{B} \cdot K)$ | $q_u$ = unreinforced ultimate bearing capacity, $N_c$ = bearing capacity factor, $b$ = width of reinforcement, $h$ = depth, $B$ = footing width, $K$ = factor | kPa, dimensionless |
| 3 | **Geogrid Aperture Shear (tensile capacity)** | $T_{allow} = \frac{T_{ult}}{RF_{id} \cdot RF_{cr} \cdot RF_{bd}}$ | $RF$ = reduction factors (installation damage, creep, biological/chemical degradation) | kN/m |
| 4 | **AASHTO Subgrade-Base Separation (geotextile)** | $O_{95} \leq 0.6 \cdot D_{85}$ (granular) | — | mm |
| 5 | **Filtration Piping Criterion** | $O_{95} \leq (1\text{ to }2) D_{85}$ | — | mm |
| 6 | **Permeability Criterion** | $k_{geotextile} \geq k_{soil}$ | Cross-plane permeability | m/s |
| 7 | **Transmissivity (in-plane drainage)** | $\theta = k_p \cdot t$ | $k_p$ = in-plane permeability, $t$ = thickness | m²/s |
| 8 | **Geosynthetic-Reinforced Embankment Slope Stability** | $FS = \frac{\sum(c'L + W\cos\alpha \tan\phi') + T_u \cdot R}{\sum W \sin\alpha \cdot R}$ | $T_u$ = ultimate geosynthetic tension along slip surface | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Tie-back Wedge Anchorage (Reinforced Wall)** | $L_e = \frac{T_{allow}}{\sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta_{avg} \cdot 2}$ | $L_e$ = anchorage length behind active wedge | m |
| 10 | **Erosion Control – Permissible Shear Stress** | $\tau_p = \tau_{allow}$ for given vegetation / geotextile | — | Pa |
| 11 | **Geocell Confinement Stress** | $\sigma_c = \frac{2M}{d} \cdot \frac{1}{1-\nu}$ | $M$ = cell wall modulus, $d$ = cell diameter, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | kPa |
| 12 | **AASHTO Subgrade Improvement Factor** | $BCR = \frac{q_{u(R)}}{q_u}$ (Bearing Capacity Ratio) | Ratio of reinforced to unreinforced capacity | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Kerala Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-specific applications of these equations:**

- **Geotextile + Geogrid under NH-66 carriageway:** Uses *Eq. 2* and *Eq. 12* to reduce aggregate thickness by 30–40%.
- **Reinforced soil walls in Munnar (Idukki):** Uses *Eq. 8* and *Eq. 9* for terrace stabilization.
- **Erosion control on Western Ghats slopes (Wayanad 2019–2024):** Uses *Eq. 10* with coir geotextiles.
- **Kochi Metro viaduct approach embankments (2013–ongoing):** Used *Eq. 7* for prefabricated vertical drains (PVDs) topped with geosynthetics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Binquet and Lee's Bearing Capacity Improvement with Geosynthetic Reinforcement

This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU Module 4 (carries 7 marks in past university exams).

### Step 1 — Define the Unreinforced Bearing Capacity

For a strip footing of width $B$ on a soil with cohesion $c$ and friction angle $\phi$, the **Terzaghi ultimate bearing capacity** is:

$$q_u = cN_c + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

where $N_c, N_\gamma$ are Terzaghi bearing capacity factors that depend solely on $\phi$.

---

### Step 2 — Conceptualize Reinforcement

When $N$ horizontal geosynthetic layers are placed within the failure zone below the footing, each layer mobilizes an additional horizontal shear resistance $\Delta q_i$ acting on a vertical plane within the slip zone.

---

### Step 3 — Express Each Layer's Contribution

For a reinforcement layer of width $b_i$ at depth $h_i$, the horizontal shear transfer at each side of the footing is:

$$\Delta q_i = \frac{2 \cdot c_a \cdot b_i \cdot h_i}{B}$$

where $c_a$ is the soil–geosynthetic interface adhesion (≈ $c$ for rough surfaces).

---

### Step 4 — Summation Across All Layers

Total bearing capacity improvement becomes:

$$q_u(R) = q_u + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta q_i = q_u + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{2 c_a b_i h_i}{B}$$

---

### Step 5 — Define the Bearing Capacity Ratio (BCR)

$$\boxed{BCR = \frac{q_u(R)}{q_u} = 1 + \frac{2 c_a}{q_u B}\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i h_i}$$

---

### Step 6 — Compute an Illustrative KTU Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A 1.5 m wide strip footing rests on soft clay ($c = 20$ kPa, $\phi = 0°$, $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³, depth $D_f = 0$). Three layers of geogrid ($c_a = 18$ kPa) of width 1.5 m are placed at depths 0.25B, 0.5B, and 0.75B. Compute the BCR.

**Solution:**

$$N_c = 5.7 \text{ (for } \phi = 0° \text{ per Terzaghi)}$$

$$q_u = cN_c = 20 \times 5.7 = 114 \text{ kPa}$$

$$h_1 = 0.25 \times 1.5 = 0.375 \text{ m}$$

$$h_2 = 0.50 \times 1.5 = 0.750 \text{ m}$$

$$h_3 = 0.75 \times 1.5 = 1.125 \text{ m}$$

$$\sum b_i h_i = 1.5 \times (0.375 + 0.750 + 1.125) = 1.5 \times 2.25 = 3.375 \text{ m}^2$$

$$BCR = 1 + \frac{2 \times 18 \times 3.375}{114 \times 1.5} = 1 + \frac{121.5}{171} = 1 + 0.7105$$

$$\boxed{BCR = 1.71}$$

**Interpretation:** The reinforced soil carries **71% more load** than the unreinforced soil at the same settlement—a major design benefit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tie-Back Wedge Anchorage Length for MSE Walls

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is essential for *embankment/wall* sub-questions in KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Active Earth Pressure

The lateral thrust on a vertical wall of height $H$ (Rankine):

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2}K_a \gamma H^2$$

$$K_a = \tan^2\left(45 - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

---

### Step 2 — Required Anchorage Length

The geosynthetic must extend a distance $L_e$ beyond the active wedge to mobilize enough friction (or interlock) to resist $P_a$:

$$T_{allow} \cdot n = P_a \cdot \text{SF} \cdot \text{coverage factor}$$

For a single reinforcement layer:

$$L_e = \frac{T_{design}}{\sigma_v \cdot \tan\delta_{avg} \cdot (2)}$$

where $\sigma_v = \gamma \cdot z$ (vertical stress at depth $z$).

---

### Step 3 — Total Reinforcement Length

$$L_T = L_a + L_e$$

where $L_a$ is the active wedge width = $(H - z) \tan(45 - \phi/2)$ and $L_e$ = anchorage length behind the wedge.

---

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** MSE wall $H = 6$ m, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $\delta = 25°$. Geogrid $T_{ult} = 80$ kN/m. Reduction factors $RF_{ID} = 1.3, RF_{CR} = 2.5, RF_{BD} = 1.1$. Compute $L_e$ at mid-height ($z = 3$ m).

**Step 4.1 — Allowable tensile strength:**

$$T_{allow} = \frac{80}{1.3 \times 2.5 \times 1.1} = \frac{80}{3.575} = 22.38 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4.2 — Vertical stress at $z = 3$ m:**

$$\sigma_v = 18 \times 3 = 54 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4.3 — Anchorage length:**

$$L_e = \frac{22.38}{54 \times \tan 25° \times 2} = \frac{22.38}{54 \times 0.4663 \times 2}$$

$$\boxed{L_e = \frac{22.38}{50.36} = 0.444 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Function–Mechanism–Design Equation

| Function | Primary Mechanism | Critical Design Equation | Typical Kerala Use |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Separation | Particle retention | $O_{95} \leq (1\text{–}2) D_{85}$ | NH-66 pavements |
| Reinforcement | Tensile mobilization | $BCR = 1 + \frac{2c_a}{q_u B}\sum b_i h_i$ | Munnar hill roads |
| Filtration | Cross-plane flow | $\psi \geq 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}$ | Idukki dam toe drains |
| Drainage | In-plane transmissivity | $\theta = k_p t$ | Kochi Metro PVDs |
| Barrier | Zero permeability | $k \leq 10^{-13}$ cm/s | Brahmapuram solid waste plant |
| Protection | Energy absorption | Mass $\geq 270$ g/m² | Landfill cushioning |

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any six functions of geosynthetics in geotechnical engineering. State one example application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The six primary functions are:

1. **Separation** – Geotextile between subgrade and sub-base in flexible pavements (NH-66 Kerala).
2. **Reinforcement** – Geogrid in reinforced soil walls (Munnar terrace stabilization).
3. **Filtration** – Geotextile filter behind retaining wall drainage (Kochi Metro abutments).
4. **Drainage** – Geocomposite drains in highway edge drains (Kozhikode bypass).
5. **Barrier** – HDPE geomembrane in landfill liners (Brahmapuram, Kochi).
6. **Protection** – Nonwoven cushion geotextile over geomembrane in solid waste facilities.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the difference between a geogrid and a geotextile? Give two specific uses of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Geotextile | Geogrid |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| Structure | Woven or non-woven fabric | Polymer mesh with apertures |
| Primary function | Separation, filtration, drainage | Reinforcement |
| Strength mechanism | Friction with soil | Interlock + bearing on apertures |
| Uses | (a) Subgrade separation, (b) Filter drains | (a) MSE wall reinforcement, (b) Pavement base reinforcement |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the six functions of geosynthetics with sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The six primary functions of geosynthetics, as defined by ASTM D4439 and adopted by IS 16014, are:

**1. Separation:**
A geotextile is placed between a soft subgrade (e.g., clay, CBR < 3%) and a granular sub-base. It prevents:
- Pumping of fines from subgrade into the aggregate
- Loss of design thickness of the aggregate layer
This prolongs pavement life. The criteria for selection are:
$$O_{95} \leq (1 \text{ to } 2) D_{85} \text{ of base soil}$$
$$k_{geotextile} \geq k_{soil}$$

**2. Reinforcement:**
Geogrids and high-strength woven geotextiles add tensile strength to soil. Mechanism: interface friction + interlock. For a strip footing on soft clay with $N$ layers of reinforcement, the BCR (Bearing Capacity Ratio) per Binquet-Lee is:
$$BCR = 1 + \frac{2c_a}{q_u B}\sum_{i=1}^{N} b_i h_i$$
Kerala example: Munnar hill-road widening where geogrid-reinforced walls reach heights up to 12 m.

**3. Filtration:**
A geotextile replaces graded granular filters in retaining wall drains and French drains. Permits water passage while preventing piping of fines. Christopher and Holtz (1985) criteria:
$$\psi = \frac{k_n}{t} \geq 0.1 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**4. Drainage:**
Transmissivity ($\theta$) measures the in-plane flow capacity:
$$\theta = k_p \cdot t$$
Used in edge drains of Kerala highways, prefabricated vertical drains (PVDs) for Kochi Metro approach embankments.

**5. Barrier (Containment):**
Geomembranes (HDPE, LLDPE, PVC) with permeability $k \leq 1 \times 10^{-13}$ cm/s prevent fluid/gas migration. Used in Brahmapuram solid waste landfill, Kochi.

**6. Protection:**
Nonwoven geotextiles (mass $\geq 270$ g/m²) cushion geomembranes against puncture from angular cover soil aggregates.

---

**(b) Discuss the application of geosynthetics in road pavements with reference to a Kerala State case study. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> [!TIP]
> **Kerala Case Study: NH-66 Six-Laning (Thiruvananthapuram–Kasargod, ongoing since 2022)**

**Background:** Kerala's NH-66 traverses coastal alluvium, lateritic soils, and soft clay patches (especially in Alappuzha, Ernakulam, Kozhikode, Kannur). Conventional flexible pavement design on these soils led to severe rutting and settlement.

**Geosynthetic Solution Adopted:**

1. **Subgrade Separation:** Non-woven polypropylene geotextile (mass 200 g/m²) placed on prepared subgrade.
2. **Base Reinforcement:** Biaxial geogrid (tensile strength 30 kN/m) at the sub-base–base interface.
3. **Edge Drainage:** Geocomposite edge drains to evacuate infiltrated water during monsoon.

**Design Outcome:**

- Aggregate thickness reduced from 600 mm to 400 mm (33% reduction).
- Estimated pavement life extended from 10 years to 15+ years.
- Cost saving ~12% on initial construction.
- Reduction in rut depth by ~50% over the design period.

**Performance:** During the **2023 monsoon**, sections with geogrid reinforcement reported ~40% less settlement than adjacent unreinforced sections.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the role of geosynthetics in the stability of reinforced embankments over soft soils. Discuss the design considerations with reference to Kerala's coastal regions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Geosynthynthetics in Embankment Stability:**

Geosynthetics (geogrids, high-strength geotextiles) are placed at the **base of embankments** on soft clay to:

1. **Provide tensile reinforcement at the base**, acting as a horizontal anchor that resists the lateral spreading tendency of the embankment fill.
2. **Increase the bearing capacity** of the soft foundation.
3. **Reduce differential settlement** through membrane effect.
4. **Allow faster construction** by reducing the factor of safety concerns during staged loading.

**Design Considerations for Kerala's Coastal Regions:**

1. **Subsoil profile:** Typically very soft to medium clay (Alappuzha–Kuttanad) with undrained shear strength $c_u = 5$–$20$ kPa.
2. **Groundwater:** High water table (within 0.5 m of ground surface).
3. **Settlement:** Consolidation settlement of 0.5–1.5 m; PVDs are often combined with geosynthetics.
4. **Stability analysis:** Uses modified Bishop's circular slip with reinforcement force $T$ acting tangentially:
$$FS = \frac{\sum (c'L + W\cos\alpha \tan\phi') + T_u \cdot \cos\theta \cdot R}{\sum W\sin\alpha \cdot R}$$
5. **Geosynthetic selection:** High-modulus, low-creep materials (PET geogrids preferred over HDPE in long-term applications).
6. **Construction sequence:** Stage construction with pore-pressure monitoring.

**Kerala Example:** Approach embankments of **Kochi Metro Rail** (2013) used basal geogrid reinforcement combined with stone columns and PVDs.

---

**(b) Discuss the application of geosynthetics in erosion control with two Kerala case studies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case Study 1: Wayanad District Landslide Rehabilitation (2019–2024)**

Following the devastating **Kavalappara (Meppadi) and Puthumala landslides (Aug 2019)** that killed >70 people, the **Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA)** and **NHAI** implemented large-scale bio-engineering with **coir geotextiles**:

- Coir geotextile blankets (700 g/m², open weave) were rolled over reshaped slopes.
- Fast-growing native grass species (Vetiver, Lemon grass) were planted through openings.
- Function: temporary soil retention (coir biodegrades in 3–5 years) while vegetation establishes.
- Outcome: reduced post-monsoon slope failures by ~60% in treated zones.

**Case Study 2: Bharathapuzha River Bank Protection, Palakkad (2021–2023)**

The Bharathapuzha (Kerala's second-longest river) suffered severe bank erosion during the 2018 and 2019 floods. **Geotextile tubes (geotubes)** of 1.5–3.0 m diameter were filled with sand dredged from the river bed and stacked along the eroding banks:

- Geotubes act as gravity retaining structures.
- Internal nonwoven geotextile skin retains sand while allowing water to drain.
- Combined with rock armour and vegetation.
- Outcome: ~1.2 km of river bank stabilized; no major erosion during 2022–2024 monsoons.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks in this question by:
> - Writing generic functions without naming the **specific Kerala project**.
> - Not specifying the **type of geosynthetic** used (don't just say "geotextile" — say "woven polypropylene geotextile, 200 g/m²").
> - Failing to mention **design parameters** like $O_{95}$, $k$, $T_{ult}$.
> - Skipping the **performance outcome** with quantitative data.

---

## 5.3 Part C Question (Optional Higher Order — 5 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Sample Practice]`**

**Discuss the role of geosynthetics in railway track substructure with reference to Indian Railways (Konkan Railway, Bhopal–Nagpur, and Kerala's Sabari Rail).**

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Functions in railway substructure:**
   - Separation between ballast and subballast.
   - Reinforcement of weak subgrade.
   - Drainage to prevent waterlogging of subgrade.
   - Reduction in ballast attrition.
2. **Konkan Railway experience:** Used nonwoven geotextiles over lateritic subgrade in coastal Maharashtra/Goa, improving track stability.
3. **Indian Railways RDSO Specification:** RDSO/2018/GE:0014 mandates use of geotextiles for new line construction in problematic soil.
4. **Kerala Sabari Rail Project (Angamaly–Erumely):** Will use geogrid + PVD combination for embankments on Kuttanad soft clays.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

### Core Knowledge

- [ ] Geosynthetics are **polymeric** engineering materials used with soil/rock.
- [ ] Six primary functions: **Separation, Reinforcement, Filtration, Drainage, Barrier, Protection**.
- [ ] Major types: geotextile, geogrid, geonet, geomembrane, geocomposite, geocell, GCL, geofoam.
- [ ] Common polymers: **PP, PE, PET, PA, PVC**.

### High-Yield Formulas

- [ ] BCR (Binquet–Lee): $BCR = 1 + \frac{2c_a}{q_u B}\sum b_i h_i$
- [ ] Allowable tensile strength: $T_{allow} = \frac{T_{ult}}{RF_{ID} \cdot RF_{CR} \cdot RF_{BD}}$
- [ ] Anchorage length: $L_e = \frac{T_{design}}{\sigma_v \tan\delta \cdot 2}$
- [ ] Transmissivity: $\theta = k_p \cdot t$
- [ ] Filter criterion: $O_{95} \leq (1\text{–}2) D_{85}$

### Kerala-Specific Memory Aids

- [ ] **Pavements:** NH-66, Kochi bypass — geogrid + nonwoven geotextile.
- [ ] **Embankments:** Kochi Metro, Sabari Rail — basal geogrid + PVDs.
- [ ] **Railways:** Konkan Railway model, RDSO specifications.
- [ ] **Erosion Control:** Coir geotextiles in Wayanad, geotubes in Bharathapuzha.
- [ ] **Kuttanad soft clays:** $c_u = 5$–$20$ kPa, settlement 0.5–1.5 m.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

- [ ] Do NOT confuse **cross-plane permeability ($k_n$)** with **in-plane permeability ($k_p$)**.
- [ ] Do NOT use **geogrid** for filtration — use **geotextile**.
- [ ] Do NOT forget **reduction factors (RF)** when computing allowable tensile strength.
- [ ] Do NOT assume geosynthetics are **biodegradable** (except coir/jute) — most are petrochemical and last 75–100+ years.

### Numerical Practice Tips

- [ ] For BCR problems, always compute $q_u$ first using Terzaghi before applying reinforcement formula.
- [ ] For anchorage length problems, $\sigma_v$ varies with depth — compute at mid-height for first-order approximation.
- [ ] Always specify the **safety factor** (typically 1.3–1.5 for permanent structures).
